items.label: Install compiler (global or dev dep)
items.code:
npm i -D typescript
npx tsc --version

items.label: Compile a single file → .js
items.code:
tsc app.ts        # emits app.js
tsc app.ts --outDir dist

items.label: Init a project config
items.code: tsc --init        # creates tsconfig.json

items.label: Compile whole project (uses tsconfig)
items.code:
tsc               # compiles per tsconfig, no file args
tsc --watch       # recompile on save
items.note: Passing file args to tsc IGNORES tsconfig.json.

items.label: Type-check only, no output
items.code: tsc --noEmit
items.note: Great for CI / pre-commit gates.

items.label: Run TS directly (no manual build)
items.code: npx tsx app.ts    # or: npx ts-node app.ts

items.label: Language of TS
items.note: TS is a STRONGLY-typed superset of JS, statically checked at compile time. It compiles (transpiles) down to plain JS; types are fully erased at runtime — no runtime cost, no runtime checks.

items.label: Minimal sane config
strict
items.note: Umbrella flag: turns on noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, etc. Always keep true.

items.label: target vs module
items.note: target = JS syntax level emitted; module = module system (CommonJS | ESNext | NodeNext).

items.label: outDir / rootDir
items.note: Keep source in rootDir, build output in outDir so they don't mix.

items.label: noImplicitAny / strictNullChecks
items.note: Flag untyped params; make null|undefined distinct types you must handle.

items.label: Primitives & annotations
Type inference — skip redundant annotations
items.code:
let count = 5;        // inferred number
count = "no";         // Error
items.note: Let TS infer locals; annotate function params & public APIs.

items.label: Functions: params & return
items.code:
function add(a: number, b: number): number { return a + b; }
const greet = (n: string): void => console.log(n);

items.label: Optional & default params
items.code:
function log(msg: string, level?: string) {}
function inc(x: number, by = 1) {}
items.note: Optional params must come after required ones.

items.label: any / unknown / never
items.note: Prefer unknown over any — forces a type check before use.

items.label: Union & literal types
items.code:
let x: string | number;
let dir: "left" | "right";

items.label: null vs undefined
items.code: let v: string | null = null;
items.note: With strictNullChecks, null isn't assignable to string.

items.label: Interface (object shape)
Type alias (any type, not just objects)
Interface extends / merges
items.code: interface Admin extends User { role: string }
items.note: Interfaces are open — same-name declarations MERGE. Types cannot re-declare.

items.label: Type composition (intersection)
items.code: type Admin = User & { role: string };

items.label: Interface vs Type — pick
items.note: Interface for object/class shapes & public APIs (extendable, mergeable). Type for unions, primitives, tuples, mapped/conditional types.

items.label: Method / index signatures
Array declarations (two syntaxes)
items.code:
let nums: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
let strs: Array<string> = ["a"];

items.label: Array of objects
items.code: let users: User[] = [{ id: 1, name: "a", createdAt: new Date() }];

items.label: readonly array
items.code:
let ro: readonly number[] = [1, 2];
// ro.push(3) → Error

items.label: Tuple (fixed length + types)
items.code:
let pair: [string, number] = ["age", 30];
let rgb: [number, number, number];
items.note: Order & length are type-checked; useful for return-multiple.

items.label: Numeric enum
items.code:
enum Direction { Up, Down, Left, Right }
Direction.Up      // 0
items.note: Auto-increments from 0; numeric enums are reverse-mapped.

items.label: String enum
items.note: String enums are safer/readable; no reverse mapping.

items.label: const enum (inlined, zero runtime)
items.code: const enum Log { Info, Warn }
items.note: Emits no JS object — values inlined. Or skip enums, use a union of literals.

items.label: Generic function
Generic with array
items.code: function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined { return arr[0]; }

items.label: Constraints (extends)
items.note: Constrains T so you can safely access .length.

items.label: Generic interface / type
items.code:
interface ApiResp<T> { data: T; status: number }
type Pair<K, V> = { key: K; value: V };

items.label: Generic class
Multiple type params + defaults
items.code: function merge<A, B = {}>(a: A, b: B) { return { ...a, ...b }; }

items.label: Built-in utility types
items.code: Partial Required Readonly Pick Omit Record ReturnType Awaited
items.note: Prefer these over hand-rolling generic transforms.

items.label: Named exports
Named imports
items.code:
import { PI, add } from "./math";
import { add as sum } from "./math";   // rename

items.label: Default export / import
items.note: One default per module; import name is arbitrary.

items.label: Mix default + named
items.code: import User, { PI } from "./mod";

items.label: Namespace / wildcard import
items.code:
import * as math from "./math";
math.add(1, 2);

items.label: Re-export (barrel index.ts)
items.code:
export { add } from "./math";
export * from "./user";

items.label: Type-only import/export
items.code:
import type { User } from "./user";
export type { ID } from "./types";
items.note: Erased at compile time — avoids circular-import & runtime pitfalls.

tips: Types are erased at runtime — you cannot `if (x instanceof MyInterface)`. Use type guards, `typeof`, `in`, or discriminated unions to narrow.
Prefer `unknown` over `any`: `any` disables all checking and silently spreads; `unknown` forces you to narrow before use.
`tsc file.ts` ignores tsconfig.json entirely — run bare `tsc` (or `tsc -p tsconfig.json`) to honor project settings.
Turn on `strict` from day one. Retrofitting strictNullChecks onto a large codebase is painful.
Interfaces merge across declarations (declaration merging) and can `extends`; type aliases cannot be reopened — use interfaces for extensible public shapes.
Avoid enums when a union of string literals works: `type Status = 'active' | 'done'` is lighter, fully erased, and easier to narrow.
Use `import type`/`export type` for type-only symbols to prevent accidental runtime imports and circular-dependency issues.
Let inference do the work for locals and return types; annotate function parameters and exported API boundaries explicitly.

items.label: Authentication vs Authorization
items.note: AuthN = who are you (login). AuthZ = what can you do (roles/permissions). Different concerns.

items.label: Install the usual stack
items.code: npm i express jsonwebtoken bcrypt cookie-parser cors

items.label: Never store plaintext passwords — hash with bcrypt
items.note: bcrypt auto-salts. Never use md5/sha256 for passwords.

items.label: Keep the secret out of code
items.note: Leaking the secret = anyone can forge valid tokens.

items.label: Sign a token (login success)
Verify a token (protected route)
items.code:
const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
// decoded.id  -> throws if invalid/expired

items.label: Structure: header.payload.signature
items.note: 3 base64url parts joined by '.'. Payload is only ENCODED, not encrypted — anyone can read it. Never put secrets/passwords in it.

items.label: Decode without verifying (debug only)
items.code: jwt.decode(token); // no signature check — never trust for auth

items.label: Store token after login (frontend)
items.code: localStorage.setItem("token", token);

items.label: Send on every request via Authorization header
items.note: Convention is the 'Bearer <token>' prefix.

items.label: Set a default header once
items.code: axios.defaults.headers.common["Authorization"] = "Bearer " + token;

items.label: Logout = remove it
items.code: localStorage.removeItem("token");

items.label: Tradeoff
items.note: Simple, works cross-domain/mobile. But JS-readable → any XSS can steal it. Not auto-sent by browser.

items.label: Set an httpOnly cookie on login
Read cookies with cookie-parser
Clear on logout
items.code: res.clearCookie("token");

items.label: Tradeoff
items.note: Auto-sent by browser every request; httpOnly blocks XSS token theft. But vulnerable to CSRF → use sameSite + CSRF tokens.

items.label: httpOnly
items.note: Blocks document.cookie access from JS. Defeats XSS token exfiltration.

items.label: secure
items.note: Cookie only sent over HTTPS. Required when sameSite: 'none'.

items.label: sameSite
items.code: sameSite: "strict" | "lax" | "none"
items.note: strict = never cross-site; lax = sent on top-level GET nav (default-ish); none = always but needs secure.

items.label: maxAge / expires
items.code:
maxAge: 3600000            // relative ms
expires: new Date(Date.now()+3600000)
items.note: No maxAge/expires = session cookie (dies on browser close).

items.label: domain / path
items.code: domain: ".example.com", path: "/"
items.note: Controls scope — which hosts/routes the cookie is sent to.

items.label: signed cookies
items.note: Signs value so client tampering is detectable.

items.label: Signup: hash + store
Signin: compare + issue token
Auth middleware (header)
Auth middleware (cookie variant)
items.code: const token = req.cookies.token; // rest identical

items.label: Protect a route
items.code: app.get("/me", auth, (req, res) => res.json({ id: req.userId }));

items.label: CORS must allow credentials for cookies
items.note: credentials:true + wildcard origin is rejected by browsers.

items.label: Send cookies from React
items.code:
axios.get("/me", { withCredentials: true });
// or global: axios.defaults.withCredentials = true;
items.note: fetch equivalent: credentials: 'include'.

items.label: Login flow (header/localStorage)
items.code:
const { data } = await axios.post("/signin", { email, password });
localStorage.setItem("token", data.token);

items.label: Protect a page

tips: JWT payload is base64-encoded, NOT encrypted — decode it on jwt.io. Never store passwords/secrets in the payload.

localStorage token = exposed to XSS (any injected JS reads it). httpOnly cookie = exposed to CSRF instead. Pick your threat and mitigate it (sameSite/CSRF tokens for cookies, strict CSP for localStorage).
Always set expiresIn on tokens — a stolen token with no expiry is valid forever.
For cookie auth cross-origin: backend needs cors({ credentials:true, origin:<exact> }) AND frontend needs withCredentials:true. Both or it silently fails.
sameSite:'none' REQUIRES secure:true — browsers drop it otherwise. Localhost over http can bite you here.
Use bcrypt.compare (constant-time), never === on hashes; never hash passwords with md5/sha.
Return 401 for 'not authenticated' (no/bad token) and 403 for 'authenticated but not allowed'.
Keep JWT_SECRET in env, long and random; rotating it invalidates all existing tokens.

items.label: Request/Response (HTTP)
items.note: Client initiates, server responds once. Stateless, short-lived. REST/GraphQL. No server push.

items.label: Short polling
items.code: setInterval(() => fetch('/updates'), 3000)
items.note: Client re-asks on a timer. Simple, but wasteful + high latency between polls.

items.label: Long polling
items.note: Server holds request open until data ready, then client re-requests. Fallback when WS unavailable.

items.label: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
items.code:
const es = new EventSource('/stream');
es.onmessage = e => console.log(e.data);
items.note: One-way server→client over HTTP, auto-reconnect built in. No client→server channel.

items.label: WebSockets
items.note: Full-duplex, persistent, bidirectional over a single TCP connection. Use for chat, live cursors, games, real-time dashboards.

items.label: WebRTC
items.note: Peer-to-peer (browser↔browser) for media/data. Needs a signaling server (often WS) to establish.

items.label: Upgrade handshake
items.code:
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: <base64>
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
items.note: Starts as HTTP GET; server replies 101 Switching Protocols. Same TCP socket is reused after.

items.label: Schemes
items.code:
ws://host:port   // plain
wss://host:port  // TLS (use in prod)

items.label: Frames, not messages
items.note: Protocol is frame-based (text/binary/ping/pong/close). Long-lived and stateful — no per-message HTTP overhead.

items.label: Stays open
items.note: No new handshake per message = low latency. Cost: server holds a connection/memory per client — plan for scale.

items.label: Install
items.code:
npm i ws            # runtime
npm i -D @types/ws  # TS types

items.label: Standalone server
Attach to existing HTTP server
items.note: Lets WS and REST share one port. Use { noServer:true } + server.on('upgrade') for path/auth-based routing.

items.label: Broadcast to all clients
JSON message protocol
items.note: Wrap everything in a { type, payload } envelope; parse defensively in try/catch.

items.label: Auth on upgrade
items.note: Browsers can't set custom headers on WS — pass tokens via query string or a post-connect auth message.

items.label: Browser WebSocket API
Guard sends until OPEN
items.code: if (ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) ws.send(msg);
items.note: readyState: 0 CONNECTING, 1 OPEN, 2 CLOSING, 3 CLOSED. Sending before OPEN throws.

items.label: React (useEffect cleanup)
items.note: Always close on unmount. In StrictMode dev double-invoke, guard against duplicate connections.

items.label: Reconnect with backoff
items.note: Native WS does NOT auto-reconnect. Cap the delay; reset retry counter on successful open.

items.label: Can't run WS in route handlers
items.note: App Router / API routes are serverless/short-lived — no long-lived socket. Run the WS server as a separate process.

items.label: Separate ws service pattern
items.note: Deploy Next.js (frontend/HTTP) and a standalone Node `ws` server independently; client connects to the ws service URL via env var.

items.label: Custom server (self-hosted only)
items.note: Only works when you control the Node process. NOT supported on Vercel serverless — use a dedicated host (Render/Railway/EC2).

items.label: Client URL via env
items.code: const ws = new WebSocket(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_WS_URL!);
items.note: NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix exposes it to the browser bundle.

items.label: The problem
items.note: Sockets are stateful + sticky to one instance. With N servers, a user on server A can't directly reach a user on server B.

items.label: Sticky sessions at LB
items.note: A client must keep hitting the same instance (the socket lives there). Enable session affinity / IP hash on the load balancer.

items.label: Redis Pub/Sub fan-out
items.note: Every WS instance subscribes; a message published on any node reaches all nodes, which relay to their local sockets.

items.label: Horizontal architecture
items.note: LB → [ws-1, ws-2, ws-3] all connected to a Redis (or Kafka/NATS) pub/sub bus. Add instances to scale connections.

items.label: Heartbeat / dead-connection reap
items.note: TCP can silently drop; ping/pong detects zombies so you free memory. Clear the interval on wss 'close'.

items.label: Managed alternatives
items.note: socket.io (rooms + @socket.io/redis-adapter), Ably, Pusher, or Redis for presence/state — skip hand-rolling scale.

tips: ws message events give you a Buffer, not a string — always .toString() (or JSON.parse(raw.toString())) before use.
Wrap JSON.parse in try/catch: a single malformed frame from a client shouldn't crash the whole server.
Browsers can't set custom headers (e.g. Authorization) on a WebSocket — authenticate via query param or a first post-connect message.
Native WebSocket has zero built-in reconnection — implement exponential backoff yourself or use reconnecting-websocket / socket.io.
Vercel and most serverless platforms can't host long-lived WS — run the socket server on a persistent host (Render, Railway, Fly, EC2).
One process = one CPU core; sockets don't share memory across instances. Use Redis pub/sub + sticky sessions to scale out.
Always ws.close() on client unmount and clean up server timers on 'close' — leaked sockets/intervals exhaust memory fast.
Use wss:// (TLS) in production; ws:// is blocked from https:// pages by mixed-content rules.

items.label: Relational (SQL): Postgres, MySQL, SQLite
items.note: Structured tables + fixed schema, ACID transactions, strong consistency. Default choice for most CRUD apps.

items.label: NoSQL: MongoDB (document), Redis (KV), Cassandra (wide-col), Neo4j (graph)
items.note: Flexible/denormalized schema, horizontal scale. Good for caching, huge write volume, unstructured data.

items.label: Why SQL over NoSQL
items.note: Enforced schema catches bad data at insert; JOINs avoid duplicating data; transactions give all-or-nothing correctness (money, inventory). Relationships are first-class.

items.label: When NoSQL wins
items.note: Rapidly changing/unknown schema, extreme horizontal scale, or key-value/cache workloads. Trade consistency for availability/speed.

items.label: ACID (why relational is safe)
items.note: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the guarantee that a transaction fully commits or fully rolls back.

items.label: Run Postgres fast (Docker)
items.code: docker run -d --name pg -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mypass -p 5432:5432 postgres

items.label: Connect with psql
items.code: psql "postgresql://postgres:mypass@localhost:5432/postgres"
items.note: Full connection string format: postgresql://USER:PASS@HOST:PORT/DBNAME

items.label: Create / drop a database
items.code:
CREATE DATABASE myapp;
DROP DATABASE myapp;
items.note: Or from shell: createdb myapp / dropdb myapp

items.label: psql meta-commands
items.code:
\l          -- list databases
\c myapp    -- connect to db
\dt         -- list tables
\d users    -- describe table
\q          -- quit
items.note: Backslash commands are psql-only, not SQL.

items.label: CREATE TABLE with common types + constraints
items.code:
CREATE TABLE users (
  id       SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  username VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  email    VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  age      INT CHECK (age >= 0),
  active   BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
  created  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
items.note: SERIAL = auto-increment int. Prefer GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY on PG10+. Use BIGSERIAL for large tables.

items.label: Core types
items.note: INT/BIGINT, SERIAL, VARCHAR(n)/TEXT, BOOLEAN, TIMESTAMP/TIMESTAMPTZ, NUMERIC(10,2) for money, DATE, JSONB, UUID.

items.label: Constraints
items.note: PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, DEFAULT, CHECK(...), REFERENCES (FK). Constraints reject bad rows at write time.

items.label: Alter schema
Index for fast lookups
items.code: CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
items.note: PK/UNIQUE are auto-indexed. Index columns you filter/JOIN on; each index slows writes.

items.label: INSERT (return the new row)
items.note: RETURNING avoids a second SELECT to get the generated id.

items.label: SELECT with filter, sort, limit
items.code:
SELECT id, username FROM users
WHERE active = true AND age > 18
ORDER BY created DESC
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 0;

items.label: UPDATE (always scope with WHERE)
items.code: UPDATE users SET active = false WHERE id = 5;
items.note: No WHERE = updates EVERY row. Same trap on DELETE.

items.label: DELETE
items.code: DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 5;

items.label: Aggregates + GROUP BY
items.note: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters after aggregation.

items.label: Upsert
items.code:
INSERT INTO users (username, email) VALUES ('alice','a@x.com')
ON CONFLICT (username) DO UPDATE SET email = EXCLUDED.email;

items.label: Install
items.code: npm install pg

items.label: Connect with a Pool (preferred)
items.note: Pool reuses connections across requests. A single Client is fine for scripts, not for a server.

items.label: Parameterized query (async/await)
items.note: res.rows is an array of objects; res.rowCount is the count.

items.label: NEVER string-concat user input (SQL injection)
items.code:
// BAD: `SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='${email}'`
// GOOD: pool.query('... WHERE email = $1', [email])
items.note: $1, $2 placeholders are the fix — the driver escapes safely.

items.label: Minimal Express endpoint
Transaction (all-or-nothing)
items.note: Must use a single checked-out client for a transaction, not pool.query.

items.label: One-to-Many (user has many posts)
items.note: FK lives on the 'many' side. ON DELETE CASCADE removes child rows with the parent.

items.label: One-to-One
items.note: FK is also the PK (or UNIQUE) → at most one row per user.

items.label: Many-to-Many (join/junction table)
items.note: Composite PK prevents duplicate enrollments.

items.label: FK referential integrity
items.note: You can't insert a post with a user_id that doesn't exist, and can't delete a referenced user unless CASCADE/SET NULL is set.

items.label: INNER JOIN — only matching rows
items.note: Rows with no match on either side are dropped.

items.label: LEFT JOIN — keep all left rows
items.note: Users with zero posts still appear; p.title is NULL for them.

items.label: Many-to-many via junction
items.code:
SELECT s.name, c.title
FROM students s
JOIN student_courses sc ON sc.student_id = s.id
JOIN courses c        ON c.id = sc.course_id;

items.label: JOIN types cheat
items.note: INNER = intersection. LEFT/RIGHT = keep all of one side. FULL = keep both. CROSS = every combination (cartesian).

items.label: Aggregate across a join
items.code:
SELECT u.username, COUNT(p.id) AS post_count
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN posts p ON p.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.id, u.username;
items.note: LEFT JOIN + COUNT(child.id) correctly yields 0 for empty parents (COUNT(*) would give 1).

tips: Always parameterize ($1, $2) — never interpolate user input into SQL. This is the #1 way to prevent SQL injection.
UPDATE/DELETE without a WHERE clause hits every row. Test the WHERE with a SELECT first.
Use a connection Pool in servers, not a single Client — and always client.release() in a finally block.
Index columns you filter or JOIN on frequently, but not everything: each index adds write cost and storage.
Use TIMESTAMPTZ (not TIMESTAMP) and NUMERIC (not FLOAT) for money to avoid timezone bugs and rounding errors.
Transactions require one checked-out client with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK — pool.query() runs each statement on a possibly different connection.
Prefer LEFT JOIN when you must keep parent rows with no children; INNER JOIN silently drops them.
Store DATABASE_URL in env vars, never hardcode credentials; load with dotenv in dev.

items.label: Run via Docker (fastest, recommended)
items.code:
docker run -d --name redis -p 6379:6379 redis
# with persistence volume
docker run -d --name redis -p 6379:6379 -v redis-data:/data redis redis-server --appendonly yes
items.note: Default port 6379. -d = detached. Data lost on container rm unless volume mounted.

items.label: Install natively
items.code:
# macOS
brew install redis && brew services start redis
# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install redis-server && sudo systemctl start redis
# Windows: use Docker or WSL2 (no official native build)

items.label: Connect with the CLI
items.code:
redis-cli                 # localhost:6379
redis-cli -h host -p 6379 -a <password>
redis-cli ping            # -> PONG
redis-cli -u redis://default:pass@host:6379

items.label: Managed / hosted
items.code:
# Redis Cloud, AWS ElastiCache, Upstash
# connect via TLS URL:
redis-cli -u rediss://default:<pass>@<host>:6379
items.note: rediss:// (double s) = TLS. Managed services are the norm in production.

items.label: Quick health/inspect
items.code:
redis-cli info
redis-cli monitor          # live stream of every command (debug only)
redis-cli keys '*'         # NEVER in prod - blocks; use SCAN

sections.title: Redis as a DB — Core Commands

items.label: Strings (get/set + expiry)
items.code:
SET user:1 "chan"
GET user:1
SET session:abc "data" EX 3600     # expire in 3600s
SETEX session:abc 3600 "data"       # same thing
TTL session:abc                     # seconds left, -1 none, -2 gone
INCR counter                        # atomic ++
INCRBY counter 10
items.note: SET ... NX = set only if not exists (great for locks). GET on missing key = nil.

items.label: Hashes (objects)
items.code:
HSET user:1 name chan age 30
HGET user:1 name
HGETALL user:1
HINCRBY user:1 age 1
items.note: Store an object as one key instead of many string keys.

items.label: Sets & Sorted Sets
items.code:
SADD tags a b c            # unique members
SMEMBERS tags
SISMEMBER tags a          # 1/0
ZADD leaderboard 100 chan 90 alice   # score member
ZRANGE leaderboard 0 -1 WITHSCORES REV  # top-first
items.note: Sorted sets = leaderboards, rate limiters, delayed queues (score = timestamp).

items.label: Keys, expiry & delete
items.code:
EXPIRE key 60
PERSIST key                # remove TTL
DEL key
EXISTS key
SCAN 0 MATCH user:* COUNT 100   # cursor-based, non-blocking

items.label: When to use Redis as DB
items.note: In-memory = blazing fast but volatile. Ideal for caching, sessions, counters, rate-limiting, leaderboards. Not a durable primary store unless AOF/RDB persistence tuned — treat as cache-first.

items.label: Producer pushes, worker pops (FIFO)
items.code:
# producer
LPUSH jobs '{"id":1,"task":"email"}'
# consumer (FIFO): push left, pop right
RPOP jobs
LLEN jobs                  # queue depth
items.note: LPUSH + RPOP = FIFO. LPUSH + LPOP = LIFO (stack). Pick one convention and stick to it.

items.label: Blocking pop (efficient worker loop)
items.code:
BRPOP jobs 0               # block forever until an item
BRPOP jobs 5               # block up to 5s, then nil
# returns [listName, value]
items.note: Use BRPOP, NOT a RPOP polling loop — blocking avoids hammering the server. timeout 0 = wait indefinitely.

items.label: Reliable queue (survive worker crash)
items.code:
BLMOVE jobs processing LEFT RIGHT 0
# ... do work ...
LREM processing 1 <value>   # ack: remove from processing
items.note: Plain BRPOP loses the message if the worker dies mid-job. BLMOVE atomically moves to a 'processing' list so you can recover/retry. (RPOPLPUSH/BRPOPLPUSH are the deprecated equivalents.)

items.label: Pub/Sub vs Queue — the key difference
items.note: Queue (list): message consumed by exactly ONE worker, persists until popped, work is load-balanced. Pub/Sub: fire-and-forget broadcast to ALL current subscribers, nothing stored — offline subscribers miss it.

items.label: Streams (durable queue upgrade)
items.code:
XADD mystream '*' task email
XREAD BLOCK 0 STREAMS mystream $
XGROUP CREATE mystream grp1 0
XREADGROUP GROUP grp1 c1 COUNT 1 STREAMS mystream >
items.note: Redis Streams = persistent log + consumer groups + acks (XACK). Use over lists when you need replay, multiple consumer groups, or delivery guarantees.

items.label: node-redis (official) setup
items.note: v4+ is promise-based and requires explicit await client.connect(). Commands are camelCase (client.hSet).

items.label: Basic commands (async)
items.note: Redis stores strings — JSON.stringify/parse objects yourself.

items.label: ioredis (popular alt) setup
items.note: ioredis auto-connects (no .connect()), uses lowercase method names, has built-in cluster/sentinel support.

items.label: Worker loop (blocking pop)
items.note: Run the blocking loop on a DEDICATED client — the connection is busy while blocked and can't issue other commands.

items.label: Subscribe & publish
items.code:
# terminal 1
SUBSCRIBE news
# terminal 2
PUBLISH news "hello"        # -> returns # of subscribers that got it
items.note: PUBLISH returns the count of receivers. If 0 subscribers, the message vanishes — no storage, no replay.

items.label: Pattern subscribe
items.code:
PSUBSCRIBE news.*          # matches news.sports, news.tech
PUBLISH news.sports "goal!"
PUNSUBSCRIBE news.*
items.note: Glob-style: * (any chars), ? (one char), [ae] (set).

items.label: Inspect channels
items.code:
PUBSUB CHANNELS           # active channels with subscribers
PUBSUB NUMSUB news        # subscriber count per channel
PUBSUB NUMPAT             # active pattern subs

items.label: Subscriber (needs its own connection)
items.note: CRITICAL: a subscribed client cannot run normal commands. Use client.duplicate() so your main client stays free for get/set.

items.label: Publisher
items.code: await client.publish('news', JSON.stringify({ title: 'hi' }));
items.note: Publish from the main client; subscribe on the duplicated one.

items.label: Pattern subscribe (node-redis v4)
ioredis pub/sub (event-based)
items.note: ioredis emits a 'message' event rather than a per-channel callback.

items.label: Typical exercise: producer + worker via list queue
items.note: Classic 'code submission' style: HTTP handler pushes job, separate worker process blocks on BRPOP, pushes result to a per-user list/channel.

items.label: Return result to a specific client
items.note: Combine queue (work distribution) + pub/sub (push result back) for request/response over Redis.

items.label: Decision cheat
items.note: One consumer, must-not-lose, load-balance -> List/Stream queue. Broadcast to many, real-time, OK to miss if offline -> Pub/Sub. Need replay + acks + groups -> Streams.

tips: Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget: no persistence, no delivery guarantee. Offline or slow subscribers miss messages entirely — use Streams or a list queue when you need durability.
A subscribed connection is 'locked' into subscriber mode and can't run GET/SET/etc. Always client.duplicate() (node-redis) or use a second client for subscribing.
Prefer blocking BRPOP/BLMOVE over polling RPOP in a loop — polling wastes CPU and network round-trips.
Plain BRPOP loses the job if the worker crashes mid-processing. Use BLMOVE into a 'processing' list (or Streams with XACK) for at-least-once delivery.
Never run KEYS * in production — it blocks the single-threaded server. Use SCAN with a cursor instead.
Redis is single-threaded: each command is atomic, but a slow command (KEYS, big SORT, large MULTI) stalls everyone. Keep operations small.
Redis is in-memory and volatile by default — enable AOF (appendonly yes) or RDB snapshots if you can't afford to lose data on restart.
Redis stores only strings/bytes. Always JSON.stringify on write and JSON.parse on read; set EX/TTL on cache keys so memory doesn't grow unbounded.

items.label: CI (Continuous Integration)
items.note: Every push/PR auto-runs lint + tests + build so broken code is caught before merge. Keep branches short-lived, merge often.

items.label: CD — Delivery vs Deployment
items.note: Continuous DELIVERY = every green build is release-ready but a human clicks deploy. Continuous DEPLOYMENT = green build ships to prod automatically, no gate.

items.label: Typical pipeline stages
items.code: checkout -> install -> lint -> test -> build -> dockerize -> push -> deploy -> pull & run

items.label: Where it runs
items.note: GitHub Actions spins up an ephemeral VM (ubuntu-latest) per job; filesystem/env do NOT persist across jobs unless you use artifacts/cache.

items.label: Trigger-on-merge model
items.note: CI on PRs (push to feature branch), CD on push to main. Gate prod behind branch protection + required checks.

items.label: File location
items.code: .github/workflows/ci.yml   # any *.yml under this dir is auto-detected
items.note: Repo must have Actions enabled. One file = one workflow.

items.label: Minimal workflow skeleton
items.code:
name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

items.label: Trigger events (on:)
items.code:
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request:
  workflow_dispatch:        # manual 'Run workflow' button
  schedule: [{ cron: '0 2 * * *' }]

items.label: Checkout the repo (required first step)
items.code: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
items.note: Without this the runner has no source code. fetch-depth: 0 for full history/tags.

items.label: Setup a language runtime
items.code:
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: 20
    cache: npm            # caches ~/.npm keyed on lockfile

items.label: Run a shell step
items.code:
- name: Install
  run: npm ci             # ci = clean, lockfile-exact, deletes node_modules

items.label: job dependencies
items.code:
jobs:
  test: { runs-on: ubuntu-latest, steps: [...] }
  deploy:
    needs: test           # deploy waits for test to pass
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

items.label: Standard CI job
items.code:
jobs:
  ci:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm run build
      - run: npm test

items.label: npm ci vs npm install
items.note: Use npm ci in CI: fails if package-lock.json is out of sync, reproducible, faster. Never npm install in pipelines.

items.label: Matrix (test across versions)
items.code:
strategy:
  matrix:
    node: [18, 20, 22]
steps:
  - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    with: { node-version: ${{ matrix.node }} }

items.label: Fail fast / continue
items.code:
strategy:
  fail-fast: false        # let all matrix legs finish
items.note: Default fail-fast: true cancels siblings on first failure.

items.label: Manual cache (non-node)
items.code:
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.cache
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/lockfile') }}

items.label: Path filter — skip unaffected apps
items.code:
on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'apps/web/**'
      - 'packages/ui/**'
      - '.github/workflows/**'
items.note: Workflow only fires when matching files change — avoids rebuilding the whole monorepo.

items.label: Run in a subdir
items.code:
- name: Build web
  working-directory: apps/web
  run: npm ci && npm run build
items.note: Or set defaults.run.working-directory at job level.

items.label: Matrix over packages
items.code:
strategy:
  matrix:
    app: [web, admin, api]
steps:
  - run: npm run build --workspace=apps/${{ matrix.app }}

items.label: Turborepo / affected builds
items.code: - run: npx turbo run build --filter=...[origin/main]
items.note: Turbo/Nx compute the dependency graph and build only affected + downstream packages; caches unchanged ones.

items.label: npm workspaces
items.code:
npm ci                          # installs ALL workspaces from root lockfile
npm run build -w apps/web       # target one workspace

items.label: Dockerfile (multi-stage, monorepo app)
items.code:
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]

items.label: Login to registry (Docker Hub)
Build & push in one step
items.code:
- uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
  with:
    context: .
    file: ./apps/web/Dockerfile
    push: true
    tags: |
      myuser/web:latest
      myuser/web:${{ github.sha }}
items.note: Tag with the commit SHA for immutable, rollback-able deploys; latest is a moving pointer.

items.label: Buildx + layer cache
items.code:
- uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
- uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
  with:
    cache-from: type=gha
    cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

items.label: Push to GHCR instead
items.note: GITHUB_TOKEN is auto-provided; add permissions: { packages: write } to the job.

sections.title: Deploy step — SSH to server & pull the image

items.label: Deploy job (gated on CI)
items.code:
deploy:
  needs: build
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
  steps:
    - uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
      with:
        host: ${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }}
        username: ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}
        key: ${{ secrets.SSH_KEY }}
        script: |
          docker pull myuser/web:latest
          docker stop web || true
          docker rm web || true
          docker run -d --name web -p 80:3000 --restart unless-stopped myuser/web:latest

items.label: Pull the image on the box
items.code: docker pull myuser/web:latest
items.note: Server must be logged in (docker login) if the repo is private.

items.label: Zero-ish downtime swap
items.note: || true so the run doesn't fail on first deploy when no container exists yet.

items.label: Prune old images
items.code: docker image prune -f
items.note: Servers fill up fast pulling every SHA tag — prune or cron it.

items.label: Compose-based deploy
items.code: docker compose pull && docker compose up -d
items.note: Pulls new images referenced in compose.yml and recreates changed services only.

items.label: Reference a secret
items.code: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_TOKEN }}
items.note: Masked in logs. NEVER echo raw secrets. Repo/org level or per-environment.

items.label: Add secrets via gh CLI
items.code:
gh secret set DOCKERHUB_TOKEN
gh secret set SSH_KEY < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
gh secret list

items.label: Built-in GITHUB_TOKEN
items.code:
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write
items.note: Auto-generated per run, scoped to the repo; grant least privilege explicitly.

items.label: Contexts / expressions
items.code:
${{ github.sha }}      # commit
${{ github.ref }}      # refs/heads/main
${{ github.actor }}    # who triggered
${{ env.MY_VAR }}

items.label: Environment-scoped deploys
items.code:
jobs:
  deploy:
    environment: production   # enables required reviewers / protection rules
items.note: Adds a manual approval gate — turns continuous delivery into deployment-with-a-gate.

items.label: Job-level env

tips: Tag Docker images with ${{ github.sha }}, not just latest — you get immutable builds and can roll back to any commit instantly.
Use npm ci (never npm install) in pipelines: it's reproducible, respects the lockfile, and fails loudly on drift.
In monorepos, gate workflows with on.push.paths so touching apps/web doesn't rebuild apps/api — huge CI-time savings.
needs: creates the dependency graph; without it jobs run in parallel and a deploy can race ahead of its tests.
|| true after docker stop/rm so the deploy script survives the first run when the container doesn't exist yet.
Cache aggressively: cache: npm in setup-node and type=gha for Docker layers cut minutes off every run.
Guard deploy with if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' so PR builds test but never ship to prod.
Runners are ephemeral — nothing persists between jobs. Pass data via actions/upload-artifact / download-artifact.

sections.title: Concept: NextAuth & Why Not JWT + localStorage

items.label: What is NextAuth (Auth.js)
items.note: Full auth library for Next.js: handles OAuth (Google/GitHub/...), email, and credentials login, sessions, CSRF, and callback routing out of the box. Backend + frontend helpers in one package.

items.label: Why NOT store a JWT in localStorage
items.note: localStorage is readable by ANY JS on the page → XSS steals the token. No httpOnly. Can't auto-send on requests. Hard to invalidate/rotate server-side.

items.label: What NextAuth does instead
items.note: Session token in an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie — JS can't read it, browser sends it automatically, built-in CSRF token. Session lookup happens server-side.

items.label: Session strategies
items.note: CredentialsProvider ONLY works with strategy: "jwt".

items.label: Install (v4)
items.code: npm install next-auth

items.label: Required env vars (.env.local)
items.code:
NEXTAUTH_URL=http://localhost:3000
NEXTAUTH_SECRET=<random-string>

items.label: Generate a secret
items.code: openssl rand -base64 32
items.note: Without NEXTAUTH_SECRET set, JWT sessions fail in production.

items.label: Provider secrets
items.code:
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=...
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=...
GITHUB_ID=...
GITHUB_SECRET=...

items.label: Catch-all route concept
items.note: [...nextauth] captures ALL sub-paths: /api/auth/signin, /signout, /callback/:provider, /session, /csrf, /providers. One handler serves every auth endpoint.

items.label: Pages Router: pages/api/auth/[...nextauth].ts
App Router: app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts
items.note: Folder is [...nextauth], file is route.ts. Export handler as BOTH GET and POST.

items.label: Give NextAuth access to the catch-all
items.note: The [...nextauth] path is literal — the folder/file must be exactly named that so NextAuth's internal router can resolve /api/auth/* to its handlers. Keep authOptions in its own module to reuse with getServerSession.

items.label: Credentials provider (email/password)
items.note: Return object = the user; return null to reject. Requires strategy: "jwt".

items.label: Google provider
items.note: Authorized redirect URI in Google Console: http://localhost:3000/api/auth/callback/google

items.label: GitHub provider
items.note: GitHub OAuth App callback URL: http://localhost:3000/api/auth/callback/github

items.label: Add fields to token & session
items.note: session callback only exposes what you copy from the token. user is only present on the first jwt call.

items.label: Read session on the server (App Router)
Read session in API route (Pages Router)
items.code:
import { getServerSession } from "next-auth/next";
const session = await getServerSession(req, res, authOptions);

items.label: Wrap app in SessionProvider
items.note: useSession() throws without a SessionProvider ancestor.

items.label: useSession
signIn
signOut
items.code:
import { signOut } from "next-auth/react";
signOut({ callbackUrl: "/" });

items.label: Point NextAuth to custom pages
items.note: NextAuth redirects to these instead of its built-in UI.

items.label: Custom signup / signin page
items.note: Signup (creating the user) is your own API route + db insert; then call signIn to log them in.

items.label: Protect routes via middleware
items.note: Redirects unauthenticated users to the signIn page automatically.

tips: CredentialsProvider requires session: { strategy: "jwt" } — it does not work with the database strategy.
authorize() must return a user object on success and null on failure; a thrown error surfaces as ?error=... on your error page.
OAuth callback URLs must match EXACTLY in the provider console: /api/auth/callback/google and /api/auth/callback/github (update for prod domain).
Custom session fields must be copied through BOTH callbacks: set them on token in jwt(), then read from token in session().
Always set NEXTAUTH_SECRET in production — missing it breaks JWT encryption/verification.
useSession() needs a <SessionProvider> ancestor; in the App Router that means a "use client" wrapper component in layout.tsx.
In App Router, keep authOptions in its own file (e.g. lib/auth.ts) so both the [...nextauth] route and getServerSession can import it.
Use signIn(..., { redirect: false }) for credentials so you can show inline errors instead of a full-page redirect.

items.label: Three backend services + one frontend, own tsconfig each
items.code:
upload/    # producer: clone repo -> S3 -> queue
deploy/    # worker: queue -> build -> S3
request/   # serve dist/<id>/* by subdomain
frontend/  # React (Vite) dashboard

items.label: Core deps (each Node service)
items.code:
npm i express cors redis aws-sdk simple-git
npm i -D typescript @types/express @types/cors ts-node

items.label: Run a service (dev)
items.code: npx tsc -b && node dist/index.js   # or: npx ts-node src/index.ts

items.label: Local Redis via Docker
items.code: docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis

items.label: S3 client pointed at Cloudflare R2
items.note: R2 is S3-compatible: same SDK, just set endpoint. Never hardcode keys.

items.label: Upload one file
items.note: aws-sdk v2 calls need .promise() to await.

items.label: Recursively list local files (source walk)
Non-enumerable deployment id (not auto-increment)
items.note: Random id doubles as subdomain + S3 key prefix; sequential ids let others enumerate deployments.

items.label: POST /deploy — clone, upload source, enqueue
items.note: Return id immediately; the slow build happens async in the worker.

items.label: GET /status?id= — for frontend polling
Redis clients
Blocking pull loop
items.note: brPop blocks with no busy-wait. LPUSH+BRPOP = FIFO at-least-once queue.

items.label: Build with child_process, capture logs + exit code
items.note: npm install runs untrusted postinstall scripts — sandbox in a container in prod.

items.label: Download S3 prefix (stream each object)
Upload built dist under its own prefix
items.note: Keep source (output/<id>) and build (dist/<id>) separate; artifact keyed by id => idempotent, rollback-able.

items.label: Resolve subdomain -> id, path -> S3 key
items.note: Default to index.html; for SPA client routes fall back to index.html on NoSuchKey, else return 404.

items.label: MIME by extension (assets 404/blank if wrong)
items.code:
const contentType = (p: string) =>
  p.endsWith(".html") ? "text/html"
: p.endsWith(".css")  ? "text/css"
: p.endsWith(".js")   ? "application/javascript"
: p.endsWith(".svg")  ? "image/svg+xml"
: "application/octet-stream";

items.label: Upload then poll status until deployed
items.note: Polling is stateless + reconnect-proof; fine for short builds. Use SSE/WebSocket for live log streaming.

items.label: Surface the live URL
items.note: Subdomains on localhost work in Chrome without /etc/hosts edits.

items.label: Env-based backend URL
items.code: const UPLOAD_URL = import.meta.env.VITE_UPLOAD_URL; // Vite exposes VITE_* only

items.label: Wildcard DNS -> request handler
items.code: *.yourdomain.com  A  <request-handler-ip>
items.note: One record routes every deployment subdomain to the same server; id parsed from Host header.

items.label: Status state machine (hash in Redis)
items.code: uploaded -> building -> deployed | failed
items.note: Atomic transitions; on build exit!=0 set status=failed and store stderr for the UI.

items.label: Sandbox untrusted builds
items.code: # per-build ephemeral container, no host mounts, network + cpu/mem limits, hard timeout
items.note: Malicious postinstall can exfiltrate secrets — never build on the host.

items.label: Durability upgrade path
items.code: Redis LPUSH/BRPOP -> Redis Streams (consumer groups) / SQS (visibility timeout + DLQ)
items.note: Naive RPOP loses in-flight jobs on worker crash; idempotent id-keyed builds make retries safe.

items.label: CDN in front of serving
items.note: Cache keyed on host+path so most requests never hit origin/S3.

tips: aws-sdk v2 calls are lazy — forget .promise() and nothing runs (or you await undefined). Migrate to @aws-sdk/client-s3 v3 for real promises.
Two Redis roles: the deploy worker BRPOPs the queue; the upload service HSETs status. Don't reuse a single subscribed connection for commands — a client in subscribe mode can't run normal commands.
Keep source and build artifacts under different prefixes (output/<id> vs dist/<id>). Serving the raw source instead of dist is the classic 'blank page' bug.
Set Content-Type from the file extension. Serving .js/.css as text/plain (or octet-stream) makes the browser refuse them and the site loads unstyled/broken.
Random, non-enumerable ids only — sequential ints let anyone guess other users' subdomains, and can collide under concurrent uploads.
At-least-once queue means a job can run twice; make builds idempotent and keyed by id so a re-run overwrites the same artifact rather than duplicating.
Capture build stdout/stderr + exit code and flip status to failed — a silent build failure looks identical to 'still building' to the user.
On localhost, id.localhost resolves automatically in Chrome; on a real domain you need a wildcard *.domain.com DNS record pointing at the request handler.

items.label: Layout — apps + shared packages
items.code:
apps/user-app        # Next.js (App Router, server actions, NextAuth)
apps/merchant-app    # Next.js merchant
apps/bank-webhook    # Express server (mock bank callback)
packages/db          # Prisma client + schema (imported everywhere)
packages/ui  packages/store
items.note: One Prisma schema/client lives in packages/db; every app imports it — never duplicate the schema.

items.label: Boot the DB (Postgres via Docker)
items.code: docker run -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mysecretpassword -d -p 5432:5432 postgres
items.note: Put DATABASE_URL in packages/db/.env, e.g. postgresql://postgres:mysecretpassword@localhost:5432/postgres

items.label: Install + migrate + generate + seed
items.code:
npm install                 # from repo root
cd packages/db
npx prisma migrate dev --name init
npx prisma generate
npx prisma db seed          # seeds alice/bob + balances
items.note: prisma generate is what makes the typed client available to the apps; re-run after any schema edit.

items.label: Run everything
items.code:
npm run dev                 # turbo runs dev across all apps
npm run dev -- --filter=user-app   # just one app
items.note: turbo.json: dev task has cache:false + persistent:true; build has dependsOn ['^build'].

items.label: Balance — money is Int (paise), never float
items.code:
model Balance {
  id     Int  @id @default(autoincrement())
  userId Int  @unique
  amount Int
  locked Int
  user   User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}
items.note: userId @unique = one balance per user. Store smallest currency unit as Int; floats corrupt money.

items.label: OnRampTransaction + status enum
items.code:
model OnRampTransaction {
  id        Int          @id @default(autoincrement())
  status    OnRampStatus
  token     String       @unique
  provider  String
  amount    Int
  startTime DateTime
  userId    Int
  user      User         @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}
enum OnRampStatus { Success Failure Processing }
items.note: token is @unique — it's the idempotency key the webhook matches on.

items.label: p2pTransfer — two relations to User
items.code:
model p2pTransfer {
  id         Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  amount     Int
  timestamp  DateTime
  fromUserId Int
  fromUser   User     @relation("FromUserRelation", fields:[fromUserId], references:[id])
  toUserId   Int
  toUser     User     @relation("ToUserRelation",   fields:[toUserId],   references:[id])
}
items.note: Two FKs to the same table REQUIRE named relations, else prisma validate errors.

items.label: Back-relations on User
Server action — create Processing txn + token
items.note: Never credit Balance here — the client is untrusted. Credit only on the bank webhook.

items.label: Bank webhook — credit + flip status atomically
items.note: Array $transaction = atomic batch. Guard on status:Processing so a retried webhook can't double-credit.

items.label: increment / decrement atomic ops
items.code: data: { amount: { increment: n } }   // or { decrement: n }
items.note: Runs UPDATE ... SET amount = amount + n in the DB — no read-modify-write in JS.

items.label: Interactive $transaction with row lock
items.note: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE takes a pessimistic lock so a concurrent transfer blocks until commit — kills the lost-update/double-spend race.

items.label: Array form vs interactive form
items.note: Transfers need interactive: you must lock, read balance, branch on sufficiency, then write.

items.label: Why $queryRaw for the lock
items.note: Prisma has no first-class SELECT FOR UPDATE, so drop to tx.$queryRaw. Default Read Committed alone does NOT stop a read-check-write race — you need the explicit lock (or Serializable + retry).

items.label: Insert ledger row inside the SAME transaction
items.note: Debit + credit + ledger insert commit or roll back together — balances and history can never diverge.

items.label: Migrate after adding the model
items.code: cd packages/db && npx prisma migrate dev --name add_p2p_transfer && npx prisma generate

items.label: Server-side fetch scoped to session user
items.note: Get the user id from getServerSession — NEVER from props/query string, or it's an IDOR. Scope with OR fromUserId/toUserId.

items.label: Async server component page
items.note: App Router server component can await Prisma directly — no API route needed.

items.label: Render sent vs received
items.code:
transactions.map(t => (
{t.type} {t.type === "Sent" ? "- " : "+ "}{t.amount / 100} INR {t.time.toDateString()}
))
items.note: Divide by 100 for display since amount is stored in paise.

tips: Money is an Int in paise/cents — never float/double (binary floats can't represent decimal currency). Divide by 100 only at display time.
Onramp: credit Balance ONLY in the bank webhook, never in the click-time server action — the client is untrusted.
Make the webhook idempotent: match on the unique token and update only rows still in Processing; the bank retries on timeout and would otherwise double-credit.
Transfers MUST be an interactive $transaction with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the sender's balance row — array-form $transaction and default Read Committed don't stop the lost-update race.
Two FKs from p2pTransfer to User need named @relation("FromUserRelation"/"ToUserRelation") or prisma validate fails.
Write the p2pTransfer ledger row inside the same $transaction as the balance moves so audit trail and balances always agree.
Re-run npx prisma generate (and migrate dev) after every schema change, from packages/db — the apps import that generated client.
Scope the P2P history query to the session user id (getServerSession), never a client-supplied id — otherwise it's an IDOR leaking others' transactions.

items.label: Build-any-project flow
items.note: Feature list -> UI/UX (end-user + merchant) -> DB schema -> architecture/stack -> bootstrap -> iterate. Decide the schema BEFORE coding routes.

items.label: Core features (wallet clone)
items.note: End user: on-ramp (add money via bank), P2P transfer, balance view, txn history. Merchant: receive money, cash-out. Two separate apps, one shared DB.

items.label: Stack
items.code:
Next.js (App Router) + TypeScript + TailwindCSS
Prisma + PostgreSQL
NextAuth (Credentials/phone) + bcrypt
Recoil (client state)
Turborepo monorepo

items.label: Monorepo layout
items.code:
apps/
  user-app/       # end-user Next.js app
  merchant-app/   # merchant Next.js app
packages/
  db/     # prisma client + schema (@repo/db)
  store/  # recoil atoms/selectors (@repo/store)
  ui/     # shared React components (@repo/ui)
items.note: Two frontends share one DB package -> single source of truth for the wallet schema.

items.label: Scaffold Turborepo
items.code:
npx create-turbo@latest
# pick npm; gives apps/web,docs + packages/ui,eslint,ts-config
items.note: Rename/duplicate apps/web into user-app and merchant-app; delete docs if unused.

items.label: Run everything from root
items.code:
npm install            # installs all workspaces
npm run dev            # turbo runs every app's dev in parallel
items.note: Give each app a distinct port: dev script -> "next dev --port 3001".

items.label: Local Postgres (Docker)
items.code: docker run -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mysecretpassword -d -p 5432:5432 postgres

items.label: Env vars (per app + db)
items.code:
DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:mysecretpassword@localhost:5432/postgres"
NEXTAUTH_URL=http://localhost:3001
JWT_SECRET=verysecret
items.note: DATABASE_URL lives in packages/db/.env; NEXTAUTH_URL/JWT_SECRET in each app's .env.

items.label: Init inside the db package
items.code:
cd packages/db
npm install prisma @prisma/client
npx prisma init

items.label: Schema models
items.code:
model User {
  id      Int     @id @default(autoincrement())
  email   String? @unique
  name    String?
  number  String  @unique
  password String
  Balance Balance[]
  OnRampTransaction OnRampTransaction[]
  sentTransfers p2pTransfer[] @relation("FromUser")
  receivedTransfers p2pTransfer[] @relation("ToUser")
}
model Balance {
  id Int @id @default(autoincrement())
  userId Int @unique
  amount Int
  locked Int
  user User @relation(fields:[userId], references:[id])
}
model OnRampTransaction {
  id Int @id @default(autoincrement())
  status OnRampStatus
  token String @unique
  provider String
  amount Int
  startTime DateTime
  userId Int
  user User @relation(fields:[userId], references:[id])
}
enum OnRampStatus { Success Failure Processing }
items.note: Store money in the smallest unit (paise) as Int -> amount*100. Never use float for currency.

items.label: Migrate + generate
items.code:
npx prisma migrate dev --name init
npx prisma generate
npx prisma studio   # GUI to inspect data

items.label: Export a singleton client
items.note: Singleton avoids exhausting connections on Next.js hot-reload.

items.label: Expose it as @repo/db/client
Create the store package
items.code:
cd packages/store
npm install recoil

items.label: Atom
Selector (derived total)
Client-only Providers wrapper
items.note: RecoilRoot needs "use client". Wrap {children} in layout.tsx with <Providers>. next-transpilePackages: ["@repo/store"] in next.config.js.

items.label: Install
items.code:
npm install next-auth bcrypt
npm install -D @types/bcrypt   # in the app

items.label: authOptions (Credentials/phone)
Route handler (App Router)
Read session on server
Generic Appbar (packages/ui) - dumb/presentational
items.note: Keep it prop-driven (no next-auth import) so both apps reuse it.

items.label: AppbarClient wrapper (per app) - wires auth
Sidebar item (active-route highlight)
Transfer / dashboard page (server component)
items.note: Fetch on the server, pass to client cards. Divide by 100 for display (paise -> rupees).

items.label: On-ramp via server action
items.note: "use server" action callable directly from a client button; no manual API route needed.

items.label: Seed script
Wire + run the seed
Landing page redirect
items.note: redirect() throws internally -> put it at top level of a server component, not inside try/catch.

tips: Money = Int in paise (amount * 100). Floats round wrong; convert to rupees (/100) only at display.
Keep packages/ui components dumb (props only). Do auth/state in a per-app *Client wrapper so both user-app and merchant-app reuse the UI.
RecoilRoot and SessionProvider must be in a 'use client' Providers component; add transpilePackages:['@repo/store','@repo/ui'] to next.config.js.
Prisma client must be a global singleton, else Next.js hot-reload spawns dozens of connections ('too many clients').
Set NEXTAUTH_URL and JWT_SECRET per app, and DATABASE_URL in packages/db/.env; run migrate/generate from inside packages/db.
Prefer server actions ('use server') over manual /api routes for mutations like on-ramp and transfer — less boilerplate, typesafe.
P2P transfer must be a prisma.$transaction: lock sender balance, decrement sender, increment receiver — all-or-nothing to avoid partial transfers.
getServerSession(authOptions) in server components/actions; useSession() only in client components. Redirect unauthenticated users to /api/auth/signin.

items.label: What is AWS
items.note: Cloud provider renting compute/storage/network on demand. EC2 = Elastic Compute Cloud = virtual servers (VMs) you rent by the second/hour.

items.label: Instance = a rented server
items.note: Pick a Region (physical location, e.g. ap-south-1) close to users. Instance stays in one AZ (availability zone).

items.label: AMI (Amazon Machine Image)
items.note: OS template. Ubuntu Server LTS is the common default; Amazon Linux 2023 uses dnf/yum + user ec2-user.

items.label: Instance types / free tier
items.code: t2.micro / t3.micro  # 1 vCPU, 1GB RAM — free tier eligible
items.note: Family + size. t = burstable/general. Free tier: 750 hrs/month of micro for 12 months.

items.label: Key pair
items.note: Download the .pem ONCE at launch — you cannot re-download it. It's your SSH private key. Lose it = lose SSH access.

items.label: Security Group = firewall
items.note: Controls inbound/outbound ports. Nothing is reachable unless you open the port here. Default: only SSH open, or nothing.

items.label: Launch flow (console)
items.note: EC2 → Launch instance → Name → AMI (Ubuntu) → t2.micro → create/select key pair → configure Security Group → Launch.

items.label: Open ports in Security Group
items.code:
SSH    TCP  22    Source: My IP (or 0.0.0.0/0)
HTTP   TCP  80    Source: 0.0.0.0/0
HTTPS  TCP  443   Source: 0.0.0.0/0
Custom TCP 3000  Source: 0.0.0.0/0   # only while testing app directly
items.note: 0.0.0.0/0 = the whole internet. Lock SSH to your IP. Remove the app port (3000) once nginx fronts it.

items.label: Public IP vs Elastic IP
items.note: Public IP changes on every stop/start. Allocate an Elastic IP and associate it for a stable address (free while attached to a running instance).

items.label: Get the public address
items.note: Instance details → 'Public IPv4 address' or 'Public IPv4 DNS'. This is what you SSH to and point DNS at.

items.label: Fix key permissions (Linux/macOS)
items.code: chmod 400 my-key.pem
items.note: SSH refuses keys that are group/world-readable ('UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE').

items.label: Connect
items.code: ssh -i my-key.pem ubuntu@<public-ip>
items.note: User = ubuntu (Ubuntu AMI), ec2-user (Amazon Linux), admin (Debian). Wrong user is the #1 'Permission denied' cause.

items.label: Windows key permission fix
items.code:
icacls my-key.pem /inheritance:r
icacls my-key.pem /grant:r "%USERNAME%:R"
items.note: Or use the pem directly from PowerShell/OpenSSH; PuTTY needs a .ppk (convert via PuTTYgen).

items.label: Connection timeout vs refused
items.note: Timeout = Security Group not allowing port 22 (or wrong IP). Refused = sshd down / wrong port.

items.label: Update packages first
items.code: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

items.label: Install git + clone
items.code:
sudo apt install git -y
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git
cd repo
items.note: Private repo? Use a deploy key or a PAT in the HTTPS URL.

items.label: Install Node (via nvm)
items.code:
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.1/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc
nvm install --lts
items.note: nvm avoids permission issues vs apt's node; keeps versions per-user.

items.label: Install deps & run
items.code:
npm install
node index.js   # or: npm start
items.note: Foreground process dies when you log out. Use pm2/systemd for persistence (below).

items.label: Keep it alive with pm2
items.code:
npm i -g pm2
pm2 start index.js --name app
pm2 startup    # generates a boot service — run the printed command
pm2 save
items.note: pm2 restarts on crash and on reboot. pm2 logs / pm2 restart app to manage.

items.label: Test locally on the box first
items.code: curl http://localhost:3000
items.note: Works locally but not from browser = firewall/Security Group, not your app.

items.label: Test from your machine
items.code:
curl http://<public-ip>:3000
# browser: http://<public-ip>:3000
items.note: Requires that port open in the Security Group. This step is only pre-nginx.

items.label: Bind to 0.0.0.0, not 127.0.0.1
items.note: App listening on localhost only is unreachable externally even with the port open. Listen on 0.0.0.0 (or let nginx proxy to localhost).

items.label: Install & start
items.code:
sudo apt install nginx -y
sudo systemctl enable --now nginx
items.note: Now port 80 serves the default nginx page — confirms 80 is reachable.

items.label: Server block → proxy to app
items.note: nginx on 80/443 (public) forwards to your app on localhost:3000. Now you can close 3000 in the Security Group.

items.label: WebSocket / SSE upgrade
items.note: Required for socket.io / ws; without it connections hang or drop.

items.label: Test config + reload
items.code:
sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl reload nginx
items.note: Always nginx -t before reload — a bad config won't reload but a restart on bad config leaves nginx down.

items.label: Point DNS first
items.note: Create an A record for your domain → Elastic IP. Certbot's HTTP-01 challenge needs the domain resolving to this box on port 80.

items.label: Install certbot (Let's Encrypt)
items.code: sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx -y

items.label: Issue + auto-configure nginx
items.code: sudo certbot --nginx -d your-domain.com -d www.your-domain.com
items.note: Edits your server block to add 443/SSL and redirect 80→443 automatically. Free 90-day cert.

items.label: Auto-renewal
items.code:
sudo certbot renew --dry-run
sudo systemctl status certbot.timer
items.note: Renewal is installed as a systemd timer/cron by default. Certs expire in 90 days — the timer renews at ~60.

items.label: Verify
items.code: curl -I https://your-domain.com
items.note: Ensure 443 is open in the Security Group, else HTTPS times out even with a valid cert.

tips: Security Group is the top cause of 'can't connect' — the OS may be fine but the port is closed. Check it first.
Public IP changes on stop/start; allocate an Elastic IP before pointing DNS or you'll re-edit records constantly.
chmod 400 the .pem and SSH as the AMI's user (ubuntu/ec2-user) — wrong perms or wrong user = 'Permission denied'.
Never bake the .pem into a repo or share it; you can't re-download it and it grants full server access.
A raw 'node index.js' dies on logout — use pm2 (or a systemd unit) so the app survives disconnects and reboots.
Front the app with nginx on 80/443 and proxy to localhost:PORT; then close the app port in the Security Group.
Run 'sudo nginx -t' before every reload; reload (not restart) on a bad config so a live server isn't taken down.
DNS must resolve to the box and port 80 must be open before running certbot — the HTTP-01 challenge needs both.

items.label: Why Docker
items.note: Kills "works on my machine": ships app + deps + runtime as one portable unit; fast, reproducible onboarding & deploys.

items.label: Containers vs VMs
items.note: Containers share the host kernel (MBs, boot in ms). VMs virtualize hardware w/ a full guest OS (GBs, slow boot).

items.label: History (why it exists)
items.note: dotCloud, 2013. Originally wrapped Linux LXC; now uses runc + containerd. Containers are Linux cgroups + namespaces.

items.label: Install check
items.code:
docker --version
docker info
docker run hello-world
items.note: Docker Desktop on Win/Mac runs a Linux VM under the hood; `docker info` confirms the daemon is up.

items.label: Image vs Container
items.note: Image = read-only blueprint (built once, versioned by tag). Container = a running (or stopped) instance of an image. One image → many containers.

items.label: Run a container
items.code: docker run -d --name web -p 8080:80 nginx
items.note: -d detached, --rm auto-delete on exit, -it interactive shell.

items.label: List containers
items.code:
docker ps        # running
docker ps -a     # all (incl. stopped)

items.label: Shell into a running container
items.code: docker exec -it web sh   # or bash
items.note: Debug live containers here; alpine images only have `sh`.

items.label: Logs
items.code: docker logs -f web       # -f = follow/tail

items.label: Stop / start / remove
items.code:
docker stop web
docker start web
docker rm web            # -f to force-remove running

items.label: Images
items.code:
docker images
docker pull redis:7
docker rmi nginx

items.label: Reclaim space
items.code: docker system prune -a --volumes
items.note: Removes stopped containers, unused images/networks/volumes. Irreversible.

items.label: Publish a port
items.code: docker run -p 3000:3000 myapp
items.note: Format is -p HOST:CONTAINER. Without -p the container port is unreachable from host.

items.label: Multiple / random ports
items.code:
docker run -p 8080:80 -p 443:443 img
docker run -P img        # -P = auto-map all EXPOSEd ports

items.label: Single env var
items.code: docker run -e NODE_ENV=production -e PORT=3000 myapp

items.label: Env file
items.code: docker run --env-file .env myapp
items.note: File is KEY=VALUE per line, no quotes/spaces around =.

items.label: EXPOSE (Dockerfile)
items.code: EXPOSE 3000
items.note: Documentation only — does NOT publish. You still need -p at runtime.

items.label: Typical Node app
items.code:
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "index.js"]

items.label: FROM
items.code: FROM node:20-alpine
items.note: Base image + tag. Always pin a tag; `latest` drifts. alpine/slim = smaller.

items.label: WORKDIR / COPY / RUN
items.code:
WORKDIR /app        # cwd for later cmds
COPY src ./src      # host -> image
RUN npm run build   # runs at build time
items.note: RUN executes during build (bakes into image); each RUN = a layer.

items.label: CMD vs ENTRYPOINT
items.code:
ENTRYPOINT ["node"]
CMD ["index.js"]
items.note: CMD is the default, overridable by `docker run img <args>`. ENTRYPOINT is fixed; combine so run-args append to it. Use exec (JSON) form.

items.label: ARG vs ENV
items.code:
ARG VERSION=1.0     # build-time only
ENV APP_VERSION=$VERSION  # persists in container
items.note: ARG values vanish after build; ENV is available at runtime.

sections.title: Build, Run & Push to Docker Hub

items.label: Build & tag
items.code: docker build -t saichandan/myapp:v1 .
items.note: `.` is the build context (dir sent to daemon). -t names/tags it.

items.label: Run your image
items.code: docker run -d -p 3000:3000 saichandan/myapp:v1

items.label: Tag an existing image
items.code: docker tag myapp saichandan/myapp:latest

items.label: Login & push
items.code:
docker login
docker push saichandan/myapp:v1
items.note: Image name MUST be prefixed with your Docker Hub username to push.

items.label: Pull elsewhere
items.code: docker pull saichandan/myapp:v1

items.label: Every instruction = a layer
items.note: FROM/COPY/RUN each create a cached, read-only layer. Container adds a thin writable layer on top.

items.label: Inspect layers
items.code:
docker history saichandan/myapp:v1
docker image inspect myapp

items.label: Cache = build speed
items.note: Docker reuses a layer if the instruction + its inputs are unchanged. First changed layer busts cache for all layers below it.

items.label: Order matters (key optimization)
items.code:
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
items.note: Copy manifest & install deps BEFORE copying source. Code edits then skip the slow npm install layer.

items.label: .dockerignore
items.code:
node_modules
.git
*.log
Dockerfile
items.note: Shrinks build context & final image; keeps secrets/junk out. Like .gitignore.

items.label: Multi-stage build
items.code:
FROM node:20 AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN npm ci && npm run build

FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
items.note: Ship only artifacts, drop build toolchain → far smaller, safer images.

items.label: Named volume (persist data)
items.code:
docker volume create pgdata
docker run -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16
items.note: Container FS is ephemeral — data dies with `rm`. Volumes survive, managed by Docker.

items.label: Bind mount (live dev)
items.code: docker run -v "$(pwd)":/app node:20
items.note: Maps a host dir into the container — edits reflect instantly. On Windows PS use ${PWD}.

items.label: Volume commands
items.code:
docker volume ls
docker volume rm pgdata

items.label: Custom network + DNS
items.code:
docker network create mynet
docker run -d --name db --network mynet postgres
docker run --network mynet myapp   # reach db at host "db"
items.note: Containers on a user-defined network resolve each other by container name. Default bridge does NOT give name DNS.

items.label: Network drivers
items.code: docker network ls
items.note: bridge (default, isolated), host (share host net, no isolation), none (no net).

items.label: docker-compose.yml
items.code:
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://db/app
    depends_on: [db]
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
volumes:
  pgdata:
items.note: Compose auto-creates a shared network so services reach each other by service name.

items.label: Compose lifecycle
items.code:
docker compose up -d --build
docker compose logs -f
docker compose down -v
items.note: down -v also drops named volumes. Newer syntax is `docker compose` (space), not `docker-compose`.

tips: CMD is overridden by args after the image name (`docker run img echo hi`); ENTRYPOINT isn't — pair them for a fixed binary + default args.
Always COPY package.json + install deps before COPY-ing source, so code changes don't invalidate the dependency layer.
Add a .dockerignore (node_modules, .git) — it shrinks the build context and prevents leaking secrets into layers.
-p HOST:CONTAINER order trips everyone up; the container port is the right-hand side and must match what your app listens on.
Anything written inside a container is lost on `docker rm` — mount a named volume for databases and any state you must keep.
Only containers on the SAME user-defined network can resolve each other by name; the default bridge requires --link or IPs.
Prefer alpine/slim bases and multi-stage builds to cut image size (and attack surface) by an order of magnitude.
`docker exec -it <id> sh` is your go-to for debugging a live container; `docker logs -f` tails stdout/stderr.

items.label: Linux (Ubuntu/Debian) — official convenience script
items.code: curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
items.note: Fast path; for prod prefer the apt repo install from docs.docker.com.

items.label: macOS / Windows
items.code:
# Install Docker Desktop from docker.com
# includes engine, CLI, compose, and a Linux VM
items.note: On Windows use the WSL2 backend for near-native Linux perf.

items.label: Run docker without sudo (Linux)
items.code:
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
# log out/in for the group to apply
items.note: Adding to the docker group = root-equivalent. Only for trusted users.

items.label: Confirm it works
items.code:
docker version      # client + daemon
docker info         # system-wide status
docker run hello-world
items.note: If `Cannot connect to the Docker daemon` — the daemon/Desktop isn't running.

items.label: Run software without polluting your host
items.code: docker run -d -p 5432:5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=pw postgres
items.note: Spin up Postgres/Redis/Mongo in seconds; delete the container, host stays clean.

items.label: Reproducible environments ("works on my machine" killer)
items.note: The image pins OS libs + runtime + deps. Same image runs identically anywhere.

items.label: Isolation
items.note: Each container = isolated filesystem, network, and processes. Two apps can need different Node/Python versions with no conflict.

items.label: Image vs Container
items.note: Image = immutable blueprint (a class). Container = a running instance of that image (an object).

items.label: Docker Hub — the default public registry
items.code:
docker pull redis          # = docker.io/library/redis:latest
docker pull node:20-alpine
items.note: Bare names like `redis`/`node` are Official Images. Browse: hub.docker.com.

items.label: Image reference anatomy
items.code:
registry/namespace/repo:tag
ghcr.io/acme/api:1.4.2
items.note: No tag => `:latest` (a mutable pointer, not a stable version — pin real tags).

items.label: Other registries
items.code:
ghcr.io/...        # GitHub Container Registry
quay.io/...        # Red Hat Quay
<acct>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/...  # AWS ECR
items.note: Fully-qualified names bypass Docker Hub entirely.

items.label: Authenticate to pull/push private images
items.code:
docker login                 # Docker Hub
docker login ghcr.io -u USER
items.note: Token/PAT goes in the password prompt, not your account password.

items.label: Pull / list / remove
items.code:
docker pull nginx:1.27
docker images            # or: docker image ls
docker rmi nginx:1.27

items.label: Build from a Dockerfile
items.code:
docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
docker build -t myapp:1.0 -f api.Dockerfile .
items.note: `.` is the build context (files sent to daemon). Add a .dockerignore.

items.label: Tag & push
items.code:
docker tag myapp:1.0 user/myapp:1.0
docker push user/myapp:1.0

items.label: Inspect / history
items.code:
docker inspect nginx:1.27
docker history myapp:1.0
items.note: `inspect` dumps JSON — pipe to `jq` or use `--format`.

items.label: Run (foreground) vs detached
items.code:
docker run -it ubuntu bash        # interactive shell
docker run -d --name web -p 8080:80 nginx
items.note: -p HOST:CONTAINER maps ports. -it = interactive TTY; -d = background.

items.label: Env vars & volumes
items.code:
docker run -d \
  -e NODE_ENV=production \
  -v $(pwd)/data:/app/data \
  --name api myapp:1.0
items.note: -v HOST:CONTAINER persists data past container life. Use named volumes for DBs.

items.label: List / stop / start / remove
items.code:
docker ps            # running
docker ps -a         # all incl. stopped
docker stop web && docker rm web
docker rm -f web     # force-kill + remove
items.note: `docker run --rm ...` auto-deletes the container on exit.

items.label: Logs & exec into a running container
items.code:
docker logs -f web
docker exec -it web sh    # or bash
items.note: `exec` runs a NEW process; use it to debug a live container.

items.label: Copy files & check stats
items.code:
docker cp web:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf .
docker stats           # live CPU/mem

items.label: Cleanup
items.code:
docker system prune -a        # dangling images, stopped containers, networks
docker volume prune
items.note: -a also removes unused (not just dangling) images. Frees a LOT of disk.

items.label: PostgreSQL
items.code:
docker run -d --name pg -p 5432:5432 \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=pw -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16

items.label: Redis
items.code: docker run -d --name redis -p 6379:6379 redis:7

items.label: MongoDB
items.code:
docker run -d --name mongo -p 27017:27017 \
  -e MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD=pw mongo:7

items.label: MySQL
items.code:
docker run -d --name mysql -p 3306:3306 \
  -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=pw mysql:8

items.label: Nginx (static/reverse proxy)
items.code:
docker run -d -p 8080:80 \
  -v $(pwd)/site:/usr/share/nginx/html:ro nginx

items.label: Node / language base images
items.code: docker run -it --rm -v $(pwd):/app -w /app node:20-alpine npm ci
items.note: `-alpine` tags are tiny; `-slim` is a middle ground. Great for one-off tooling.

items.label: Minimal Node Dockerfile
items.code:
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "index.js"]
items.note: Copy manifests + install BEFORE copying source so the deps layer caches.

items.label: Multi-container with Compose (docker-compose.yml)
items.code:
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    depends_on: [db]
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: pw
items.note: YAML file; `depends_on` controls start order, not readiness.

items.label: Compose lifecycle
items.code:
docker compose up -d     # build+start
docker compose logs -f
docker compose down      # add -v to drop volumes
items.note: Modern Docker: `docker compose` (space), not the old `docker-compose` binary.

tips: `:latest` is a mutable tag, not "newest stable" — always pin explicit versions (postgres:16, node:20-alpine) for reproducibility.
-p HOST:CONTAINER order trips everyone up: the LEFT number is the port on your machine, the RIGHT is inside the container.
Container data vanishes on `docker rm`. Anything you must keep (DB files) needs a volume (-v).
`docker exec -it <c> sh` to poke around a live container; `docker run` makes a fresh one — don't confuse the two.
Alpine images sometimes lack bash/glibc — use `sh` for exec, and watch for native-module build failures.
`docker system prune -a` reclaims tons of disk but deletes all unused images — you'll re-pull them next run.
Add a .dockerignore (node_modules, .git) or your build context balloons and builds crawl.
On Windows/macOS, `localhost` inside a container is NOT your host — use `host.docker.internal` to reach host services.

items.label: Middleware = code that runs BEFORE a request is completed
