items.note: Runs on every matching request; can rewrite, redirect, block, or mutate headers/cookies before the route handler/page renders.

items.label: Single file at project root (or inside src/)
items.code:
my-app/
  middleware.ts   <- here (NOT in app/)
  app/
  src/middleware.ts  <- OR here if using src/
items.note: One middleware.ts per project. It's the top-level file, sibling to app/ (or inside src/).

items.label: Minimal skeleton
items.note: Export a function named `middleware` (or `export default`). `config.matcher` scopes it.

items.label: Runtime
items.note: Edge runtime by default (no Node APIs like fs). Next 15.2+ can opt into Node via `export const config = { runtime: 'nodejs' }` (experimental).

items.label: NextResponse.next() — let request continue
items.code: return NextResponse.next()

items.label: Redirect (changes URL in browser)
items.code: return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url))
items.note: Pass an absolute URL; build with `new URL(path, request.url)`.

items.label: Rewrite (proxy — URL stays, content differs)
items.code: return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL('/proxy', request.url))
items.note: Browser bar unchanged; internally serves another path.

items.label: Return JSON / block early
items.code: return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 })

items.label: Read request URL parts
items.code:
const { pathname, searchParams } = request.nextUrl
searchParams.get('token')

items.label: Read headers / cookies
items.code:
request.headers.get('authorization')
request.cookies.get('session')?.value

items.label: Geo / IP (Next 15+)
items.code:
import { geolocation, ipAddress } from '@vercel/functions'
const { country } = geolocation(request)
items.note: `request.geo` and `request.ip` were REMOVED in Next 15 — use @vercel/functions helpers.

items.label: Protect routes, redirect unauthenticated users
Preserve intended destination after login
Inject a response header
Set a cookie on the response
Forward/modify REQUEST headers to downstream
items.note: Pass headers to route handlers/server components; read via `headers()`.

items.label: A/B test via rewrite
items.code:
const bucket = Math.random() < 0.5 ? 'a' : 'b'
return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL(`/home-${bucket}`, request.url))

items.label: Single path with wildcard
items.code: export const config = { matcher: '/about/:path*' }
items.note: `:path*` = zero-or-more segments. `:path` = exactly one.

items.label: Multiple matchers (array)
Match everything EXCEPT static/api (most common)
items.note: Negative lookahead skips assets & _next internals — huge perf win.

items.label: Conditional matcher (has / missing)
items.note: type: 'header' | 'cookie' | 'query' | 'host'.

items.label: In-code branching (when matcher isn't enough)

tips: Matcher values MUST be static string literals — they're analyzed at build time, so you can't compute them at runtime or import them from variables.
Always return a NextResponse (or nothing). Forgetting to `return NextResponse.next()` on the pass-through path silently breaks the request.
Edge runtime: no Node.js APIs (fs, net, most crypto). Keep middleware light — it runs on EVERY matching request, so no DB calls or heavy work.
Exclude _next/ and static assets in your matcher, otherwise middleware fires for every image/JS chunk and tanks performance.
Set cookies/headers on the RESPONSE object you return — mutating `request.cookies` only affects downstream reads, not the browser.
Build redirect/rewrite URLs with `new URL(path, request.url)` — a bare string like '/login' throws (needs absolute URL).
Only ONE middleware.ts per project. Compose multiple concerns by branching on `request.nextUrl.pathname` inside it (or chain helper functions).
`redirect` changes the browser URL (client sees it); `rewrite` keeps the URL but serves different content (invisible proxy). Pick deliberately.

sections.title: Prefix Sums — build once, query O(1)

items.label: Standard 1-indexed prefix array (size n+1)
items.note: Size n+1 with leading 0 removes the l===0 edge case.

items.label: Range sum of a[l..r] inclusive
items.code: const sum = prefix[r + 1] - prefix[l];
items.note: With 1-indexed prefix, no special-casing needed.

items.label: Running-prefix trick when you only need the answer (no array)
items.code:
let run = 0;
for (const x of a) { run += x; /* use run */ }
items.note: If you never re-query a range, skip storing the full array.

items.label: P1 · Highest Altitude (LC 1732)
items.note: Altitudes ARE the prefix sums of gain; start at 0, track the max.

sections.title: Frequency Arrays — count in O(n), O(1) space for fixed alphabet

items.label: Lowercase letters -> size-26 array
items.code:
const freq = new Array(26).fill(0);
for (const c of s) freq[c.charCodeAt(0) - 97]++;
items.note: 'a'=97. Use 65 for uppercase, 48 for digits. Faster than a Map for small fixed sets.

items.label: Arbitrary / large keys -> Map
items.code:
const freq = new Map();
for (const x of a) freq.set(x, (freq.get(x) || 0) + 1);

items.label: P2 · Most frequent character
items.note: First-seen ties win because > (not >=) never overwrites an equal max.

sections.title: Difference Array — range updates + prefix (line sweep)

items.label: Add val to every index in [l, r] for many queries
items.note: Each query becomes O(1); rebuild in O(n) at the end. This is prefix sum run in reverse.

items.label: P3 · Maximum platforms needed (timeline / freq buckets)
items.note: dep+1 because a train on the platform at its departure minute still occupies it.

items.label: P3 · Two-pointer variant (sort arr & dep)
items.note: Use <= (arrival == departure overlaps -> needs its own platform).

sections.title: Prefix + HashMap — count subarrays by property

items.label: P4 · Subarray sums divisible by K (LC 974)
items.note: Two prefixes with equal remainder => the slice between them is divisible by k. Seed {0:1}.

items.label: Sibling pattern · subarray sum == k (LC 560)
items.note: Same skeleton: count how many earlier prefixes make the gap equal the target.

items.label: Fixed-size window (length k)
Variable window (longest satisfying a condition)
items.note: Use while (not if) to shrink — the window may need multiple removals.

items.label: P5 · K-Beauty of a Number (LC 2269)
items.note: Fixed window of k digits; a substring like '00' -> 0 must be excluded.

items.label: P6 · Substrings containing all of a, b, c (LC 1358)
items.note: For each right end, all substrings starting <= min(last a,b,c) are valid. -1 sentinel makes the term 0.

items.label: P7 · Count Nice Subarrays — sliding window (LC 1248)
items.note: "Exactly k" of a monotone count = atMost(k) − atMost(k−1). Treat odd as 1 (x & 1).

items.label: P7 · Same problem via prefix-count + Map
items.note: Map odd-count exactly like subarray-sum==k: odds is the prefix, k is the target.

sections.title: Pattern Recognition — pick the tool fast

items.label: "Sum/answer over range [l..r]", static array
items.note: Prefix sum. Precompute once, answer each query in O(1).

items.label: "Count subarrays where sum/count has property P"
items.note: Prefix + HashMap of seen prefixes (== k, divisible by k, equal remainder). Seed {0:1}.

items.label: "Apply many range updates, then read"
items.note: Difference array: +val at l, −val at r+1, one prefix pass to materialize.

items.label: "Contiguous window with a constraint (longest/shortest/count)"
items.note: Sliding window. Fixed size -> evict a[r-k]; variable -> shrink with while(!valid).

items.label: "Exactly K ... " of a monotone quantity
items.note: atMost(K) − atMost(K−1). Turn a hard equality into two easy at-most windows.

items.label: General approach (the technique)
items.note: Brute force first -> spot repeated work -> replace it with prefix (cumulative) or window (incremental) so each step is O(1).

tips: Make prefix arrays size n+1 with a leading 0 — kills the l===0 special case and makes a[l..r] = prefix[r+1]-prefix[l].
Normalize negative remainders: ((sum % k) + k) % k. JS % keeps the sign of the dividend, which breaks the divisible-by-k Map.
Seed prefix-count maps with {0: 1} — it represents the empty prefix so subarrays starting at index 0 get counted.
In difference arrays and platform sweeps, size the array n+1 (or T+2) so the r+1 / dep+1 decrement never overflows.
Shrink variable windows with while, not if — one new right element can violate the constraint by more than one unit.
Maximum platforms: use <= when comparing arrival to departure; a train arriving exactly when another leaves still needs its own platform.
K-Beauty and any 'divides num' check must guard against d === 0 before num % d (e.g. substring '00').
'Exactly k' is almost always atMost(k) - atMost(k-1); guard atMost(m) with m<0 -> return 0.

items.label: What is an ORM
items.note: Object-Relational Mapper: maps DB tables <-> code objects/models. Write type-safe queries in JS/TS instead of raw SQL.

items.label: Why use one
items.note: Type safety, autocomplete, migrations, no hand-written SQL, DB-agnostic queries, guards against injection & schema drift.

items.label: What Prisma is
items.note: Next-gen Node/TS ORM = 3 parts: Prisma Schema (declarative model), Prisma Migrate (SQL migrations), Prisma Client (generated, type-safe query builder).

items.label: Two ORM styles
items.note: Prisma is schema-first (define models in schema.prisma). Contrast: code-first / Active-Record (TypeORM, Sequelize).

items.label: New project
items.code:
mkdir app && cd app
npm init -y
npm i -D prisma typescript ts-node @types/node
npx tsc --init

items.label: Prisma CLI + init
items.code: npx prisma init
items.note: Creates prisma/schema.prisma and .env with DATABASE_URL. Default datasource is postgresql.

items.label: Pick DB at init
items.code:
npx prisma init --datasource-provider postgresql
# or: sqlite | mysql | sqlserver | mongodb
items.note: SQLite is the zero-setup choice for learning.

items.label: Install client runtime
items.code: npm i @prisma/client
items.note: Runtime dep; prisma CLI stays a dev dep.

items.label: Datasource block
Generator block
Postgres URL
items.code: DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/mydb?schema=public"

items.label: SQLite URL
items.note: Relative to the schema file location.

items.label: .env is git-ignored
items.note: prisma init auto-adds .env to .gitignore. Never commit real credentials.

items.label: Basic model
items.code:
model User {
  id       Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  email    String @unique
  name     String?
  age      Int    @default(0)
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
}
items.note: ? = nullable/optional. No ? = required (NOT NULL).

items.label: Field attributes
items.code:
@id  @unique  @default(...)  @updatedAt
@map("col_name")  @db.VarChar(255)
items.note: @map renames the DB column; @@map renames the table.

items.label: Default helpers
Enums
items.code:
enum Role { USER ADMIN }
model User { role Role @default(USER) }

items.label: Composite / block attrs
items.code:
@@unique([firstName, lastName])
@@index([email])
@@id([a, b])   // composite PK

items.label: 1-to-many
items.note: FK field (userId) lives on the 'many' side; the scalar + relation field both required.

items.label: 1-to-1
items.code:
model User    { id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) profile Profile? }
model Profile { id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) user User @relation(fields:[userId], references:[id]) userId Int @unique }
items.note: @unique on the FK enforces one-to-one.

items.label: Many-to-many (implicit)
items.code:
model Post { id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) tags Tag[] }
model Tag  { id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) posts Post[] }
items.note: Prisma manages the hidden join table automatically.

items.label: Referential actions
items.code: user User @relation(fields:[userId], references:[id], onDelete: Cascade, onUpdate: Cascade)

items.label: Self-relation
Create + apply migration (dev)
items.code: npx prisma migrate dev --name init
items.note: Generates SQL in prisma/migrations, applies it, and regenerates the client.

items.label: Prototype without migration files
items.code: npx prisma db push
items.note: Pushes schema straight to DB (no migration history). Great for early iteration/SQLite.

items.label: (Re)generate client
items.code: npx prisma generate
items.note: Run after every schema change if you didn't use migrate dev. Outputs to node_modules/@prisma/client.

items.label: Prisma Studio (GUI)
items.code: npx prisma studio
items.note: Visual DB browser at localhost:5555 to explore/edit rows.

items.label: Introspect existing DB
items.code: npx prisma db pull
items.note: Reverse-engineers schema.prisma from an existing database.

items.label: Deploy migrations (prod)
items.code: npx prisma migrate deploy
items.note: Applies pending migrations non-interactively; no schema changes generated.

items.label: Reset DB (dev only)
items.code: npx prisma migrate reset
items.note: Drops + recreates + re-seeds. Destroys all data.

items.label: Instantiate
items.code:
import { PrismaClient } from "@prisma/client";
const prisma = new PrismaClient();
items.note: Create ONE instance and reuse it — never new PrismaClient() per request.

items.label: Create
Read many + filter
Read one
items.code:
await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: 1 } });
await prisma.user.findFirst({ where: { name: "Alice" } });

items.label: select vs include
items.note: Can't use select and include at the same top level.

items.label: Update
Delete
items.code:
await prisma.todo.delete({ where: { id: 1 } });
await prisma.todo.deleteMany({ where: { userId: 1 } });

items.label: Upsert
Nested write (create w/ relation)
Transaction
Setup
GET todos
POST todo
PUT mark done
Listen
items.code: app.listen(3000, () => console.log("http://localhost:3000"));

items.label: Params are strings
items.note: req.params/query are strings — Number(req.params.id) before passing to an Int where clause or Prisma throws a type error.

tips: Regenerate the client after EVERY schema change: `prisma migrate dev` does it automatically, but `db push` / manual edits need `npx prisma generate`.
Use `db push` for fast prototyping (no history); switch to `migrate dev` once the schema stabilizes so you get versioned SQL migrations.
Instantiate PrismaClient once and reuse. In dev with hot-reload (Next.js/nodemon), cache it on globalThis to avoid connection-pool exhaustion.
`findUnique` only accepts fields that are @id or @unique; use `findFirst` for arbitrary where filters.
Optional (`?`) fields map to nullable columns; adding a required field to a table with existing rows needs a @default or the migration fails.
The FK scalar (e.g. userId) AND the relation field both live on the 'many' side — forgetting the scalar is the #1 relation error.
Never build queries via string concatenation; Prisma parameterizes everything, so pass values through `where`/`data` to stay injection-safe.
Keep DATABASE_URL in .env (git-ignored) and use `env("DATABASE_URL")` in the datasource — hardcoding the URL leaks credentials.

items.label: Stack: serverless edge backend + shared types + React FE
items.note: Cloudflare Workers + Hono (backend), PostgreSQL + Prisma (DB), Zod (validation, shared via npm), JWT (auth), React + Vite + react-router-dom + Tailwind (FE), TypeScript everywhere.

items.label: Scaffold the Hono backend on Cloudflare Workers
items.code:
npm create hono@latest backend
# template: cloudflare-workers
cd backend && npm i
npm run dev   # local at http://localhost:8787

items.label: Minimal Hono app entry (src/index.ts)
items.code:
import { Hono } from 'hono'
const app = new Hono()
app.get('/', (c) => c.text('Hello Hono!'))
export default app
items.note: Export default the app — Workers runtime calls its fetch handler. No app.listen().

items.label: Type Bindings (env) + Variables (context state) generics
items.note: Bindings type c.env.*; Variables type c.get()/c.set(). Without them TS errors on env/context access.

items.label: Install Prisma + Accelerate (connection pooling for edge)
items.code:
npm i prisma --save-dev
npm i @prisma/client @prisma/extension-accelerate
npx prisma init

items.label: schema.prisma — User + Post models
items.code:
model User {
  id       String @id @default(uuid())
  email    String @unique
  name     String?
  password String
  posts    Post[]
}
model Post {
  id        String  @id @default(uuid())
  title     String
  content   String
  published Boolean @default(false)
  author    User    @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
  authorId  String
}

items.label: Migrate (uses DIRECT url in .env) then generate edge client
items.code:
npx prisma migrate dev --name init
npx prisma generate --no-engine
items.note: --no-engine drops the query-engine binary (Workers can't run it); queries go through Accelerate. Re-run generate after every schema change.

items.label: Instantiate PrismaClient INSIDE each handler (edge client)
items.note: Import from '@prisma/client/edge'. Create per-request — Workers are stateless and have no long-lived global connection.

items.label: Two DB URLs
items.note: Runtime (wrangler.toml): pooled Accelerate string prisma://accelerate.prisma-data.net/?api_key=... . Migrations (.env): direct postgresql://... connection.

items.label: Split routers per resource (src/routes/user.ts)
items.code:
import { Hono } from 'hono'
export const userRouter = new Hono<{ Bindings: {...} }>()
userRouter.post('/signup', async (c) => { /* ... */ })
userRouter.post('/signin', async (c) => { /* ... */ })

items.label: Mount sub-routers with app.route (src/index.ts)
items.code:
import { userRouter } from './routes/user'
import { blogRouter } from './routes/blog'
app.route('/api/v1/user', userRouter)
app.route('/api/v1/blog', blogRouter)
items.note: Paths inside a router are relative to the mount prefix.

items.label: JWT auth middleware guarding /blog/*
items.note: Course sends the raw token (no 'Bearer ' prefix). If you prefix, strip it before verify.

items.label: Enable CORS (needed once FE calls the API)
items.code:
import { cors } from 'hono/cors'
app.use('/*', cors())

items.label: Signup — create user, return signed JWT
items.note: Demo hashes nothing — in real apps hash passwords (bcrypt/argon).

items.label: Signin — verify creds, return JWT
Create blog (author = c.get('userId'))
Update / get one / list all (bulk)
items.note: Use c.req.param('id') for /:id routes. Prefer select to control payload shape.

items.label: Init the shared common package
items.code:
mkdir common && cd common && npm init -y
npm i zod
npx tsc --init   # set "declaration": true, "rootDir":"./src", "outDir":"./dist"

items.label: Define schemas + inferred types (common/src/index.ts)
items.code:
import { z } from 'zod'
export const signupInput = z.object({
  email: z.string().email(),
  password: z.string().min(6),
  name: z.string().optional()
})
export type SignupInput = z.infer<typeof signupInput>
items.note: Export both the schema (runtime validation) and z.infer type (compile-time). Single source of truth for FE + BE.

items.label: Build + publish to npm (scoped, public)
items.code:
tsc -b
npm login
npm publish --access public   # name e.g. @yourname/medium-common
items.note: After each schema change: npm version patch, tsc -b, npm publish; then npm update the package in FE & BE.

items.label: Consume in backend with safeParse (never trust input)
items.note: safeParse returns { success } instead of throwing like parse().

items.label: wrangler.toml — env vars for runtime
items.code:
name = "backend"
main = "src/index.ts"
compatibility_date = "2024-01-01"

[vars]
DATABASE_URL = "prisma://accelerate.prisma-data.net/?api_key=..."
JWT_SECRET = "super-secret"
items.note: For real secrets prefer: npx wrangler secret put JWT_SECRET (keeps them out of the repo).

items.label: Login + deploy
items.code:
npx wrangler login
npm run deploy   # wrangler deploy
items.note: Gives a *.workers.dev URL. Use that as the FE's BACKEND_URL.

items.label: Test with Postman before FE (Step 8)
items.code:
POST {{url}}/api/v1/user/signup   body: raw JSON
POST {{url}}/api/v1/blog          header: Authorization: <jwt>
items.note: Copy jwt from signup/signin response into the Authorization header for /blog/* calls.

items.label: Scaffold FE + Tailwind + router + common
items.code:
npm create vite@latest frontend -- --template react-ts
cd frontend && npm i
npm i -D tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer && npx tailwindcss init -p
npm i react-router-dom axios @yourname/medium-common

items.label: Routes in App.tsx
items.code:
import { BrowserRouter, Routes, Route } from 'react-router-dom'
<BrowserRouter>
  <Routes>
    <Route path="/signup" element={<Signup />} />
    <Route path="/signin" element={<Signin />} />
    <Route path="/blogs" element={<Blogs />} />
    <Route path="/blog/:id" element={<Blog />} />
  </Routes>
</BrowserRouter>

items.label: Auth call — store JWT, navigate
Authenticated fetch — read :id param + send token
items.note: Match the header format the backend middleware expects (raw token vs 'Bearer ').

items.label: Reusable components
items.note: Auth (shared signup/signin form), LabelledInput, Quote (split-screen), Appbar, BlogCard, BlogSkeleton, Avatar. Extract data-fetching into custom hooks (useBlog, useBlogs).

tips: Create PrismaClient inside each handler, not globally — Cloudflare Workers are stateless per request and share no connection; use the '@prisma/client/edge' import + withAccelerate for pooling.
Access secrets via c.env.X on Workers (not process.env), and type them in the Hono<{ Bindings }> generic or TS will error.
Keep two DB URLs: the pooled Accelerate prisma:// URL for runtime (wrangler.toml) and a direct postgresql:// URL in .env for prisma migrate/generate.
Re-run `npx prisma generate --no-engine` after every schema change, or the client types/queries go stale.
Common package is the single source of truth: after editing schemas, npm version patch + publish, then npm update it in BOTH frontend and backend.
Use zod safeParse (returns { success }) over parse (throws); return 411 on invalid input.
Middleware sends the raw JWT in Authorization (no 'Bearer ' prefix in the course) — keep FE and verify() consistent or auth silently 403s.
Enable CORS on the backend (hono/cors) before the React app can call it, and set BACKEND_URL to the deployed *.workers.dev origin.

items.label: Create an app
items.code:
npx create-next-app@latest my-app
cd my-app && npm run dev

items.label: App Router layout
items.code:
app/
  layout.tsx      # shared shell (persists)
  page.tsx        # UI for '/'
  dashboard/
    page.tsx      # UI for '/dashboard'
items.note: A folder = a route segment; page.tsx renders it.

items.label: Static route
items.code: app/about/page.tsx  ->  /about

items.label: Dynamic route
Route group (no URL segment)
items.code: app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx  ->  /about
items.note: Parentheses group files/layouts without adding to the URL.

items.label: Navigate
Server (default)
items.note: Ships zero JS, can touch DB/secrets, can be async.

items.label: Client
items.note: Needed for state, effects, event handlers, browser APIs.

items.label: Rule
items.note: Server can import Client; Client cannot import Server (pass server-rendered JSX as children instead). Keep 'use client' at the leaves.

items.label: Root layout
items.note: Persists across navigation — Nav doesn't remount/refetch.

items.label: Nested layout
items.code: app/dashboard/layout.tsx  // wraps only /dashboard/*

items.label: Static metadata
Dynamic metadata

tips: 'use client' does NOT mean client-only — the component still server-renders once, then hydrates.
Default to Server Components; only reach for Client Components at interactive leaves to keep the JS bundle small.
Fetching in a Client Component reintroduces waterfalls + exposes endpoints — prefer fetching in a Server Component.
Route groups (folder) organise layouts without changing the URL.
A hydration mismatch = server HTML ≠ first client render (often Date.now(), random, or window access during render).

items.label: Monorepo
items.note: One repo holding many apps + packages, versioned together. NOT one giant app (that's a monolith).

items.label: Why
items.note: Atomic cross-package commits, shared code/config/tooling, single install, easy refactors, consistent deps. Cost: needs a task orchestrator to stay fast.

items.label: Polyrepo pain it solves
items.note: No more publishing internal libs to npm just to share; import via workspace protocol instead.

items.label: Typical layout
items.code:
apps/       # deployables (web, docs, api)
packages/   # shared libs (ui, config, utils)

items.label: Workspaces (built-in)
items.note: npm/yarn/pnpm all support workspaces = symlink local packages into node_modules. The foundation everything else builds on.

items.label: Turborepo
items.note: Task orchestrator + cache layer over workspaces. Doesn't bundle — runs YOUR scripts smartly.

items.label: Nx
items.note: Heavier: orchestration + generators + plugins + graph. More opinionated than Turbo.

items.label: Lerna
items.note: Original JS monorepo tool; now focuses on versioning/publishing (delegates task running to Nx).

items.label: Changesets
items.note: Common companion for versioning + changelogs + publishing packages.

items.label: Build system / bundler
items.note: tsc, webpack, vite, next build, esbuild — transforms source into artifacts.

items.label: Orchestrator (Turborepo)
items.note: Decides WHAT to run, in WHAT ORDER, in PARALLEL, and skips work via cache. Calls the build systems; never replaces them.

items.label: History
items.note: Turborepo built by Jared Palmer, acquired by Vercel (Dec 2021). v2.0 (2024) renamed turbo.json `pipeline` -> `tasks`.

items.label: Scaffold new monorepo
items.code:
npx create-turbo@latest
# pick package manager (pnpm recommended)

items.label: Add turbo to existing repo
items.code: pnpm add turbo -Dw   # -w = root/workspace

items.label: Default structure
items.code:
apps/
  web/    docs/          # Next.js apps
packages/
  ui/                     # @repo/ui shared components
  eslint-config/          # @repo/eslint-config
  typescript-config/      # @repo/typescript-config
turbo.json
package.json
pnpm-workspace.yaml

items.label: Internal package naming
items.note: Convention: @repo/* (or your org scope). private:true so it never publishes to npm.

items.label: Root package.json
items.note: private:true is REQUIRED at root. No source here — only scripts + shared devDeps.

items.label: Declare workspaces (npm/yarn)
items.code: "workspaces": ["apps/*", "packages/*"]

items.label: Declare workspaces (pnpm)
items.code:
# pnpm-workspace.yaml
packages:
  - "apps/*"
  - "packages/*"
items.note: pnpm ignores the package.json workspaces field — uses this YAML instead.

items.label: Consume a local package
items.note: workspace:* = always link the local version, never fetch from npm.

items.label: packages/ui — expose entrypoints
items.note: Modern approach ships raw TS/TSX; the consuming app transpiles it (Next: transpilePackages: ['@repo/ui']).

items.label: Import shared code
items.code: import { Button } from "@repo/ui/button";

items.label: Run a task across all packages
items.code: turbo run build

items.label: Run dev for everything
items.code: turbo run dev   # parallel, persistent

items.label: Filter to one app
items.code: turbo run build --filter=web

items.label: App + its dependencies
items.code: turbo run build --filter=web...   # ... = include deps

items.label: Only changed since a ref
items.code: turbo run build --filter='...[origin/main]'

items.label: Ignore cache / force rerun
items.code: turbo run build --force

items.label: See plan without executing
items.code: turbo run build --dry-run

items.label: Prune for Docker
items.code: turbo prune web --docker
items.note: Emits a minimal subset (out/) with only web + its deps — small, cacheable Docker layers.

items.label: Basic tasks pipeline
items.note: Key is `tasks` in v2+ (was `pipeline`). `dev` must set persistent:true (long-running) and cache:false.

items.label: ^ prefix = topological dep
items.code: "dependsOn": ["^build"]
items.note: ^build = build all internal DEPENDENCIES first. Bare "build" = a task in the SAME package must run first.

items.label: outputs = what to cache
items.note: Files Turbo saves/restores on a cache hit. Miss these and restore produces empty dirs. ! excludes.

items.label: inputs = what invalidates cache
items.code: "test": { "inputs": ["src/**", "test/**"] }
items.note: Narrow inputs so unrelated file changes don't bust the cache.

items.label: Env vars in hash
items.note: Env vars affecting output MUST be listed or Turbo serves a stale cached build.

items.label: Global invalidators
items.code: "globalDependencies": [".env", "tsconfig.json"]
items.note: Changing these busts the cache for ALL tasks.

items.label: Quiet cached logs
items.code: "outputLogs": "new-only"

items.label: How caching works
items.note: Turbo hashes inputs (files + deps + env + config). Same hash -> skips task, restores outputs + replays logs. '>>> FULL TURBO' = everything hit.

items.label: Local cache location
items.code: node_modules/.turbo   (and .turbo/)

items.label: Remote cache (share across CI/team)
items.code:
turbo login
turbo link
items.note: Free with Vercel; or self-host. CI reuses a teammate's/previous build's artifacts.

items.label: Add a React (Next.js) app
items.code:
cd apps && npx create-next-app@latest web
# then add "@repo/ui": "workspace:*" and
# transpilePackages: ['@repo/ui'] in next.config

items.label: Add a Node.js app
items.code:
mkdir apps/api
# package.json: name @repo/api, scripts build(tsc)/dev/start
# turbo picks up build/dev automatically via turbo.json tasks

items.label: Add a common shared module
items.code:
packages/utils/
  package.json  -> name "@repo/utils", exports "./index"
  src/index.ts
# consume: "@repo/utils": "workspace:*"
items.note: Run pnpm install after adding a package so symlinks + graph update.

tips: v2 breaking change: turbo.json uses `tasks`, not `pipeline`. Old repos on v1 still use `pipeline`.
Root package.json MUST be private:true, and each shared package should be private:true unless you actually publish it.
Empty dirs after a cache hit? Your `outputs` glob is wrong/missing — Turbo only restores what you list.
Stale build after changing an env var? Add it to task `env` or `globalEnv` so it enters the cache hash.
cache:false + persistent:true is mandatory for `dev` and any watch/long-running task.
Use `--filter=app...` to include deps and `--filter='[HEAD^1]'` in CI to build only what changed.
pnpm reads pnpm-workspace.yaml, NOT the package.json `workspaces` field — set the right one for your PM.
Turborepo orchestrates and caches; it never bundles. Your tsc/next/vite scripts still do the actual building.

items.label: Traditional backend server
items.code: node server.js  # one process, listens on a port, runs 24/7
items.note: Always-on: you provision, scale, patch, and pay for the box whether traffic exists or not.

items.label: Serverless backend
items.note: You ship functions; provider runs them on demand, auto-scales to zero, bills per request/CPU. No server/OS to manage.

items.label: Core trade-off
items.note: Pay-per-use + zero ops vs cold starts, execution/CPU limits, statelessness, and vendor lock-in.

items.label: Cloudflare Workers model
items.note: Runs on V8 isolates (not containers) at the edge -> ~0ms cold start, global low latency, but limited Node APIs & strict CPU-time caps.

items.label: Famous providers
items.code:
AWS Lambda
Cloudflare Workers
Vercel / Netlify Functions
Google Cloud Functions
Azure Functions
Deno Deploy

items.label: Use serverless when
items.note: Spiky/unpredictable or low/intermittent traffic, small APIs / webhooks / cron, want autoscale + pay-per-use + global latency.

items.label: Avoid serverless when
items.note: Long-running jobs, websockets/stateful sockets, steady high throughput (a VM is cheaper), heavy compute, or need full Node + native libs.

items.label: Limits to watch
items.note: Cold starts (Lambda), CPU/wall-time caps (Workers ~10-50ms CPU on free tier), payload/memory limits, no persistent local state between requests.

items.label: Scaffold new worker (recommended)
items.code:
npm create cloudflare@latest my-app
# 'C3' - offers Hono/TS templates, installs wrangler for you
items.note: Fastest path; pick the Hono template to skip framework wiring.

items.label: Install Wrangler CLI (existing project)
items.code:
npm i -D wrangler
npx wrangler --version

items.label: Login to Cloudflare
items.code: npx wrangler login

items.label: Init in current dir
items.code: npx wrangler init

items.label: Local dev server
items.code: npx wrangler dev   # http://localhost:8787, hot reload
items.note: Runs the real Workers runtime (workerd) locally, so behavior matches prod.

items.label: Minimal worker (module syntax)
items.note: env = bindings/secrets/vars; ctx = execution context. No app.listen() - the runtime calls fetch().

items.label: Route by path + method
items.code:
const url = new URL(request.url);
if (url.pathname === '/api' && request.method === 'POST') { /* ... */ }

items.label: Background work after response
items.code: ctx.waitUntil(logToAnalytics(request));
items.note: Keeps the worker alive to finish async work after the Response is returned.

items.label: Deploy to production
items.code:
npx wrangler deploy
# -> https://my-app.<subdomain>.workers.dev

items.label: Tail live logs
items.code: npx wrangler tail

items.label: Why Express doesn't fit Workers
items.note: Express targets Node's http server (req/res, app.listen). Workers use a fetch(Request)->Response event model with no long-running server.

items.label: Run Express anyway (compat)
items.code:
# wrangler.toml
compatibility_flags = ["nodejs_compat"]
items.note: Partial Node polyfill; heavier bundle, not idiomatic. Prefer Hono on Workers.

items.label: Use Hono (Workers-native, Express-like API)
items.code: npm i hono

items.label: Minimal Hono app
items.note: Tiny, zero-dependency; same code runs on Workers/Bun/Deno/Node.

items.label: Path params
Query + JSON body
Custom middleware
items.note: await next() continues the chain; return early (skip next) to short-circuit.

items.label: Auth guard (scoped path)
Built-in middleware
wrangler.toml basics
items.code:
name = "my-worker"
main = "src/index.ts"
compatibility_date = "2024-09-01"

[vars]
API_BASE = "https://api.example.com"

items.label: Secrets (encrypted)
items.code:
npx wrangler secret put DATABASE_URL
# prompts for the value; never commit it
items.note: Use for keys/DB URLs. [vars] is plaintext and bundled - not for secrets.

items.label: Local secrets/vars
items.code:
# .dev.vars  (gitignored)
DATABASE_URL="postgres://..."
items.note: Auto-loaded by wrangler dev.

items.label: Access env in Hono
items.code: app.get('/', (c) => c.text(c.env.API_BASE));
items.note: In a raw worker it's the 2nd fetch arg (env). There is no process.env at runtime.

items.label: KV / D1 bindings
items.code:
[[kv_namespaces]]
binding = "CACHE"
id = "xxxx"

[[d1_databases]]
binding = "DB"
database_name = "prod"
database_id = "xxxx"

items.label: Problem: no raw TCP
items.note: Workers can't open arbitrary TCP sockets, so classic pg/mysql drivers fail. Use HTTP-based drivers or a pooling proxy.

items.label: Prisma + Accelerate (edge)
items.code:
npm i @prisma/client @prisma/extension-accelerate
npx prisma generate --no-engine

items.label: Instantiate per-request
items.note: DATABASE_URL is the prisma:// Accelerate URL. Build the client INSIDE the handler, not at module top level.

items.label: D1 (native SQLite)
Other edge-friendly options
items.note: Neon serverless driver (@neondatabase/serverless over HTTP), Turso/libSQL, Supabase REST, Upstash Redis (HTTP).

tips: Create the Prisma client inside the request handler - Workers isolates don't persist DB connections across requests like a Node server does.
No process.env at runtime: read config from env (raw worker) or c.env (Hono), populated only from bindings/vars/secrets.
Workers bill CPU time, not wall time - avoid heavy sync loops; push slow/async work into ctx.waitUntil.
Secrets go through `wrangler secret put` (encrypted); [vars] is plaintext and shipped in the bundle - never put keys there.
Bump compatibility_date deliberately: it pins runtime behavior and changing it can alter available APIs.
Prefer Hono over Express on Workers; Express via nodejs_compat works but bloats the bundle and isn't idiomatic.
Type Hono bindings for autocomplete: `new Hono<{ Bindings: { DATABASE_URL: string } }>()`.
`wrangler dev` runs the actual workerd runtime, so local behavior mirrors prod far better than a Node mock server.

items.label: Server Component (default) — fetch directly, no useEffect
items.note: async components run on the server; secrets/DB calls stay off the client. No loading state needed — it streams.

items.label: React recap — client fetch needs useEffect + state
items.note: Runs after mount → waterfall + spinner flash. Prefer server fetch when possible.

items.label: fetch cache control (App Router extends fetch)
items.code:
fetch(url)                              // cached (default, SSG-like)
fetch(url, { cache: "no-store" })       // never cache (SSR every req)
fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 60 }})// ISR: revalidate every 60s
fetch(url, { next: { tags: ["users"] }})// tag → revalidateTag("users")
items.note: Next 15 default changed to uncached (no-store) unless you opt in.

items.label: loading.tsx — automatic route-level loader
items.note: Rendered instantly while the async page resolves — wraps the segment in a Suspense boundary for you.

items.label: Granular loading with <Suspense>
items.code:
import { Suspense } from "react";
<Suspense fallback={<Spinner/>}>
  <SlowServerComponent />
</Suspense>
items.note: Stream part of the page while the rest loads. fallback shows until the child's await resolves.

items.label: Parallel vs sequential fetches
File convention — one route.ts per path
items.code:
app/api/users/route.ts        // -> /api/users
app/api/users/[id]/route.ts   // -> /api/users/:id
items.note: Export named functions per HTTP verb: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE.

items.label: GET handler + NextResponse
POST — read JSON body
items.note: req is the standard Web Request. Use req.formData() for form posts.

items.label: Dynamic params (Next 15: params is a Promise)
items.note: Next 14: params is a plain object (no await). Next 15 made it async.

items.label: Query / search params
Reading cookies / headers
items.code:
import { cookies, headers } from "next/headers";
const token = (await cookies()).get("token")?.value; // async in Next 15
items.note: cookies()/headers() are async in Next 15, sync in 14.

sections.title: Sign-up Flow (Frontend + Backend in one app)

items.label: Why co-locate — no CORS, call /api/* directly
items.note: Moving the backend into the Next app means same-origin requests; no separate Express server, no CORS config.

items.label: Frontend form (client component)
items.note: Any component with onClick/useState/hooks must start with "use client".

items.label: Backend signup handler
items.note: Never store plaintext passwords in production — bcrypt.hash before create.

items.label: Install & init
items.code:
npm install prisma --save-dev
npm install @prisma/client
npx prisma init        # creates prisma/schema.prisma + .env

items.label: schema.prisma — datasource + model
Migrate & generate client
items.code:
npx prisma migrate dev --name init   # create + apply migration, regen client
npx prisma generate                  # regen client only
npx prisma studio                    # GUI to browse data
items.note: Use `migrate deploy` in production/CI (non-interactive).

items.label: CRUD queries
.env — connection string
items.code: DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/db"
items.note: Never commit .env. Prefix client-exposed vars with NEXT_PUBLIC_ (never for secrets).

items.label: Problem — dev hot-reload spawns many clients
items.note: Next.js hot-reloads modules; `new PrismaClient()` per import exhausts DB connections ("too many clients").

items.label: Fix — cache on globalThis
items.note: Import { prisma } everywhere. In prod one instance per lambda; the global guard only matters in dev.

items.label: "use server" — run server code from a form, no API route
items.note: Directive at top of file (or inline in an async fn). Args/return are serialized over the wire.

items.label: Wire to a form (works without JS)
items.note: action={fn} passes FormData automatically — progressive enhancement.

items.label: Call from a client component
items.note: You can import a server action into a client component and await it like a function.

items.label: Pending state with useFormStatus
Revalidate / redirect after mutation

tips: Server Components can't use hooks/state/onClick — add "use client" the moment you need interactivity or browser APIs.
Next 15 breaking changes: cookies(), headers(), params, and searchParams are now async (await them); fetch defaults to uncached.
Never expose secrets to the client: DB calls, API keys, and password hashing belong in Server Components, route handlers, or server actions — only NEXT_PUBLIC_* vars reach the browser.
Always use the singleton Prisma client from lib/prisma — importing `new PrismaClient()` per module crashes dev with connection-limit errors.
route.ts and page.tsx can't coexist in the same folder — a segment is either a page or an API route.
Server actions are the lightweight alternative to writing an /api route for form mutations — but return only serializable data and call revalidatePath to refresh stale caches.
Prefer fetching in Server Components over useEffect: it avoids client waterfalls, ships less JS, and needs no loading spinner (use loading.tsx / Suspense instead).
Hash passwords with bcrypt before storing — the raw-password examples are for learning only, never production.

sections.title: DSA vs CP + How to Approach

items.label: What is DSA
items.note: Data Structures (how you store data) + Algorithms (how you process it). Success = correct output at the best time/space Big-O, not just working code.

items.label: DSA vs CP
items.note: DSA = interview-focused: standard problems, correctness + optimal complexity. CP = timed contests: math tricks, observations, ranking/speed. For jobs start with DSA; CP later sharpens speed.

items.label: Big-O ladder (fastest to slowest)
items.code: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)
items.note: ~10^8 simple ops/sec. Use constraints to pick target: n<=10^4 -> O(n^2) fine; n<=10^6 -> need O(n) or O(n log n).

items.label: Universal problem-solving flow
items.note: 1) Brute force first (get it correct). 2) Find the bottleneck. 3) Optimize with hashing / sorting / two-pointers / prefix sums. Always eyeball edge cases before submitting.

items.label: Which language?
items.note: C++ is the DSA/CP default (STL + raw speed). Java = rich libs but verbose. Python = concise but slow (TLE risk). JS is fine for web devs. Logic is language-agnostic — pick one, master its std lib.

items.label: C++ competitive boilerplate
items.note: sync_with_stdio(false) + cin.tie(NULL) are mandatory for large inputs or you TLE on I/O alone.

items.label: Compile & run C++
items.code: g++ -std=c++17 -O2 sol.cpp -o sol && ./sol < in.txt
items.note: -O2 enables optimization; redirect stdin from a file for quick local testing.

items.label: JavaScript (Node) full-stdin read
items.note: readFileSync(0) reads all of stdin at once. Batch output into one console.log — many small logs are slow.

items.label: Python fast input
items.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
t = int(input())
items.note: Default input() is slow for large T; rebinding to sys.stdin.readline avoids TLE.

items.label: Declare & init
items.note: JS: const a = new Array(n).fill(0);  let b = [1,2,3];

items.label: Add / remove
items.code: a.push_back(x); a.pop_back();   // back: O(1)
items.note: JS: a.push()/a.pop() O(1); a.shift()/a.unshift() are O(n) — avoid in loops.

items.label: Iterate
items.code:
for (int x : a) cout << x << ' ';
for (int i = 0; i < a.size(); i++) { }
items.note: C++ operator[] has NO bounds check — out-of-range is undefined behavior, not an error.

items.label: Sort (asc / desc)
items.code:
sort(a.begin(), a.end());                  // asc
sort(a.begin(), a.end(), greater<int>());  // desc
items.note: JS: a.sort((x,y)=>x-y). NEVER call bare a.sort() on numbers — it sorts lexicographically (10 < 2).

items.label: Max / min / sum / reverse
items.note: JS: Math.max(...a); a.reduce((s,x)=>s+x,0); a.reverse(). Use 0LL seed to avoid int overflow on big sums.

items.label: Read input
items.code:
string s; cin >> s;   // one token (stops at whitespace)
getline(cin, s);      // full line incl. spaces
items.note: After cin >> x, call cin.ignore() before getline() to drop the leftover newline.

items.label: Char <-> int
items.note: JS: +ch for digit, ch.charCodeAt(0)-97 for index, String.fromCharCode(97+idx).

items.label: Frequency count (fixed alphabet)
items.code:
int cnt[26] = {0};
for (char c : s) cnt[c - 'a']++;
items.note: A size-26 array beats unordered_map when the alphabet is fixed — faster, no hashing.

items.label: Substring & reverse
items.code:
s.substr(pos, len);
reverse(s.begin(), s.end());
items.note: JS: s.slice(start,end). JS strings are immutable — build with an array + arr.join('') for heavy concatenation.

items.label: Compare
items.code: if (s1 == s2) { }   // C++: value comparison
items.note: Java uses s1.equals(s2) (== compares references!). JS === is value comparison — fine.

items.label: Problem
items.note: Count pairs (i, j) with i < j and nums[i] == nums[j].

items.label: Brute force O(n^2)
items.note: Fine for n<=10^4; TLEs beyond. This is your baseline before optimizing.

items.label: Optimal O(n) — count-so-far
items.note: Each new x forms a pair with every earlier equal value -> add freq[x] BEFORE incrementing. This trick kills most O(n^2) pair problems.

items.label: Formula variant
items.code: for (auto& [v, f] : freq) good += 1LL * f * (f - 1) / 2;
items.note: A value seen f times yields C(f,2)=f*(f-1)/2 pairs. Use 1LL* to avoid int overflow.

items.label: JS one-pass
items.code:
const m = new Map(); let good = 0;
for (const x of a) { good += m.get(x)||0; m.set(x,(m.get(x)||0)+1); }

items.label: Problem
items.note: A string is 'happy' if no two adjacent characters are equal. Count how many substrings are happy (i.e. alternating).

items.label: Optimal O(n) — running-run count
items.note: run = length of the alternating streak ending at i = number of happy substrings ending there. Resets to 1 on a repeat.

items.label: Closed form per segment
items.code: // maximal alternating segment of length L -> L*(L+1)/2 substrings
items.note: Equivalent to the running-run sum; handy for a math-only derivation.

items.label: Variant: min deletions to make happy
items.code:
int del = 0;
for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) if (s[i] == s[i-1]) del++;
items.note: Every adjacent equal pair needs one deletion. Watch for which variant the statement actually asks.

items.label: Note on Problem 3
items.note: dsa7 is unlabeled in the course — a follow-up problem drilling the same toolkit below. Reach for these four patterns first on any intro array/string problem.

items.label: Hashing / frequency
items.note: Detect duplicates, anagrams, counts, first-unique. int cnt[26] for fixed alphabet, unordered_map<int,int> otherwise. Turns 'search for a match' into O(1) lookup.

items.label: Prefix sum (range queries O(1))
items.note: O(n) build, O(1) per query. Core of the 'Intro Class 2' track (Highest Altitude, subarray sums).

items.label: Two pointers (sorted array)
items.note: Two-Sum on a sorted array in O(n) / O(1) space — no hashmap needed.

items.label: Two Sum via hashmap (unsorted)
items.note: O(n) when input isn't sorted; check for complement before inserting current.

tips: Read the constraints FIRST — n's magnitude picks your complexity, and value ranges pick your data type (int vs long long).
Counting pairs/substrings overflows 32-bit int fast: n*(n-1)/2 exceeds int around n~65536. Use long long in C++ (Number is safe to 2^53 in JS).
The 'add count-so-far, then increment' hashmap pattern collapses many O(n^2) pair problems to O(n) (see Good Pairs).
JS gotcha: [10,2,1].sort() gives [1,10,2]. Always pass a comparator: .sort((a,b)=>a-b).
C++ operator[] on vector/string is unchecked — out-of-range reads are UB, not exceptions. Guard your indices.
For a fixed alphabet use int cnt[26] instead of a hashmap — no hashing overhead, cache-friendly, and auto-sorted by letter.
Always compile C++ with -O2 and enable fast I/O (sync_with_stdio(false)); many 'wrong' TLEs are just slow I/O.
Test the four killer edge cases every time: empty, single element, all-same, all-distinct.

items.label: Init a TS project
items.code:
npm init -y
npm i -D typescript
npx tsc --init
items.note: tsc --init writes tsconfig.json with sane defaults.

items.label: Enable strict mode (needed for these utilities to be useful)
items.code:
// tsconfig.json
{ "compilerOptions": { "strict": true, "target": "ES2020" } }
items.note: strict turns on strictNullChecks — key for Partial/optional props.

items.label: Compile & run
items.code:
npx tsc          # emit JS
npx tsx index.ts  # run TS directly (no build)

items.label: Utility types are built-in
items.note: Pick/Partial/Readonly/Record/Exclude live in lib.es5.d.ts — no import needed.

items.label: Install zod for runtime validation + inference
items.code: npm i zod
items.note: zod needs strict + "strictNullChecks": true for correct inference.

sections.title: Pick<T, K> — keep a subset of keys

items.label: Select specific properties into a new type
K is constrained to keyof T
items.code: type Bad = Pick<User, "phone">; // Error: "phone" not in keyof User

items.label: Implementation (mapped type)
items.code: type Pick<T, K extends keyof T> = { [P in K]: T[P] };

items.label: Omit = inverse of Pick (drop keys)
items.code:
type NoEmail = Omit<User, "email">;
// Omit<T,K> = Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>
items.note: Omit key does NOT need to exist on T (no error) — unlike Pick.

items.label: All props become optional (great for update/patch payloads)
items.code:
function updateUser(id: number, patch: Partial<User>) { /* ... */ }
updateUser(1, { name: "neo" }); // ok, other fields skipped

items.label: Implementation
items.code: type Partial<T> = { [P in keyof T]?: T[P] };

items.label: Required<T> — inverse, strips the optional
items.code:
type Config = { host?: string; port?: number };
type FullConfig = Required<Config>; // host & port now mandatory

items.label: Not deep
items.note: Partial is shallow — nested objects stay required. Write a recursive DeepPartial if you need it.

items.label: Freeze props at the type level
Implementation
items.code: type Readonly<T> = { readonly [P in keyof T]: T[P] };

items.label: Compile-time only
items.note: No runtime protection — the emitted JS can still mutate. Use Object.freeze() for runtime immutability.

items.label: Readonly arrays / tuples
items.code:
const nums: readonly number[] = [1, 2];
nums.push(3); // Error: push does not exist on readonly array
items.note: `as const` gives you a deeply readonly literal type.

items.label: Build an object type from a key union + value type
Open-ended dictionary
items.code:
type Scores = Record<string, number>;
const s: Scores = { alice: 10, bob: 7 };

items.label: Implementation
items.code: type Record<K extends keyof any, T> = { [P in K]: T };
items.note: keyof any = string | number | symbol.

items.label: Literal-key Records are exhaustive
items.note: Record<"a"|"b", V> requires BOTH keys; missing one is a compile error — good for exhaustive lookup tables.

sections.title: Exclude<T, U> — remove members from a union

items.label: Drop union members assignable to U
items.code:
type Keys = "a" | "b" | "c";
type NoA = Exclude<Keys, "a">; // "b" | "c"

items.label: Works on any union
items.code: type T = Exclude<string | number | boolean, boolean>; // string | number

items.label: Implementation (conditional + distributive)
items.code: type Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T;
items.note: Distributes over each union member; matches collapse to never.

items.label: Extract<T, U> — the inverse (keep matches)
items.code: type Onlyab = Extract<Keys, "a" | "b">; // "a" | "b"

items.label: NonNullable<T> = Exclude<T, null | undefined>
items.code: type X = NonNullable<string | null | undefined>; // string

items.label: Define a schema, derive the TS type with z.infer
items.note: Single source of truth: one schema → runtime validation + static type.

items.label: typeof is required
items.code:
type U = z.infer<UserSchema>;        // Error: UserSchema is a value
type U = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>; // correct
items.note: Pass the schema's TYPE (typeof), not the value.

items.label: Validate at runtime
input vs output types (transforms/defaults)
items.note: z.infer === z.output. Use z.input for the pre-parse shape.

items.label: Combine with utility types
items.code: type NewUser = Omit<z.infer<typeof UserSchema>, "id">;

tips: Pick/Omit/Partial/Readonly are shallow (one level). For nested objects write recursive variants (DeepPartial, DeepReadonly).
Pick errors if a key isn't in keyof T; Omit silently ignores unknown keys — Omit is more forgiving but hides typos.
Readonly and readonly[] are compile-time only. Reach for Object.freeze() when you need runtime enforcement.
Record<K,V> with a literal-union K forces every key to be present — use it for exhaustive maps and to catch missing cases.
Exclude/Extract/NonNullable only operate on unions and rely on distributive conditional types — wrapping T in [T] disables distribution.
These utilities compose: Omit<T,K> is literally Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>; chain them freely (e.g. Partial<Pick<User,'name'>>).
In zod always write z.infer<typeof schema> — forgetting typeof passes a value where a type is expected.
When a zod schema has .transform()/.default(), z.infer gives the OUTPUT type; use z.input for the raw pre-parse shape.

items.label: Vite production build
items.code:
npm run build
# outputs to ./dist  (index.html + assets/*.[hash].js|css)
items.note: Vite default output dir is dist/. Filenames are content-hashed for cache busting.

items.label: Create React App build
items.code:
npm run build
# outputs to ./build
items.note: CRA uses build/ not dist/. Know which one your tool emits before uploading.

items.label: Set base path (S3 subfolder / non-root)
items.note: Wrong base = broken asset URLs (blank page, 404 on /assets/*). Keep '/' when served at domain root.

items.label: Verify the bundle locally before upload
items.code:
npm run preview          # Vite serves dist/ on :4173
npx serve -s build       # CRA / any static dir
items.note: -s = SPA mode: serves index.html for unknown routes so client routing works.

items.label: What a CDN does
items.note: Caches static assets at edge PoPs near users -> lower latency, less origin (S3) load, TLS termination + HTTP/2/3.

items.label: Origin vs edge
items.note: Origin = S3 bucket (source of truth). Edge = CloudFront cache copies. Cache miss -> fetch from origin, then serve cached.

items.label: Cache key & TTL
items.note: Object cached until TTL expires or you invalidate. Hashed asset filenames -> cache forever; index.html -> short/no cache.

items.label: Why not just S3 website URL
items.note: Raw S3 website endpoint = HTTP only, no custom SSL, single region latency. CloudFront adds HTTPS + global edge + custom domain.

items.label: Create bucket
items.code: aws s3 mb s3://my-app-frontend --region us-east-1
items.note: Bucket names are globally unique. For CloudFront-only access, keep it private (don't enable public site).

items.label: Enable static website hosting (public-endpoint path)
items.code:
aws s3 website s3://my-app-frontend \
  --index-document index.html \
  --error-document index.html
items.note: error-document = index.html makes SPA deep links work on the S3 website endpoint.

items.label: Allow public read (only if using public S3 endpoint)
items.code:
aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket my-app-frontend \
  --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicPolicy=false,RestrictPublicBuckets=false
# then attach bucket policy allowing s3:GetObject on arn:aws:s3:::my-app-frontend/*
items.note: Skip this entirely if using CloudFront + OAC (bucket stays fully private — preferred).

items.label: Upload the bundle (sync)
items.code: aws s3 sync ./dist s3://my-app-frontend --delete
items.note: --delete removes stale objects (old hashed assets) so the bucket mirrors dist/ exactly.

items.label: Cache headers per file type
items.code:
# long cache for hashed assets
aws s3 sync ./dist/assets s3://my-app-frontend/assets \
  --cache-control "public,max-age=31536000,immutable"
# no cache for the HTML entrypoint
aws s3 cp ./dist/index.html s3://my-app-frontend/index.html \
  --cache-control "no-cache"
items.note: index.html must NOT be long-cached or users get stale bundles pointing at deleted asset hashes.

items.label: Website endpoint URL format
items.code:
http://<bucket>.s3-website-<region>.amazonaws.com
# e.g. http://my-app-frontend.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com
items.note: HTTP only. Region-specific hostname. This is the quick smoke test before wiring CloudFront.

items.label: Website vs REST endpoint (don't mix them up)
items.note: s3-website-* = serves index/error docs, SPA-friendly. *.s3.amazonaws.com (REST) = returns XML/403 for a bare path, no index resolution.

items.label: 403 / 404 troubleshooting
items.note: 403 usually = block-public-access still on or missing bucket policy. 404 = object key wrong or index-document not set.

items.label: Create distribution (recommended: OAC + private bucket)
items.note: Origin = S3 REST domain (my-app-frontend.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com). Attach Origin Access Control (OAC) so only CloudFront can read the bucket.

items.label: OAC bucket policy
items.note: OAC replaces the deprecated OAI. Bucket public access can stay fully blocked.

items.label: Default root object
items.code: aws cloudfront ... --default-root-object index.html
items.note: Without this, hitting https://d123.cloudfront.net/ returns a 403/AccessDenied instead of your app.

items.label: Redirect HTTP -> HTTPS
items.note: Set Viewer Protocol Policy = redirect-to-https on the default cache behavior.

items.label: Invalidate cache after each deploy
items.code:
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id E123ABC \
  --paths "/index.html" "/"
items.note: Hashed assets need no invalidation. Only invalidate index.html (and /) — invalidations beyond the free 1000/mo cost money.

items.label: Request ACM cert — MUST be us-east-1
items.code:
aws acm request-certificate \
  --domain-name app.example.com \
  --validation-method DNS \
  --region us-east-1
items.note: CloudFront ONLY accepts certs from us-east-1 (N. Virginia), regardless of where your bucket lives. Validate via DNS CNAME.

items.label: Attach domain to distribution
items.note: Add app.example.com to CloudFront 'Alternate domain names (CNAMEs)' and select the ACM cert. Cert domain must cover the CNAME.

items.label: Point DNS at CloudFront
items.code:
# Route 53 (best: alias A/AAAA record)
app.example.com  ALIAS -> d123abc.cloudfront.net
# External DNS provider
app.example.com  CNAME -> d123abc.cloudfront.net
items.note: Route 53 alias lets you point an apex/root domain; a plain CNAME cannot be used on the zone apex.

items.label: The SPA deep-link problem
items.note: /dashboard has no S3 object -> S3 returns 403/404. React Router never loads. Fix: rewrite errors to index.html with 200.

items.label: CloudFront custom error responses (OAC/REST origin)
items.code:
Error code:        403   (and 404)
Response page path: /index.html
HTTP response code: 200
Error caching TTL:  0
items.note: With a private bucket + OAC, missing keys return 403 — map BOTH 403 and 404 to /index.html so client routing takes over.

items.label: S3 website-endpoint alternative
items.code:
aws s3 website s3://my-app-frontend \
  --index-document index.html --error-document index.html
items.note: If origin is the S3 *website* endpoint (not REST), error-document=index.html handles routing — but you lose OAC/private bucket.

items.label: Real 404 page (optional)
items.note: Ship a 404.html and only map genuine not-found routes to it inside your app; keep the catch-all -> index.html for real routes.

tips: ACM certs for CloudFront MUST be in us-east-1 — a cert in your bucket's region will not appear in the distribution dropdown.
Never long-cache index.html. Cache hashed assets forever (immutable, 1yr) but serve HTML with no-cache so new deploys are picked up.
Prefer OAC over public buckets: keep Block Public Access ON, let only CloudFront read S3 via the OAC bucket policy.
Deploy = sync + invalidate. `aws s3 sync --delete` then `create-invalidation --paths /index.html` — forgetting the invalidation serves the old app.
Set --default-root-object index.html or the CloudFront root URL returns AccessDenied.
Map BOTH 403 and 404 CloudFront error responses to /index.html (HTTP 200) or React Router deep links break on refresh.
Match your build's `base` (Vite) / `homepage` (CRA) to the serving path — mismatch shows a blank page with 404s on /assets/*.
Use `aws s3 sync` not `cp` for deploys; --delete prunes old hashed files so the bucket exactly mirrors dist/.

items.label: CSR — render in the browser
items.note: Server sends near-empty HTML + JS bundle; React builds DOM client-side. Fast nav after load, weak SEO/first paint. Use for auth'd dashboards, SPAs.

items.label: SSR — render per request on the server
items.note: HTML built on every request with fresh data, then hydrated. Good SEO + personalization; higher TTFB, needs a running server. Use for feeds, per-user pages.

items.label: SSG — render once at build time
items.note: HTML generated during `next build`, served from CDN. Fastest + cheapest; data is frozen until rebuild. Use for blogs, docs, marketing, product pages.

items.label: ISR — SSG that self-updates
items.note: Static page regenerated in background on a timer / on-demand. Static speed with periodic freshness. Best of SSG+SSR for semi-dynamic content.

items.label: Hydration
items.note: SSR/SSG ship HTML AND JS; the JS 'hydrates' static markup into an interactive React tree. Mismatched server/client output = hydration error.

items.label: Plain React SPA scaffold
items.code:
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react
cd my-app && npm i && npm run dev

items.label: Fetch data after mount (classic CSR)
items.note: User sees a loading spinner first — data is NOT in the initial HTML, so crawlers may miss it.

items.label: Force client rendering in Next.js App Router
items.code:
'use client';
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
items.note: 'use client' opts a component (and its imports) into the client bundle. Required for hooks, event handlers, browser APIs.

items.label: Client-only data with SWR / React Query
items.code: const { data, isLoading } = useSWR('/api/users', fetcher);
items.note: Handles caching, revalidation, dedupe — the idiomatic CSR data layer.

items.label: Pages Router: getServerSideProps
items.note: Runs on the server for EVERY request. Access ctx.req, ctx.res, ctx.query, ctx.params. Never bundled to the client.

items.label: Redirect / 404 from SSR
App Router: dynamic by default via no-store
items.note: Server Components are async. `cache: 'no-store'` makes the route render per request (SSR).

items.label: App Router: force whole segment dynamic
items.code: export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic';
items.note: Also triggered implicitly by using cookies(), headers(), or searchParams.

items.label: Pages Router: getStaticProps
items.note: Runs ONLY at build time (and during ISR). No access to req/res. Data is baked into the HTML.

items.label: Pages Router: getStaticPaths for dynamic routes
items.note: getStaticPaths tells Next WHICH dynamic pages to pre-render. Pair with getStaticProps({ params }).

items.label: fallback modes
items.code:
fallback: false   // unlisted paths -> 404
fallback: 'blocking' // SSR on first hit, then cached
fallback: true    // serve fallback UI, then swap in

items.label: App Router: static by default + generateStaticParams
items.note: App Router prerenders statically unless you opt out. Default fetch cache = 'force-cache'.

items.label: Pages Router: time-based revalidate
items.note: First request after 60s serves stale, triggers background regen; next request gets fresh HTML (stale-while-revalidate).

items.label: App Router: fetch-level revalidate
items.note: Or set for the whole segment: `export const revalidate = 60;`

items.label: On-demand revalidation (webhook / server action)
items.note: Tag a fetch to invalidate it precisely: fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } }).

items.label: fetch cache = strategy switch
items.code:
fetch(url)                              // cached -> SSG
fetch(url, { cache: 'no-store' })       // uncached -> SSR
fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 60 }}) // ISR

items.label: Route segment config exports
Server vs Client components
items.note: Everything is a Server Component by default (runs on server, 0 JS shipped). Add 'use client' only where interactivity/hooks are needed. Keep 'use client' at leaves.

items.label: Streaming SSR with Suspense
items.code:
<Suspense fallback={<Skeleton/>}>
  <SlowServerComponent/>
</Suspense>
items.note: Shell streams instantly; slow parts stream in. Improves TTFB without going full CSR.

items.label: Scaffold a Next.js app
items.code: npx create-next-app@latest my-app

items.label: Build + inspect rendering per route
items.code: next build   # prints route table + strategy legend
items.note: Read the legend to verify each page rendered as you intended.

items.label: Build output legend
items.code:
○  Static    - prerendered, no data
●  SSG       - prerendered w/ getStaticProps
ƒ  Dynamic   - server-rendered on demand (SSR)
ISR          - revalidate shown next to route

items.label: Full static export (no Node server)
items.code:
// next.config.js
module.exports = { output: 'export' };
items.note: Pure SSG to static files. Disables SSR/ISR, API routes, and image optimization defaults.

items.label: Content same for all users, changes rarely
items.note: -> SSG (blog, docs, landing).

items.label: Content same for all, changes periodically
items.note: -> ISR (product listings, pricing, news).

items.label: Per-request / per-user / real-time fresh data
items.note: -> SSR (dashboards feed, personalized, search results).

items.label: Highly interactive, SEO irrelevant, behind auth
items.note: -> CSR (admin panels, editors, internal tools).

items.label: SEO + speed both critical
items.note: -> SSG/ISR first, SSR only if data must be per-request.

items.label: Mix per page
items.note: Modern default: static shell (SSG/ISR) + client islands (CSR) + a few dynamic (SSR) routes.

tips: CSR hurts SEO & first paint because initial HTML is empty — crawlers/slow devices see a blank shell until JS runs.
getServerSideProps (SSR) runs on EVERY request; getStaticProps (SSG) runs at BUILD time. Neither ships to the client bundle — safe for secrets/DB calls.
In App Router the mental model flips: pages are static by default; you opt INTO dynamic via cache:'no-store', cookies(), headers(), or dynamic='force-dynamic'.
ISR revalidate is stale-while-revalidate: the request that triggers regen still gets the OLD page; the NEXT request gets the fresh one.
Hydration errors come from server/client output mismatch — Date.now(), Math.random(), window checks, or locale formatting. Gate them behind useEffect or suppressHydrationWarning.
'use client' is contagious downward but not upward: it marks a boundary; children become client too, but a client component can still receive server-rendered children as props.
output:'export' gives pure static hosting (S3/CDN) but kills SSR, ISR, API routes, and middleware — only choose it if the site is fully static.
Always run `next build` and read the route table (○ ● ƒ) to CONFIRM each page uses the strategy you expect — silent fallbacks to dynamic are common.

items.label: Install compiler (Linux/macOS)
items.code:
sudo apt install g++      # Debian/Ubuntu
xcode-select --install     # macOS (clang++)
items.note: g++ = GNU, clang++ = LLVM; both accept the same flags below.

items.label: Install on Windows
items.code:
# MSYS2 / MinGW-w64
pacman -S mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-gcc
items.note: Add the ...\ucrt64\bin dir to PATH so g++ resolves.

items.label: Compile a single file
items.code:
g++ main.cpp -o main
./main            # .\main.exe on Windows
items.note: No -o -> a.out (a.exe). Compiles AND links in one step.

items.label: Recommended dev flags
items.code: g++ -std=c++17 -Wall -Wextra -g main.cpp -o main
items.note: -std pins the standard; -Wall -Wextra surface bugs; -g adds debug symbols.

items.label: Optimize for release
items.code: g++ -std=c++17 -O2 main.cpp -o main
items.note: -O2 typical; -O3 aggressive. Don't mix -g and -O2 when debugging.

items.label: Check version
items.code: g++ --version

items.label: Minimal program
items.note: main MUST return int. Falling off main implicitly returns 0.

items.label: Output with cout
items.note: << chains left-to-right. Prefer \n over endl in loops (endl flushes = slow).

items.label: Read input with cin
items.code:
int age; string name;
cin >> age >> name;
items.note: >> skips leading whitespace and stops at the next whitespace.

items.label: Read a whole line
items.code:
string line;
getline(cin, line);
items.note: After cin >> x, a newline stays in the buffer — use cin.ignore() before getline.

items.label: Avoid namespace pollution
items.code: std::cout << std::endl;
items.note: `using namespace std;` is fine in small programs; avoid in headers.

items.label: Comments
Integer types
items.code:
int a = 42;          // usually 4 bytes
long long big = 9e18; // 8 bytes, ~9.2e18 max
unsigned int u = 3u; // no negatives
items.note: Use long long for values > ~2.1e9 to avoid overflow.

items.label: Floating point
items.code:
float f = 3.14f;   // ~7 digits
double d = 3.14159265; // ~15 digits
items.note: Prefer double; float literals need the f suffix.

items.label: Character & bool
items.code:
char c = 'A';   // single quotes, 1 byte
bool ok = true; // true/false -> 1/0
items.note: "A" (double quotes) is a C-string, not a char.

items.label: String (C++ std)
items.note: std::string is dynamic; std::string is NOT included by <iostream> reliably — include <string>.

items.label: const & auto
items.code:
const double PI = 3.14159;  // immutable
auto n = 10;                // deduced -> int
items.note: auto needs an initializer; const must be initialized at declaration.

items.label: Sizes & casting
items.code:
cout << sizeof(int);           // bytes
double r = (double)a / b;      // C-style
int x = static_cast<int>(3.9); // -> 3 (truncates)
items.note: Int/int division truncates: 3/2 == 1. Cast one operand to get 1.5.

items.label: if / else if / else
items.note: Condition must be in (). Braces optional for single statements but recommended.

items.label: Logical & comparison ops
items.note: && / || short-circuit: RHS skipped if result already known.

items.label: Ternary operator
items.code: int max = (a > b) ? a : b;
items.note: Great for simple assignments; don't nest deeply.

items.label: switch
items.note: Missing break = fall-through. Only works on int/char/enum, not strings/doubles.

items.label: Common == vs = bug
items.code:
if (x == 5) ...  // compare
x = 5;           // assign
items.note: if (x = 5) assigns then tests truthiness — always true here. -Wall warns.

items.label: for loop
items.note: init; condition; update. i declared in loop is scoped to it.

items.label: while loop
items.code:
int i = 0;
while (i < n) { cout << i; i++; }
items.note: Condition checked before body — may run 0 times.

items.label: do-while loop
items.code:
int i = 0;
do { cout << i; i++; } while (i < n);
items.note: Body runs at least once (condition checked after). Note trailing semicolon.

items.label: Range-based for (C++11)
items.note: Use auto& to mutate or avoid copies of large elements.

items.label: break & continue
Nested loops
items.note: break/continue affect only the innermost loop.

items.label: Definition & call
items.note: Return type before name; void = returns nothing.

items.label: Prototype (declare before use)
items.note: Needed if the definition appears after the call site.

items.label: Pass by value vs reference
items.code:
void inc(int x)  { x++; }   // copy, no effect
void inc(int& x) { x++; }   // modifies caller's var
items.note: Use & to modify the argument or avoid copying large objects.

items.label: const reference (read-only, cheap)
items.code: void print(const string& s) { cout << s; }
items.note: Idiomatic for passing big objects you won't modify.

items.label: Default arguments
items.note: Defaults go in the declaration only; must be trailing params.

items.label: Overloading
items.code:
int  area(int s)            { return s*s; }
double area(double w,double h){ return w*h; }
items.note: Same name, different parameter types/count. Return type alone can't distinguish.

items.label: Declare & initialize
items.code:
int a[5];               // uninitialized
int b[5] = {1,2,3};     // rest = 0
int c[] = {1,2,3,4};    // size deduced = 4
items.note: Fixed size must be a compile-time constant.

items.label: Access & bounds
items.code:
a[0] = 10;
cout << a[4];  // last valid index for size 5
items.note: Indices 0..size-1. Out-of-bounds is undefined behavior (no bounds check!).

items.label: Length of a stack array
items.code: int n = sizeof(a) / sizeof(a[0]);
items.note: Works only where the array's full type is visible — NOT after passing to a function.

items.label: Iterate
items.code:
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cout << a[i];
for (int x : a) cout << x;   // range-based

items.label: Pass to function
items.code:
void fill(int arr[], int n) { ... }
fill(a, 5);
items.note: Arrays decay to pointers — pass the size separately; sizeof inside gives pointer size.

items.label: 2D array
items.note: Row-major. All dimensions except the first are required in params: void f(int m[][3]).

items.label: Prefer std::vector for dynamic size
items.note: Resizable, knows its own size, safer than raw arrays.

tips: Integer division truncates: 7/2 == 3. Cast an operand to double for real division: (double)7/2.
Use long long for sums/products that can exceed ~2.1e9; int silently overflows (undefined behavior).
Uninitialized local variables and arrays hold garbage — always initialize before reading.
Prefer \n over std::endl inside loops; endl forces a buffer flush and is much slower.
In switch, a missing break causes fall-through into the next case — a classic silent bug.
Arrays passed to functions decay to pointers, so sizeof(arr) no longer gives the array size — always pass the length too.
Pass large objects (string, vector) by const& to avoid expensive copies; use & when you need to modify the caller's value.
Compile with -Wall -Wextra: it catches == vs =, unused vars, and signed/unsigned mismatches before they bite.

items.label: Create / init (JS)
items.note: new Array(5) alone makes 5 empty slots, not zeros — always .fill().

items.label: Access by index — O(1)
items.code: a[2]; // random access via base + i*size
items.note: Contiguous memory is what makes indexing constant-time.

items.label: Push / pop (end) — O(1) amortized
items.code:
a.push(4);  // append
a.pop();    // remove last
items.note: Amortized: occasional resize copies all n elements but averages to O(1).

items.label: Shift / unshift (front) — O(n)
items.code:
a.unshift(0); // insert front, shifts all right
a.shift();    // remove front, shifts all left
items.note: Every element re-indexed → avoid in hot loops; use a deque instead.

items.label: Insert / delete middle — O(n)
items.code:
a.splice(i, 0, x); // insert x at i
a.splice(i, 1);    // delete at i
items.note: Cost is from shifting the n-i trailing elements.

items.label: Linear search — O(n)
items.code:
a.indexOf(x);
a.find(v => v > 10);

items.label: Big-O — upper bound (worst case)
items.code: f(n) = O(g(n))  iff  f(n) <= c*g(n) for all n >= n0
items.note: "At most this fast-growing." The one you almost always quote.

items.label: Big-Omega Ω — lower bound (best case)
items.code: f(n) = Ω(g(n))  iff  f(n) >= c*g(n) for n >= n0
items.note: "At least this much work."

items.label: Big-Theta Θ — tight bound
items.code: f(n) = Θ(g(n))  iff  f = O(g) AND f = Ω(g)
items.note: Upper and lower match → the true growth rate.

items.label: little-o / little-ω — strict bounds
items.code: f = o(g): f grows strictly slower (ratio -> 0)
items.note: o/ω exclude the tight case; O/Ω allow equality.

items.label: Growth ranking
items.code: 1 < log n < n < n log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!
items.note: Compare by dominant term as n -> infinity; constants don't affect the class.

items.label: Drop constants
items.note: Big-O ignores multiplicative constants.

items.label: Drop lower-order terms
items.note: Keep only the fastest-growing term.

items.label: Sequential code → ADD
Nested loops → MULTIPLY
items.code:
for i in n:
  for j in n:   // O(n * n) = O(n^2)
items.note: Watch inner bounds: for j in i gives n(n+1)/2 = O(n^2) still.

items.label: Halving the input → log n
items.code: while (n > 1) n = n / 2;  // O(log n)
items.note: Doubling/halving each step ⇒ log2(n) iterations.

items.label: Separate variables stay separate
items.code:
// two different arrays sizes
O(a + b), NOT O(n)
items.note: Don't collapse independent inputs into one n.

items.label: Analyze worst case by default
items.note: Best/average also exist, but O() interviews mean worst case unless stated.

items.label: O(1) constant
items.code: arr[i]; map.get(k); push/pop
items.note: Independent of n.

items.label: O(log n) logarithmic
items.code: binary search; balanced BST op
items.note: Input cut by a constant factor each step.

items.label: O(n) linear
items.code: single pass / scan
items.note: One touch per element.

items.label: O(n log n) linearithmic
items.code: merge sort, heap sort, Array.sort()
items.note: Best possible for comparison sorting.

items.label: O(n^2) quadratic
items.code: nested loop, bubble/insertion sort, all pairs
items.note: n up to ~10^4 is usually the ceiling for O(n^2).

items.label: O(2^n) exponential
items.code: naive subsets, recursive fib without memo
items.note: Feasible only for n ~ 20-25.

items.label: O(n!) factorial
items.code: permutations, brute-force TSP

items.label: Recurrence form
items.code:
T(n) = a*T(n/b) + f(n)
// a >= 1 subproblems, each size n/b, f(n) = split+merge cost
items.note: Compare f(n) against n^(log_b a) = the "watershed" term.

items.label: Case 1 — leaves dominate
items.code:
if f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - e)),  e>0
=> T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a))
items.note: Work grows toward the bottom of the recursion tree.

items.label: Case 2 — balanced
items.code:
if f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a))
=> T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) * log n)
items.note: Every level does equal work; +log n from the level count.

items.label: Case 3 — root dominates
items.code:
if f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + e)) and regularity a*f(n/b) <= c*f(n)
=> T(n) = Θ(f(n))

items.label: Merge sort
items.code:
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)
log_2 2 = 1, f = n = n^1  -> Case 2
=> Θ(n log n)

items.label: Doesn't apply when
items.note: a<1, non-polynomial gap between f and n^(log_b a), or uneven splits — use recursion-tree / Akra–Bazzi instead.

items.label: Two pointers — O(n)
items.note: Requires sorted input for the sum/target variant.
