items.label: Sliding window — O(n)
items.note: Turns an O(n^2) subarray scan into one pass.

items.label: Prefix sum — O(n) build, O(1) range query
Kadane — max subarray, O(n)
Binary search — O(log n)
items.note: (lo+hi)>>1 avoids overflow vs (lo+hi)/2 in fixed-width langs.

items.label: Frequency / hashing — O(n)
items.code:
const freq = new Map();
for (const x of a) freq.set(x, (freq.get(x) ?? 0) + 1);
items.note: Trades O(n) space to drop time from O(n^2) to O(n).

tips: Big-O measures growth rate, not runtime — a well-tuned O(n^2) can beat O(n log n) for tiny n; asymptotics only win as n grows.
unshift/shift/splice at the front are O(n) because every element is re-indexed — reach for a deque or index pointer in hot paths.
push/pop is O(1) amortized, not strictly O(1): a resize copies all n elements, but it averages out.
Map input size to a target complexity: n<=10 → O(n!)/O(2^n) ok; n<=10^4 → O(n^2); n<=10^6 → O(n log n)/O(n).
Master Theorem needs the a*T(n/b) shape; unequal splits (e.g. T(n)=T(n/3)+T(2n/3)+n) need a recursion tree or Akra–Bazzi.
In Master Theorem the pivot is n^(log_b a): compare f(n) against it — smaller ⇒ Case 1, equal ⇒ Case 2 (adds log n), larger ⇒ Case 3.
Space complexity counts too — recursion depth (call stack) and auxiliary arrays are part of the cost, not free.
Don't collapse independent inputs: two loops over different arrays are O(a+b), not O(n) — only merge variables that are actually the same size.

items.label: std::string basics (prefer over C-strings)
items.note: at() throws std::out_of_range; operator[] is UB out of bounds.

items.label: Common operations
items.note: find returns std::string::npos (not -1) when not found.

items.label: Iterate / range-for
items.code:
for (char c : s) std::cout << c;
for (char& c : s) c = toupper(c);   // & to mutate

items.label: Number <-> string conversion
items.note: stoi throws std::invalid_argument / std::out_of_range.

items.label: C-string (char array) — legacy
items.note: Always leave room for the trailing '\0'; buffer overruns are UB.

items.label: Read input
items.code:
std::cin >> s;              // one token (stops at whitespace)
std::getline(std::cin, s);  // whole line
items.note: Mixing >> then getline: flush stray newline with std::cin.ignore().

items.label: Declare, address-of, dereference
items.code:
int x = 10;
int* p = &x;    // p holds address of x
*p = 20;        // x is now 20
std::cout << p  // prints address
          << *p; // prints value
items.note: *p in a declaration = pointer type; *p in an expression = dereference.

items.label: Null pointer
items.code:
int* p = nullptr;   // C++11, prefer over NULL/0
if (p) { /* safe */ }
items.note: Dereferencing nullptr is UB — always check before *p.

items.label: Pointer arithmetic / arrays
items.code:
int a[3] = {1,2,3};
int* p = a;      // decays to &a[0]
*(p+1);          // == a[1] == 2
p++;             // next element (scaled by sizeof(int))
items.note: Array name decays to pointer; sizeof(a) != sizeof(p).

items.label: const and pointers (read right-to-left)
Reference vs pointer
items.code:
int& r = x;   // alias, can't be null, can't rebind
int* p = &x;  // can be null, reassignable
items.note: Prefer references for params/returns when null isn't meaningful.

items.label: Pointer to pointer / member access
new / delete (single object)
items.code:
int* p = new int(42);   // heap, initialized to 42
delete p;               // free
p = nullptr;            // avoid dangling
items.note: Every new needs exactly one matching delete or it leaks.

items.label: new[] / delete[] (arrays)
items.code:
int* arr = new int[n];      // uninitialized
int* z   = new int[n]();    // zero-initialized
delete[] arr;               // MUST use delete[]
items.note: Mismatching new[] with delete (no brackets) is UB.

items.label: Smart pointers — prefer these (RAII)
items.note: unique_ptr is move-only; use shared_ptr only when ownership is shared.

items.label: unique_ptr for arrays / release
Dangling pointer trap
items.note: Set to nullptr after delete; don't return address of local variable.

items.label: Pass by value (copy)
items.code:
void f(int x)  { x = 5; }   // caller unchanged
void g(std::string s);      // copies whole string!
items.note: Cheap for primitives; expensive for big objects — copies each call.

items.label: Pass by reference (mutate caller)
items.note: No copy; caller's variable is modified directly.

items.label: Pass by const reference (read-only, no copy)
items.code:
void print(const std::string& s);  // idiomatic for large objs
double sum(const std::vector<int>& v);
items.note: Default for non-trivial types you don't need to modify.

items.label: Pass by pointer
items.code:
void inc(int* x) { if (x) (*x)++; }
int a = 1; inc(&a);
items.note: Use when arg is optional (can be nullptr) or for C-style APIs.

items.label: Default arguments
items.note: Defaults go in the declaration only, not the definition. Must be trailing.

items.label: Arrays decay to pointers
items.code:
void f(int a[], int n);  // a is really int*
// sizeof(a) inside f == sizeof(int*), not the array!
items.note: Pass length separately, or use std::vector / std::span (C++20).

items.label: Basic class + access specifiers
items.note: class members default to private; struct defaults to public.

items.label: Constructor / member init list
items.note: Prefer init lists over assignment in body; required for const/reference members.

items.label: Destructor
items.note: Called automatically at scope end / on delete. One per class.

items.label: Methods: definition & const
items.code:
int Point::getX() const { return x; }  // out-of-line
void setX(int v) { x = v; }            // inline
items.note: const methods can be called on const objects; mark getters const.

items.label: this pointer & static members
items.code:
void Point::setX(int x) { this->x = x; }  // disambiguate
static int count;      // shared across all instances
static int get() { return count; }        // no this
items.note: static members defined once outside class: int Point::count = 0;

items.label: Object on stack vs heap
items.code:
Point p(1,2);              // stack, auto-destroyed
Point* h = new Point(1,2); // heap
h->getX();
delete h;                  // manual (or use unique_ptr)

tips: Prefer std::string over char[] and smart pointers (make_unique/make_shared) over raw new/delete — RAII frees automatically and avoids leaks.
Every new pairs with delete, every new[] with delete[]. Mismatch = UB. Better: don't call them directly.
std::string::find returns std::string::npos, not -1, when the substring is missing.
Pass large objects by const reference; primitives by value; use pointer/reference only when you must mutate the caller's variable.
After delete, set the pointer to nullptr to avoid dangling use-after-free; never dereference nullptr.
Mark getters and non-mutating methods const so they work on const objects; use member init lists in constructors (mandatory for const/reference members).
Arrays decay to pointers when passed to functions — sizeof gives the pointer size, so pass the length explicitly.
class defaults to private access, struct defaults to public — otherwise identical in C++.

items.label: Clone + install both apps
items.code:
git clone <repo-url> paytm && cd paytm
cd backend  && npm install
cd ../frontend && npm install

items.label: Backend deps
items.code:
npm i express mongoose jsonwebtoken zod cors
# bcrypt optional for hashing

items.label: Backend layout
items.code:
backend/
  index.js        # app + mount /api/v1
  db.js           # mongoose models
  config.js       # JWT_SECRET
  middleware.js   # authMiddleware
  routes/
    index.js      # rootRouter
    user.js
    account.js
items.note: Keep secrets in config.js (or .env), never inline in routes.

items.label: App entry (index.js)
items.note: express.json() BEFORE routers or req.body is undefined.

items.label: Connect
items.code:
const mongoose=require('mongoose');
mongoose.connect('mongodb+srv://.../paytm');

items.label: User schema
items.note: unique:true builds an index; existing dupes must be cleared first.

items.label: Account schema (separate collection)
items.note: Store money in its own collection, referenced by userId — not on User.

items.label: Export models
rootRouter (routes/index.js)
items.note: Full path = /api/v1 + /user + /signup → /api/v1/user/signup.

items.label: Per-file router
items.note: express.Router() lets each resource live in its own file.

items.label: Read query / body / params
items.code:
const filter=req.query.filter || '';   // ?filter=abc
const {amount,to}=req.body;            // JSON body
const id=req.params.id;                // /:id

items.label: zod input schema
Signup → create user + account + JWT
items.note: safeParse returns {success,data} — never throws. Give random starting balance.

items.label: Signin → verify + JWT
items.note: jwt.sign({userId}) — put the id in the payload; read it back in middleware.

items.label: Update (protected)
Bulk search (for send-money list)
config.js
items.code: module.exports={ JWT_SECRET:'your-super-secret' };

items.label: authMiddleware
items.note: Token comes as 'Bearer <token>' — split on space. Attach userId to req, then next().

items.label: Get balance (protected)
Transfer with a DB transaction
items.note: All reads/writes must use .session(session). Any abort rolls back both $inc — no half-transfers.

items.label: Why $inc, not read-then-set
items.code: {$inc:{balance:-amount}}  // atomic
items.note: read → subtract → save races under concurrency; $inc is a single atomic op.

items.label: Frontend deps
items.code:
npm i react-router-dom axios
# tailwind for styling (optional)

items.label: App.jsx routes
items.code:
import {BrowserRouter,Routes,Route} from 'react-router-dom';
<BrowserRouter>
  <Routes>
    <Route path='/signup'    element={<Signup/>}/>
    <Route path='/signin'    element={<Signin/>}/>
    <Route path='/dashboard' element={<Dashboard/>}/>
    <Route path='/send'      element={<SendMoney/>}/>
  </Routes>
</BrowserRouter>
items.note: BrowserRouter wraps Routes; each Route maps a path to an element.

items.label: Signup/Signin submit → store token → redirect
items.note: Persist JWT in localStorage; useNavigate for programmatic redirect.

items.label: Send auth header on protected calls
items.note: Must match middleware's 'Bearer ' prefix exactly.

items.label: Users list — filter + debounce
items.note: Re-fetch on every filter change; wrap in setTimeout to debounce keystrokes.

items.label: SendMoney — read recipient from URL
items.note: Users 'Send money' button links to /send?id=..&name=.. ; read with useSearchParams.

tips: req.body is undefined unless app.use(express.json()) runs before your routers — and app.use(cors()) is required or the React app's fetches get blocked.
zod safeParse returns {success,data} and never throws; check !success and return 411 before touching the DB.
Keep money in a separate Account collection keyed by userId — don't put balance on the User doc.
Transfers MUST use mongoose.startSession()/startTransaction() with .session() on every op; abortTransaction on any failure so you never debit without crediting.
Use $inc for balance changes — read-modify-write loses updates under concurrent transfers.
JWT flow: sign {userId} on signup/signin, send as 'Bearer <token>', verify + set req.userId in authMiddleware. The 'Bearer ' prefix must match on both ends.
Frontend keeps the token in localStorage and attaches Authorization headers manually; there are no cookies in this project.
MongoDB transactions require a replica set (Atlas works by default; a bare local mongod does not) — otherwise startTransaction throws.
unique:true on username only enforces after the index builds; wipe duplicate docs before relying on it, and still do a findOne pre-check for a clean 411.

items.label: OpenAPI = language-agnostic contract for HTTP APIs
items.note: Single source of truth for endpoints, params, schemas, auth. Formerly 'Swagger' (spec renamed at 3.0).

items.label: What it buys you
items.note: Auto docs (Swagger UI / Redoc), typed client + server codegen, request/response validation, mock servers, contract testing.

items.label: Format: YAML or JSON
items.note: Both equivalent; YAML is the human-authored default. Versions: Swagger 2.0 (legacy) vs OpenAPI 3.0.x / 3.1.x (3.1 is a strict JSON Schema superset).

items.label: Design-first vs code-first
items.note: Design-first: write spec, generate stubs. Code-first: annotate code (e.g. Zod), emit spec. Hono+Zod is code-first.

items.label: Top-level skeleton
items.code:
openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  title: Notes API
  version: 1.0.0
servers:
  - url: https://api.example.com/v1
paths: {}
components: {}
items.note: openapi, info, paths are effectively required. servers replaces 2.0's host/basePath/schemes.

items.label: info — metadata
items.note: title + version mandatory. Add description, license, contact for good docs.

items.label: paths — the endpoints
items.note: Keyed by URL template, then by HTTP method (get/post/put/delete/patch).

items.label: components — reusable definitions
items.note: Holds schemas, parameters, responses, requestBodies, securitySchemes. Reference with $ref.

items.label: $ref — reuse anything
items.code:
schema:
  $ref: '#/components/schemas/Note'
items.note: Avoids duplication; can point to external files too ('./models.yaml#/Note').

items.label: An operation with a JSON body + 201
items.code:
paths:
  /notes:
    post:
      operationId: createNote
      summary: Create a note
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/NewNote'
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Created
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/Note'
items.note: operationId must be unique — codegen uses it for function names.

items.label: Reusable schema in components
items.note: format (uuid/date-time/email) is a hint; required is an array of prop names, not per-field.

items.label: Path / query params
items.code:
get:
      parameters:
        - name: id
          in: path
          required: true
          schema: { type: string }
        - name: limit
          in: query
          schema: { type: integer, default: 20 }
items.note: in: path params are always required: true.

items.label: Security scheme + apply
items.code:
components:
  securitySchemes:
    bearerAuth:
      type: http
      scheme: bearer
      bearerFormat: JWT
security:
  - bearerAuth: []
items.note: Top-level security = global default; override per-operation. Empty [] = no scopes.

items.label: Install
items.code: npm i hono @hono/zod-openapi @hono/swagger-ui
items.note: @hono/zod-openapi re-exports a patched `z` — import z from it, not from 'zod'.

items.label: Define schema, name it for components
items.note: .openapi('Note') registers it under components/schemas so $ref reuse works.

items.label: createRoute — the typed contract
items.note: Path uses {id} (OpenAPI style), not :id. Response keys are numeric status codes.

items.label: Wire handler with validated input
items.note: c.req.valid('param'|'query'|'json') returns parsed+typed data. 400 auto-returned on validation failure.

items.label: Register a security scheme
items.note: Then add `security: [{ bearerAuth: [] }]` inside createRoute.

items.label: Expose the generated JSON doc
items.note: GET /doc returns the full spec assembled from all createRoute definitions.

items.label: Mount Swagger UI
items.code:
import { swaggerUI } from '@hono/swagger-ui'

app.get('/ui', swaggerUI({ url: '/doc' }))
items.note: url must point at the served spec route. Visit /ui for interactive 'try it out' docs.

items.label: Redoc alternative (static HTML)
items.code:
<script src="https://cdn.redoc.ly/redoc/latest/bundles/redoc.standalone.js"></script>
<redoc spec-url="/doc"></redoc>
items.note: Redoc = read-only, cleaner docs; Swagger UI = interactive testing.

items.label: Handle servers behind a proxy
items.note: Set servers[].url in app.doc so 'try it out' hits the right base URL, not localhost.

items.label: openapi-typescript — types only (lightweight)
items.code: npx openapi-typescript http://localhost:3000/doc -o ./src/api.d.ts
items.note: Emits TS types, no runtime. Pair with openapi-fetch for a typed fetch wrapper.

items.label: openapi-fetch — typed runtime client
items.note: ~2kb, no codegen of methods — types drive path/param autocomplete.

items.label: Hono RPC (no external gen)
items.note: End-to-end types straight from the app type — skips OpenAPI entirely for Hono-to-Hono.

items.label: orval — full client + React Query hooks
items.code: npx orval --input ./doc.json --output ./src/gen/api.ts
items.note: Generates hooks/axios/fetch + MSW mocks. Config via orval.config.ts.

items.label: openapi-generator-cli — polyglot
items.code:
npx @openapitools/openapi-generator-cli generate \
  -i doc.yaml -g typescript-axios -o ./client
items.note: ~7 generators (typescript-axios, python, go, java...). Heavy but language-agnostic.

tips: Import `z` from '@hono/zod-openapi', never from 'zod' directly — only the patched instance carries the `.openapi()` method.
Give every operation a unique operationId: client generators derive function names from it; duplicates or missing ones produce ugly/colliding names.
OpenAPI path templates use {braces} ({id}); Hono's own router uses :colons (:id). In createRoute always use the brace form.
3.1 aligns with JSON Schema (nullable via `type: [string, null]`); 3.0 uses `nullable: true`. Generators differ in support — pin your target version.
Name your Zod schemas with `.openapi('Name')` so they land in components/schemas and get $ref'd instead of inlined — cleaner docs and smaller generated clients.
`required` at the object level is a list of property names, not a per-property flag; forgetting it makes every field optional in generated types.
Regenerate clients in CI from the live /doc endpoint (or a committed spec) so drift between server and client is caught, not shipped.
Swagger UI 'Try it out' calls the servers[].url — set it explicitly for staging/prod, or requests silently hit localhost.

sections.title: Vim/vi — modes & motions (works on any server)

items.label: Modes: Normal (nav), Insert (type), Visual (select), Cmdline (:)
items.code: Esc            # back to Normal from anywhere
items.note: vi is POSIX — present on virtually every *nix box.

items.label: Enter Insert mode
items.code:
i  a       # before / after cursor
I  A       # start / end of line
o  O       # new line below / above

items.label: Move by character / word / line
items.code:
h j k l    # left down up right
w b e      # word fwd / back / end
0 ^ $      # col0 / first non-blank / EOL

items.label: Jump around the file
items.code:
gg  G      # top / bottom
42G / :42  # go to line 42
Ctrl-d Ctrl-u  # half-page down / up
{  }       # prev / next paragraph

items.label: Find char on line, then repeat
items.code:
f( t,      # jump to / before char
;  ,       # repeat forward / backward

items.label: Counts multiply any motion/operator
items.code: 3w   5j   2dd   d3w
items.note: count + operator + motion is the whole grammar.

items.label: Operator + motion
items.code: dw  d$  c$  y}  >i{
items.note: d=delete c=change y=yank >=indent.

items.label: Text objects (edit by structure, not position)
items.code:
ciw   # change inner word
di(   # delete inside parens
ci"   # change inside quotes
dap   # delete a paragraph
cit   # change inside HTML/XML tag
items.note: i=inner, a=around (includes delimiters/space).

items.label: Whole-line ops
items.code:
dd yy cc   # delete / yank / change line
>> <<      # indent / dedent

items.label: Paste, undo, repeat
items.code:
p  P       # paste after / before
u  Ctrl-r  # undo / redo
.          # repeat last change

items.label: Visual select
items.code:
v   V   Ctrl-v   # char / line / block
gv               # reselect last selection

items.label: Search & jump
items.code:
/foo   n N   # search, next / prev
*            # search word under cursor
%            # jump to matching bracket

items.label: Substitute (find/replace)
items.code:
:%s/old/new/g     # whole file
:%s/old/new/gc    # with confirm
:'<,'>s/x/y/g     # in visual range

items.label: Registers & marks
items.code:
"ayy  "ap    # yank to / paste from reg a
ma  `a       # set mark a / jump to it
:reg  :marks # inspect

items.label: Save / quit
items.code:
:w  :wq  :x  ZZ   # write / write+quit
:q!  ZQ           # quit discard

items.label: Config entry point (Lua)
items.code: ~/.config/nvim/init.lua
items.note: $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/nvim; init.vim still works but Lua is idiomatic.

items.label: Options & leader
items.code:
vim.g.mapleader = ' '
vim.opt.number = true
vim.opt.relativenumber = true
vim.opt.expandtab = true
vim.opt.shiftwidth = 2

items.label: Keymaps in Lua
items.code:
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>w', ':w<CR>')
vim.keymap.set('i', 'jk', '<Esc>')
items.note: opts = { desc=..., silent=true, noremap=true }.

items.label: Plugin manager — lazy.nvim
items.note: :Lazy to manage; lockfile pins versions.

items.label: LSP (go-to-def, hover, rename)
items.code:
vim.lsp.enable('lua_ls')  -- nvim 0.11+
-- or require('lspconfig').ts_ls.setup{}
items.note: Use :Mason to install servers; K=hover, gd=def, <leader>rn=rename.

items.label: Diagnostics & health
items.code:
:checkhealth
:LspInfo  :Lazy  :Mason
:TSUpdate

sections.title: Vimium — Vim motions in the browser

items.label: Install
items.code: Vimium (Chrome/Edge) · Vimium-FF (Firefox)
items.note: Type 'i' for native insert mode; Esc leaves it. Excluded on some pages (e.g. new-tab).

items.label: Scroll
items.code:
j k        # down / up
d u        # half-page
gg  G      # top / bottom

items.label: Follow links via hints
items.code:
f          # open link in current tab
F          # open link in new tab
items.note: Type the highlighted hint letters to activate.

items.label: Tabs
items.code:
J K / gT gt  # prev / next tab
t            # new tab
x  X         # close / restore closed
^ (caret)    # last-visited tab

items.label: Navigate & open
items.code:
o  O       # open URL/bookmark (curr / new tab)
b  B       # open bookmark
H  L       # history back / forward

items.label: Utility
items.code:
gi         # focus first text input
yy         # copy current URL
/  n N     # find in page
?          # show all bindings

items.label: Pick an extension
items.code:
vscodevim.vim         # emulation, easy
asvetliakov.vscode-neovim  # real embedded nvim
items.note: vscode-neovim = full plugin/LSP parity; VSCodeVim = simpler, uses VSCode features.

items.label: Core settings (settings.json)
items.code:
"vim.leader": "<space>",
"vim.useSystemClipboard": true,
"vim.hlsearch": true,
"editor.lineNumbers": "relative"

items.label: Escape without reaching for Esc
Normal-mode custom binds
items.note: Non-recursive avoids remap loops; 'commands' calls VSCode actions.

items.label: Let VSCode keep some Ctrl keys
items.code: "vim.handleKeys": { "<C-d>": true, "<C-a>": false }
items.note: false = pass through to VSCode (e.g. Ctrl-a select-all).

items.label: Sessions
items.code:
tmux new -s dev     # create named
tmux ls             # list
tmux a -t dev       # attach
tmux kill-session -t dev
items.note: Sessions survive SSH disconnects — attach again later.

items.label: Prefix key (default)
items.code: Ctrl-b   # then a command key
items.note: All bindings below are 'prefix, then key'.

items.label: Windows (tabs)
items.code:
c        # new window
n p      # next / prev
0-9      # jump to window N
,        # rename
&        # kill window

items.label: Panes (splits)
items.code:
%        # split vertical
"        # split horizontal
o / arrows  # move focus
z        # zoom/unzoom pane
x        # kill pane

items.label: Detach & copy mode
items.code:
d        # detach (session keeps running)
[        # enter copy/scroll mode (q to exit)

items.label: Config ~/.tmux.conf
items.code:
set -g prefix C-a        # rebind prefix
unbind C-b
set -g mouse on
set -g base-index 1
items.note: Reload live: prefix + : then `source-file ~/.tmux.conf`.

items.label: Tiling WM by OS
items.code:
macOS : yabai + skhd  ·  AeroSpace
Linux : i3 / sway / Hyprland
Windows: komorebi  ·  FancyZones
items.note: Auto-arranges windows in a grid; drive focus/move/resize from the keyboard.

items.label: i3 config (~/.config/i3/config)
items.code:
bindsym $mod+Return exec alacritty
bindsym $mod+j focus left
bindsym $mod+1 workspace 1
bindsym $mod+Shift+q kill
items.note: $mod is usually Super/Alt; reload with $mod+Shift+r.

items.label: Which shell rc file loads when
items.code:
bash login   : ~/.bash_profile -> ~/.bashrc
bash non-login: ~/.bashrc
zsh (default): ~/.zshrc
login (both) : ~/.profile / ~/.zprofile
items.note: Interactive-but-non-login (new terminal tab) reads .bashrc/.zshrc — put aliases there.

items.label: Aliases, exports, functions
items.code:
alias gs='git status'
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
export EDITOR=nvim
mkcd(){ mkdir -p "$1" && cd "$1"; }

items.label: Reload without new shell
items.code: source ~/.zshrc   # or: . ~/.zshrc
items.note: exec $SHELL -l for a clean login re-init.

items.label: Prompt & framework add-ons
items.code:
oh-my-zsh · zinit · starship
eval "$(starship init zsh)"
items.note: starship is shell-agnostic (bash/zsh/fish/pwsh).

tips: vi/vim is guaranteed on almost every server (POSIX) — learn the motions once and you can edit anywhere over SSH.
Think grammar, not shortcuts: count + operator + text-object (e.g. 2ci") composes infinitely; text objects (ciw, di() beat raw motions.
The dot command '.' repeats your last change — pair it with n (search) for fast repetitive edits, and stay out of Visual mode when '.' would do.
Remap Caps Lock to Esc (or Ctrl) at the OS level — the single biggest ergonomic win for Vim and tmux.
Rebind the tmux prefix off Ctrl-b (Ctrl-a or Ctrl-Space) and enable mouse on while you learn — reload with source-file.
Know your rc file: aliases/exports go in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc (interactive), PATH/env for login shells in ~/.profile/~/.zprofile.
Keep dotfiles in a git repo and symlink them (GNU stow or a bootstrap script) so every machine is one clone away.
In VSCode prefer vscode-neovim for real plugin/LSP parity; use vim.handleKeys to hand specific Ctrl-combos back to VSCode.

items.label: Built-in hooks you'll compose
items.code:
const [v, setV] = useState(0);       // local state
useEffect(() => {...}, [deps]);       // side effects
const ref = useRef(null);             // mutable box / DOM
const memo = useMemo(() => calc, [d]);// cache value
const cb = useCallback(fn, [d]);      // cache fn identity
const ctx = useContext(MyContext);    // read provider
items.note: Hooks are functions starting with `use` that let function components hook into React state/lifecycle.

items.label: Rules of Hooks (enforced by eslint-plugin-react-hooks)
items.code:
// Call at the TOP LEVEL only:
if (cond) useState();   // BAD - conditional
for (...) useEffect();   // BAD - loop
function handler() { useState(); } // BAD - nested fn
items.note: Only call hooks at the top level of a component OR another hook. Same order every render — React tracks them by call index.

items.label: Effect cleanup runs before re-run & on unmount
items.note: Return a function to tear down subscriptions/timers/listeners. StrictMode double-invokes effects in dev to surface missing cleanup.

items.label: A custom hook = a function named useX that calls other hooks
items.note: It's just extraction/reuse of stateful logic. Each call gets its OWN isolated state — hooks share logic, not state.

items.label: Return shape: array for 2 values, object for many
items.code:
return [value, setValue];              // destructure w/ any name
return { data, loading, error, refetch }; // self-documenting
items.note: Arrays let callers rename; objects avoid positional mistakes when returning 3+ things.

items.label: Accept + forward deps so effects stay reactive
items.note: Whatever the hook uses from arguments/state must be in the dep array — treat the hook like a mini-component.

items.label: useFetch — data/loading/error triad with abort
items.note: AbortController cancels the in-flight request when `url` changes or the component unmounts — prevents setting state on stale/unmounted responses.

items.label: Add refetch via a manual trigger
useDebounce — debounce fast-changing search input
items.note: Cleanup clears the previous timeout so the effect only fires after the value stops changing for `delay` ms.

items.label: useLocalStorage — state synced to localStorage
items.note: Lazy initializer `() => ...` reads storage only once. Wrap getItem in try/catch for SSR / private-mode / bad JSON.

items.label: useWindowSize — track viewport on resize
items.note: Always remove the listener in cleanup or you leak on every mount. Consider throttling onResize (see timer section).

items.label: useMediaQuery — reactive breakpoints
useOnlineStatus / useClickOutside
useInterval — declarative setInterval (Dan Abramov pattern)
items.note: The ref keeps the latest callback without resetting the interval each render. Pass `delay = null` to pause.

items.label: useTimeout — same ref trick, one-shot
useThrottle — cap update frequency (scroll/resize)
items.note: Throttle = at most once per interval (steady stream). Debounce = only after activity stops. Pick throttle for scroll, debounce for search.

items.label: usePrevious — remember last render's value

tips: Name every custom hook `useSomething` — the `use` prefix is what lets the linter apply the Rules of Hooks and lets you call other hooks inside.
Custom hooks share LOGIC, not STATE. Two components calling the same hook each get fully independent state — there's no magic global.
Every value from props/state used inside an effect must be in its dependency array. Silencing eslint-plugin-react-hooks warnings hides stale-closure bugs.
Always return a cleanup function from effects that add listeners, timers, or subscriptions — otherwise you leak and get 'setState on unmounted component' warnings.
For data fetching, use AbortController to cancel stale requests; otherwise a slow earlier response can overwrite a newer one (race condition).
Timer/interval hooks: stash the callback in a useRef and update it in a separate effect, so the interval isn't torn down and recreated every render.
Debounce for 'wait until user stops' (search boxes); throttle for 'run at most every N ms' (scroll, resize, mousemove). They are not interchangeable.
Prefer battle-tested libs (usehooks-ts, react-use, @uidotdev/usehooks, or TanStack Query for fetching) over hand-rolling — but know the internals for interviews.

items.label: Why
items.note: Stop brute-force/credential-stuffing, OTP guessing, scraping, spam signups, cost blow-ups (SMS/email/LLM), and app-layer DoS.

items.label: High-value endpoints to protect
items.note: /login, /signup, /forgot-password, /reset, /verify-otp, /payment, public search & any unauthenticated write. Auth + reset are #1 targets.

items.label: Defense layers (outer→inner)
items.note: Edge CDN/WAF (Cloudflare) → LB/API gateway → app middleware (express-rate-limit) → DB constraints. Never rely on a single layer.

items.label: Trust the proxy first
items.code:
app.set('trust proxy', 1); // behind CF/nginx/ELB
// else req.ip = LB IP and every client shares one bucket
items.note: Without this, X-Forwarded-For is ignored and all users look like one IP.

items.label: Basic global limiter
items.note: Sends RateLimit-* headers; 429 on exceed.

items.label: Strict limiter on sensitive routes
items.note: Tighter budget for OTP/reset/login than for read APIs.

items.label: Key by IP + identity (not IP alone)
items.code: keyGenerator: (req) => `${req.ip}:${req.body?.email ?? ''}`
items.note: Per-account budget so one shared NAT/office IP can't lock everyone out, and attacker can't spread guesses across accounts.

items.label: Distributed store (multi-instance)
items.note: In-memory limiter resets on restart and is per-process — useless behind >1 pod. Use Redis.

items.label: Slow-down instead of hard block
items.code:
import slowDown from 'express-slow-down';
app.use(slowDown({ windowMs: 60_000, delayAfter: 20, delayMs: (h)=>h*200 }));
items.note: Adds latency progressively — friendlier for legit bursty users.

items.label: Naive forgot: 6-digit OTP in memory
items.note: Math.random() is not cryptographic; 6 digits = 10^6 space; never expires.

items.label: Naive reset: no attempt cap
items.note: No rate limit + no lockout + loose == = brute-forceable.

items.label: Exploit: brute force all 6-digit OTPs
items.note: At even 1k req/s, 10^6 codes fall in ~minutes. Production = same script + concurrency.

items.label: Use a crypto token, not a 6-digit code
items.note: Unbrute-forceable search space; store the HASH so a DB leak can't reset accounts.

items.label: Persist with expiry + attempt counter
items.note: Short TTL (10-15 min), single-use, invalidate on success/logout.

items.label: Timing-safe compare (if you keep short codes)
items.code: const ok = crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(a), Buffer.from(b));
items.note: Prevents timing side-channel; buffers must be equal length. Prefer full tokens over OTPs.

items.label: Lockout after N attempts
items.note: Combine with the rate limiter — layered, not either/or.

items.label: Prevent user enumeration
items.code:
// /forgot: same 200 + generic message whether or not email exists
res.json({ message: 'If an account exists, a link was sent' });
items.note: Also keep response timing constant; don't leak 'user not found'.

sections.title: The Problem: IP Limits Aren't Enough → CAPTCHA

items.label: Why IP rate limiting still fails
items.note: Attackers rotate IPs via botnets/residential proxies (thousands of IPs), so per-IP budgets never trip. Need a proof-of-humanity + edge protection.

items.label: Cloudflare Turnstile — client widget
items.note: Privacy-friendly, no image puzzles. reCAPTCHA is the Google equivalent.

items.label: Turnstile — server-side verify (required!)
items.note: Token is single-use and expires (~300s). Never trust the client widget alone.

items.label: reCAPTCHA v3 — score-based
items.note: v3 returns 0.0-1.0; tune threshold. v2 is the checkbox/image challenge.

items.label: Proxy DNS through Cloudflare
items.note: Set the record to Proxied (orange cloud). Traffic hits CF edge first — that's what enables WAF, rate limiting, and L3/4 DDoS scrubbing.

items.label: Hide / lock down the origin
items.note: If the real server IP leaks, attackers bypass CF entirely. Firewall origin to only accept CF IP ranges (or use cloudflared Tunnel — no public inbound port).

items.label: WAF rate limiting rule (edge)
items.code:
# Security > WAF > Rate limiting rules
Expression: (http.request.uri.path eq "/reset")
Rate: 5 requests / 1 min  per  IP
Action: Block  (or Managed Challenge)
items.note: Blocks at the edge before load ever reaches origin — cheaper and faster than app-layer.

items.label: Under Attack Mode / Bot Fight Mode
items.code:
# Overview > Under Attack Mode: ON  (JS challenge for all)
# Security > Bots > Bot Fight Mode: ON
items.note: Flip Under Attack on during an active L7 flood; it interstitials every visitor. Turn off after.

items.label: Managed Challenge vs hard Block
items.note: Managed Challenge lets humans through (Turnstile-style) while stopping bots — fewer false positives than outright Block on shared IPs.

items.label: L3/L4 volumetric DDoS
items.note: Handled automatically by CF's anycast network (and AWS Shield on AWS). Your job is L7: rate limits, WAF rules, CAPTCHA, caching static responses.

tips: Rate limit by identity (email/user/API key), not IP alone — botnets rotate thousands of IPs and NAT makes many users share one IP.
Set app.set('trust proxy', ...) behind any proxy/LB, or req.ip is the proxy and everyone shares a single bucket. Don't set it to true blindly — spoofable X-Forwarded-For.
In-memory rate limiters reset on restart and are per-process; anything running >1 instance needs a Redis/shared store.
6-digit OTPs with no attempt cap fall in minutes. Prefer 256-bit crypto.randomBytes tokens; if you must use OTPs, cap attempts, expire fast, and single-use them.
Store a HASH of reset tokens/OTPs, never the raw value — a DB leak otherwise = account takeover.
Always verify CAPTCHA tokens server-side; the client widget passing means nothing. Turnstile/reCAPTCHA tokens are single-use and expire (~5 min).
Keep /forgot responses generic and constant-time ('if an account exists...') to block user enumeration.
CAPTCHA + edge WAF are defense-in-depth on top of app rate limiting, not replacements. And lock your origin to Cloudflare IPs or the whole edge is bypassable.

items.label: Stateless — no memory between requests; every request self-contained
items.note: Scales horizontally trivially — any instance can serve any request. Default choice for HTTP APIs.

items.label: Stateful — holds data in process memory across requests
items.note: Used for real-time (WS), low-latency, ephemeral data. WS connection object CANNOT be serialized to a DB — must stay in memory.

items.label: The scaling problem: N instances don't share memory
items.note: Instance A's `rooms` Map is invisible to instance B. Two players on different pods can't see each other → need sticky sessions or a shared bus (Redis pub/sub).

items.label: Sticky sessions — pin a client to one instance
items.code:
# nginx: hash on client IP so a socket always hits same backend
upstream ws { ip_hash; server a:8080; server b:8080; }
items.note: Band-aid for stateful WS. Breaks on pod restart; doesn't help cross-room broadcast. Real fix = pub/sub layer.

items.label: Module-level variable = persists for process lifetime
items.note: Node keeps the module loaded; the closure variable lives as long as the process.

items.label: Single-threaded event loop → no locks for simple state
items.note: Safe because no two handlers run truly in parallel. BUT an `await` in the middle yields the loop — check-then-act across an await CAN race.

items.label: Restart / redeploy wipes everything
items.note: In-memory state is ephemeral. Never keep durable data (payments, user records) only in RAM.

items.label: worker_threads / cluster do NOT share memory
items.note: Each worker has its own heap → its own copy of your 'singleton'. Memory sharing needs SharedArrayBuffer or an external store.

items.label: Class basics — fields, private, constructor, methods
Classic Singleton — private ctor + static getInstance()
items.note: Lazy init. Guarantees one instance app-wide within a single process.

items.label: Idiomatic Node singleton — export a live instance
items.note: Simplest and most common in Node/TS. Module resolution caches the module, so every importer gets the one instance.

items.label: Why singleton here: one shared source of truth for state
items.note: Connection pool, config, in-memory store, pub/sub client — things that must be exactly one per process.

items.label: Model: publishers → topic/channel → subscribers (decoupled)
items.note: Publisher doesn't know who listens. Many subscribers per channel. Fire-and-forget, no reply.

items.label: In-process bus — Node EventEmitter
items.note: Only works within ONE process. Listeners run synchronously in registration order.

items.label: Cross-process pub/sub — Redis (the scaling fix)
items.note: MUST use two clients: a connection in subscribe mode can't run normal commands. This lets instance A's publish reach subscribers on instance B.

items.label: Singleton Redis manager (one pub, one sub per process)
items.note: Singleton so the whole process shares ONE Redis pub/sub pair + one subscription registry.

items.label: Subscribe local client, and subscribe Redis only on first interest
items.note: Only call Redis SUBSCRIBE once per channel per instance; track local sockets in the Map.

items.label: Fan out Redis message to local sockets
items.note: Any instance publishes → Redis broadcasts → every instance's fanout pushes to its own connected clients. Cross-node broadcast solved.

items.label: Unsubscribe + cleanup on disconnect
items.note: Leak guard: drop the Redis subscription when no local socket cares, else channels accumulate forever.

tips: `export const x = new X()` is a de-facto singleton via Node's module cache — but it's one-per-process, NOT one-per-cluster. Horizontal scaling breaks the illusion.
Redis pub/sub needs TWO connections: a client in subscribe mode rejects normal commands. Reuse a single publisher client for all publishes.
In-memory singleton state diverges across instances the moment you scale past 1 pod. Sync via Redis pub/sub, or keep truth in a shared DB/Redis.
Lazy `getInstance()` isn't thread-safe in general, but Node's single thread makes the null-check fine — unless you use worker_threads/cluster, where each worker gets its own instance.

A check-then-act (`if (seats>0) { await ...; seats-- }`) with an `await` between IS racy even in single-threaded Node — the event loop yields at await. Decrement before awaiting, or use atomic Redis ops.
Singletons leak state between tests. Add a `reset()` method or prefer dependency injection so test cases start clean.
Duplicate module instances (mixed ESM/CJS, symlinked deps, different import paths/casing on Windows) create TWO 'singletons'. Import from one canonical path.
Never store durable data (orders, balances) only in a process-memory singleton — a deploy or crash wipes it. RAM state = ephemeral cache/session/room only.

items.label: Every recursion = base case + recursive case
items.note: Write the base case first. Missing/wrong base case => RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded.

items.label: Head vs tail recursion (work before vs after the call)
items.code:
function tail(n){ if(n===0) return; console.log(n); tail(n-1); } // prints n..1
function head(n){ if(n===0) return; head(n-1); console.log(n); } // prints 1..n
items.note: Where you place the work relative to the call flips the print/build order — the stack unwinds LIFO.

items.label: Factorial — classic combine(f(n-1))
items.code: const fact = n => n <= 1 ? 1 : n * fact(n - 1);

items.label: Return-value vs accumulator/void style
Map an iterative loop to f(n) = combine(f(n-1))
items.note: Mechanical fluency: identify the last unit of work, delegate the rest to the smaller call.

items.label: Power a^b via recursion
items.code: const pow = (a, b) => b === 0 ? 1 : a * pow(a, b - 1);

items.label: Fast power — O(log b) (halve the exponent)
items.note: Compute half ONCE then square it — calling fastPow twice makes it O(b) again.

items.label: Reverse a number / string recursively
items.code: const rev = s => s === '' ? '' : rev(s.slice(1)) + s[0];

items.label: Pattern: split → solve subproblems → combine
items.note: Subproblems are independent (unlike backtracking). Cost = split + subcalls + combine.

items.label: Merge sort — T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n)=O(n log n)
items.note: The O(n) combine (merge) step dominates here.

items.label: Binary search — T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1)=O(log n)
items.note: Only one subproblem recursed into => log depth, not a full tree.

items.label: Master Theorem — T(n)=aT(n/b)+O(n^d)
items.note: Quick way to read off complexity from the recurrence.

items.label: 3-step recipe
items.note: Don't trace the whole call tree — trust the subcall and just write the combine step.

items.label: Correctness = induction (3 checks)
items.note: (a) base case correct; (b) each call strictly shrinks toward base (termination); (c) combine correct assuming subcall is.

items.label: Complexity from the recursion tree
items.note: Branching factor + depth => size of tree => time.

items.label: Space is O(max depth), always
items.note: Every un-returned frame is live at once. Balanced tree=O(log n); skewed/linked-list-shaped=O(n).

items.label: Core template
items.note: The pop()/undo is the signature of backtracking — forget it and later branches see dirty state.

items.label: Subsets (power set) — 2^n
Permutations — n!
Prune early = the whole point
items.note: Abandon a branch the instant it can't yield a valid solution (N-Queens, Sudoku). Pruning is what beats brute force.

items.label: Climb n stairs (1 or 2 steps) = Fibonacci
items.code:
// naive: O(2^n) — overlapping subproblems recomputed
const climb = n => n <= 2 ? n : climb(n - 1) + climb(n - 2);
items.note: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). Slow because ways(n-3)... recomputed exponentially.

items.label: Top-down memoization — O(n) time / O(n) space
items.note: Cache the first result for each distinct subproblem => every state solved once.

items.label: Bottom-up DP + O(1) space (roll 2 vars)
Recognizing when to memoize
items.note: Overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure => DP. This is the recursion→DP bridge.

items.label: Check / raise the effective limit
items.note: JS engines (V8) do NOT do tail-call optimization in practice — deep tail recursion still overflows.

items.label: Recursion → explicit stack (iterative DFS)
items.note: Same algorithm, but frames live on the heap (large) instead of the call stack (small/limited).

items.label: Trampoline (simulate TCO)
items.code:
const trampoline = fn => (...a) => { let r = fn(...a); while (typeof r === 'function') r = r(); return r; };
const sumT = trampoline(function s(n, acc = 0){ return n === 0 ? acc : () => s(n - 1, acc + n); });
items.note: Return a thunk instead of calling; loop drives it — flat stack for deep tail recursion.

tips: Base case FIRST, and prove the input strictly shrinks toward it — that guarantees termination.
"Maximum call stack size exceeded" almost always means a missing/unreachable base case or input not shrinking.
Space cost of recursion is O(max depth), even when the logic looks O(1) — every pending frame is live.
Two recursive calls => suspect exponential time; if subproblems overlap, memoize to collapse it to O(n).
Backtracking: always undo (pop/restore) your choice after the recursive call, or sibling branches inherit dirty state.
V8/Node does NOT optimize tail calls — rewrite deep recursion as an explicit-stack loop or use a trampoline.
When copying results out of a mutable path in backtracking, push a COPY ([...path]) — not the shared reference.
Compute-once then reuse: fastPow/divide-and-conquer must store the half result, not call the function twice.

items.label: Iterative search (returns index or -1)
items.note: Array MUST be sorted. O(log n) time, O(1) space. Use `lo + (hi-lo)/2`, never `(lo+hi)/2`.

items.label: Loop invariant choice
items.note: `while (lo <= hi)` with `hi = mid-1`/`lo = mid+1` for exact-match. Use `while (lo < hi)` with `hi = mid` for boundary/bound searches (see below).

items.label: Recursive form
items.code:
const bs = (a, t, lo=0, hi=a.length-1) =>
  lo > hi ? -1
  : a[(lo+hi)>>1] === t ? (lo+hi)>>1
  : a[(lo+hi)>>1] < t ? bs(a,t,((lo+hi)>>1)+1,hi)
  : bs(a,t,lo,((lo+hi)>>1)-1);
items.note: O(log n) stack depth. Iterative is preferred to avoid call overhead.

items.label: Infinite-loop trap
items.note: If you write `while(lo<hi)` and set `lo = mid` (not mid+1), you loop forever when hi=lo+1. Always advance one side past mid.

items.label: lower_bound — first index with a[i] >= t
items.note: This is the C++ std::lower_bound. Returns count of elements < t.

items.label: upper_bound — first index with a[i] > t
items.note: count of t in range = upperBound(t) - lowerBound(t).

items.label: First & last occurrence of t
JS has no built-in lower_bound
items.note: There is no std lib binary search in JS. `Array.indexOf` is O(n). Roll your own or reuse the templates above.

sections.title: Binary Search on the Answer (monotonic predicate)

items.label: When to use
items.note: Answer space is monotone: if x works, everything >= x works (or <=). Binary search the value, not an index. Classics: min ship capacity, Koko eating bananas, sqrt, allocate books.

items.label: Generic 'minimum feasible' template
items.note: For 'maximum feasible', use mid = lo+((hi-lo+1)>>1) (upper mid) and lo=mid / hi=mid-1.

items.label: Integer sqrt
items.note: Upper-mid variant prevents infinite loop when setting lo = mid.

items.label: Float answer (precision loop)
items.note: For reals loop a fixed count (~100) or until hi-lo < eps.

items.label: Unsorted array → hash map, O(n)
items.note: Check `need` BEFORE inserting nums[i] so you don't pair an element with itself.

items.label: Sorted array → two pointers, O(n) / O(1)
items.note: Only works on sorted input. If you must sort first it becomes O(n log n) and you lose original indices.

items.label: Two-sum via binary search
items.note: O(n log n). Two-pointer is strictly better on sorted data; BS variant matters when array is pre-sorted and you query many targets.

items.label: Count pairs / all pairs
items.note: Sorted + two pointers naturally extends: on match, move both and skip duplicates for distinct pairs (3Sum builds on this).

items.label: Char <-> code
items.code:
'a'.charCodeAt(0)        // 97
String.fromCharCode(97)  // 'a'
s[i].charCodeAt(0) - 97  // 0..25 index for lowercase
items.note: Strings are immutable in JS — `s[i] = 'x'` silently does nothing. Convert with `s.split('')` to mutate.

items.label: Frequency array (lowercase)
items.code:
const f = new Array(26).fill(0);
for (const ch of s) f[ch.charCodeAt(0) - 97]++;
items.note: O(1) space vs a Map. Use a Map for unicode / arbitrary charsets.

items.label: Common ops
items.code:
s.slice(i, j)      // substring [i, j)
s.split('').reverse().join('')  // reverse
s.includes('ab')   // substring test
[...s].sort().join('')          // sort chars
items.note: `slice` accepts negatives; `substring` does not and swaps args if start>end.

items.label: Anagram check

sections.title: Sliding window — Q: Vasya and String

items.label: Problem
items.note: String of 'a'/'b', you may flip <= k chars. Find longest substring of a single letter. Answer = max over 'keep a' and 'keep b' windows.

items.label: Solution — two-pass sliding window
items.note: O(n) time, O(1) space. `flips` = count of the OTHER char inside the window; keep it <= k.

items.label: Variable-size window recipe
items.note: Grow right, shrink left while invalid, record window size. Generalizes 'longest substring with <= k of X'.

items.label: Related: longest substring without repeats

tips: Precondition #1 for binary search: the array is SORTED (or the predicate is monotonic). If it isn't, sort first or you'll get garbage.
Use `lo + ((hi - lo) >> 1)` for mid. `(lo + hi) / 2` overflows in fixed-width languages and `/2` on non-integers needs a floor in JS.
Pick ONE template style and memorize it: `[lo, hi]` closed with `<=` for exact match; `[lo, hi)` half-open with `<` for lower/upper bound. Mixing them causes off-by-one and infinite loops.
'Binary search on the answer' is the highest-leverage pattern — whenever the answer is monotone (feasible past a threshold), search the value space in O(log(range)).
Two Sum: hash map for unsorted (O(n)), two pointers for sorted (O(n), O(1) space). Sorting just to two-pointer costs O(n log n) and destroys original indices.
JS strings are immutable and there is no built-in binary search — carry your own lowerBound/upperBound helpers into contests.
Sliding window replaces an O(n^2) substring scan with O(n): expand right, contract left while the window is invalid. Vasya-style 'flip <= k' problems track the count of the minority char.
Off-by-one sanity check: run your BS on arrays of size 0, 1, 2 and with target smaller than all / larger than all elements before trusting it.

items.label: Install toolchain (rustup)
items.code:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
# Windows: download rustup-init.exe
items.note: Installs rustc, cargo, rustup. `rustup update` to upgrade; `rustup default stable`.

items.label: Verify install
items.code: rustc --version && cargo --version

items.label: New project / new lib
items.code:
cargo new my_app        # binary (src/main.rs)
cargo new my_lib --lib  # library (src/lib.rs)
items.note: Creates Cargo.toml + git repo. `cargo init` inside an existing dir.

items.label: Build / run / check
items.code:
cargo run           # compile + run
cargo build         # debug build -> target/debug
cargo build --release  # optimized -> target/release
cargo check         # typecheck only, no codegen (fast)
items.note: `cargo check` is the fastest feedback loop; use it while iterating.

items.label: Format, lint, test
items.code:
cargo fmt           # rustfmt
cargo clippy        # linter, catches idiom issues
cargo test          # run #[test] fns
items.note: Clippy is the single biggest idiom teacher — run it often.

items.label: VSCode setup
items.code: // install extension: rust-analyzer (NOT the deprecated 'Rust')
items.note: rust-analyzer gives inline types, go-to-def, inlay hints. Add CodeLLDB for debugging.

items.label: Hello world
items.note: `println!` is a macro (note the `!`), not a function.

items.label: Immutable by default / mut
items.note: Reassigning an immutable `let` is a compile error. Mutability is opt-in.

items.label: Type annotation & inference
items.note: Types inferred when unambiguous; integer default i32, float default f64.

items.label: Shadowing
items.code:
let s = "42";
let s: i32 = s.parse().unwrap();  // new var, new type
items.note: Shadowing creates a NEW binding — can change type; different from `mut`.

items.label: Constants
items.code: const MAX: u32 = 100_000;
items.note: Must have explicit type, UPPER_SNAKE, evaluated at compile time.

items.label: Scalar types
items.code:
// i8..i128/isize, u8..u128/usize, f32/f64, bool, char
let c: char = 'z';  // 4 bytes, Unicode scalar
items.note: `usize`/`isize` match pointer width; use `usize` for indexing.

items.label: if / else (expression)
items.code: let big = if n > 10 { "yes" } else { "no" };
items.note: `if` is an expression; both arms must return the same type. No parens needed around condition.

items.label: loop / while / for
items.note: `loop { break val; }` yields a value. Label loops with `'outer:` to break outer.

items.label: Iterate a collection
items.code:
let arr = [1, 2, 3];
for x in arr.iter() { println!("{x}"); }

items.label: Function signature
items.note: Trailing expression (no `;`) is the return value. `return` also works but is non-idiomatic for the last line.

items.label: Ownership rule
items.note: Each value has ONE owner; when owner drops, value freed. Assignment/passing moves heap data.

items.label: Clone (deep copy)
items.code: let s2 = s1.clone();  // explicit deep copy, s1 still valid
items.note: Copy types (integers, bool, char, f64, fixed arrays of Copy) copy implicitly; heap types move.

items.label: Borrowing (references)
items.code:
fn len(s: &String) -> usize { s.len() }
len(&s1);           // borrow, s1 keeps ownership
items.note: `&` = shared/immutable borrow. Can have many `&` at once.

items.label: Mutable borrow
items.note: Only ONE `&mut` at a time, and no `&` while a `&mut` is live (data-race prevention at compile time).

items.label: Stack vs heap (mental model)
items.code:
let x = 5;                 // stack: fixed size, fast
let s = String::from("x"); // ptr/len/cap on stack, data on heap
items.note: Stack = LIFO, known size, cheap. Heap = dynamic size (String, Vec, Box), pointer lives on stack.

items.label: Slices
items.code:
let s = String::from("hello world");
let hello = &s[0..5];   // &str slice, borrows s
items.note: Slices are borrows into a contiguous sequence; `&str` is a string slice, `&[T]` a array/vec slice.

items.label: Struct definition
items.note: Tuple structs: `struct Point(i32, i32);`  Unit struct: `struct Unit;`

items.label: impl block (methods)
items.note: `&self` borrows, `&mut self` mutates, `self` consumes. No `self` param = associated fn (call as `User::new`).

items.label: Enums with data
items.note: Each variant can carry different data. Enums are Rust's tagged unions / sum types.

items.label: match (exhaustive)
items.note: `match` MUST be exhaustive; use `_ =>` as catch-all. It's an expression returning a value.

items.label: if let / while let
items.note: Concise single-pattern match; skip the boilerplate when you only care about one variant.

items.label: match guards & binding
Derive traits
items.note: `{:?}` needs Debug, `{:#?}` pretty-prints. Common derives: Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Default.

items.label: Option enum
items.code:
enum Option<T> { Some(T), None }
let found: Option<i32> = v.iter().position(|&x| x == 3).map(|i| i as i32);
items.note: Replaces null. Compiler forces you to handle `None`.

items.label: Result enum
? operator (propagate)
items.note: `?` unwraps Ok/Some or returns the Err/None early. Only usable in fns returning Result/Option.

items.label: unwrap / expect
items.code:
let n = "5".parse::<i32>().unwrap();       // panics on Err
let n = "5".parse::<i32>().expect("bad"); // panics with msg
items.note: Fine for prototypes/tests; avoid in library code — prefer `?` or match.

items.label: Handle Option safely
items.note: Combinators: `unwrap_or`, `unwrap_or_else`, `map`, `and_then`, `ok_or`.

items.label: panic! (unrecoverable)
items.code: panic!("unreachable state");
items.note: Unwinds the stack. Use for bugs/invariants, not expected errors.

items.label: Vec (growable array)
items.note: `v.get(i)` returns Option (safe); indexing panics on out-of-bounds.

items.label: String vs &str
items.note: `String` = owned/growable heap; `&str` = borrowed view. `.to_string()` / `.into()` to convert.

items.label: HashMap
items.note: `entry().or_insert()` is the idiomatic upsert pattern.

items.label: Iterator chains
items.note: Iterators are lazy — nothing runs until a consumer (`sum`, `collect`, `for`, `count`).

items.label: collect into a Vec
items.code: let doubled: Vec<i32> = v.iter().map(|x| x * 2).collect();
items.note: `collect()` often needs a type hint (turbofish `::<Vec<_>>()` or annotation).

items.label: enumerate / zip
items.code:
for (i, val) in v.iter().enumerate() { }
for (a, b) in va.iter().zip(vb.iter()) { }

sections.title: Advanced: Traits, Generics, Lifetimes, Concurrency, Async

items.label: Trait (shared behavior)
items.note: Traits = interfaces. `impl Trait for Type`. Default methods can be overridden.

items.label: Generics + trait bounds
items.note: `<T: Bound>` or `where T: Bound`. Bounds let you call trait methods on generic types.

items.label: dyn / impl Trait
items.code:
fn make() -> impl Summary { User::new("a".into()) }   // static
let items: Vec<Box<dyn Summary>> = vec![];            // dynamic dispatch
items.note: `impl Trait` = monomorphized (fast). `dyn Trait` = runtime vtable, needs Box/&.

items.label: Lifetimes
items.note: Lifetimes tie output ref validity to inputs; no runtime cost — purely compile-time checking.

items.label: Threads
items.note: Move data in with `move ||`. Share with `Arc<Mutex<T>>` for mutable shared state.

items.label: Arc + Mutex (shared mutable)
items.note: `Arc` = atomic refcount for sharing across threads; `Mutex` = interior mutability with lock.

items.label: Async (tokio)
items.note: `async fn` returns a Future; nothing runs until `.await`ed on a runtime (tokio/async-std).

items.label: Declarative macros
items.note: Macros expand at compile time. `!` marks a macro call (println!, vec!, format!).

items.label: External deps (Cargo.toml)
items.note: `cargo add serde --features derive` edits Cargo.toml for you. Crates from crates.io.

tips: Run `cargo clippy` constantly — it teaches idiomatic Rust and catches the mistakes the compiler allows but shouldn't.
The borrow checker rule of thumb: many immutable borrows OR exactly one mutable borrow, never both simultaneously.
Move vs Copy: primitives (i32, bool, char, f64) copy implicitly; heap types (String, Vec, Box) MOVE — use `.clone()` when you genuinely need a second owner.
Avoid `.unwrap()`/`.expect()` in real code; propagate with `?` or handle with `match`. Reserve panics for true invariant violations.
`match` must be exhaustive — the compiler forces you to handle every enum variant, including `None`, which is how Rust kills null-pointer bugs.
Iterators are lazy: chains of `map`/`filter` do nothing until a consumer like `collect`, `sum`, or a `for` loop drives them.
Prefer `&str` for function params and `String` for owned/returned data; prefer `&[T]` over `&Vec<T>` for slice params.
String indexing (`s[0]`) is NOT allowed — strings are UTF-8; use `.chars()`, `.bytes()`, or slice byte ranges carefully.

items.label: RPC = call a function that runs on another machine
items.note: Client invokes a local stub; args are serialized, sent over the network, executed server-side, result returned. Hides transport so a remote call looks like a local one.

items.label: Dumb RPC over plain HTTP/JSON
items.note: Contrast with REST: RPC is action/verb-centric (/getUser), REST is resource/noun-centric (GET /users/1). The 'dumb' version is just JSON-over-HTTP with a method field.

items.label: Common RPC protocols
items.note: JSON-RPC (JSON over HTTP/WS, human-readable), XML-RPC (legacy, verbose), SOAP (XML + WSDL contract, enterprise), gRPC (Protobuf over HTTP/2, binary, fastest + typed contract). Choose gRPC for internal high-throughput service-to-service.

items.label: proto3 file skeleton
items.note: Field numbers (=1,=2) are the wire identity — NEVER change/reuse them once shipped. Names can change; numbers cannot.

items.label: Scalar types
items.code:
double float
int32 int64 uint32 uint64
sint32 sint64   // zigzag, better for negatives
fixed32 fixed64 sfixed32 sfixed64
bool string bytes
items.note: proto3 defaults: numbers=0, string="", bool=false. Unset vs default is indistinguishable unless you wrap or use 'optional'.

items.label: Repeated (arrays) & maps
items.code:
repeated string tags = 4;
map<string, int32> scores = 5;
items.note: repeated = list; map keys must be integral or string. Maps are unordered on the wire.

items.label: Nested messages, enums, optional
items.note: First enum value MUST be 0 (the default). 'optional' brings back has-field presence in proto3.

items.label: oneof (tagged union)
items.note: Setting one field clears the others. Good for variant types.

items.label: service + rpc definitions
items.note: Every rpc takes exactly one request message and returns one response message — wrap primitives in a message.

items.label: Four call types
items.code:
rpc Unary(Req) returns (Res);              // 1<->1
rpc ServerStream(Req) returns (stream Res);// 1->N
rpc ClientStream(stream Req) returns (Res);// N->1
rpc BiDi(stream Req) returns (stream Res); // N<->N
items.note: 'stream' keyword on either side. Streaming rides HTTP/2 multiplexing — no new connections per message.

items.label: Install
items.code:
npm i @grpc/grpc-js @grpc/proto-loader
npm i -D grpc-tools ts-proto   # for codegen / TS types
items.note: grpc-js is pure JS (no native addon). proto-loader parses .proto at runtime — no build step needed.

items.label: Load proto at runtime
items.note: keepCase:true keeps proto field names as-is; otherwise they're camelCased in JS.

items.label: Implement + start server
items.note: Handler signature: (call, callback). call.request holds the deserialized message. bindAsync replaces the deprecated bind+start.

items.label: Client (unary)
items.note: Callback-style API. Wrap in util.promisify or a Promise for async/await.

items.label: Server-streaming handler
items.note: Streaming handlers get NO callback — use call.write/end and listen to 'data'/'end'/'error' events.

items.label: Generate TS types with ts-proto
items.code:
protoc \
  --plugin=./node_modules/.bin/protoc-gen-ts_proto \
  --ts_proto_out=./src/gen \
  --ts_proto_opt=outputServices=grpc-js,esModuleInterop=true \
  ./proto/todo.proto
items.note: Generates typed message interfaces + typed service definitions so call.request and responses are checked.

items.label: Type the handler with generated types
items.note: Runtime proto-loader gives you `any`; codegen (ts-proto / grpc-tools) is what gives real compile-time safety.

items.label: grpc.web / codegen for browsers
items.code:
protoc --js_out=import_style=commonjs \
  --grpc-web_out=import_style=typescript,mode=grpcwebtext:./out todo.proto
items.note: Browsers can't speak raw HTTP/2 gRPC — you need grpc-web + an Envoy proxy or a compatible gateway.

items.label: Return a gRPC status error
items.code: cb({ code: grpc.status.NOT_FOUND, message: 'todo missing' });
items.note: Use canonical codes (OK, INVALID_ARGUMENT, NOT_FOUND, UNAUTHENTICATED, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED...) — not HTTP codes.

items.label: Metadata (headers) + deadline
items.note: Deadlines are absolute timestamps and propagate across hops. Always set one to avoid hung calls.

tips: Field numbers are forever: reserve removed ones with `reserved 3, 5;` and `reserved "oldName";` so nobody reuses the tag and corrupts data.
proto3 can't tell 'unset' from 'default 0/""/false' — use `optional` for presence, or a wrapper/oneof when zero is a meaningful value.
gRPC needs HTTP/2 end-to-end; browsers can't do it directly — use grpc-web behind Envoy, or a REST gateway (grpc-gateway).
proto-loader = zero-build but untyped (`any`); ts-proto / protoc codegen = real TypeScript types. Pick codegen for anything non-trivial.
Streaming handlers have no callback: drive them with call.write()/call.end() and handle 'data'/'end'/'error' events — mixing the two styles is a common bug.
Always set a deadline on client calls; without one a stuck server hangs the client indefinitely. Deadlines propagate to downstream services automatically.
Use ServerCredentials.createInsecure() only in dev — production needs TLS via createSsl(); mismatched insecure/secure creds fail with cryptic UNAVAILABLE errors.
Enum's first value must be 0 and acts as the default/UNKNOWN sentinel; don't assign business meaning to the zero value.

items.label: Node structure
items.note: A list is just a chain of nodes; you hold a reference to head. Empty list = head is null.

items.label: List wrapper (track head/tail/size)
items.note: Keeping a tail pointer makes append O(1) instead of O(n).

items.label: Build from array
Traverse / print
items.note: Loop condition is `while (cur)`, not `cur.next` — the latter skips the last node.

items.label: Dummy (sentinel) head trick
items.note: Eliminates edge cases when the head itself may be inserted/deleted.

items.label: Prepend (push front) — O(1)
items.code: head = new Node(val, head);

items.label: Append (push back) — O(1) with tail, else O(n)
Insert at index i
items.note: Link the new node's next BEFORE reassigning prev.next, or you lose the tail.

items.label: Delete by value
Search / get at index
items.note: Access is O(n) — no random indexing like arrays.

items.label: Complexity cheat
items.note: Prepend O(1) | Append O(1)*/O(n) | Insert/Delete at pos O(n) | Search O(n) | Access-by-index O(n). Extra advantage over arrays: O(1) insert/delete once you HOLD the node/prev pointer.

items.label: Iterative — 3 pointers (prev/cur/next)
items.note: O(n) time, O(1) space. Return prev — cur is null at the end.

items.label: Recursive
items.note: O(n) stack space. `head.next.next = head` flips the pointer coming back up.

items.label: Reverse sublist [L, R]
items.note: Head-insertion (LeetCode 92). Positions are 1-indexed.

items.label: Has cycle? (bool)
items.note: Guard both fast && fast.next before double-hop or you deref null.

items.label: Find cycle START node
items.note: After meeting, reset one pointer to head; they meet again at the cycle entry (math: 2(F+a)=F+a+nC).

items.label: Cycle length
HashSet alternative — O(n) space
items.note: Simpler but uses extra memory; Floyd's is O(1) space and preferred in interviews.

items.label: Digits stored in REVERSE order
items.note: Loop while EITHER list has nodes OR carry != 0 — a final carry (e.g. 5+5) needs an extra node.

items.label: Forward order variant (LeetCode 445)
items.note: Reverse both lists first (or push vals onto stacks), add, then reverse the result — or build result by prepending.

items.label: Node with prev + next
Push front
Push back
Delete a node in O(1) (given the node)
items.note: DLL's superpower: delete without a prev-search. Basis for LRU cache.

items.label: Traverse backward
items.code:
let cur = this.tail;
while (cur) { console.log(cur.val); cur = cur.prev; }
items.note: Extra prev pointer = 2x pointer bookkeeping; every insert/delete must fix BOTH directions.

items.label: Find middle (slow/fast)
items.note: For even length this returns the SECOND middle. Start fast at head.next for the first middle.

items.label: Nth node from end (remove)
items.note: Advance fast by n first, then move both — gap of n locates the node before target.

items.label: Merge two sorted lists
Remove duplicates from SORTED list
items.note: Only advance cur when NOT deleting, else you skip runs of 3+ dupes.

items.label: Palindrome check
items.note: Find middle -> reverse second half -> compare both halves node by node. O(n) time, O(1) space.

tips: Always save `cur.next` in a temp before reassigning it during reversal/insertion — otherwise you orphan the rest of the list.
Use a dummy/sentinel node whenever the head might be deleted or inserted (delete-by-value, remove-nth, merge, add-two-numbers) to kill head edge cases.
For fast pointer double-hops, guard `while (fast && fast.next)` — checking only `fast` throws on `fast.next.next`.
Loop traversal uses `while (cur)`, not `while (cur.next)` — the latter drops the last node.
Reversal returns `prev` (the last non-null node), not `cur` (which ends up null).
In Add Two Numbers, keep looping while `carry` is nonzero even after both lists end (e.g. 5+5 -> extra '1' node).
DLL insert/delete must update BOTH prev and next on both neighbors, plus head/tail when touching the ends.
Floyd's cycle detection is O(1) space; reserve the HashSet version for when you also need to return the exact node quickly or the list is short.

items.label: Vertical (scale up) — add CPU/RAM/disk to one box
items.note: Simplest: no code change, no distributed complexity. Hard ceiling (biggest instance), single point of failure, downtime to resize, cost grows non-linearly.

items.label: Horizontal (scale out) — add more machines/instances behind a LB
items.note: Near-infinite headroom + HA/redundancy. Needs stateless app, load balancer, shared state store. Requires the app to NOT keep session/data in local memory.

items.label: Rule of thumb
items.note: Scale vertically first (cheap, easy) until you hit a single-node limit or need HA, then go horizontal. DB usually scales up + read-replicas before sharding.

items.label: Statelessness is the prerequisite for horizontal
items.note: Move sessions, cache, rate-limit counters, uploads to Redis/DB/S3. Sticky sessions are a crutch, not a fix.

items.label: Load balancing strategies
items.note: round-robin (default), least-conn (uneven request cost), ip-hash/sticky (stateful fallback). Health checks evict dead nodes.

items.label: cluster module — one worker per CPU core (Node is single-threaded)
items.note: Uses all cores on ONE box (a form of scale-out within a machine). Workers share the listening socket; no shared memory.

items.label: PM2 cluster mode — production process manager
items.code:
pm2 start app.js -i max      # one instance per core
pm2 scale app +3             # add 3 workers
pm2 reload app               # zero-downtime rolling restart
pm2 start app.js -i 0        # 0/max = all cores
items.note: `reload` (graceful) vs `restart` (hard). `pm2 startup` + `pm2 save` to survive reboots.

items.label: Nginx as reverse-proxy / load balancer across boxes
items.note: In front of N machines each running the Node app. Add `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $remote_addr;` and trust proxy in Express.

items.label: Trust the proxy in Express (correct client IP / protocol)
items.code: app.set('trust proxy', 1);

items.label: Graceful shutdown (drain in-flight before exit)
items.code: process.on('SIGTERM', () => server.close(() => process.exit(0)));
items.note: Required so LB/PM2 can rotate instances without dropping requests.

items.label: Average RPS from daily traffic
items.code:
RPS = daily_requests / 86400   # 86,400 s/day
# 100M req/day  ->  ~1,157 RPS avg
items.note: Peak ≈ 2–3× average. Size for peak, not average.

items.label: Little's Law — concurrency needed
items.code:
concurrency = RPS * avg_latency_seconds
# 1000 RPS * 0.2s  ->  200 concurrent in flight
items.note: Drives worker count, thread/connection pool size, and instance count.

items.label: Instances needed
items.code:
instances = ceil(peak_RPS / per_instance_RPS)
# add headroom (~30%) + N+1 for failover

items.label: Storage sizing
items.code:
storage = rows * bytes_per_row * (1 + index_overhead)
# 1B rows * 200B * 1.5  ->  ~300 GB
items.note: Account for indexes (often 20–100% of table size), WAL, and growth rate.

items.label: DB connection budget
items.note: Postgres default max_connections ~100. Each connection ~5–10MB. Use PgBouncer/pooling; pool size ≈ cores*2, NOT one per app worker × instances.

items.label: Handy units
items.note: 1 day ≈ 86.4k s; 1M/day ≈ 12 RPS. Read:write ratios drive caching + replica decisions.

items.label: Create / drop a B-tree index (default)
items.code:
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
DROP INDEX idx_users_email;
items.note: B-tree serves =, <, >, BETWEEN, ORDER BY, IN, LIKE 'prefix%'. Not LIKE '%x'.

items.label: Build without locking writes (production)
items.code: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_users_email ON users(email);
items.note: Slower, can't run in a txn; a failed run leaves an INVALID index (DROP + retry). Plain CREATE INDEX takes a write lock on the table.

items.label: Unique index / constraint
items.code:
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users(email);
ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT uq_email UNIQUE(email);
items.note: PKs and UNIQUE constraints auto-create a backing index. FKs do NOT — index FK columns yourself.

items.label: Inspect + verify usage
items.code:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='a@b.com';
\d users        -- list indexes (psql)
items.note: Look for 'Index Scan' vs 'Seq Scan'. Check pg_stat_user_indexes for unused indexes to drop.

items.label: Index type cheat sheet
items.code:
B-tree  -- default, equality + range + sort
Hash    -- equality only (=)
GIN     -- jsonb, arrays, full-text (@>, ?, @@)
GiST    -- geometric, ranges, nearest-neighbor
BRIN    -- huge append-only tables (time-series)
items.note: GIN for containment/search; BRIN is tiny + fast on naturally-ordered columns like created_at.

items.label: Cost of indexes
items.note: Each index slows INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and consumes disk. Index for read patterns you actually run; don't index everything.

items.label: Composite (multi-column) — leftmost-prefix rule
items.code: CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(user_id, created_at);
items.note: Serves WHERE user_id=? , WHERE user_id=? AND created_at>? , and (user_id) alone. Does NOT serve created_at alone. Put the equality/most-selective column first.

items.label: Partial index — index a subset of rows
items.code:
CREATE INDEX idx_active ON orders(user_id)
  WHERE status = 'active';
items.note: Smaller + faster. Query's WHERE must match the predicate to use it. Great for soft-deletes / hot subsets.

items.label: Expression (functional) index
items.note: Enables case-insensitive / computed lookups. Column-only query won't hit it.

items.label: Covering index (INCLUDE) — index-only scan
items.code: CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(user_id) INCLUDE (total, status);
items.note: Payload columns live in the index so the query avoids heap fetches. Needs recent VACUUM (visibility map) for index-only scan.

items.label: GIN on JSONB / arrays
items.code:
CREATE INDEX ON docs USING GIN (data jsonb_path_ops);
SELECT * FROM docs WHERE data @> '{"active":true}';

items.label: Full-text search
Single-field id / unique / index
items.note: @unique = unique index; @@index = plain index; @id = PK (indexed).

items.label: Composite index / composite unique
items.note: @@unique also gives you a compound lookup key for `where: { authorId_slug: {...} }`.

items.label: Apply schema changes
items.code:
npx prisma migrate dev --name add_indexes   # dev
npx prisma migrate deploy                    # prod
npx prisma generate                          # regen client
items.note: Prisma emits CREATE INDEX in the migration SQL; edit the SQL for CONCURRENTLY on big tables.

items.label: Index-type / DB-specific
items.code:
@@index([data], type: Gin)          // Postgres GIN
@@fulltext([title, body])           // MySQL fulltext

items.label: 1:1 relation
items.note: FK column carries @unique to enforce one-to-one.

items.label: 1:N relation
items.note: FK lives on the 'many' side. Prisma does NOT auto-index FKs on Postgres — add @@index([authorId]).

items.label: M:N relation (implicit vs explicit)
1NF — atomic values, no repeating groups
items.code:
-- BAD:  phones = '555-1, 555-2'
-- GOOD: separate phones table, one row per phone
items.note: Each cell holds a single value; each row is unique (has a key).

items.label: 2NF — 1NF + no partial dependency on a composite PK
items.note: Every non-key column depends on the WHOLE composite key, not part of it. Split out columns that depend on only one key component.

items.label: 3NF — 2NF + no transitive dependency
items.code:
-- BAD: orders(id, userId, userEmail)  -- email depends on userId, not order
-- GOOD: orders(id, userId) + users(id, email)
items.note: Non-key columns depend only on the key. Everyday target for OLTP schemas.

items.label: Relationship → schema mapping
items.code:
1:1  -> FK + UNIQUE on the FK
1:N  -> FK on the 'many' side
M:N  -> junction/join table (PK = both FKs)

items.label: Always index foreign keys
items.code: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id);
items.note: Speeds joins and cascades; without it, deleting a parent does a seq scan of children.

items.label: Normalize vs denormalize trade-off
items.note: Normalized = no update anomalies, less storage, more joins. Denormalize (duplicate/cache columns) only for proven read-hot paths; keep copies in sync deliberately.

tips: Scale vertically first (zero code change); go horizontal when you hit a single-node ceiling or need HA. The blocker is almost always in-memory state, not CPU.
Horizontal scaling requires stateless app servers: push sessions, cache, rate-limit counters, and uploads to Redis/DB/S3 before adding instances.
node cluster / PM2 -i max scales across cores on ONE machine; a load balancer (Nginx/ELB) scales across machines. You usually want both layers.
Composite index obeys the leftmost-prefix rule: (a,b) helps WHERE a and WHERE a AND b, never WHERE b alone. Order columns equality-first, then range/sort.
Always CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on production tables — the plain form takes a write lock. On failure it leaves an INVALID index you must DROP and rebuild.
Verify every index with EXPLAIN ANALYZE — an unused index is pure write/disk overhead. Drop indexes with zero scans in pg_stat_user_indexes.
FKs are NOT auto-indexed in Postgres (or Prisma on Postgres). Add the index yourself or joins, cascades, and parent deletes will seq-scan.
Expression and partial indexes only fire when the query's WHERE matches exactly — lower(email) index needs WHERE lower(email)=…, and a partial index needs its predicate.

items.label: Why WebRTC
items.note: Browser-native, plugin-free P2P real-time media + arbitrary data. Sub-500ms latency over UDP (SRTP/DTLS), encrypted by default. Alternatives (WebSocket/HLS) add server hop + latency.

items.label: Signaling
