items.note: How peers exchange SDP + ICE candidates BEFORE the P2P link exists. NOT part of the WebRTC spec — you build it (WebSocket, HTTP, even a QR code).

items.label: SDP (Session Description Protocol)
items.note: Text blob describing codecs, resolutions, IPs, encryption keys. Exchanged as offer/answer.

items.label: ICE
items.note: Interactive Connectivity Establishment — framework that gathers candidate address:port pairs and probes them to find a working path through NATs.

items.label: STUN
items.note: Lightweight server that tells a peer its public IP:port (server-reflexive candidate). Free (e.g. stun:stun.l.google.com:19302). Handles ~80% of NATs.

items.label: TURN
items.note: Relay server that forwards media when direct P2P fails (symmetric NAT). Bandwidth-heavy, must be authenticated. Needed for ~15-20% of connections.

items.label: Candidate types
items.note: host (local LAN) < srflx (STUN/public) < relay (TURN). ICE picks the best working candidate-pair.

items.label: Capture camera/mic
items.code:
const stream = await navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({ video: true, audio: true });
localVideo.srcObject = stream;
items.note: Requires a secure context (HTTPS or localhost). Returns a rejected promise otherwise.

items.label: Screen share
items.code: const screen = await navigator.mediaDevices.getDisplayMedia({ video: true, audio: false });
items.note: Cannot be combined with getUserMedia in one call — capture separately.

items.label: Create the peer connection
Add local tracks (send media)
items.code: stream.getTracks().forEach(track => pc.addTrack(track, stream));
items.note: Adding a track fires onnegotiationneeded — do this before/around offer creation.

items.label: Receive remote media
Watch connection health
items.code: pc.onconnectionstatechange = () => console.log(pc.connectionState);
items.note: States: new → connecting → connected → disconnected → failed → closed. Treat 'failed' as fatal (restart ICE).

items.label: Caller — create & send offer
Callee — answer the offer
Caller — apply the answer
items.code: await pc.setRemoteDescription(answer);

items.label: Trickle ICE — send candidates
items.note: Send each candidate as it's found — don't wait for full gathering. null candidate = gathering complete.

items.label: Add remote candidates
items.code: await pc.addIceCandidate(new RTCIceCandidate(candidate));
items.note: Can arrive before setRemoteDescription — buffer them in an array and flush after remote desc is set, or use perfect negotiation.

items.label: Perfect negotiation (glare-proof)
items.note: Designate one peer 'polite' (rolls back on collision) and one 'impolite' to resolve simultaneous offers.

items.label: Open a channel (initiator)
Receive a channel (other side)
Unreliable / low-latency mode
items.code: pc.createDataChannel('game', { ordered: false, maxRetransmits: 0 });
items.note: UDP-like semantics for game state / cursors — skips retransmits, avoids head-of-line blocking.

items.label: Backpressure
items.note: Sending faster than the link drains bloats the buffer — gate on bufferedAmount.

items.label: Minimal WebSocket relay (Node ws)
items.note: Server only shuttles offer/answer/candidate JSON — it never touches media in a P2P topology.

items.label: Room-scoped routing
items.note: In production key sockets by roomId and forward only within the room. Track peer join/leave to trigger renegotiation.

items.label: Run coturn (STUN+TURN)
items.code:
turnserver -a -u user:pass -r example.com \
  --min-port=49152 --max-port=65535 \
  --listening-port=3478 --fingerprint
items.note: Self-hosting TURN is the reliability fix for corporate/symmetric NATs. Use TLS (turns:) + short-lived credentials in prod.

items.label: Force relay (test TURN)
items.code: new RTCPeerConnection({ iceServers, iceTransportPolicy: 'relay' });
items.note: Drops host/srflx candidates so only TURN paths are used — verifies your TURN server actually works.

items.label: Read live stats
items.note: Key report types: inbound-rtp, outbound-rtp, candidate-pair (chosen path + RTT), remote-inbound-rtp (round-trip + loss reported by peer).

items.label: Round-trip time
items.code:
if (r.type === 'candidate-pair' && r.state === 'succeeded')
  console.log('RTT', r.currentRoundTripTime);

items.label: Cap send bitrate
Switch track (screen share toggle)
items.code:
const sender = pc.getSenders().find(s => s.track.kind === 'video');
await sender.replaceTrack(screenTrack);
items.note: replaceTrack swaps media WITHOUT renegotiation — smoother than remove/add.

items.label: Cleanup (release camera)
items.code:
stream.getTracks().forEach(t => t.stop());
pc.close();
items.note: Forgetting t.stop() leaves the camera light on and the device locked.

items.label: simple-peer (thin P2P wrapper)
items.note: Bundles offer/answer/ICE into one opaque 'signal' blob. You still ship it over your own channel.

items.label: Other P2P libs
items.note: PeerJS (hosted broker + client), Daily/Twilio (managed SDKs). Fine for 1:1 or tiny groups.

items.label: Mesh
items.note: Every peer ↔ every peer: N·(N-1)/2 connections, each uploads N-1 copies. Zero server media cost but dies past ~4-5 participants (uplink + CPU).

items.label: SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit)
items.note: Each peer uploads ONCE to the server; server forwards streams to others. Scales to dozens+. Servers: mediasoup, LiveKit, ion-sfu, Janus, Jitsi Videobridge.

items.label: Simulcast (SFU)
items.note: Send multiple quality layers; SFU forwards the layer each receiver can handle.

items.label: MCU (Multipoint Control Unit)
items.note: Server decodes + mixes all streams into one composite. Lowest client bandwidth/CPU, highest server cost. Used for recording, PSTN bridges, legacy endpoints.

tips: getUserMedia/getDisplayMedia only work in a secure context — HTTPS or localhost. Deploying to plain HTTP silently breaks camera access.
Signaling is your responsibility — WebRTC defines no wire protocol for exchanging SDP/candidates. Any transport (WS/HTTP/SSE) works.
ICE candidates often arrive before setRemoteDescription resolves. Buffer them and flush after remote desc is set, or adopt the perfect-negotiation pattern.
Always deploy TURN for production. STUN alone fails behind symmetric/corporate NATs (~15-20% of users). Test with iceTransportPolicy: 'relay'.
Mesh topology does not scale — uplink is O(N) per peer. Switch to an SFU (mediasoup/LiveKit) beyond ~4 participants.
Handle simultaneous offers (glare) with perfect negotiation: one polite peer that rolls back, one impolite peer that ignores the incoming offer.
Use replaceTrack() to swap camera↔screen or change quality without a full renegotiation round-trip.
On teardown, call track.stop() on every track AND pc.close() — closing the connection alone leaves the camera/mic hardware active.

items.label: Install Jest + supertest
items.code:
npm i -D jest supertest
# ESM: add cross-env & flag, or use babel-jest / ts-jest

items.label: Install Vitest + supertest
items.code:
npm i -D vitest supertest
npm i -D @vitest/coverage-v8

items.label: package.json scripts
items.note: Jest equivalent: "test": "jest --watch", CI uses plain `jest`.

items.label: vitest.config.ts
items.note: globals:true lets you skip imports of describe/it/expect (Jest-like).

items.label: Jest config (jest.config.js)
items.note: Jest ESM needs `node --experimental-vm-modules` or babel-jest; Vitest handles ESM natively.

items.label: Run subsets / filters
items.code:
vitest run auth           # files matching 'auth'
vitest -t "creates user"  # by test name
jest -t "creates user"

items.label: Suite / test
Lifecycle hooks
items.code:
beforeAll(async () => { /* once */ })
afterAll(async () => { /* once */ })
beforeEach(() => {})
afterEach(() => {})

items.label: Value matchers
items.code:
expect(x).toBe(5)            // Object.is / referential
expect(obj).toEqual({a:1})   // deep, ignores undefined
expect(obj).toStrictEqual({a:1}) // deep + types + undefined
expect(arr).toContain('x')
expect(s).toMatch(/regex/)

items.label: Async assertions
items.code:
await expect(fetchUser()).resolves.toEqual({id:1})
await expect(boom()).rejects.toThrow('fail')

items.label: Error assertion
items.code: expect(() => parse('')).toThrow(TypeError)
items.note: Wrap the call in a fn — passing parse('') throws before expect runs.

items.label: Mock-call matchers
items.code:
expect(fn).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1)
expect(fn).toHaveBeenCalledWith('a', 2)
expect(fn).toHaveReturnedWith(42)

items.label: Separate app from listen
items.note: Critical: import `app` in tests so supertest can bind an ephemeral port itself.

items.label: GET request
POST with JSON body
items.code:
const res = await request(app)
  .post('/users')
  .send({ name: 'ada' })
  .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
expect(res.status).toBe(201)
expect(res.body).toHaveProperty('id')

items.label: Chained expectations
items.code:
await request(app)
  .get('/users/1')
  .expect('Content-Type', /json/)
  .expect(200)
items.note: .expect() throws on mismatch — no separate assertion needed.

items.label: Validation / error path
Create a mock fn
Mock a whole module
items.note: vi.mock/jest.mock is HOISTED to top of file — factory can't close over outer vars; use vi.hoisted() if needed.

items.label: Return-value control
Spy on existing method
items.code:
const spy = vi.spyOn(mathUtils, 'add')
mathUtils.add(2, 3)
expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalledWith(2, 3) // REAL impl still runs
spy.mockReturnValue(99)                // now overridden

items.label: Spy vs Mock
items.note: Spy: wraps a real fn, records calls, real code runs unless you override. Mock: fully synthetic replacement, no real impl. Use spy to observe, mock to isolate/replace.

items.label: Deep mocking nested objects
items.note: mockDeep auto-mocks every nested method/property — ideal for ORMs/clients.

items.label: Reset between tests
items.note: Or config `clearMocks: true`. clear≠reset≠restore — mixing them up leaks state across tests.

items.label: In-memory Mongo (integration)
items.code: npm i -D mongodb-memory-server
items.note: Spins a real MongoDB in RAM — tests real queries without a shared server.

items.label: Wire up memory server
Clean state per test
items.note: Prevents order-dependent flakiness from leftover docs.

items.label: Mock the DB (unit test)
items.code:
vi.mock('../models/User.js')
User.findById.mockResolvedValue({ _id: '1', name: 'ada' })
items.note: Mock the model when unit-testing a service in isolation; use memory-server when the query itself is under test.

items.label: API is near drop-in
items.code:
jest.fn()      -> vi.fn()
jest.mock()    -> vi.mock()
jest.spyOn()   -> vi.spyOn()
jest.useFakeTimers() -> vi.useFakeTimers()
jest.clearAllMocks() -> vi.clearAllMocks()

items.label: Keep Jest globals
items.note: Set test.globals:true in vitest config so describe/it/expect need no import — otherwise `import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest'`.

items.label: Why switch
items.note: Vitest: native ESM/TS, no babel, faster HMR watch, shares Vite config. Jest ESM needs experimental flags + transforms.

items.label: Timers & mocks import
items.code: import { vi } from 'vitest'  // replaces global `jest`
items.note: `jest` global doesn't exist in Vitest — rename or alias.

items.label: Gotchas
items.note: Snapshot format differs slightly; `jest.requireActual` -> `vi.importActual` (async). Mock hoisting semantics match.

items.label: GitHub Actions workflow
items.code:
.github/workflows/test.yml:
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run test:run -- --coverage
items.note: Use `vitest run` / `jest` (non-watch) in CI — watch mode hangs the runner.

items.label: Fail fast / gate merges
items.code:
- run: npm run lint
- run: npm run test:run
items.note: Make the job a required status check on the branch protection rule so red tests block merge.

items.label: Unit tests
items.note: One module in isolation, deps mocked. Fast, many. E.g. a pure service function with mocked repo.

items.label: Integration tests
items.note: Multiple real units together (route + service + in-memory DB). Supertest + mongodb-memory-server lives here.

items.label: E2E tests
items.note: Full stack via HTTP/browser (Playwright/Cypress), real-ish env. Slowest, fewest — run in separate CI job/stage.

items.label: Pyramid ratio
items.note: Many unit > some integration > few E2E. Push logic down to fast unit tests; reserve E2E for critical user flows.

tips: Export `app` separately from `app.listen()` — supertest binds its own ephemeral port, so a listening server in the module causes port conflicts and open handles.
toBe = Object.is (references/primitives); toEqual = deep, ignores undefined; toStrictEqual = deep + checks undefined props and types. Wrong choice = false pass/fail.
vi.mock / jest.mock are hoisted above imports — the factory runs before any top-level const, so it can't reference outer variables. Use vi.hoisted() to share values.
clearAllMocks (wipe calls), resetAllMocks (calls + implementations), restoreAllMocks (undo spies). Set clearMocks:true in config or call in afterEach to stop state leaking between tests.
Use mockResolvedValue/mockRejectedValue for async deps — mockReturnValue(Promise.resolve()) works but is noisier and easy to forget to await.
Clean DB collections in afterEach with mongodb-memory-server; leftover docs cause order-dependent flaky tests that pass alone but fail in the suite.
In CI always run the non-watch command (`vitest run`, plain `jest`) or the job hangs forever waiting for file changes.
Prefer spyOn when you need the real implementation to run while asserting it was called; reach for a full mock only when you must replace behavior or cut a slow/external dependency.

items.label: Three pillars
items.note: Logs = discrete events (what happened). Metrics = numeric aggregates over time (how much/how fast). Traces = request path across services. NewRelic ingests all three.

items.label: Monitoring vs Alerting vs Status page
items.note: Monitoring = collect+visualize. Alerting = notify a human when a threshold breaks. Status page = public/internal uptime dashboard for stakeholders (e.g. statuspage.io, Better Uptime).

items.label: SLI / SLO / SLA
items.note: SLI = measured signal (latency, error rate). SLO = internal target (99.9% < 300ms). SLA = contractual promise with penalties. Alert on SLO burn, not raw spikes.

items.label: Golden signals to watch
items.note: Latency, Traffic (throughput), Errors, Saturation (CPU/mem/disk). Everything else is usually derivable from these.

items.label: Structured logs > plain strings
items.note: JSON logs let you filter/aggregate in NRQL (metrics on logs). Never log secrets/PII/tokens.

items.label: Install & create logger
Log levels (npm scale)
items.code: error:0  warn:1  info:2  http:3  verbose:4  debug:5  silly:6
items.note: Logger emits messages at its level and BELOW the number (i.e. more severe). level:'info' drops debug/silly.

items.label: Log with structured metadata
items.code:
logger.info('order_created', { orderId, amount, userId });
logger.error('db_write_failed', { err });  // errors() format captures .stack
items.note: 2nd arg merges into JSON output — this is what makes 'metrics on logs' possible.

items.label: Pretty console in dev, JSON in prod
items.code:
format: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'
  ? winston.format.json()
  : winston.format.combine(winston.format.colorize(), winston.format.simple())

items.label: HTTP request logging (Express)
items.code:
const morgan = require('morgan');
app.use(morgan('combined', { stream: { write: m => logger.http(m.trim()) } }));

items.label: Ship logs to NewRelic w/ trace context
items.note: Enricher injects trace.id/span.id so 'Logs in context' links a log line to its APM transaction.

items.label: Install agent
items.code:
npm i newrelic
# copy config template to project root
cp node_modules/newrelic/newrelic.js ./newrelic.js

items.label: Require FIRST — before anything else
items.code:
require('newrelic');           // must be line 1 of entrypoint
const express = require('express');
items.note: Or use node --require newrelic app.js. Loading it late means the agent can't patch libraries → no data.

items.label: Config via env (12-factor, no file)
items.code:
NEW_RELIC_LICENSE_KEY=xxxxx
NEW_RELIC_APP_NAME=auth-api
NEW_RELIC_NO_CONFIG_FILE=true
NEW_RELIC_LOG_LEVEL=info
NEW_RELIC_DISTRIBUTED_TRACING_ENABLED=true
items.note: app_name groups instances into ONE APM entity. Same name across pods = aggregated.

items.label: What APM auto-captures
items.note: Throughput (rpm), response time, error rate, Apdex, transaction traces, DB/external call breakdown, slow queries — zero manual instrumentation.

items.label: Custom instrumentation
Entity = anything NewRelic monitors
items.note: APM service, host, container, browser app, lambda, K8s cluster. Each has a GUID. Everything in the platform is scoped to an entity.

items.label: Tag entities for filtering
items.code: NEW_RELIC_LABELS="env:prod;team:payments;region:ap-south-1"
items.note: Tags power entity search, dashboard FACETs, and alert scoping. Tag consistently (env/team/service).

items.label: Infrastructure agent (host metrics)
items.code:
curl -Ls https://download.newrelic.com/install/newrelic-cli/scripts/install.sh | bash
NEW_RELIC_API_KEY=xxx NEW_RELIC_ACCOUNT_ID=123 /usr/local/bin/newrelic install
items.note: Infra tab = CPU, memory, disk, network, processes per host. Separate agent from APM.

items.label: Infra data in NRQL
items.code:
SELECT average(cpuPercent) FROM SystemSample FACET hostname TIMESERIES
SELECT latest(memoryUsedBytes/memoryTotalBytes)*100 FROM SystemSample FACET hostname

items.label: Shape
items.code: SELECT <func> FROM <EventType> WHERE <cond> FACET <attr> SINCE <time> TIMESERIES
items.note: SQL-like but time-series first. Common event types: Transaction, TransactionError, Log, SystemSample, PageView, Metric.

items.label: Throughput & error rate
items.code:
SELECT rate(count(*), 1 minute) FROM Transaction WHERE appName='auth-api' TIMESERIES
SELECT percentage(count(*), WHERE error IS true) FROM Transaction SINCE 30 minutes ago

items.label: Percentiles (p95 / p99)
items.code:
SELECT percentile(duration, 50, 95, 99) FROM Transaction
FACET name SINCE 1 hour ago TIMESERIES
items.note: duration is in seconds. FACET name = break down per endpoint/transaction.

items.label: Metrics on logs
items.code:
SELECT count(*) FROM Log WHERE level='error' FACET service, message SINCE 1 day ago
SELECT count(*) FROM Log WHERE message LIKE '%timeout%' TIMESERIES 5 minutes
items.note: Once logs are JSON, any field becomes a filterable/aggregatable dimension.

items.label: Time & bucketing helpers
items.code:
SINCE 1 hour ago UNTIL 30 minutes ago
TIMESERIES AUTO        -- auto bucket width
COMPARE WITH 1 week ago  -- overlay prior period
LIMIT 100

items.label: Aggregations
items.code:
count(*)  average(x)  sum(x)  max(x)  min(x)
uniqueCount(userId)  latest(x)  histogram(duration, 50, 20)  apdex(duration, t:0.5)

items.label: What a percentile means
items.note: p95 = 95% of requests were faster than this value; only 5% (the tail) were slower. p99 exposes worst-case UX that averages hide.

items.label: Why not average
items.note: avg is skewed by outliers and hides bimodal behavior. A 50ms avg can still mean p99=4s → 1% of users suffer. SLOs target p95/p99, never mean.

items.label: Percentiles don't add / average
items.note: You cannot average p99s across hosts or time buckets to get an overall p99. Query the raw distribution over the full window instead.

items.label: Dashboard = saved NRQL widgets
items.note: Build charts by pasting NRQL; each widget is one query. Use FACET for breakdowns, TIMESERIES for trends, billboard for single-number KPIs. Add variables (template) to make dashboards reusable across services/env.

items.label: NRQL alert condition
items.code:
-- signal query (must return one value / thin FACET)
SELECT percentile(duration, 95) FROM Transaction WHERE appName='auth-api'
-- threshold: static, > 0.5 for at least 5 minutes
items.note: Static (fixed number), Baseline (deviation from learned normal), or Anomaly. Set 'for at least N min' to avoid flapping.

items.label: Policy vs Condition vs Incident
items.note: Condition = one rule. Policy = group of conditions + how they roll up into incidents. Breach opens an Incident/Issue ('Problem'); notifiers fire; auto-closes on recovery.

items.label: Notifiers / channels
items.note: Route issues to Slack, email, PagerDuty, Opsgenie, or generic Webhook via a Workflow (filter issues → destination). Set severity + de-dup to avoid alert fatigue.

items.label: Webhook notifier payload
Status page
items.note: Public-facing uptime + incident history for users (statuspage.io / Better Stack / instatus). Feed it synthetic checks or alert webhooks. Separate concern from internal dashboards.

tips: require('newrelic') MUST be the very first line loaded — load it late and the agent can't monkey-patch http/express/pg, so you get zero data.
Give every instance the SAME NEW_RELIC_APP_NAME so replicas aggregate into one APM entity; a typo silently creates a second ghost service.
Alert and set SLOs on p95/p99, never on average — the mean hides the slow tail that actually hurts users.
Percentiles can't be averaged or summed across hosts/buckets; recompute over the full window or you'll report a wrong p99.
Log JSON, not string concatenation — unstructured logs can't be FACETed or aggregated into metrics in NRQL.
Winston level filters by severity: level:'info' emits error/warn/info but DROPS debug — bump LOG_LEVEL=debug to see them.
Add 'for at least N minutes' + recovery threshold on alert conditions to stop flapping and alert-fatigue.
Never log secrets, tokens, or PII — logs are queryable, retained, and widely readable; scrub before shipping to NewRelic.

items.label: Install client lib
items.code: npm install prom-client
items.note: prom-client is the official Node exporter; Prometheus pulls (scrapes) — the app never pushes.

items.label: Import + default process metrics
items.note: collectDefaultMetrics gives CPU, heap, event-loop lag, GC for free. Register scopes which metrics an endpoint exposes.

items.label: Expose /metrics endpoint
items.note: register.metrics() is async and returns text/plain in the Prometheus exposition format. This is the URL Prometheus scrapes.

items.label: Counter — monotonically increasing (requests, errors)
items.note: Only .inc() — never decreases. End name with _total by convention. Query rate() not the raw value.

items.label: Gauge — value that goes up AND down (active conns, queue depth, temp)
items.note: Use for point-in-time snapshots. .set() overwrites; don't use rate() on gauges.

items.label: Histogram — distribution/latency, produces buckets + _sum + _count
items.note: Emits _bucket{le="..."}, _sum, _count. Choose buckets to bracket your SLO. Buckets are cumulative (le = 'less than or equal').

items.label: Summary — client-side quantiles (rarely needed)
items.note: Prefer Histogram: summaries can't be aggregated across instances; histograms can via histogram_quantile().

items.label: Single middleware instruments every route
items.note: res.on('finish') captures the real final status code. Use req.route.path (templated) not req.path to avoid high-cardinality label blowup.

items.label: Keep metric defs in one module
items.code:
// metrics.js
module.exports = { register, reqCounter, httpDur, active };
items.note: Define each metric ONCE at module load. Re-registering the same name throws 'A metric with the name X has already been registered'.

items.label: Label cardinality discipline
items.note: Never label with user IDs, emails, timestamps, or raw URLs — each unique combo = a new time series and can OOM Prometheus. Keep labels to bounded enums (method, route template, status class).

items.label: prometheus.yml — scrape config
items.code:
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'node-app'
    metrics_path: /metrics   # default
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['host.docker.internal:3000']
items.note: From a container, reach the host app via host.docker.internal (Docker Desktop) — not localhost, which is the container itself.

items.label: Run Prometheus in Docker
items.code:
docker run -p 9090:9090 \
  -v $(pwd)/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml \
  prom/prometheus
items.note: UI at http://localhost:9090. Check Status > Targets to confirm your app is UP and being scraped.

items.label: Reload config without restart
items.code: curl -X POST http://localhost:9090/-/reload
items.note: Requires the flag --web.enable-lifecycle at startup. Otherwise restart the container.

items.label: Raw + filtered selection
items.code:
http_requests_total
http_requests_total{route="/user", code=~"2.."}
items.note: = exact, != not, =~ regex match, !~ regex no-match. Instant vector = one point per series.

items.label: rate() — per-second average over window (the go-to for counters)
items.code:
rate(http_requests_total[5m])
sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (route)
items.note: ALWAYS wrap counters in rate()/increase() — raw counter numbers reset to 0 on restart and are meaningless alone. [5m] = range vector.

items.label: increase() / irate()
items.code:
increase(http_requests_total[1h])   # total growth over 1h
irate(http_requests_total[5m])      # instant rate (last 2 samples)
items.note: irate for volatile/fast-moving graphs; rate for smoother alerting.

items.label: p95/p99 latency from histogram
items.code:
histogram_quantile(
  0.95,
  sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
)
items.note: Must aggregate by (le). Accuracy is bounded by your bucket boundaries.

items.label: Error ratio
items.code:
sum(rate(http_requests_total{code=~"5.."}[5m]))
/
sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
items.note: Graph tab renders any query over time; Table tab shows instant values.

items.label: docker-compose.yml — Prometheus + Grafana together
items.code:
services:
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus
    ports: ['9090:9090']
    volumes:
      - ./prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana
    ports: ['3001:3000']
    environment:
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin
    volumes:
      - grafana-data:/var/lib/grafana
volumes:
  grafana-data:
items.note: Map Grafana to 3001 to avoid clashing with a Node app on 3000 (Grafana listens on 3000 internally). The named volume persists dashboards across restarts.

items.label: Add Prometheus as a data source
items.code: URL: http://prometheus:9090
items.note: Use the compose SERVICE NAME (prometheus), not localhost — containers resolve each other by service name on the shared network. Connections > Data sources > Add > Prometheus > Save & test.

items.label: Provision datasource as code (optional)
items.code:
# ./grafana/provisioning/datasources/ds.yml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Prometheus
    type: prometheus
    access: proxy
    url: http://prometheus:9090
    isDefault: true
items.note: Mount to /etc/grafana/provisioning/datasources so it's auto-configured on boot — no manual clicks.

items.label: Build a panel
items.note: New dashboard > Add visualization > pick Prometheus > paste a PromQL query (e.g. rate(...)). Import community dashboards by ID via Dashboards > Import (e.g. Node Exporter Full).

items.label: Prometheus alert rule file
items.code:
# alerts.yml
groups:
  - name: app-alerts
    rules:
      - alert: HighErrorRate
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{code=~"5.."}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels: { severity: critical }
        annotations:
          summary: "5xx error rate > 5% for 5m"
items.note: for: makes the condition hold N minutes before firing (avoids flapping). State goes Pending -> Firing.

items.label: Wire rules + Alertmanager into prometheus.yml
items.code:
rule_files:
  - 'alerts.yml'
alerting:
  alertmanagers:
    - static_configs:
        - targets: ['alertmanager:9093']
items.note: Prometheus evaluates rules and fires to Alertmanager, which handles routing/dedup/silencing and sends to Slack, email, PagerDuty, etc.

items.label: Grafana-native alerts (no Alertmanager needed)
items.note: Alerting > Alert rules > New: define a query + threshold, set evaluation interval + pending period, attach a contact point (Slack/email webhook) and notification policy. Simpler for a single-team setup.

tips: Prometheus PULLS metrics by scraping /metrics on an interval — your app just exposes an endpoint, it never pushes. For short-lived jobs use the Pushgateway.
Always wrap counters in rate()/increase() — a bare counter resets to 0 on restart and its absolute value is meaningless.
Label cardinality is the #1 way to kill Prometheus: never put user IDs, request IDs, timestamps, or raw URLs in labels. Use route templates and status classes.
Prefer Histograms over Summaries — histogram buckets aggregate across instances via histogram_quantile(); summary quantiles cannot be summed.
Choose histogram buckets around your SLO/expected latency; percentile accuracy is limited by bucket boundaries, and too many buckets inflate series count.
In Docker, use service names between containers (http://prometheus:9090) and host.docker.internal to reach an app on the host — never localhost across the network boundary.
Confirm scraping in Prometheus UI > Status > Targets (state UP) before debugging dashboards; a blank Grafana panel is usually a broken target or wrong datasource URL.
Metric naming: base unit suffix (_seconds, _bytes), _total for counters, and histograms auto-emit _bucket/_sum/_count. Define each metric exactly once or registration throws.

items.label: Before k8s: manual ops
items.note: SSH into VMs, run containers by hand, manual scaling, manual restart on crash, load balancer configured by hand. No self-healing, drift between machines.

items.label: After k8s: declarative + self-healing
items.note: You declare desired state (YAML); the control loop continuously reconciles actual→desired. Auto-restart, auto-reschedule on node death, rolling updates, service discovery, horizontal scaling.

items.label: Cluster = control plane + worker nodes
items.note: Control plane: api-server (front door), etcd (state store), scheduler (picks node), controller-manager (runs reconcile loops). Workers run kubelet + container runtime + kube-proxy.

items.label: Smallest deployable unit = Pod, not container
items.note: A Pod wraps 1+ containers sharing network (same IP/localhost) and volumes. You rarely create Pods directly — a Deployment manages them.

items.label: kind (k8s in Docker) — fast local
items.code:
kind create cluster --name dev
kind get clusters
kind delete cluster --name dev

items.label: minikube — single-node VM/driver
items.code:
minikube start --nodes 1
minikube status
minikube stop && minikube delete

items.label: k3d (lightweight k3s in Docker)
items.code: k3d cluster create dev --agents 2

items.label: Check the cluster is up
items.code:
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes -o wide

items.label: kubeconfig / contexts
items.code:
kubectl config get-contexts
kubectl config use-context kind-dev
kubectl config current-context
items.note: Default kubeconfig: ~/.kube/config (Windows: %USERPROFILE%\.kube\config). KUBECONFIG env var can point elsewhere.

items.label: kubectl is a REST client to the API server
items.note: Everything is an API object. kubectl get pods == GET /api/v1/namespaces/default/pods. See raw calls with -v=8.

items.label: Discover API resources / versions
items.code:
kubectl api-resources          # kinds, shortnames, apiGroup
kubectl api-versions           # served group/versions
kubectl explain deployment.spec  # field docs

items.label: apiVersion cheat: core vs apps
items.note: Pod/Service/ConfigMap → apiVersion: v1 (core group). Deployment/ReplicaSet → apps/v1.

items.label: Read-only inspection
items.code:
kubectl get pods -A -o wide
kubectl describe pod <name>
kubectl logs <pod> -f --tail=100
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container>   # multi-container

items.label: Exec / debug into a pod
items.code:
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- sh
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

items.label: Namespaces
items.code:
kubectl get ns
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=dev
items.note: Objects are namespaced by default; nodes and PVs are cluster-scoped.

items.label: Run a pod imperatively (quick test)
items.code:
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx:1.27 --port=80
kubectl get pod nginx -w
items.note: kubectl run creates a bare Pod (not managed) — deleting it does NOT recreate it.

items.label: Pod manifest (declarative)
items.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx
  labels:
    app: nginx
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.27
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80

items.label: Apply / delete a manifest
items.code:
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
kubectl delete -f pod.yaml

items.label: Stop / delete the pod
items.code:
kubectl delete pod nginx
kubectl delete pod nginx --grace-period=0 --force  # immediate
items.note: A bare Pod stays deleted. A Pod owned by a ReplicaSet/Deployment gets recreated — you must delete the owner instead.

items.label: Reach the pod locally (no service yet)
items.code:
kubectl port-forward pod/nginx 8080:80
# then curl localhost:8080
items.note: port-forward is for debugging only, not for real traffic.

items.label: ReplicaSet: keeps N identical pods alive
items.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet
metadata:
  name: nginx-rs
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.27
items.note: selector.matchLabels MUST match template.metadata.labels or apply is rejected. Rarely authored directly — Deployments own ReplicaSets.

items.label: Deployment: manages ReplicaSets + rollouts
items.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx-deploy
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.27

items.label: Create a deployment imperatively
items.code:
kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx:1.27 --replicas=3
kubectl get deploy,rs,pods

items.label: Scale
items.code: kubectl scale deployment nginx-deploy --replicas=5

items.label: Rolling update (change image) + rollback
items.code:
kubectl set image deploy/nginx-deploy nginx=nginx:1.28
kubectl rollout status deploy/nginx-deploy
kubectl rollout history deploy/nginx-deploy
kubectl rollout undo deploy/nginx-deploy
items.note: Deployment spins up a new ReplicaSet and shifts pods over gradually — zero-downtime. ReplicaSet alone cannot do rolling updates or rollbacks.

items.label: Why Deployment over ReplicaSet?
items.note: Deployment adds versioned rollouts, rollback, and controlled surge/unavailable during updates. Use Deployment for stateless apps; use ReplicaSet almost never on its own.

items.label: ClusterIP (default) — internal only
items.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
items.note: Stable virtual IP + DNS (nginx-svc.<ns>.svc.cluster.local) load-balancing across pods matching the selector. Not reachable from outside.

items.label: NodePort — expose on every node's port
items.code:
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
    nodePort: 30080   # 30000-32767
items.note: Reach via <nodeIP>:30080. Good for local/dev; ugly for prod.

items.label: LoadBalancer — external cloud LB
items.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-svc
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
items.note: Cloud provider provisions a real external LB and fills EXTERNAL-IP. On bare kind/minikube it stays <pending> unless you run metallb / `minikube tunnel`.

items.label: Expose imperatively
items.code:
kubectl expose deployment nginx-deploy \
  --type=LoadBalancer --port=80 --target-port=80
kubectl get svc nginx-deploy -w

items.label: port vs targetPort vs nodePort
items.note: port = the Service's port; targetPort = container port it forwards to; nodePort = port opened on nodes (NodePort/LB only).

items.label: apply Deployment → running pods
items.note: kubectl → api-server validates & writes object to etcd → Deployment controller creates a ReplicaSet → ReplicaSet controller creates Pod objects → scheduler assigns each Pod to a node → kubelet on that node pulls image & starts container → kubelet reports status back to api-server.

items.label: Self-healing loop
items.note: If a pod/node dies, actual state < desired (replicas). The ReplicaSet controller notices and creates replacements — no human action needed.

items.label: Traffic path with a Service
items.note: Client → Service IP → kube-proxy (iptables/IPVS rules) → one of the pods matching the label selector. Endpoints object is auto-updated as pods come and go.

tips: Label selectors are the glue: a Service or ReplicaSet finds pods ONLY by matchLabels. A typo in labels = 0 endpoints and silent 'connection refused'.
Deleting a pod owned by a Deployment/ReplicaSet just triggers a recreate. To actually stop the app, delete or scale the owning Deployment (kubectl scale --replicas=0 / kubectl delete deploy).
selector.matchLabels is immutable on a Deployment after creation — pick labels carefully; changing them forces a delete+recreate.
kubectl apply (declarative, merges) vs kubectl create (imperative, fails if exists). Use apply -f for GitOps-style repeatable manifests.
On kind/minikube, LoadBalancer EXTERNAL-IP stays <pending> forever without metallb; use `minikube tunnel` or NodePort/port-forward for local access.
Always pin image tags (nginx:1.27), never :latest — :latest breaks rollout history/rollback and makes deploys non-reproducible.
kubectl describe <obj> and kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp are your first stop for 'why is my pod not running' (ImagePullBackOff, CrashLoopBackOff, FailedScheduling).
Set --namespace once (kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=dev) to stop forgetting -n and 'losing' your resources in the wrong namespace.

items.label: Swarm
items.note: A cluster of Docker Engines running in 'swarm mode'. Built into the Docker CLI — no extra install.

items.label: Manager node
items.note: Maintains cluster state via Raft, schedules tasks, exposes the swarm API. Odd count recommended (1/3/5). Quorum = (N/2)+1. 3 managers tolerate 1 failure, 5 tolerate 2.

items.label: Worker node
items.note: Only runs tasks (containers). Cannot manage the swarm. Managers are also workers by default unless drained.

items.label: Service
items.note: The desired state definition: image + replicas + config. You declare intent; the swarm reconciles reality to match.

items.label: Task
items.note: A single scheduled slot that runs exactly one container. A service with 3 replicas = 3 tasks. Tasks are immutable — a failed task is replaced, not restarted in place.

items.label: Container
items.note: The actual running instance backing a task. Chain: Service -> Tasks -> Containers.

items.label: Declarative + self-healing
items.note: If a container dies or a node drops, the manager reschedules tasks to converge back to the declared replica count.

items.label: Raft consensus
items.note: Manager quorum stores cluster state. Losing quorum freezes management (existing tasks keep running, but you can't schedule changes).

items.label: Initialize swarm on node1 (manager)
items.code: docker swarm init --advertise-addr <MANAGER_IP>
items.note: --advertise-addr is required when the host has multiple IPs. Prints the worker join command with a token.

items.label: Join node2 as worker
items.code: docker swarm join --token SWMTKN-1-xxxx <MANAGER_IP>:2377
items.note: Run on node2. Copy-paste the exact command that `swarm init` printed.

items.label: Re-print join tokens
items.code:
docker swarm join-token worker
docker swarm join-token manager
items.note: Worker and manager tokens differ. Use the manager token to add another manager.

items.label: Verify cluster
items.code: docker node ls
items.note: Only works on a manager. Leader shows `*` in MANAGER STATUS as 'Leader'.

items.label: Rotate a compromised token
items.code: docker swarm join-token --rotate worker

items.label: Leave the swarm
items.code:
docker swarm leave           # worker
docker swarm leave --force   # last manager

items.label: List nodes
items.code: docker node ls

items.label: Inspect a node
items.code: docker node inspect <NODE> --pretty

items.label: Promote / demote
items.code:
docker node promote <NODE>   # worker -> manager
docker node demote <NODE>    # manager -> worker

items.label: Change availability
items.code: docker node update --availability drain <NODE>
items.note: active | pause | drain. `drain` evicts tasks and stops scheduling — use before maintenance. Common on managers to keep them workload-free.

items.label: Label nodes for placement
items.code: docker node update --label-add region=us-east <NODE>
items.note: Reference in constraints: --constraint 'node.labels.region==us-east'.

items.label: Remove a down node
items.code:
docker node rm <NODE>          # node must have left first
docker node rm --force <NODE>

items.label: Create a service
items.code: docker service create --name web --replicas 3 -p 8080:80 nginx
items.note: -p 8080:80 publishes via the routing mesh — reachable on EVERY node's :8080, not just where a task runs.

items.label: List services & their replica health
items.code: docker service ls
items.note: REPLICAS column shows running/desired, e.g. 2/3 means one task not yet up.

items.label: List tasks of a service (where they run)
items.code: docker service ps web
items.note: Shows NODE, CURRENT STATE, and error/exit info for failed tasks.

items.label: Inspect service definition
items.code: docker service inspect web --pretty

items.label: Stream logs across all tasks
items.code: docker service logs -f web

items.label: Remove a service
items.code: docker service rm web
items.note: Stops and removes all its tasks/containers cluster-wide.

items.label: Global service (one task per node)
items.code: docker service create --mode global --name node-exporter prom/node-exporter
items.note: Use for agents/monitoring. No --replicas with global mode.

items.label: Scale replicas
items.code:
docker service scale web=5
docker service update --replicas 5 web
items.note: Both work; `scale` can set multiple services at once: `docker service scale web=5 api=3`.

items.label: Rolling image update
items.code: docker service update --image nginx:1.27 web
items.note: Replaces tasks gradually per update policy — zero-downtime if configured well.

items.label: Tune the rollout policy
items.code:
docker service update \
  --update-parallelism 2 \
  --update-delay 10s \
  --update-failure-action rollback \
  web
items.note: parallelism = tasks updated at once; delay = wait between batches.

items.label: Manual rollback
items.code: docker service rollback web
items.note: Reverts to the previous spec.

items.label: Change published port / env / mounts
items.code: docker service update --env-add LOG=debug --publish-add 9090:90 web

items.label: Deploy a stack from a compose file
items.code: docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml myapp
items.note: Requires compose file version '3.x'. `docker run`/`build`-only options (build, depends_on ordering) are ignored — swarm needs pre-built images.

items.label: deploy block (swarm-specific config)
items.code:
services:
  web:
    image: nginx:1.27
    ports: ["8080:80"]
    deploy:
      replicas: 3
      placement:
        constraints: ["node.role==worker"]
      restart_policy:
        condition: on-failure
      update_config:
        parallelism: 1
        delay: 10s
items.note: The `deploy:` key is only honored by `stack deploy`, ignored by `docker compose up`.

items.label: List / inspect stack
items.code:
docker stack ls
docker stack services myapp
docker stack ps myapp

items.label: Tear down a stack
items.code: docker stack rm myapp

items.label: Create an overlay network (multi-host)
items.code: docker network create --driver overlay backend
items.note: Overlay spans all nodes. Attach with `--network backend` on service create.

items.label: Service discovery via DNS + VIP
items.note: Services on the same overlay reach each other by service name (e.g. http://api:3000). Swarm load-balances across tasks behind a virtual IP.

items.label: Routing mesh vs host mode
items.code: --publish mode=host,target=80,published=8080
items.note: Default (mesh) exposes the port on every node. host mode binds only on nodes actually running a task.

items.label: Secrets
items.code:
echo "s3cr3t" | docker secret create db_pass -
docker service create --secret db_pass mysql
items.note: Mounted at /run/secrets/db_pass (in-memory tmpfs). Managers store them encrypted in Raft.

items.label: Configs (non-sensitive files)
items.code:
docker config create nginx_conf ./nginx.conf
docker service create --config source=nginx_conf,target=/etc/nginx/nginx.conf nginx

tips: Most swarm commands (node ls, service *, stack *) only run on a MANAGER. Workers can't query cluster state.
Run managers in odd numbers. Quorum is (N/2)+1 — 2 managers is worse than 1 (any single loss breaks quorum and freezes management).
--advertise-addr matters on multi-NIC/cloud hosts; picking the wrong IP means workers can't reach the manager on :2377.
Required ports between nodes: TCP 2377 (cluster mgmt), TCP+UDP 7946 (node discovery), UDP 4789 (overlay/VXLAN data). Open these in firewalls/security groups.
`docker service ps <svc> --no-trunc` reveals full error messages for stuck/failed tasks (image pull errors, constraint mismatches).
Swarm needs pre-built, pushed images — there is no build step across nodes. Push to a registry every node can pull from.
The `deploy:` key in a compose file is ignored by `docker compose up`; it only applies under `docker stack deploy`.
Drain your managers (`--availability drain`) in production so control-plane nodes don't also carry app workload.

items.label: Local cluster (kind)
items.code:
kind create cluster --name dev
kubectl cluster-info --context kind-dev
items.note: kind runs nodes as Docker containers; ports must be mapped via cluster config for ingress.

items.label: Local cluster (minikube)
items.code:
minikube start --driver=docker
minikube tunnel   # needed for type=LoadBalancer + ingress
items.note: Without `minikube tunnel`, LoadBalancer EXTERNAL-IP stays <pending>.

items.label: Cloud cluster (EKS / GKE)
items.code:
eksctl create cluster --name prod --nodes 3
gcloud container clusters create prod --num-nodes 3
aws eks update-kubeconfig --name prod   # writes kubeconfig
items.note: Cloud gives you real LoadBalancer + dynamic PV provisioning out of the box.

items.label: Contexts & apply loop
items.code:
kubectl config get-contexts
kubectl config use-context kind-dev
kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml
kubectl get pods,svc,ingress -o wide
items.note: Everything is `apply -f` (declarative). `kubectl get all -n <ns>` for a quick snapshot.

items.label: ClusterIP (default, internal only)
items.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata: { name: web }
spec:
  selector: { app: web }   # must match pod labels
  ports:
    - port: 80          # service port
      targetPort: 3000  # container port
items.note: Reachable in-cluster only. DNS: web.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

items.label: NodePort (exposes on every node)
items.code:
spec:
  type: NodePort
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 3000
      nodePort: 30080   # 30000-32767
items.note: Hit any nodeIP:30080. Ugly high ports; fine for dev/testing.

items.label: LoadBalancer (cloud external IP)
items.code:
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports: [{ port: 80, targetPort: 3000 }]
items.note: Provisions a real cloud LB. On bare-metal/kind stays <pending> without MetalLB.

items.label: Quick expose
items.code: kubectl expose deployment web --port=80 --target-port=3000 --type=LoadBalancer

items.label: Downside → why Ingress exists
items.note: One LoadBalancer per service = one cloud LB each = $$ + a public IP each. Services are L4 only: no host/path routing, no TLS termination, no single entrypoint.

items.label: Namespaces (isolate resources)
items.code:
kubectl create namespace app
kubectl apply -f x.yaml -n app
kubectl get pods -n app
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=app
items.note: Controllers usually live in their own ns (e.g. ingress-nginx, cert-manager).

items.label: Install NGINX ingress controller
items.code:
helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx -n ingress-nginx --create-namespace
# kind/manifest alt:
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/static/provider/cloud/deploy.yaml
items.note: Controller = the actual pod (a reverse proxy) that watches Ingress objects. Ingress object = just the routing config.

items.label: Ingress routing rules
items.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata: { name: app-ing }
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: app.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /api
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service: { name: api, port: { number: 80 } }
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service: { name: web, port: { number: 80 } }
items.note: One LB fronts many services via host/path. pathType: Prefix | Exact | ImplementationSpecific.

items.label: Try Traefik instead
items.code:
helm repo add traefik https://traefik.github.io/charts
helm install traefik traefik/traefik -n traefik --create-namespace
items.note: Same Ingress API works; set ingressClassName: traefik. Swappable controllers, one spec.

items.label: Create ConfigMap
items.code:
kubectl create configmap app-cfg \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info \
  --from-file=config.json
items.note: Non-sensitive config only. Stored in plaintext in etcd.

items.label: Create Secret
items.code:
kubectl create secret generic db-sec \
  --from-literal=PASSWORD=s3cr3t
# YAML: use stringData (plain) or data (base64)
kind: Secret
type: Opaque
stringData: { PASSWORD: s3cr3t }
items.note: `data:` values must be base64. Decode: kubectl get secret db-sec -o jsonpath='{.data.PASSWORD}' | base64 -d

items.label: Consume as env vars
Consume as mounted volume
items.note: Mounted values update live (env vars do NOT — pod needs restart).

items.label: ConfigMap vs Secret
items.note: Same API shape. Secrets are base64 (NOT encryption), can be RBAC-restricted + encrypted-at-rest, mounted as tmpfs. Use Secret for tokens/passwords/certs; ConfigMap for everything else.

items.label: Install cert-manager
items.code:
helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
helm install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager \
  -n cert-manager --create-namespace --set crds.enabled=true

items.label: ClusterIssuer (Let's Encrypt)
items.note: Use staging server first to avoid LE rate limits.

items.label: Auto-TLS via Ingress annotation
items.code:
metadata:
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt
spec:
  tls:
    - hosts: [app.example.com]
      secretName: app-tls   # cert-manager fills this
items.note: cert-manager watches the tls block, solves ACME challenge, writes the cert Secret, and auto-renews.

items.label: Docker volumes (baseline)
items.code:
docker run -v mydata:/var/lib/data img      # named volume
docker run -v $(pwd):/app img               # bind mount
items.note: Container FS is ephemeral; volumes outlive the container. k8s generalizes this.

items.label: emptyDir (ephemeral)
items.note: Lives & dies with the POD (survives container restart, NOT pod deletion/reschedule). Good for scratch/cache.

items.label: PersistentVolumeClaim (what pods use)
items.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata: { name: data }
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  resources: { requests: { storage: 5Gi } }
  storageClassName: standard
items.note: Pod references the CLAIM, never the PV directly: volumes: [{ name: d, persistentVolumeClaim: { claimName: data } }]

items.label: Static PV (admin pre-provisions)
items.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata: { name: pv1 }
spec:
  capacity: { storage: 5Gi }
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  hostPath: { path: /mnt/data }   # or nfs/csi
items.note: PVC binds to a matching PV. No StorageClass → manual binding.

items.label: Dynamic / automatic PV (StorageClass)
items.note: PVC names the StorageClass → PV is provisioned on the fly. This is the default in cloud clusters.

tips: Service selector labels MUST match pod template labels exactly, or EXTERNAL/ENDPOINTS stay empty — debug with `kubectl get endpoints <svc>`.
One entrypoint rule: use ONE LoadBalancer for the ingress controller, then route everything else through Ingress objects (ClusterIP services behind it).
Ingress does nothing without a controller running — installing the Ingress object alone is a common newbie trap.
Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted. Don't commit them to git; enable etcd encryption-at-rest and restrict via RBAC.
env-var-injected ConfigMaps/Secrets need a pod restart to pick up changes; volume-mounted ones update live (with a short kubelet sync delay).
Test cert-manager against Let's Encrypt STAGING first — the prod endpoint rate-limits ~5 certs/domain/week.
emptyDir data is lost when the pod is deleted or rescheduled to another node — only PVCs survive that. For DBs, always use a PVC.
accessModes matter: ReadWriteOnce = one node at a time (most block storage); use ReadWriteMany (NFS/CSI) only if multiple pods must write simultaneously.

items.label: Linting = static analysis that flags bugs, bad patterns & style before runtime (unused vars, no-undef, ==). Formatting = whitespace/quotes/semicolons (Prettier's job).
items.note: Lint = correctness; format = cosmetics. Keep them separate.

items.label: Install + scaffold config
items.code:
npm i -D eslint
npm init @eslint/config@latest   # interactive; writes eslint.config.js

items.label: Run
items.code:
npx eslint .            # lint cwd
npx eslint . --fix      # auto-fix fixable rules
npx eslint src --max-warnings=0   # CI: fail on any warning

items.label: package.json scripts
Base JS config — array of config objects, first-match-wins merge
Global ignore = an object with ONLY an `ignores` key
items.code: { ignores: ["dist/**", "coverage/**", "**/*.min.js"] }
items.note: .eslintignore is gone in flat config — ignores live here. node_modules & dotfiles ignored by default.

items.label: Legacy .eslintrc (v8 / opt-in)
items.note: Force legacy in v9: ESLINT_USE_FLAT_CONFIG=false. Removed entirely in v10.

items.label: Severity: "off"/0, "warn"/1, "error"/2 (nonzero exit)
Disable inline
File-level directive
items.code:
/* eslint-disable */          // whole file
/* eslint no-console: "error" */  // override one rule for file

items.label: Report unused disables (catch stale suppressions)
items.code: npx eslint . --report-unused-disable-directives

items.label: TypeScript via typescript-eslint helper
items.note: Type-aware rules need languageOptions.parserOptions.projectService: true (slower).

items.label: React + hooks (flat)
items.note: react-in-jsx-scope off for React 17+ new JSX transform. Vite adds eslint-plugin-react-refresh.

items.label: create-next-app installs eslint-config-next automatically
items.code: npx next lint        # deprecated in Next 16 — call eslint directly instead

items.label: Flat config with FlatCompat (Next 15 default)
items.note: core-web-vitals = stricter perf rules; next/typescript adds TS rules.

items.label: Install & run
items.code:
npm i -D prettier
npx prettier --write .    # format in place
npx prettier --check .    # CI: fail if unformatted

items.label: .prettierrc.json + .prettierignore
items.code:
{
  "semi": true,
  "singleQuote": true,
  "trailingComma": "all",
  "printWidth": 80,
  "tabWidth": 2
}
items.note: .prettierignore mirrors .gitignore syntax (dist, build, *.md).

items.label: eslint-config-prettier — turns OFF ESLint rules that fight Prettier
items.note: Recommended approach: ESLint lints, Prettier formats, config-prettier keeps them from overlapping.

items.label: eslint-plugin-prettier — runs Prettier AS a lint rule (optional)
items.code:
// reports formatting as "prettier/prettier" errors
rules: { "prettier/prettier": "error" }
items.note: Convenient but slower & noisier; large repos usually run prettier standalone instead.

items.label: Extensions
items.code:
dbaeumer.vscode-eslint
esbenp.prettier-vscode

items.label: .vscode/settings.json — format + fix on save
items.note: "explicit" replaced the old boolean true in recent VSCode ESLint versions.

items.label: Airbnb config
items.code:
npx install-peerdeps --dev eslint-config-airbnb
// extends: "airbnb", "airbnb/hooks"
items.note: Very opinionated. Flat-config support lags — use eslint-config-airbnb-extended for v9.

items.label: Standard / others
Install & init Husky v9
items.code:
npm i -D husky lint-staged
npx husky init   # adds "prepare":"husky" + creates .husky/pre-commit
items.note: prepare script auto-installs hooks on npm install for teammates.

items.label: .husky/pre-commit (v9 — no shebang/husky.sh needed)
items.code: npx lint-staged

items.label: lint-staged config (package.json) — runs only on STAGED files
items.note: lint-staged appends staged filenames to each command — don't pass your own globs (would re-lint the whole repo).

items.label: Alternative: Lefthook (fast, parallel, language-agnostic)
items.code:
# lefthook.yml
pre-commit:
  parallel: true
  commands:
    lint:
      glob: "*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}"
      run: npx eslint {staged_files} --fix
      stage_fixed: true

items.label: Bypass a hook (emergencies only)
items.code: git commit --no-verify -m "wip"

tips: ESLint v9 defaults to flat config (eslint.config.js). .eslintrc + .eslintignore are deprecated and dropped in v10 — migrate or pin v8.
Order matters: put eslint-config-prettier (and any *-config-prettier) LAST in the flat array so it can disable earlier formatting rules.
A flat-config object with ONLY `ignores` is a global ignore; ignores alongside other keys only scopes THAT block.
Lint vs format: let Prettier own whitespace/quotes/commas and ESLint own logic. Avoid eslint-plugin-prettier on big repos — run `prettier --write` separately for speed.
React 17+ new JSX transform → turn off react/react-in-jsx-scope and react/jsx-uses-react (false positives otherwise).
Type-aware TS rules require parserOptions.projectService/project; they're much slower, so scope them to source files only.
In CI use `eslint --max-warnings=0` and `prettier --check .` so warnings and unformatted code fail the build.
Husky v9 hook files are plain scripts — the old `#!/bin/sh` + `. "$(dirname)/_/husky.sh"` header is gone; `next lint` is deprecated in Next 16, call eslint directly.

items.label: Unit test — pure fn / single module, mock all I/O (fast, most numerous)
items.note: No network/DB/filesystem. Milliseconds. Run on every save.

items.label: Integration test — multiple real units wired together (route + service + real DB)
items.note: Hits a REAL test database, not mocks. Verifies SQL, migrations, serialization.

items.label: E2E test — full stack through the browser as a user (frontend+backend+DB)
items.note: Slowest, fewest. Cypress/Playwright drive a real browser against a running app.

items.label: Install stack
items.code:
npm i -D vitest supertest
npm i -D cypress
npx vitest        # watch
npx cypress open  # interactive runner

items.label: Isolated test DB (never your dev/prod DB)
items.code:
# .env.test
DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5433/appdb_test"
items.note: Different PORT/DB name than dev so a test wipe can't nuke real data.

items.label: Migrations applied to the test DB before the suite runs
items.code: npx prisma migrate deploy   # or: prisma db push for throwaway schema

items.label: Deterministic seed / clean slate per run
items.note: Tests must not depend on ordering or leftover rows.

items.label: App exportable without auto-listening
items.note: Export `app` so supertest can bind it without an open port.

items.label: One-off Postgres container
items.code:
docker run -d --name pg-test -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres \
  -e POSTGRES_DB=appdb_test -p 5433:5432 postgres:16

items.label: docker-compose for repeatable local + CI (docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up -d)
items.code:
# docker-compose.test.yml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: appdb_test
    ports: ['5433:5432']
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD-SHELL','pg_isready -U postgres']
      interval: 2s
      retries: 10

items.label: Wait until ready before migrating
items.code:
docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up -d --wait
npx prisma migrate deploy
items.note: --wait blocks until healthcheck passes; avoids race on first connect.

items.label: Testcontainers (spin DB from inside the test process)
items.code:
const pg = await new PostgreSqlContainer('postgres:16').start()
process.env.DATABASE_URL = pg.getConnectionUri()
items.note: Ephemeral, port randomised — great for CI parallelism.

items.label: Separate config so unit and integration run independently
globalSetup — start DB + migrate ONCE for the whole run
items.note: globalSetup runs in a separate context — you can't share JS objects, only side effects/env.

items.label: npm scripts
items.code:
"test": "vitest run",
"test:int": "vitest run --config vitest.config.integration.ts",
"test:e2e": "cypress run"

items.label: Load .env.test
items.code:
import 'dotenv/config'
// or: vitest --config ... with test.env / setupFiles
items.note: Set NODE_ENV=test and point DATABASE_URL at the test DB before Prisma client instantiates.

items.label: beforeAll — expensive one-time setup (connect client, create server)
items.code:
import { beforeAll, afterAll, beforeEach, describe, it, expect } from 'vitest'
const prisma = new PrismaClient()
beforeAll(async () => { await prisma.$connect() })
afterAll(async () => { await prisma.$disconnect() })

items.label: beforeEach — reset state so tests are independent
items.note: beforeAll runs ONCE per file; beforeEach runs before EVERY it(). Clean in beforeEach, not after — a crashed test still leaves a clean slate for the next.

items.label: Assert through the HTTP layer with supertest (route → DB round trip)
Hook order (nested describe)
items.note: beforeAll(outer) → beforeAll(inner) → beforeEach(outer) → beforeEach(inner) → test → afterEach(inner→outer) → afterAll. Return a fn from beforeEach to auto-run as afterEach cleanup.

items.label: Postgres as a service container
items.code:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env: { POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres, POSTGRES_DB: appdb_test }
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 2s --health-retries 10
    env:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/appdb_test
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx prisma migrate deploy
      - run: npm run test:int
items.note: `services` are managed by the runner (started/healthchecked before steps) — no docker-compose needed in CI.

items.label: Gate merges on green tests
items.note: Make the job a required status check on the protected branch so failing integration/e2e blocks the PR.

items.label: Run E2E in CI
items.code:
- run: npm run build && npm start &
- run: npx wait-on http://localhost:3000
- run: npx cypress run
items.note: Start the app, wait for the port, then run headless Cypress.

items.label: Config
A spec — cypress/e2e/login.cy.ts
items.note: Prefer stable data-cy attributes over CSS/text selectors.

items.label: Commands don't return — chain, don't await
items.code:
cy.get('.btn').click()          // queued, retries automatically
// WRONG: const el = await cy.get(...)
items.note: Cypress commands are async but not Promises; use .then() to read values. Assertions auto-retry until timeout.

items.label: Stub network / seed via API for speed
items.code:
cy.intercept('GET', '/api/users', { fixture: 'users.json' }).as('users')
cy.request('POST', '/test/seed', { email: 'a@b.com' })  // reset backend state
cy.wait('@users')

items.label: Reusable steps as custom commands
items.note: Log in via API not the UI in setup — faster and less flaky than clicking through the form every test.

items.label: Run modes
items.code:
npx cypress open   # GUI, watch, time-travel debugger
npx cypress run    # headless, for CI (records video/screenshots on fail)

tips: Isolation first: each test must set up and tear down its own data. A shared, mutating DB with order-dependent tests is the #1 source of flake.
Clean in beforeEach (TRUNCATE ... RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE), not afterEach — a crashed test then can't poison the next one.
beforeAll = once per file (connect DB, boot server); beforeEach = before every it() (reset rows). Mixing these up causes cross-test bleed or slow suites.
Set fileParallelism:false (or maxWorkers:1) when integration test files share one DB, or give each worker its own schema/DB to keep parallelism.
Never point tests at dev/prod: guard with a separate DATABASE_URL and refuse to run if NODE_ENV !== 'test'. TRUNCATE on the wrong DB is unrecoverable.
Bump hook/test timeouts (hookTimeout, testTimeout ~30s) — Docker start + migrate is slow and the default 5s will falsely fail the first run.
In CI use `services:` (managed containers with healthchecks) and run `prisma migrate deploy` before the suite; wait-on the port before Cypress.
Cypress commands are queued, not awaited — never `await cy.get()`. Assert with .should() (auto-retries) and select via data-cy attributes to survive markup changes.

items.label: Two axes of scaling
items.note: Horizontal = more pod replicas (stateless, preferred). Vertical = bigger CPU/mem per pod (VPA, restarts pods). This lesson = horizontal.

items.label: Set replicas declaratively in the Deployment
items.code:
spec:
  replicas: 3   # kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml
items.note: Source of truth. HPA overrides this field at runtime — don't fight it by re-applying replicas.

items.label: Scale imperatively (quick, ad-hoc)
items.code: kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5

items.label: Watch replicas roll out
items.code:
kubectl get deploy myapp -w
kubectl get pods -l app=myapp -o wide

items.label: 'Horizontal pod accelerator' = Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)
items.note: Controller that auto-adjusts replicas based on observed metrics (CPU/mem/custom). Reconciles every ~15s.

items.label: HPA + `kubectl top` need metrics-server running
items.code: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
items.note: No metrics-server => HPA shows targets as <unknown> and never scales.

items.label: Local clusters (minikube/kind/k3d) need --kubelet-insecure-tls
items.code:
# minikube shortcut
minikube addons enable metrics-server
items.note: On kind, patch the deployment args to add --kubelet-insecure-tls or it CrashLoops on cert errors.

items.label: Verify it works
items.code:
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods

items.label: Create imperatively
items.code:
kubectl autoscale deployment myapp \
  --cpu-percent=50 --min=2 --max=10

items.label: Declarative HPA (autoscaling/v2 — current API)
items.code:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: myapp-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: myapp
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 50
items.note: averageUtilization is a % of the pod's CPU *request*, NOT the limit. No request set => HPA can't compute % => won't scale.

items.label: Multiple / memory metrics
items.code:
- type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: 200Mi
items.note: With several metrics, HPA computes desiredReplicas for each and takes the MAX.

items.label: Tune reaction speed (v2 behavior block)
items.code:
behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
items.note: Default scale-down window is 300s (prevents flapping). Scale-up is fast by default.

items.label: Inspect
items.code:
kubectl get hpa
kubectl describe hpa myapp-hpa   # see events + current metric

items.label: Core formula
items.code:
desiredReplicas =
  ceil[ currentReplicas * (currentMetric / desiredMetric) ]

items.label: Worked example
items.code:
3 pods, avg CPU = 90%, target = 50%
= ceil[ 3 * (90/50) ] = ceil[5.4] = 6 pods
items.note: Result is clamped to [minReplicas, maxReplicas].

items.label: Tolerance dead-zone (default 10%)
items.note: If |currentMetric/desiredMetric - 1| <= 0.1, HPA does nothing — avoids thrashing on tiny fluctuations.

items.label: Ratio scale-down example
items.code:
6 pods, avg CPU = 20%, target = 50%
= ceil[ 6 * (20/50) ] = ceil[2.4] = 3 pods

items.label: Set in the container spec (per container)
items.code:
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
items.note: requests = guaranteed / used for scheduling + HPA %. limits = hard ceiling.

items.label: CPU units
items.code: 1000m = 1 vCPU core   (100m = 0.1 core)
items.note: CPU is compressible: over limit => throttled, not killed.

items.label: Memory units
items.note: Memory is incompressible: exceed limit => OOMKilled. Use Mi/Gi (binary), not M/G.

items.label: QoS classes (drives eviction order)
items.note: Guaranteed = requests==limits on all containers. Burstable = requests<limits. BestEffort = none set (evicted first under pressure).

items.label: Requests power the scheduler AND the autoscaler
items.note: Scheduler sums requests vs node allocatable. Cluster autoscaler adds nodes when requests can't fit; HPA % is relative to requests.

items.label: What it does
items.note: HPA scales pods; Cluster Autoscaler scales NODES. Distinct controller, usually cloud-managed (EKS/GKE/AKS node groups).

items.label: Scale-up trigger
items.note: Fires when pods are stuck Pending due to insufficient CPU/mem (unschedulable). Adds a node from the node group.

items.label: Scale-down trigger
items.note: Removes a node underutilized (default <50%) for ~10min if its pods can reschedule elsewhere. Blocked by pods with no controller, local storage, or restrictive PDBs.

items.label: Diagnose why a pod won't schedule
items.code:
kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Pending
kubectl describe pod <pod>   # look at Events: 0/3 nodes available
items.note: No requests set => CA can't reason about capacity and may not add nodes correctly.

items.label: Full elastic stack
items.note: HPA (pods) + Cluster Autoscaler (nodes) + requests/limits together = end-to-end elastic scaling.

items.label: Generate CPU load against a Service
items.code:
kubectl run -it --rm load --image=busybox:1.28 --restart=Never -- \
  /bin/sh -c "while true; do wget -q -O- http://myapp; done"

items.label: Watch HPA react in real time
items.code: kubectl get hpa myapp-hpa -w

items.label: Live resource usage
items.code: kubectl top pods -l app=myapp --containers

items.label: Expose the deployment (target for HPA/load)
items.code: kubectl expose deployment myapp --port=80 --target-port=8080

items.label: Reset an HPA experiment
items.code:
kubectl delete hpa myapp-hpa
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=1

tips: No resource *requests* = HPA can't compute CPU % and stays <unknown>. Always set requests before enabling HPA.
HPA utilization % is measured against the request, never the limit — a common source of confusion.
HPA needs metrics-server; on kind/minikube add --kubelet-insecure-tls or it CrashLoops on TLS.
Don't `kubectl apply` a Deployment with a hardcoded `replicas:` while an HPA manages it — they fight. Omit replicas or use apply server-side with the field ignored.
Default 10% tolerance means small metric drifts won't trigger scaling — expected, not a bug.
Memory over limit => OOMKilled (incompressible); CPU over limit => throttled (compressible). Size memory limits carefully.
Scale-down is deliberately slow (300s stabilization window) to prevent flapping; scale-up is fast.
HPA scales pods, Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes — you usually need both; nodes only get added when pods are Pending for lack of capacity.

items.label: Workspace layout (pnpm + turbo)
items.code:
apps/
  web/        # Next.js — problems, submit UI, contests, leaderboard
  setter/     # problem-authoring UI/CLI + boilerplate generator
  judge/      # worker: pulls queue, runs code in Docker
packages/
  db/         # Prisma schema + client (shared)
  common/     # zod types, verdict enum, language configs
items.note: Split web / setter / judge so the judge (untrusted-code runner) scales & deploys independently.

items.label: Scaffold
items.code:
pnpm create next-app apps/web --ts
pnpm add -w prisma @prisma/client ioredis zod
pnpm dlx turbo run dev   # runs all apps

items.label: Core stack
items.note: Next.js (React) FE + API, Node/Express or Next route handlers BE, Postgres+Prisma (durable state), Redis (submission queue), Docker (per-submission sandbox).

items.label: Core models
items.code:
model Problem {
  id        String @id @default(cuid())
  slug      String @unique
  title     String
  statement String
  difficulty Int
  timeLimitMs Int   @default(1000)
  memoryKb    Int   @default(262144)
  hidden    Boolean @default(true)
  submissions Submission[]
  defaultCode DefaultCode[]  // per-language boilerplate
}

model Submission {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId    String
  problemId String
  contestId String?
  code      String
  language  String
  status    SubmissionStatus @default(PENDING)
  runtimeMs Int?
  memoryKb  Int?
  failedTest Int?
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  @@index([userId, problemId])
  @@index([contestId])
}

enum SubmissionStatus { PENDING AC WA TLE MLE RE CE }
items.note: Contest / ContestParticipant / rating history are separate tables. Keep rows small.

items.label: Big test data → object store
items.note: Store test inputs/outputs in S3 (or a git-backed problems repo); keep only a pointer/path in Postgres — never BLOB megabytes of tests into a row.

items.label: Migrate
items.code:
pnpm prisma migrate dev --name init
pnpm prisma generate

items.label: Submit endpoint (return fast)
items.note: API writes PENDING + enqueues, then returns. Never run untrusted code in the request handler.

items.label: Worker pulls (blocking pop)
items.note: Redis list = simplest broker. BullMQ (`new Queue/Worker`) adds retries, rate-limit, priority (contest > practice) for free.

items.label: Hardened container per submission
items.code:
docker run --rm \
  --network none \                 # no internet / data exfil
  --read-only --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m \ # ro rootfs, scratch only
  --memory 256m --memory-swap 256m \  # MLE cap (swap==mem disables swap)
  --pids-limit 64 \                # kill fork bombs
  --cpus 1 --ulimit cpu=2 \        # CPU-TIME cap → SIGKILL
  --cap-drop ALL --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  -u 1000:1000 \                   # non-root
  judge-cpp /run.sh
items.note: memory-swap MUST equal memory or MLE never fires. gVisor/Firecracker for stronger isolation than plain Docker.

items.label: Compile once, then loop tests
items.note: Compile once, reuse binary across all tests. norm() = trim trailing whitespace/newlines before diff.

items.label: Problem folder = source of truth
items.code:
problems/two-sum/
  Problem.md        # statement (markdown)
  Structure.md      # I/O signature (parsed)
  tests/
    0.in  0.out
    1.in  1.out
  boilerplate/      # generated per language
    function.cpp function.js ...

items.label: Structure.md format
items.code:
Problem Name: "Sum of Two"
Function Name: sumOfTwo
Input Structure:
Input Field: int a
Input Field: int b
Input Field: list<int> arr
Output Structure:
Output Field: int result

items.label: Generate stubs + I/O harness
items.note: One canonical I/O contract → add a language or problem without hand-writing a harness per pair. A parser/test mismatch shows up as bogus WA that looks like a platform bug.

items.label: Polling (MVP — do this first)
items.note: Stateless, trivially scalable, good enough for MVP. Cost is redundant requests.

items.label: SSE (one-way push)
WebSocket + Redis pub/sub
items.note: Needed for live leaderboard/bidirectional. Worker publishes to Redis; the web node holding the socket subscribes — pub/sub bridges worker → correct socket across instances.

items.label: CPU time, not wall time
items.note: Measure CPU time (ulimit cpu / cgroup cpuacct) so an overloaded host doesn't unfairly TLE a correct solution — keep a wall-clock backstop to kill infinite loops.

items.label: Per-language limit multiplier
items.code: const limit = base * ({ cpp:1, java:2, python:3 }[lang] ?? 1);
items.note: Slower runtimes get a higher/multiplied limit for the same problem. Warm the runtime to keep JIT/cold-start out of the budget.

items.label: Rating = post-contest batch
items.note: Update once results are settled — NOT live during the round. Elo/Glicko-style: beat higher-rated → gain more.

items.label: Live leaderboard
items.code:
redis.zadd(`contest:${cid}`, score, userId);
redis.zrevrange(`contest:${cid}`, 0, 49, 'WITHSCORES');
items.note: Redis sorted set for live standings; denormalized standings table for durable ranking — avoid full-table scans of submissions.

tips: --memory-swap MUST equal --memory or the OOM killer never fires and MLE submissions silently swap instead of failing.
Enforce limits with CPU time (ulimit cpu / cgroups), not wall-clock — wall time makes verdicts flaky under host load. Always keep a wall backstop.
Fresh, short-lived container per submission; never reuse one — sharing leaks state and hidden test data between users.
Never judge inline in the request handler: write PENDING + enqueue, return in ~50ms. Judging is CPU-bound and would collapse a web worker under a contest spike.
Compile once, reuse the binary across all tests; a compile failure is CE and must short-circuit before any test runs.
A boilerplate-generator / test-file I/O mismatch surfaces as spurious WA that looks like a platform bug — the setter's output is a load-bearing correctness surface.
Store test data in object storage with pointers in Postgres; keep Submission rows small and index (userId, problemId) + (contestId).
Compute leaderboards from a denormalized standings table / Redis sorted set — never rank by scanning the full submissions table per request.

items.label: Built-in crypto module (no install)
items.code:
const crypto = require('crypto');
// or ESM: import crypto from 'node:crypto';

items.label: Elliptic-curve keys/signatures (secp256k1, Bitcoin's curve)
items.note: secp256k1 is the exact curve Bitcoin & Ethereum use.

items.label: Quick REPL to experiment with hashes
