items.code: node -e "console.log(require('crypto').createHash('sha256').update('hi').digest('hex'))"

items.label: Hash any string to hex
items.note: digest() empties the hash object — call it once per createHash().

items.label: Hash structured data deterministically
items.note: Same input -> same 64-char (256-bit) hex, always. Key ordering must be stable.

items.label: Core properties to memorize
items.note: Deterministic, one-way (irreversible), avalanche (1-bit change -> totally different hash), fixed-length output, collision-resistant.

items.label: Hex vs raw length
items.code:
// 256 bits = 32 bytes = 64 hex chars
crypto.createHash('sha256').update('').digest('hex').length // 64
items.note: SHA-256 output is always 64 hex chars regardless of input size.

items.label: Find a nonce so hash starts with N zeros
items.note: The only way to find the nonce is brute-force guessing — that's the 'work'.

items.label: Difficulty = number of leading zeros required
items.note: Each extra leading zero makes it ~16x harder (hex). Verifying is instant; finding is expensive — asymmetric by design.

items.label: Verify PoW (cheap)
Block structure
items.note: prevHash is what chains blocks — tampering with block N breaks every block after it.

items.label: Mine a block (embed PoW)
Chain + genesis block + validation
items.note: A 'currency' is just a chain whose data is a ledger of signed transactions.

items.label: Transaction shape
items.code: const tx = { from: senderPubKey, to: recipientPubKey, amount: 10 };
items.note: 'from' = null for coinbase/mining reward (money creation).

items.label: Generate a keypair (your wallet)
items.note: Public key = who you are; private key = proof you're you. Ownership = knowing the private key.

items.label: Sign a transaction
Anyone can verify with only the public key
items.code:
const pub = ec.keyFromPublic(publicKey, 'hex');
const valid = pub.verify(txHash, sig); // true/false
items.note: Signature proves the sender authorized it without revealing the private key. Can't be forged, can't be repudiated.

items.label: Double SHA-256 (Bitcoin's actual hash)
items.note: Bitcoin hashes block headers with SHA256(SHA256(x)).

items.label: Hard-limit supply & issuance
items.note: 21M BTC cap. Reward halves every 210,000 blocks (~4 yrs): 50 -> 25 -> 12.5 -> 6.25 -> 3.125 BTC (2024).

items.label: Timing & difficulty
items.note: ~10 min target block time. Difficulty re-adjusts every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks) to hold that 10-min average.

items.label: UTXO model (not account balances)
items.note: You spend whole 'unspent transaction outputs' and get change back; balance = sum of your UTXOs. Ethereum uses accounts instead.

items.label: Units
items.note: 1 BTC = 100,000,000 satoshis (1e-8). Smallest unit = 1 sat.

items.label: Merkle root
items.note: All txs in a block hashed pairwise into one root hash stored in the header — lets you prove a tx is included without the full block.

items.label: Coinbase transaction
items.note: First tx in a block; has no input, creates the block reward + fees for the miner. This is how new BTC is minted.

items.label: The problem it solves
items.note: Trust without a central authority (bank/gov). No single party can rewrite history or double-spend.

items.label: Immutability
items.note: Each block commits to prevHash, so editing old data forces re-mining every subsequent block faster than the network — practically impossible.

items.label: Consensus (Nakamoto / longest chain)
items.note: Honest nodes accept the chain with the most cumulative PoW. Attacker needs >50% of hash power to rewrite it.

items.label: Decentralization vs distribution
items.note: Every full node keeps a full copy of the ledger; anyone can verify independently. No trusted intermediary.

items.label: Double-spend prevention
items.note: Ordering txs in mined blocks + signatures makes spending the same coin twice detectable and rejectable.

tips: digest() consumes the hash object — you must createHash() fresh for every hash; reusing one throws.
Higher difficulty = exponentially more work: adding one leading hex zero multiplies expected attempts by ~16.
Never log or commit a private key. Losing it = losing funds; leaking it = losing funds. There is no reset.
JSON.stringify is only deterministic if key order is stable — sort keys before hashing transactions or two nodes will disagree.
PoW is asymmetric: finding a valid nonce is brute-force expensive, but any node verifies it with a single hash.
Changing any field in a block changes its hash, which breaks prevHash of the next block — that cascade is what makes tampering detectable.
Bitcoin uses double SHA-256 and the secp256k1 curve specifically — match those if you're mimicking real Bitcoin behavior.
A 51% attack lets you reorder/reverse recent txs, but not steal coins you have no private key for or mint beyond the reward schedule.

items.label: Function declaration (hoisted)
items.note: Hoisted to top of scope — callable before its line.

items.label: Function expression (not hoisted)
items.note: The const binding is TDZ; calling before definition throws.

items.label: Arrow function
items.note: No own `this`/`arguments`; inherits from enclosing scope. Can't be a constructor.

items.label: Functions are first-class values
items.note: Functions are objects — assign, store in arrays/objects, return them.

items.label: Sync = runs top-to-bottom, one line blocks the next
items.note: Single-threaded: while a sync call runs, nothing else executes.

items.label: Blocking the main thread (bad)
items.note: Busy-wait blocks the event loop; UI/other requests stall.

items.label: Synchronous file read (blocks)
items.note: `*Sync` APIs block the whole process — fine for CLI startup, bad in servers.

items.label: I/O-bound task
items.code:
// waiting on disk / network / DB
fs.readFile(...)
fetch('https://api...')
db.query('SELECT ...')
items.note: Bottleneck is external wait, not the CPU — async wins here.

items.label: CPU-bound task
items.note: Bottleneck is the processor; async doesn't help — offload to Worker threads / child processes.

items.label: Why async matters for I/O
items.note: During I/O the thread would otherwise sit idle; async lets it serve other work while the OS handles the wait.

items.label: Handling CPU-bound work off the main thread
items.code:
const { Worker } = require('worker_threads');
new Worker('./crunch.js');
items.note: Callbacks/promises don't parallelize CPU work — you need real threads/processes.

sections.title: I/O in the real world (Node fs)

items.label: Async read with callback (non-blocking)
items.note: Node convention: callback signature is (err, result) — error first.

items.label: Async write
Sync variant for comparison
items.note: Sync uses try/catch; async passes err into the callback.

items.label: setTimeout — the canonical async primitive
items.code:
setTimeout(() => console.log('later'), 1000);
console.log('now'); // 'now' then 'later'
items.note: Delay is a minimum, not a guarantee — callback runs after the stack clears AND the timer fires.

items.label: Pass a function into another function
items.note: A callback is just a function passed as an argument to be invoked later.

items.label: Named vs inline callback
items.note: Pass the reference `log`, not `log()` — the latter calls it immediately.

items.label: Callback that takes its own args
Deferred execution via callback
items.note: Result isn't returned — it's delivered later through the callback.

items.label: Sequencing dependent async ops (nesting)
items.note: Nested callbacks = 'pyramid of doom'; promises/async-await flatten this.

items.label: Error-first pattern (Node standard)
items.note: Always check `err` first before touching the result.

items.label: You cannot `return` an async result
items.note: The function returns before the callback runs. Use the callback (or a promise) to get the value out.

items.label: The moving parts
items.note: Call Stack (runs sync code) + Web/Node APIs (handle timers, I/O off-thread) + Callback/Task Queue + Event Loop (moves queued callbacks onto the stack when it's empty).

items.label: How a callback gets executed
items.note: Even with 0ms, B waits until the stack is empty — the loop drains sync code first.

items.label: Microtasks run before macrotasks
items.note: Promise `.then`/`await` continuations (microtasks) drain fully before the next timer/I/O callback.

items.label: Single-threaded, non-blocking
items.note: One call stack. Concurrency comes from offloading waits to the runtime (libuv/browser), not from parallel JS execution.

tips: Node callbacks are error-first: `(err, result) => {}` — handle `err` before using `result`.
Pass a function reference (`cb`) not a call (`cb()`); the latter executes it right away and passes the return value.
`setTimeout(fn, 0)` does NOT run immediately — it defers `fn` until the current stack clears.
Async doesn't speed up CPU-bound work; it only helps you not block during I/O waits. Use Worker threads for heavy compute.
You can't `return` a value produced inside an async callback — the outer function has already returned. Deliver it via the callback.
Deeply nested callbacks (callback hell) are hard to read and error-handle — reach for Promises / async-await for sequential async.
Microtasks (Promises) always drain before the next macrotask (timers, I/O), regardless of delay.
Prefer async `fs.readFile` over `fs.readFileSync` in servers — the Sync version blocks every other request.

items.label: Avoid the spikes — consistency > cramming
items.note: Don't binge 8h then vanish for a week. 1-2 focused hours daily beats weekend spikes; the dropout risk is the gap, not the pace.

items.label: "What would I do if I was you?" — build, don't just watch
items.note: Passive video-watching ≈ 0 retention. Pause, type every snippet yourself, then rebuild it from scratch without the video.

items.label: Select your level — Beginner vs Advanced/DevOps track
items.note: Beginner: start HTML/CSS/JS fundamentals. If you already know JS + React, skip ahead and start at the backend/DevOps level. Don't sit through material you know.

items.label: Good things to hash out (before Day 1)
items.code:
# accounts to create upfront
- GitHub (auth, portfolio, deploys)
- Vercel / Netlify (free frontend hosting)
- Discord (cohort community + doubt support)
items.note: Get the boring setup out of the way so nothing blocks you mid-lesson.

items.label: Take notes + ship projects publicly
items.note: Push every exercise to GitHub. A green contribution graph + deployed links is your interview proof.

Ctrl+Shift+P  command palette
Alt+Click     multi-cursor
Ctrl+/        toggle comment

items.code:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">

</head>
<body>
  <!-- content -->
  <script src="script.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
items.note: viewport meta is mandatory for responsive/mobile. Put <script> at end of body (or use defer).

items.label: Semantic layout elements
items.code:
<header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>,
<article>, <aside>, <footer>
items.note: Use these over generic <div> — better a11y + SEO. One <main> per page.

items.label: Headings & text
items.code: ..
items.note: Exactly one <h1> per page; don't skip heading levels for styling.

items.label: Links & images
items.code: Link
items.note: alt is required for a11y. target=_blank needs rel=noopener for security.

items.label: Lists
items.code:
• item
  1. step

items.label: Form skeleton
items.code:
<form action="/submit" method="POST">
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>
  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>
items.note: name is the key sent to the server. Match label for= to input id= for a11y + click-to-focus.

items.label: Input types
items.code:
type="text | email | password | number"
type="checkbox | radio | date | file"
<textarea></textarea>
<select><option value="1">One</option></select>
items.note: Radios sharing the same name= act as one group.

items.label: Validation attributes
items.code:
required  minlength="3"  maxlength="20"
min="0"  max="100"  pattern="[0-9]+"
placeholder="hint"

items.label: Attach CSS
Selectors
items.code:
*            /* all */
tag          /* p, div */
.class       /* .btn */
#id          /* #nav */
.a .b        /* descendant */
.a > .b      /* direct child */
a:hover      /* pseudo-class */
input:focus

items.label: Specificity (who wins)
items.code: inline > #id > .class > tag > *
items.note: !important overrides all — avoid it; it's a debugging trap. More specific selector wins ties by source order (last one applies).

items.label: Box model — always reset
items.note: border-box makes width include padding+border — sane sizing. Default content-box adds them on top.

items.label: Units
items.code:
px    /* fixed */
rem   /* relative to root font-size (16px) */
em    /* relative to parent font-size */
%     /* relative to parent */
vw/vh /* viewport width/height */
items.note: Use rem for font-size/spacing so it scales; px for borders.

items.label: Enable flex on the container
Align along main + cross axis
items.note: justify-content follows flex-direction. Swap to column and the axes flip.

items.label: Common centering (both axes)
items.note: The classic "center a div" — 3 lines.

items.label: Item growth & wrapping
items.code:
.item { flex: 1; }        /* grow to fill equally */
.container { flex-wrap: wrap; }

items.label: Grid (2D layout)
items.note: 1fr = 1 fraction of free space. Use grid for 2D (rows+cols), flex for 1D.

items.label: Auto-responsive grid (no media query)
items.code:
grid-template-columns:
  repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr));
items.note: Cards auto-reflow: as many 200px+ columns as fit.

items.label: position
items.code:
position: relative;  /* anchor for children */
position: absolute;  /* vs nearest positioned ancestor */
position: fixed;     /* vs viewport (sticky navbar) */
position: sticky; top: 0;
items.note: absolute needs a relative ancestor or it anchors to <body>.

items.label: Media query (mobile-friendly)
items.note: Prefer mobile-first with min-width. Requires the viewport meta tag in HTML.

items.label: Colors & background

tips: Consistency beats intensity: short daily sessions avoid the 'spike then quit' trap that kills most cohort learners.
Type every snippet by hand and rebuild from scratch — watching lessons without coding retains almost nothing.
Skip to your real level. If you know HTML/CSS/JS already, don't re-watch fundamentals; start where it's new.
Always add box-sizing: border-box on * and reset margin/padding — the #1 source of unexpected layout width.
Set the <meta name="viewport"> tag or media queries and responsive layouts silently do nothing on mobile.
Flexbox = one dimension (row OR column), Grid = two dimensions (rows AND cols). Pick by the problem, not habit.
Never fight specificity with !important — write a more specific selector instead; !important compounds into unmaintainable CSS.
Use Live Server (auto-reload) + Prettier format-on-save from day one so you focus on code, not manual refresh/formatting.

items.label: Check Node install
items.code:
node -v
npm -v
items.note: JS runs in the browser and in Node.js.

items.label: Run a script file
items.code: node app.js

items.label: Interactive REPL
items.code: node
items.note: Type expressions, get instant results. Ctrl+C twice to exit.

items.label: Browser console
items.code:
// F12 -> Console tab
console.log('hello');
items.note: Fastest playground; no setup.

items.label: Init a project
items.code: npm init -y
items.note: Creates package.json.

items.label: Log output
Declare (prefer const, then let)
items.code:
const PI = 3.14;   // cannot reassign
let count = 0;     // reassignable
var old = 1;       // avoid: function-scoped
items.note: const/let are block-scoped; var is function-scoped + hoisted.

items.label: 7 primitive types
items.code:
"str"        // string
42, 3.14     // number (one type for int+float)
true/false   // boolean
undefined    // declared, no value
null         // intentional empty
10n          // bigint
Symbol('id') // symbol
items.note: typeof null === 'object' (historic bug).

items.label: Check type at runtime
items.code:
typeof "hi"     // 'string'
typeof 42       // 'number'
typeof undefined// 'undefined'
typeof [] // 'object' — use Array.isArray([])

items.label: Template literals
items.code:
const name = 'Sam';
`Hi ${name}, 1+1=${1+1}`;
items.note: Backticks; supports interpolation + multi-line.

items.label: String basics
items.code:
'abc'.length        // 3
'abc'.toUpperCase() // 'ABC'
'a,b'.split(',')    // ['a','b']
'  x '.trim()       // 'x'

items.label: Single-threaded
items.code: // one call stack, one thing at a time
items.note: Async work is offloaded, results queued via the event loop.

items.label: Synchronous by default
items.note: Code runs top-to-bottom, line-by-line unless you use callbacks/promises/async.

items.label: Dynamically typed
items.code:
let x = 5;   // number
x = 'five';  // now string — legal
items.note: Types attach to values, not variables. No compile-time type checks.

items.label: Interpreted + JIT compiled
items.note: No separate build step; engines (V8) JIT-compile hot paths for speed.

items.label: Loosely typed / coercion
items.code:
'5' + 1   // '51'  (number -> string)
'5' - 1   // 4     (string -> number)
true + 1  // 2
items.note: + favors string concat; other math operators coerce to number.

items.label: Falsy values (memorize)
items.code: false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN
items.note: Everything else is truthy — including '0', [], {}.

items.label: Strict vs loose equality
items.code:
0 == '0'   // true  (coerces)
0 === '0'  // false (type + value)
items.note: Always use === / !== to avoid surprises.

items.label: Logical + short-circuit
items.code:
a && b    // b if a truthy, else a
a || b    // a if truthy, else b
!a        // negate

items.label: Nullish coalescing / optional chaining
items.code:
user?.name          // undefined if user null
port ?? 3000        // 3000 only if port null/undefined
items.note: ?? differs from || : keeps 0 and ''.

items.label: Ternary
items.code: const label = age >= 18 ? 'adult' : 'minor';

items.label: Statements end with ;
items.note: ASI auto-inserts semicolons, but be explicit to avoid edge-case bugs.

items.label: if / else if / else
switch
items.note: break each case or it falls through (used above intentionally).

items.label: Classic for
while / do-while
items.code:
while (cond) { }
do { } while (cond);

items.label: for...of (values)
items.code: for (const item of [10,20,30]) { }
items.note: Use for arrays/strings/iterables.

items.label: for...in (keys)
items.code: for (const key in {a:1,b:2}) { }
items.note: Iterates object keys — avoid on arrays.

items.label: Declaration (hoisted)
Expression
items.code: const add = function(a, b) { return a + b; };

items.label: Arrow function
items.code:
const add = (a, b) => a + b;
const sq = x => x * x;   // 1 arg, implicit return
items.note: No own `this` — inherits from enclosing scope.

items.label: Default parameters
items.code: function greet(name = 'guest') { }

items.label: Rest params + spread
items.code:
function sum(...nums) { return nums.length; }
Math.max(...[1,2,3]);   // spread into args

items.label: Callbacks
items.code: [1,2,3].forEach(n => console.log(n));
items.note: A function passed as an argument to be called later.

items.label: Create & access
items.code:
const a = [1, 2, 3];
a[0]        // 1
a.length    // 3
a.at(-1)    // 3 (last)

items.label: Add / remove (mutating)
items.code:
a.push(4);    // add end
a.pop();      // remove end
a.unshift(0); // add front
a.shift();    // remove front
a.splice(1,2);// remove 2 from idx 1

items.label: map — transform
items.note: Returns a NEW array; same length.

items.label: Copy / merge with spread
items.code:
const copy = [...a];
const all  = [...a, ...b];
items.note: Arrays are reference types — assignment copies the reference, not the data.

items.label: Create & access
Add / update / delete
Destructuring
items.code:
const { name, age = 0 } = user;
const [first, ...rest] = [1,2,3];
items.note: Rename: const { name: n } = user;

items.label: Spread / merge objects
items.code: const patched = { ...user, age: 40 };
items.note: Shallow copy; later keys win.

items.label: Iterate object
items.code:
Object.keys(user)    // ['name','age']
Object.values(user)  // ['Sam', 30]
Object.entries(user) // [['name','Sam'],...]

items.label: Nested / methods
items.note: Method shorthand; `this` = the object when called as obj.greet().

items.label: JSON convert
items.code:
JSON.stringify(user)      // obj -> string
JSON.parse('{"a":1}')     // string -> obj
items.note: stringify drops undefined/functions.

tips: Use === / !== always; == coerces types and hides bugs (0 == '' is false but 0 == '0' is true).
Objects and arrays are reference types: `const b = a` copies the pointer, so mutating b changes a. Copy with {...a} / [...a].
const prevents reassignment, NOT mutation — you can still push to a const array or edit a const object's fields.
Prefer const by default; reach for let only when you must reassign; avoid var (function-scoped + hoisted).
Memorize the 6 falsy values: false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN — everything else is truthy (even [] and {}).
map/filter/reduce return new arrays and don't mutate; push/pop/splice/sort mutate in place.
Arrow functions have no own `this`/`arguments` — don't use them as object methods that need `this`.
typeof null is 'object' and typeof [] is 'object' — use Array.isArray() to detect arrays.

items.label: Define a class + constructor
items.note: Methods live on the prototype (shared); fields set in constructor are per-instance. `new` is mandatory.

items.label: static members & getters/setters
items.note: `static` = called on the class itself. Getters/setters are accessed like properties (no parens).

items.label: Inheritance: extends + super
items.note: In a subclass constructor, `super(...)` must run before any `this` access or it throws ReferenceError.

items.label: Private fields (#)
items.note: `#name` is truly private (enforced by engine), unlike the `_convention`.

items.label: instanceof / method check
items.code:
p instanceof Person;   // true
new Dog("Rex") instanceof Animal;  // true (walks prototype chain)

items.label: Create a Promise (executor runs immediately)
items.note: 3 states: pending → fulfilled (resolve) | rejected (reject). Settles once; later resolve/reject calls are ignored.

items.label: Consume with .then/.catch/.finally
items.note: `.then` returns a NEW promise → chainable. Return a value to pass down; return a promise to wait for it.

items.label: Chaining (flatten async steps)
items.code:
fetch(url)
  .then(res => res.json())   // returned promise is awaited
  .then(data => data.id)
  .then(id => console.log(id))
  .catch(err => /* one catch handles whole chain */);
items.note: A single trailing .catch catches rejections from any prior step.

items.label: Instant promises
items.code:
Promise.resolve(1);           // already-fulfilled
Promise.reject(new Error("x")); // already-rejected

items.label: Promise.all — wait for all, fail fast
items.code: const [a, b] = await Promise.all([fetchA(), fetchB()]);
items.note: Rejects immediately if ANY input rejects; resolves to array of values in input order.

items.label: Promise.allSettled — never rejects
items.code:
const results = await Promise.allSettled([p1, p2]);
// [{status:'fulfilled',value}, {status:'rejected',reason}]
items.note: Use when you want every outcome regardless of individual failures.

items.label: Promise.race — first to settle wins
items.code: await Promise.race([fetch(url), timeout(5000)]);
items.note: Settles (fulfill OR reject) as soon as the first input settles. Classic for timeouts.

items.label: Promise.any — first fulfilled wins
items.code: await Promise.any([mirror1(), mirror2()]);
items.note: Ignores rejections; rejects with AggregateError only if ALL reject.

items.label: async function always returns a Promise
items.note: A thrown error inside becomes a rejected promise. `return x` == `Promise.resolve(x)`.

items.label: await unwraps a promise
items.note: `await` only valid inside `async` fns (or top-level in ES modules). Awaiting a non-promise just returns the value.

items.label: try/catch = your .catch
Arrow & method forms
items.code:
const f = async (x) => x * 2;
const obj = { async load() { await ...; } };

items.label: Run in parallel, not serial
items.note: Awaiting one-by-one when calls are independent needlessly serializes them.

items.label: Callback hell (the problem)
items.note: Rightward drift + repeated error checks + no composability. Hard to read & maintain.

items.label: Same logic with async/await (flat)
items.note: Linear, readable, single try/catch handles all errors.

items.label: Promisify a callback API
items.note: Node: `import { promisify } from 'util'` wraps err-first callbacks automatically.

sections.title: Defining your own async fn & error-first callbacks vs reject

items.label: Write a promise-returning helper (delay)
items.note: No built-in sleep in JS — this is the idiom.

items.label: Error-first callback convention (Node classic)
items.note: Convention: callback(err, result). Success => err is null/undefined. Caller must check err every time.

items.label: Promise reject equivalent
items.note: reject(err) ≈ passing err to error-first callback; resolve(v) ≈ callback(null, v). Errors propagate up automatically instead of manual re-passing.

items.label: Reject with Error objects, not strings
items.code:
reject(new Error("not found"));   // ✅ stack trace
// reject("not found")            // ❌ no stack, harder to debug

items.label: Forgetting await = you get the Promise, not the value
items.code:
const data = getUser();        // Promise { <pending> }
const data = await getUser();  // actual value

items.label: await inside .map returns array of promises
items.code: const users = await Promise.all(ids.map(id => getUser(id)));
items.note: `arr.map(async ...)` yields Promise[]; wrap in Promise.all. `forEach` does NOT await — avoid it.

items.label: Sequential loop (when order/backpressure matters)
items.note: `for...of` with await runs serially; use when each step depends on the last.

items.label: this binding in class methods passed as callbacks
items.code:
btn.onclick = this.handle.bind(this);
// or arrow class field: handle = () => { this.x }
items.note: Passing `obj.method` loses `this`. Bind or use an arrow class field.

tips: async function ALWAYS returns a Promise; `throw` inside becomes a rejection, `return x` becomes a resolved value.
One trailing `.catch()` (or a single try/catch) handles rejections from the whole chain — don't wrap every step.
Independent awaits run serially and waste time; batch them with Promise.all for concurrency.
A Promise settles exactly once — extra resolve/reject calls are silently ignored.
Always reject/throw `new Error(...)`, never a raw string, so you keep a stack trace.
`await` is only legal inside `async` functions (or top-level of an ES module) — otherwise SyntaxError.
`Array.forEach(async ...)` does NOT wait; use `for...of` with await (serial) or `map` + `Promise.all` (parallel).
In a subclass, call `super(...)` before touching `this`, and remember arrow functions don't get their own `this`.

items.label: Kafka = distributed, append-only commit log for event streaming
items.note: Pull-based, durable, replayable. NOT a queue that deletes on read — messages persist per retention.

items.label: Broker
items.note: One Kafka server. A cluster = many brokers sharing partitions.

items.label: Topic
items.note: Named stream/category of messages (e.g. 'orders').

items.label: Partition
items.note: Ordered, immutable log within a topic. Unit of parallelism. Ordering guaranteed ONLY within a partition, never across a topic.

items.label: Offset
items.note: Sequential id of a message inside a partition. Consumers track 'where they are' by committing offsets.

items.label: Producer / Consumer
items.note: Producers write; consumers read. Decoupled — neither knows the other.

items.label: Consumer group
items.note: Consumers sharing a groupId that SPLIT a topic's partitions among themselves.

items.label: Replication factor / retention
items.note: RF = copies of a partition across brokers. Retention = keep by time/size (log.retention.hours), not by consumption.

items.label: KRaft vs ZooKeeper
items.note: Modern Kafka runs metadata in KRaft mode (no separate ZooKeeper needed).

items.label: Run a broker (KRaft, no ZooKeeper)
items.code: docker run -p 9092:9092 apache/kafka:3.7.1

items.label: Shell into the container
items.code:
docker ps                       # get container id
docker exec -it <id> /bin/bash
cd /opt/kafka/bin               # all *.sh tools live here

items.label: Create a topic with partitions
items.code:
./kafka-topics.sh --create --topic quickstart \
  --partitions 3 --replication-factor 1 \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

items.label: List / describe topics
items.code:
./kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
./kafka-topics.sh --describe --topic quickstart --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
items.note: --describe shows leader/replicas per partition.

items.label: Console producer / consumer (quick sanity check)
items.code:
./kafka-console-producer.sh --topic quickstart --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
./kafka-console-consumer.sh --topic quickstart --from-beginning --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

items.label: Inspect a consumer group (offsets + LAG)
items.code:
./kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group group-1 \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
items.note: CURRENT-OFFSET vs LOG-END-OFFSET gives LAG per partition — key debugging tool.

items.label: Install
items.code: npm install kafkajs

items.label: Create client (shared across producer/consumer/admin)
Admin: create topic with N partitions from code
items.note: Idempotent — returns false if the topic already exists.

items.label: Graceful shutdown
items.note: Disconnect triggers a clean group rebalance instead of a session timeout.

items.label: Minimal producer
Send WITH a key (controls routing)
items.note: Same key → same partition → per-key ordering preserved. Use key = entity id (userId, orderId).

items.label: Batch multiple messages / multiple topics
Pin to an explicit partition
items.code: messages: [{ value: 'x', partition: 0 }]
items.note: Overrides the partitioner — bypasses key hashing entirely.

items.label: Minimal consumer
items.note: fromBeginning only applies the FIRST time a group has no committed offset; ignored afterward.

items.label: Manual offset commit (at-least-once control)
items.note: Commit offset+1 = the NEXT offset to read. Default autoCommit can lose/replay on crash.

items.label: Batch processing
Concurrency across partitions
items.code: await consumer.run({ partitionsConsumedConcurrently: 3, eachMessage });
items.note: Runs eachMessage in parallel across partitions; still serial within one partition.

items.label: Default routing: key present
items.note: partition = hash(key) % numPartitions → deterministic, sticky per key.

items.label: Default routing: no key
items.note: Round-robin / sticky across partitions → good load spread, NO ordering guarantee.

items.label: Custom partitioner
Match Java hashing (KafkaJS v2 gotcha)
items.code:
const { Partitioners } = require('kafkajs');
kafka.producer({ createPartitioner: Partitioners.LegacyPartitioner });
items.note: KafkaJS v2 changed the default partitioner and warns. Use LegacyPartitioner to keep old key→partition mapping / match other clients.

items.label: Choosing a key
items.note: Pick a key with even cardinality (userId good; country bad → hot partition). Adding partitions later re-hashes keys and breaks existing per-key ordering.

sections.title: Consumers vs partitions — the three cases

items.label: Case 1: consumers < partitions
items.note: Some consumers own multiple partitions. Works, but uneven load. E.g. 3 partitions, 2 consumers → 2+1 split.

items.label: Case 2: consumers == partitions
items.note: Ideal 1:1 mapping — max parallelism, each consumer owns exactly one partition.

items.label: Case 3: consumers > partitions
items.note: Extra consumers sit IDLE. Max useful consumers in a group = partition count. Add partitions to scale further.

items.label: Multiple consumer groups = broadcast (pub/sub)
items.code:
kafka.consumer({ groupId: 'analytics' });
kafka.consumer({ groupId: 'billing' });
items.note: Each group tracks its own offsets, so each group receives a FULL copy of every message. Within a group = load-balanced; across groups = fan-out.

tips: Ordering is per-partition only — never across a topic. Need global order? Use 1 partition (kills parallelism) or a shared key.
Same key → same partition → same consumer. Route related events (same userId) with a key to keep them ordered and co-located.
A consumer group can't use more consumers than partitions — extras stay idle. Provision partitions for your peak consumer count up front.
Kafka does not delete on read; it's retention-based. Replay by resetting offsets: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --reset-offsets --to-earliest --execute.
KafkaJS v2 changed the default partitioner (emits a warning). Set Partitioners.LegacyPartitioner for consistent hashing with Java/other producers.
Commit offset = last processed + 1. Committing message.offset (not +1) causes the last message to be reprocessed on restart.
Rebalancing pauses ALL consumption in the group. Keep eachMessage fast and call heartbeat() in long loops, or bump sessionTimeout, to avoid being kicked.
Adding partitions to a live topic breaks key→partition stability (keys re-hash) — plan partition count ahead; you can't cleanly decrease it.

items.label: Bank = centralized ledger
items.note: One trusted party (bank/DB) holds balances; they can freeze, reverse, or edit rows. Auth = password + their say-so.

items.label: Blockchain = decentralized ledger
items.note: State replicated across nodes; no central admin. 'Ownership' = control of a private key. No password reset — lose the key, lose the funds.

items.label: Auth model shift
items.note: Banks: prove identity to a server. Blockchain: prove key ownership by signing — verified by anyone, no login.

items.label: Address = public identity
items.note: Derived from the public key (Solana: base58 of pubkey; Ethereum: last 20 bytes of keccak256(pubkey)). Safe to share, like an account number.

items.label: Units
items.code:
1 byte = 8 bits
1 bit  -> 0 or 1
1 byte -> 0..255  (2^8 values)
hex nibble = 4 bits; 1 byte = 2 hex chars
items.note: A 256-bit key = 32 bytes = 64 hex chars.

items.label: Node Buffer / Uint8Array is the raw byte container
items.code: const buf = Buffer.from("hello", "utf8"); // <Buffer 68 65 6c 6c 6f>\nbuf.length; // 5 bytes\nnew Uint8Array([104,101,108,108,111]);
items.note: Crypto libs speak bytes (Uint8Array), not strings. Convert explicitly.

items.label: utf8 <-> hex
items.code: Buffer.from("Hi").toString("hex");          // '4869'\nBuffer.from("4869", "hex").toString("utf8"); // 'Hi'

items.label: base64 (data transport, +33% size)
items.code: Buffer.from("Hi").toString("base64");        // 'SGk='\nBuffer.from("SGk=", "base64").toString();

items.label: base58 (Solana/Bitcoin addresses)
items.code: import bs58 from "bs58";\nconst s = bs58.encode(bytes);   // Uint8Array -> string\nconst b = bs58.decode(s);       // string -> Uint8Array
items.note: base58 drops ambiguous chars (0/O, I/l) — no line-break issues. Not the same alphabet as base64.

items.label: Hashing = one-way fingerprint
items.note: Deterministic, fixed-length, irreversible. Same input -> same hash. Used for integrity, IDs, block/tx hashes. NOT for hiding recoverable data.

items.label: SHA-256 (32-byte digest)
items.code: import { createHash } from "crypto";\ncreateHash("sha256").update("data").digest("hex");

items.label: keccak-256 (Ethereum's hash)
items.code: import { keccak256, toUtf8Bytes } from "ethers";\nkeccak256(toUtf8Bytes("hello")); // '0x1c8a...'
items.note: Ethereum uses keccak256, NOT the NIST SHA3-256 — different padding.

items.label: Encryption = two-way, reversible with a key
items.note: Ciphertext -> plaintext given the key. Symmetric = same key both ways; Asymmetric = key pair.

items.label: Symmetric encrypt (AES-256-GCM)
items.note: Never reuse an (key, iv) pair. GCM's authTag detects tampering.

items.label: Core idea
items.note: Private key = secret (sign/decrypt). Public key = shareable (verify/encrypt). Deriving pub from priv is easy; reverse is infeasible.

items.label: Curves by chain
items.note: Solana -> ed25519. Ethereum & Bitcoin -> secp256k1. Match the curve to the chain or the address won't be valid.

items.label: Node native ed25519 keypair
items.code: import { generateKeyPairSync } from "crypto";\nconst { publicKey, privateKey } =\n  generateKeyPairSync("ed25519");

items.label: @noble/ed25519 (raw bytes)
items.code: import * as ed from "@noble/ed25519";\nconst priv = ed.utils.randomPrivateKey();        // 32 bytes\nconst pub  = await ed.getPublicKeyAsync(priv);   // 32 bytes
items.note: Use the *Async fns out of the box; sync fns need ed.hashes.sha512 wired up.

items.label: Solana Keypair
items.note: secretKey is 64 bytes (seed 32 + pubkey 32). Store it, never expose it.

items.label: Restore from secret
items.code: Keypair.fromSecretKey(Uint8Array.from(saved64));\nKeypair.fromSeed(seed32); // seed-only restore

items.label: Sign & verify (proves key ownership)
items.code: const msg = new TextEncoder().encode("gm");\nconst sig = await ed.signAsync(msg, priv);\nconst ok  = await ed.verifyAsync(sig, msg, pub); // true
items.note: Signature proves the holder of priv approved msg — without revealing priv.

items.label: Ethereum sign (ethers v6)
items.code: import { Wallet } from "ethers";\nconst w = Wallet.createRandom();\nconst sig = await w.signMessage("gm");

items.label: Tx flow on chain
items.note: 1) build tx (from, to, amount, recentBlockhash/nonce) 2) sign with priv 3) broadcast 4) validators verify sig against from-address 5) tx included in a block.

items.label: Solana transfer tx
items.note: Amounts are integers in base units (lamports/wei) — never floats.

items.label: Why HD
items.note: One mnemonic -> deterministic tree of infinite keypairs. Back up 12/24 words once; regenerate every account from it.

items.label: Mnemonic (BIP-39)
items.code: import * as bip39 from "bip39";\nconst mnemonic = bip39.generateMnemonic();   // 12 words (128-bit)\nbip39.validateMnemonic(mnemonic);

items.label: Mnemonic -> seed
items.code: const seed = bip39.mnemonicToSeedSync(mnemonic); // 64-byte Buffer
items.note: Optional passphrase is a 25th word: mnemonicToSeedSync(m, pass).

items.label: Derivation path (BIP-44)
items.note: ' = hardened index. Solana wallets use all-hardened path (no change/index level).

items.label: Derive Solana keypair #i
Derive Ethereum wallet (ethers)
Solana wallet from scratch
Ethereum wallet from scratch
items.code: import { Wallet } from "ethers";\nconst w = Wallet.createRandom();\nw.address; w.privateKey; w.mnemonic.phrase;

items.label: Multi-account loop
items.note: Bump the account index to get the next address from the same seed.

items.label: Install deps
items.code: npm i @solana/web3.js ed25519-hd-key bip39 bs58 ethers tweetnacl

tips: Private key = money. Anyone with it can drain funds instantly; there's no reset, no support desk. Never log, commit, or paste it.
Match curve to chain: Solana=ed25519, Ethereum/Bitcoin=secp256k1. A key from the wrong curve produces an invalid/unusable address.
Solana secretKey is 64 bytes (32-byte seed + 32-byte pubkey), not 32. Use Keypair.fromSecretKey for the 64-byte blob, fromSeed for the 32-byte seed.
Hashing is one-way — don't reach for it to 'hide' recoverable data; use encryption. Encryption is reversible with a key; hashing never is.
Amounts are integer base units: lamports (1 SOL = 1e9) and wei (1 ETH = 1e18). Floats cause rounding bugs and rejected txns.
The mnemonic IS the wallet — same 12/24 words regenerate every key. Order matters; a passphrase acts as a hidden 25th word that changes the whole tree.
' in a derivation path means hardened. Solana uses an all-hardened path (m/44'/501'/i'/0'); Ethereum uses m/44'/60'/0'/0/i — copy them exactly.
Ethereum uses keccak256, not SHA3-256 (different padding) — using the wrong one yields wrong hashes/addresses. Address = last 20 bytes of keccak256(pubkey).

items.label: Exchange = order matching engine that pairs buyers with sellers of an asset at an agreed price
items.note: It never trades; it only matches counterparties and settles balances.

items.label: Market / trading pair — always base_quote
items.code:
SOL_USDC  ->  base = SOL (what you buy/sell), quote = USDC (what you price it in)
price 120.5 means 1 SOL = 120.5 USDC
items.note: Buy = spend quote to get base. Sell = give base to get quote.

items.label: Order book — resting bids (buys) and asks (sells)
items.code:
asks (sell)  121.0 x 1.5   <- lowest ask = best sell
             120.8 x 4.0
----------- spread -----------
bids (buy)   120.5 x 3.2   <- highest bid = best buy
             120.4 x 10
items.note: Bids sorted price DESC, asks price ASC. Best prices sit at the top.

items.label: Spread & mid price
items.code:
spread   = bestAsk - bestBid          // 121.0 - 120.5 = 0.5
midPrice = (bestAsk + bestBid) / 2

items.label: Depth — cumulative quantity available at each price level
items.note: Deep book = lots of resting liquidity = small price impact per trade.

items.label: Bid
items.note: Highest price a buyer will pay.

items.label: Ask / Offer
items.note: Lowest price a seller will accept.

items.label: Maker
items.note: Order that rests on the book and adds liquidity (usually a limit order that doesn't cross). Lower fees.

items.label: Taker
items.note: Order that matches an existing resting order and removes liquidity. Higher fees.

items.label: Fill
items.note: A (partial or full) execution of an order against a counterparty at a price.

items.label: Slippage
items.note: Diff between expected price and actual avg fill price when eating through the book.

items.label: Ticker
items.note: 24h rolling stats for a market: last price, high, low, volume, % change.

items.label: Kline / Candlestick
items.note: OHLCV bucketed by interval (1m, 1h, 1d). Open/High/Low/Close/Volume.

items.label: Liquidity
items.note: How much can be traded without moving price. Provided by makers.

items.label: Limit — execute at a set price or better; rests on book if it doesn't cross
items.code: { side: "buy", price: "120.5", quantity: "2", type: "limit" }
items.note: Buy limit fills at <= price; sell limit fills at >= price. Maker if it rests.

items.label: Market — fill immediately at best available price(s), no price guarantee
items.code: { side: "buy", quantity: "2", type: "market" }  // no price field
items.note: Always a taker. Walks the book -> slippage. Lock funds by worst-case, not price.

items.label: IOC (Immediate-Or-Cancel) — fill whatever it can now, cancel the remainder
items.code: { side: "buy", price: "120.5", quantity: "5", type: "ioc" }
items.note: Never rests on the book. Partial fills allowed.

items.label: Stop / Stop-limit — dormant until trigger price hit, then becomes market/limit
items.note: Not held in the main book; kept in a trigger set and injected when price crosses.

sections.title: Order Lifecycle (how an order is made)

items.label: End-to-end flow
items.code:
1. Client -> POST /api/v1/order (REST)
2. API validates, LPUSH job to Redis queue with a unique clientId
3. Engine RPOP job -> checkAndLockFunds -> match against book
4. Engine creates fills, updates balances, adjusts book
5. Engine PUBLISH depth/trade/ticker to pubsub channels
6. Engine PUBLISH result to channel `clientId`
7. API (subscribed to clientId) resolves promise -> HTTP response
8. DB processor consumes pubsub -> persists to TimescaleDB

items.label: Lock funds before matching
items.note: Reject early on insufficient balance so the engine never produces an invalid state.

items.label: Matching (price-time priority)
items.note: Same price -> FIFO (earliest order first). Remove fully-filled resting orders.

items.label: Fill shape
items.code:
interface Fill { price: string; qty: number; tradeId: number;
                 otherUserId: string; markerOrderId: string; }

items.label: Service split — API is stateless, Engine is the single source of truth
items.code:
Frontend(Next.js)
   |  REST + WS
   v
API Server (Express, stateless, N replicas)
   |  LPUSH "messages"          ^ SUBSCRIBE clientId
   v                            |
[ Redis ]  --queue--> Engine (single instance, in-mem order book)
   ^  PUBLISH depth@ / trade@ / ticker@   |
   |                                      v
WS Server (fan-out)              DB Processor -> TimescaleDB
items.note: Engine MUST be a single process so the in-memory book stays consistent.

items.label: Redis as queue (durable, ordered, backpressure)
Redis pub/sub for broadcast (trades, depth deltas, ticker, RPC replies)
items.code:
redis.publish(`depth@${market}`, JSON.stringify(depthDelta));
redis.publish(clientId, JSON.stringify(orderResult)); // request/response over pubsub

items.label: Snapshot & recovery
items.code:
// engine periodically flushes state
fs.writeFileSync("./snapshot.json", JSON.stringify({ orderbooks, balances }));
items.note: On boot, load snapshot then replay any queued messages. Keeps restarts safe.

items.label: Create order
items.code:
POST /api/v1/order
{ market, price, quantity, side, userId }

items.label: Cancel order
items.code:
DELETE /api/v1/order
{ orderId, market }

items.label: Open orders for a user
items.code: GET /api/v1/order/open?userId=1&market=SOL_USDC

items.label: Order book depth
items.code:
GET /api/v1/depth?symbol=SOL_USDC
-> { bids: [[price,qty]...], asks: [[price,qty]...] }

items.label: Recent trades
items.code: GET /api/v1/trades?symbol=SOL_USDC

items.label: Klines (candles) — from TimescaleDB continuous aggregate
items.code: GET /api/v1/klines?symbol=SOL_USDC&interval=1m&startTime=..&endTime=..

items.label: Tickers (24h stats)
items.code: GET /api/v1/tickers

items.label: Klines materialized view (TimescaleDB)
items.code:
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW klines_1m
WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS
SELECT time_bucket('1 minute', time) AS bucket,
       first(price,time) AS open, max(price) AS high,
       min(price)  AS low,        last(price,time) AS close,
       sum(quantity) AS volume
FROM trades GROUP BY bucket;
items.note: Continuous aggregate auto-refreshes; don't compute OHLCV per-request in the API.

items.label: Subscribe / unsubscribe protocol
depth@ stream — incremental book deltas
items.note: Send deltas, not full book, every tick. qty "0" means delete that price level.

items.label: trade@ stream — each execution
items.code: { e:"trade", s:"SOL_USDC", p:"120.5", q:"2", t:9931, m:false }
items.note: m = buyer is maker (side that was resting).

items.label: Server maps Redis pubsub -> subscribed sockets
items.code:
redis.subscribe(`trade@${market}`, (msg) =>
  subscribers[`trade@${market}`].forEach(s => s.send(msg)));
items.note: WS server holds subscription map only; it never touches the order book.

items.label: Setup
items.code:
npm init -y
npm i express redis uuid
npm i -D typescript @types/express @types/node ts-node

items.label: Message type constants (shared with engine)
RedisManager — request/response over queue + pubsub (singleton)
items.note: One channel per request keyed by uuid; engine publishes the reply to that channel.

items.label: Order route wiring
Depth route (read-only query to engine)
Engine consumer loop (separate process)

tips: Engine is single-threaded and single-instance — never horizontally scale it, or the in-memory order book forks. Scale the stateless API/WS layers instead.
Store price & quantity as strings and do integer math in the smallest unit (lamports/cents). Floats silently corrupt balances.
Match price-time priority: best price first, FIFO within a price level. Break the match loop the moment the resting price stops crossing.
Lock funds up front (buy locks quote = price*qty, sell locks base = qty) and reject insufficient balance before matching — the engine must never emit an invalid state.
Push depth as incremental deltas over WS (qty "0" = remove level), not the full book each tick — full snapshots kill bandwidth on active markets.
Keep the engine off the DB. It publishes trades/fills to pub/sub; a separate DB processor persists them so matching stays microsecond-fast.
Use a Redis list as the job queue (LPUSH from API, BRPOP in engine) for ordering + durability + natural backpressure; use pub/sub only for broadcast and RPC replies.
Snapshot the order book + balances to disk periodically and reload on boot; otherwise an engine restart loses every resting order.

items.label: Part 1 = client side
items.note: Next.js UI, order book / depth view, klines. Fixes the REST + WS contract the backend must now satisfy.

items.label: Part 2 = server side
items.note: API server + in-memory matching Engine + durable queue between them + async DB persistence + real-time market-data fan-out.

items.label: Monorepo services
items.code:
api/     # Express, accepts orders
engine/  # single-process matching engine (in-mem book)
ws/      # WebSocket fan-out
db/      # DB processor (drains 'db' queue → TimescaleDB)
items.note: Redis sits in the middle as broker + queue. Frontend from Part 1 unchanged.

items.label: Interview framing
items.note: Decomposition first: name each service + its responsibility before writing code. Design against an agreed contract, not in a vacuum.

items.label: Order path (write)
items.code:
client → API (LPUSH orders) → Engine (BRPOP)
     → match in-memory book
     → PUBLISH result to channel `id`   (API resolves HTTP)
     → LPUSH events to `db` queue        (async persist)
     → PUBLISH depth@X / trade@X         (WS fan-out)
items.note: One hot path (deterministic matching) separated from slow/best-effort concerns (DB, client fan-out).

items.label: Why single-threaded + in-memory engine
items.note: Matching needs a total order (price-time priority) → single thread kills lock contention/races + is deterministically replayable. In-mem = microsecond latency vs a DB round-trip per match.

items.label: Cost of that design
items.note: Throughput ceiling = 1 core; durability problem. Solve with snapshots + ordered event log, NOT more threads.

items.label: Crash recovery
items.note: Every order is on the durable Redis list before processing; snapshot the book periodically + replay events since the snapshot's sequence number. Downstream writes must be idempotent.

items.label: DB kept off hot path
items.code:
// engine emits, never awaits a DB write
RedisManager.getInstance().pushMessage({ type: TRADE_ADDED, data });
items.note: Dedicated db/ processor drains the queue → TimescaleDB. Trade-off: eventual consistency; carry a monotonic seq for idempotent, ordered writes.

items.label: Why a LIST not pub/sub for orders
items.note: LPUSH/BRPOP is a durable, at-least-once work queue: messages persist until popped, gives backpressure, load-balances across consumers. Orders must never be silently dropped.

items.label: API side — enqueue order
items.code: await client.lPush("orders", JSON.stringify({ clientId: id, message }));
items.note: clientId is a unique per-request id used as the reply channel.

items.label: Engine side — blocking drain loop
items.note: Prefer BRPOP (blocks) over a while-true RPOP busy-loop that pegs CPU. LPUSH + BRPOP = FIFO.

items.label: node-redis v4 client
Sync REST over async engine (sendAndAwait)
items.note: API subscribes to channel = unique id, then enqueues. Engine PUBLISHes result to `id`; promise resolves, HTTP responds.

items.label: Engine replies to the API
items.code: this.publisher.publish(clientId, JSON.stringify(payload));

items.label: Market-data fan-out channels
items.code:
publish("depth@BTC_USDC", JSON.stringify(depth));
publish("trade@BTC_USDC",  JSON.stringify(trade));
items.note: Fire-and-forget, no persistence. Perfect for depth/trade (many subs, best-effort, only latest matters).

items.label: Pub/Sub is NOT for orders
items.note: No subscriber connected = message gone forever. Unacceptable for orders (use the list); fine for ephemeral market data.

items.label: Separate pub vs sub connection
items.note: A node-redis client in subscribe mode can't run normal commands — use one connection to publish/enqueue and a second to subscribe.

items.label: Engine.process — command dispatch
Orderbook.addOrder
items.note: Fully filled → don't rest. Partial → remainder joins the book.

items.label: matchBid core
items.note: Price-time priority. Then drop fully-filled asks: this.asks = this.asks.filter(a => a.filled < a.quantity).

items.label: Balance check + lock
items.note: On buy: verify quote balance ≥ price*qty, lock it; on sell: lock base asset. Update both users' available/locked after each fill. Reject if insufficient.

items.label: Explicit getInstance (lazy init)
items.note: Lazy = created on first use, can wait for config/env. Private constructor blocks `new`.

items.label: Free module-level singleton
items.code:
// redis.ts
export const redisManager = new RedisManager();  // import cache dedupes per process
items.note: import/require caches the module → every importer gets the same instance. Eager init at import time. Enough for most cases.

items.label: What is a singleton here
items.note: RedisManager (one connection, avoids exhausting/interleaving), the Engine (one source of truth for the book), SubscriptionManager (one channel→socket map).

items.label: Downsides (say these in interview)
items.note: Controlled global → hidden coupling, hard to mock/reset in tests, init-order/circular-dep issues. DI is often cleaner.

items.label: CRITICAL: per-process only
items.note: Singleton ≠ cluster-wide single instance. PM2 cluster / k8s replicas each get their OWN heap + singleton. Fine for stateless managers; CATASTROPHIC if you run two matching engines on the same market.

items.label: SubscriptionManager (singleton) — maps
items.code:
private subscriptions: Map<string, string[]> = new Map();        // userId → channels
private reverseSubscriptions: Map<string, string[]> = new Map(); // channel → userIds
items.note: reverse map lets a Redis message find every socket for that channel in O(1).

items.label: Subscribe once per channel, ref-count users
items.note: Add socket to an existing Redis sub instead of re-subscribing per user → no duplicate delivery.

items.label: Redis → local sockets (last-hop delivery)
items.note: Redis broker fans out cross-instance; the in-mem singleton is only the last hop to locally-connected sockets.

items.label: Unsubscribe on disconnect
items.note: Skipping this leaks closures + dead-socket refs → heap grows until the process falls over.

items.label: Scale WS horizontally
items.note: Run N stateless WS servers, each subscribes to the Redis channels. Registry stays process-local; shared truth is in Redis. In-process EventEmitter instead of Redis = publish on A never reaches a client on B.

sections.title: Message Types & Config at a Glance

items.label: API → Engine (MessageToEngine)
items.code: CREATE_ORDER | CANCEL_ORDER | GET_DEPTH | GET_OPEN_ORDERS | ON_RAMP

items.label: Engine → API (MessageFromOrderbook)
items.code: ORDER_PLACED | ORDER_CANCELLED | OPEN_ORDERS | DEPTH

items.label: Engine → db queue (DbMessage)
items.code: TRADE_ADDED | ORDER_UPDATE   // LPUSH to `db` list

items.label: Engine → ws channels (WsMessage)
items.code: depth@<MARKET> | trade@<MARKET>   // PUBLISH

items.label: Channel / queue names
items.code:
orders          # Redis list (durable work queue)
<clientId>      # pub/sub reply channel per request
db              # Redis list (persistence)
depth@BTC_USDC  # pub/sub market data
trade@BTC_USDC

items.label: Persistence store
items.note: TimescaleDB (Postgres + time-series) for klines/trades; the db/ processor writes it, materialized views build candles.

tips: Orders travel via the durable LIST (LPUSH/BRPOP); market data via PUB/SUB. Never send an order over pub/sub — no subscriber = message lost forever.
Always add a timeout to sendAndAwait: a crashed engine mid-order leaves the HTTP request hanging. Surface it as a 5xx, and unsubscribe to avoid channel leaks.
Keep exactly ONE matching engine per market. A singleton is per-process, so two engine instances on the same market = double-spend / corrupted book.

Engine writes DB events fire-and-forget to a queue — it must never await disk I/O on the matching path. Carry a monotonic sequence number so replays/retries are idempotent (no duplicate rows on restart).
Prefer BRPOP over a while(true){ RPOP } busy-loop — the latter pegs a CPU core spinning on an empty queue.
In node-redis, a connection in subscribe mode can't issue normal commands — keep separate publisher and subscriber clients.
Unsubscribe from Redis + prune the maps on socket 'close'. Missing unsubscribe-on-disconnect leaks dead sockets and grows the heap until the process dies.
Wrap each pub/sub subscriber callback in try/catch so one throwing subscriber doesn't abort the whole fan-out; ref-count so you subscribe once per channel, not per user.

items.label: Create a node
items.code: const div = document.createElement("div");

items.label: Set visible text (safe, no HTML parsing)
items.code: div.textContent = todo.title;
items.note: Prefer over innerHTML for user input — avoids XSS injection.

items.label: Set inner HTML (parses tags)
items.code: el.innerHTML = `${title}`;
items.note: Unsafe with untrusted data; slower on repeated writes.

items.label: Append / prepend children
items.code:
parent.appendChild(div);      // single node
parent.append(a, b, "text");   // multiple + strings
parent.prepend(div);           // insert first

items.label: Insert HTML at a position without reparsing parent
items.code:
el.insertAdjacentHTML("beforeend", `
  • ${t}
    • `);
items.note: positions: beforebegin | afterbegin | beforeend | afterend

items.label: Attributes & data-*
Classes
By id (fastest)
items.code: const el = document.getElementById("todos");

items.label: CSS selector — first / all
items.code:
document.querySelector(".todo");        // first match | null
document.querySelectorAll(".todo");     // static NodeList

items.label: Loop a NodeList
items.code:
document.querySelectorAll(".todo")
  .forEach(el => el.remove());
items.note: NodeList has forEach; HTMLCollection (.children) does not — spread it: [...el.children]

items.label: Walk the tree
Match a selector on an element
items.code: if (e.target.matches(".delete-btn")) { /* ... */ }

items.label: Single source of truth: UI is a function of state
items.code:
let todos = [];   // the ONLY thing you mutate
// UI = render(todos). Never hand-patch the DOM ad hoc.
items.note: Change state -> call render(). Don't surgically edit DOM in event handlers.

items.label: render(): wipe + rebuild from state
items.note: Simplest correct pattern. Every render reflects current state exactly.

items.label: Build one row (factory fn)
Batch inserts with a fragment (perf)
items.note: replaceChildren() replaces all kids in one shot; avoids N layout thrashes.

items.label: Read input, push to state, re-render
Stable unique id
items.code: id: crypto.randomUUID()   // or Date.now() for simple apps
items.note: Array index is NOT a stable id — it shifts after deletes.

items.label: Wire the button / Enter key
Remove from state by id, then re-render
items.note: filter returns a NEW array — reassign it. Delete from STATE, not the DOM directly.

items.label: Toggle done (same pattern via map)
Yank a single node (when not using full re-render)
items.code:
el.remove();                        // modern
el.parentElement.removeChild(el);   // legacy equivalent

items.label: Per-element handler (simple, from render)
items.code: del.onclick = () => deleteTodo(todo.id);
items.note: .onclick overwrites; addEventListener stacks multiple.

items.label: Event delegation — one listener on the parent
items.note: Survives re-renders: listener lives on the stable parent, not on rebuilt rows.

items.label: dataset is always a string
items.code: deleteTodo(Number(btn.dataset.id));
items.note: Coerce with Number()/parseInt before comparing to numeric ids.

items.label: Prevent form reload

tips: State is the source of truth: mutate the `todos` array, then call render(). Never patch the DOM directly in handlers — the two will drift out of sync.
Full re-render (innerHTML = "" then rebuild) is dead simple and correct for small lists; reach for DocumentFragment / replaceChildren only when it gets slow.
Use textContent, not innerHTML, for user-entered todo titles — innerHTML with untrusted input is an XSS hole.
dataset values are strings. Coerce (Number()/parseInt) before comparing dataset.id to numeric ids, or IDs will never match.
Give each todo a stable unique id (Date.now()/crypto.randomUUID()) — deleting by array index breaks after the first removal shifts everything.
filter() and map() return NEW arrays — you must reassign (todos = todos.filter(...)); they don't mutate in place.
Prefer event delegation (one listener on the parent) over per-row handlers: it keeps working after re-renders and avoids leaking listeners.
On a form, always e.preventDefault() in the submit handler or the page reloads and your state is wiped.

items.label: What the DOM is
items.note: Browser parses HTML into a live in-memory tree of node objects (document -> html -> head/body -> ...). JS mutates this tree; the rendered page reflects it instantly. The DOM is NOT the HTML source file.

items.label: Root entry points
items.code:
document            // whole page
document.documentElement  // <html>
document.head
document.body
items.note: document is a global; everything hangs off it.

items.label: Static HTML
items.code: • Buy milk
items.note: Markup shipped as-is from the server; content fixed at load, no JS mutation.

items.label: Dynamic HTML
items.note: JS creates/updates/removes nodes at runtime in response to data, events, timers, fetches.

items.label: Run JS after DOM is parsed
items.code:
<script src="app.js" defer></script>
// or
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', init);
items.note: Selecting elements before they exist returns null. defer runs script after parse; inline scripts at end of <body> also work.

items.label: By CSS selector (modern default)
items.code:
document.querySelector('.card > h2');   // first match or null
document.querySelectorAll('li.done');    // static NodeList
items.note: querySelector takes ANY CSS selector. querySelectorAll snapshot does NOT auto-update.

items.label: By id / class / tag (older, faster)
items.code:
document.getElementById('todos');          // Element or null
document.getElementsByClassName('item');   // live HTMLCollection
document.getElementsByTagName('li');       // live HTMLCollection
items.note: getElementById takes the id WITHOUT '#'. getElementsBy* return LIVE collections that update as the DOM changes.

items.label: Scoped search (within an element)
items.code:
const card = document.querySelector('.card');
card.querySelector('button');   // searches only inside card

items.label: Iterate matches
items.note: NodeList has forEach. HTMLCollection does NOT — spread it: [...coll].forEach(...).

items.label: Traverse from a node
items.code:
el.parentElement
el.children          // element-only, HTMLCollection
el.firstElementChild / el.lastElementChild
el.nextElementSibling / el.previousElementSibling
el.closest('.card')  // nearest ancestor matching selector
items.note: Prefer *Element* variants; childNodes/firstChild include text/whitespace nodes.

items.label: Text vs HTML
items.code: el.textContent = 'Hi x'; // literal text, safe el.innerHTML = 'Hi'; // parses HTML el.innerText; // rendered text (layout-aware, slower)
items.note: NEVER put untrusted input in innerHTML (XSS). Use textContent for user data.

items.label: Attributes
items.note: Many map to properties: el.id, el.href, el.value, el.disabled = true.

items.label: data-* attributes
items.note: kebab-case in HTML -> camelCase in dataset.

items.label: Classes
items.note: Prefer classList over overwriting el.className (which wipes all classes).

items.label: Inline styles
items.note: CSS 'background-color' -> JS 'backgroundColor'. Prefer toggling classes over per-property styling.

items.label: Form values
items.note: value is a property, not an attribute — getAttribute('value') returns the initial default, not current input.

items.label: Create + insert (safe pattern)
items.note: createElement makes a detached node; nothing renders until it's inserted.

items.label: Insertion methods
items.code:
parent.append(node, 'text');   // end (multiple, allows strings)
parent.prepend(node);          // start
ref.before(node);              // as previous sibling
ref.after(node);               // as next sibling
ref.replaceWith(node);         // swap out
items.note: Modern & flexible. Older: appendChild(node) (single node only), insertBefore(new, ref).

items.label: insertAdjacentHTML / Element
items.code:
el.insertAdjacentHTML('beforeend', '
  • x
    • '); // positions: beforebegin | afterbegin | beforeend | afterend
items.note: Parses HTML at a precise spot without clobbering existing children (unlike innerHTML +=). Watch XSS.

items.label: Clone a node
items.code: const copy = node.cloneNode(true);  // true = deep (with children)
items.note: cloneNode(false) copies the element only, no descendants. Clones have no event listeners.

items.label: Moving vs copying
items.note: Inserting an already-attached node MOVES it (a node can have only one parent). Clone first if you need it in two places.

items.label: Remove a node
items.code: el.remove();                 // modern, self-removal
items.note: No args, removes el from its parent.

items.label: Remove a child (legacy)
items.code: parent.removeChild(childEl);
items.note: Returns the removed node; throws if not actually a child.

items.label: Clear all children
items.note: replaceChildren() with no args is the clean way. Loop-based while(el.firstChild) el.removeChild(el.firstChild) also works.

items.label: Replace children with new set
items.code: list.replaceChildren(li1, li2, li3);
items.note: Atomic swap of the entire child list.

items.label: Build nested structure
items.note: Template literals are handy but ${} of user data is an XSS hole — escape or use textContent.

items.label: DocumentFragment (batch inserts)
items.note: Fragment isn't rendered; appending it moves its children and empties it. Avoids per-item reflow in loops.

items.label: <template> for reusable markup
items.note: template.content is inert (not rendered/queried by page) until cloned + inserted.

items.label: Event delegation (dynamic lists)
items.note: One listener on the parent handles current AND future children — no need to rebind after adding nodes.

items.label: Render a data array
items.note: Map data -> nodes, then replaceChildren once.

tips: querySelectorAll returns a STATIC NodeList; getElementsByClassName/TagName return LIVE HTMLCollections that mutate mid-loop — snapshot with [...coll] before iterating.
textContent for user-supplied data, innerHTML only for trusted markup. innerHTML += re-parses and destroys existing event listeners and input state.
Selecting elements before the DOM parses returns null — use defer, DOMContentLoaded, or place <script> at end of <body>.
input.value is a live property; getAttribute('value') returns only the initial HTML default, not what the user typed.
Inserting an existing node moves it (one parent max); cloneNode(true) first to duplicate. Clones do NOT carry event listeners.
Batch DOM writes with DocumentFragment or a single replaceChildren/append to avoid layout thrashing (reflow per insert).
Use event delegation on a stable parent for dynamically added children instead of rebinding listeners each time you add a node.
CSS hyphenated props become camelCase in JS: background-color -> el.style.backgroundColor; and prefer toggling classes over setting many inline styles.

items.label: Recommended: install via nvm (manage multiple versions)
items.code:
# macOS/Linux
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.1/install.sh | bash
nvm install --lts
nvm use --lts

# Windows -> use nvm-windows (installer .exe) or the node .msi
items.note: Prefer the LTS build for assignments; avoid mixing global installs across versions.

items.label: Verify install
items.code:
node -v   # e.g. v22.x
npm -v    # npm ships with node
items.note: If not found, restart the terminal / re-source shell profile so PATH updates.

items.label: List / switch versions (nvm)
items.code:
nvm ls
nvm install 20
nvm use 20

items.label: Bun as an alternative runtime (covered later in course)
items.code:
curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
bun run index.js
items.note: Not required for week 2; Node is the default for these assignments.

sections.title: Run JS with Node & project setup

items.label: Run a single file
items.code:
node index.js
node ./easy/palindrome.js

items.label: REPL (interactive scratchpad)
items.code:
node
> [1,2,3].map(x => x*2)
> .exit   # or Ctrl+D
items.note: Great for testing one-liners without a file.

items.label: Init a project + package.json
items.code: npm init -y
items.note: Creates package.json so you can add deps and scripts.

items.label: Install deps (assignments often use jest to self-test)
items.code:
npm install            # install from package.json
npm install --save-dev jest
items.note: node_modules is generated locally — never commit it; use .gitignore.

items.label: npm scripts
Auto-restart on save (dev loop)
items.code: npx nodemon index.js
items.note: Node 18+ also has built-in watch: node --watch index.js

items.label: CommonJS (Node default, what most week-2 assignments use)
Multiple exports
items.code:
module.exports = { add, sub };
const { add, sub } = require('./math');

items.label: ES Modules (opt in)
items.note: With ESM you must include the .js extension in relative imports.

items.label: Sum / reduce an array
items.code: const total = arr.reduce((acc, n) => acc + n, 0);

items.label: Count vowels
items.code: const count = (str.match(/[aeiou]/gi) || []).length;
items.note: || [] guards against null when there are no matches.

items.label: Palindrome check (normalize first)
items.code:
const clean = str.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z0-9]/g, '');
const ok = clean === [...clean].reverse().join('');

items.label: Expenditure analysis (group/sum by key)
Find largest element
items.code: const max = Math.max(...arr);   // arr must be non-empty

items.label: Anagram check (sorted-chars key)
items.code:
const norm = s => [...s.toLowerCase()].sort().join('');
const isAnagram = (a, b) => norm(a) === norm(b);

items.label: Time a synchronous function
items.note: For sub-ms precision use performance.now() (ms as float).

items.label: Class with state (e.g. Todo / Counter assignment)
Frequency map
items.code:
const freq = {};
for (const ch of str) freq[ch] = (freq[ch] || 0) + 1;

items.label: Read a file (sync, simplest for a one-off script)
items.code:
const fs = require('fs');
const text = fs.readFileSync('./a.txt', 'utf-8');
items.note: Pass 'utf-8' or you get a raw Buffer instead of a string.

items.label: Count words in a file
items.code:
const words = text.trim().split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean);
const count = words.length;

items.label: Clean a file: strip extra spaces, write back
items.code:
const cleaned = text.replace(/\s+/g, ' ').trim();
fs.writeFileSync('./a.txt', cleaned);

items.label: Parse / stringify JSON
items.code:
const data = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('./todos.json', 'utf-8'));
fs.writeFileSync('./todos.json', JSON.stringify(data, null, 2));
items.note: null, 2 pretty-prints with 2-space indent.

items.label: Defer work with setTimeout
items.code: setTimeout(() => console.log('ran after ~1s'), 1000);
items.note: Delay is a minimum, not a guarantee — it queues after current sync code finishes.

items.label: Callback-style function
Read file asynchronously (callback API)
items.note: Error-first callback: always handle err before using data.

items.label: Print without blocking the event loop
items.note: Even 0ms defers to after the synchronous stack drains.

items.label: Promisify a delay
items.code:
const wait = ms => new Promise(res => setTimeout(res, ms));
await wait(1000);

items.label: fs Promises API + async/await
items.note: await only works inside an async function (or top-level in ESM).

items.label: Sequential vs parallel
Error handling
items.note: Promise chains use .catch(); async/await uses try/catch.

items.label: Convert callback API to promise

tips: node -v / npm -v is your first sanity check — if the shell can't find node, reopen the terminal so PATH refreshes.
Assignments usually export a function via module.exports and are graded by jest — run npm test, don't add console.logs that break the return contract.
Return the value, don't console.log it. Test runners check what your function returns, not what it prints.
fs sync methods (readFileSync) block the event loop — fine for tiny CLI scripts, never for servers.
Always pass 'utf-8' to fs read calls or you'll get a Buffer instead of a string.
setTimeout delay is a floor, not exact; async code runs after the current synchronous stack finishes.
await must live inside an async function; forgetting await gives you a pending Promise, not the value.
Use Promise.all for independent async tasks to run them in parallel instead of awaiting one-by-one.

items.label: Hash a string (ethers v6)
items.note: ETH uses original Keccak-256, NOT the NIST-standardized SHA3-256 — they differ.

items.label: Event topic / function selector
items.code:
id("Transfer(address,address,uint256)");     // 32-byte topic0
id("transfer(address,uint256)").slice(0, 10); // 0xa9059cbb (4-byte selector)
items.note: Selector = first 4 bytes of keccak256(canonical signature).

items.label: Derive ETH address from public key
items.note: Address is keccak256(pubKey)[12:], then EIP-55 checksummed (mixed-case).

items.label: Request envelope (spec 2.0)
items.code: { "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "eth_blockNumber", "params": [] }
items.note: Stateless, transport-agnostic (HTTP POST or WS). id echoed back to match response.

items.label: Response envelope
items.code:
{ "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": "0x120fa1c" }
// on failure: { ..., "error": { "code": -32000, "message": "..." } }
items.note: Check for `error` before `result`; a 200 HTTP status can still carry an RPC error.

items.label: Raw curl call
items.code:
curl https://eth.llamarpc.com -X POST \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_blockNumber","params":[]}'
items.note: Batch = send an array of request objects in one POST.

items.label: ETH denominations
items.code:
1 ETH  = 10^18 wei
1 gwei = 10^9  wei   // gas prices are quoted in gwei
items.note: All on-chain amounts are integers in wei — never floats.

items.label: SOL denominations
items.code: 1 SOL = 10^9 lamports  // LAMPORTS_PER_SOL = 1_000_000_000
items.note: getBalance returns lamports; divide by LAMPORTS_PER_SOL for display.

items.label: Convert in ethers v6
items.code:
import { formatEther, parseEther, parseUnits, formatUnits } from "ethers";
formatEther(1000000000000000000n);   // "1.0"
parseEther("1.0");                   // 1000000000000000000n
parseUnits("20", "gwei");            // 20000000000n
items.note: parse* returns bigint; keep amounts as bigint end-to-end to avoid precision loss.

items.label: Convert in Solana web3.js
items.note: eth_getBalance returns hex — do BigInt(hexResult) before dividing.

items.label: Providers (the 'backend' node)
items.code:
// ETH: Alchemy / Infura / QuickNode / public eth.llamarpc.com
// SOL: clusterApiUrl('devnet'|'mainnet-beta') / Helius / QuickNode
items.note: Wallet is frontend; the RPC node is a shared full node you query over HTTP/WS.

items.label: Ethers provider
items.note: WebSocketProvider for subscriptions (new blocks, logs).

items.label: Solana connection
items.code:
import { Connection, clusterApiUrl } from "@solana/web3.js";
const conn = new Connection(clusterApiUrl("devnet"), "confirmed");
items.note: Commitment: processed < confirmed < finalized. Use confirmed for UX, finalized for money.

items.label: getBalance
items.code: await conn.getBalance(pubkey); // lamports (number)
items.note: Raw: method 'getBalance', params ['<base58 pubkey>'].

items.label: requestAirdrop (devnet)
items.code:
const sig = await conn.requestAirdrop(pubkey, 1 * LAMPORTS_PER_SOL);
await conn.confirmTransaction(sig);

items.label: getAccountInfo
items.code: await conn.getAccountInfo(pubkey); // { lamports, owner, data, executable }

items.label: getLatestBlockhash
items.code: const { blockhash, lastValidBlockHeight } = await conn.getLatestBlockhash();
items.note: Required to build a tx; blockhash expires (~150 slots).

items.label: getSignaturesForAddress
items.code: await conn.getSignaturesForAddress(pubkey, { limit: 10 }); // tx history

items.label: getParsedTransaction
items.code: await conn.getParsedTransaction(sig, { maxSupportedTransactionVersion: 0 });

items.label: sendTransaction
items.code: await conn.sendTransaction(tx, [keypair]);
items.note: Returns signature; confirm separately.

items.label: eth_getBalance
items.code:
await provider.getBalance(addr); // bigint wei
// raw params: ['0xADDR', 'latest'] -> hex wei
items.note: Block tag: 'latest' | 'safe' | 'finalized' | 'pending' | hex block #.

items.label: eth_getTransactionCount (nonce)
items.code: await provider.getTransactionCount(addr, "pending");
items.note: Use 'pending' to include queued txs when building the next nonce.

items.label: eth_gasPrice / fees
items.code: const fee = await provider.getFeeData(); // { gasPrice, maxFeePerGas, maxPriorityFeePerGas }

items.label: eth_estimateGas
items.code: await provider.estimateGas({ to, value, data });

items.label: eth_call (read contract, no tx)
items.code: await provider.call({ to, data: id('totalSupply()').slice(0,10) });
items.note: Free, no state change, no signature.

items.label: eth_sendRawTransaction
items.code: await provider.broadcastTransaction(signedRawTxHex);
items.note: You sign locally, then push the signed raw tx.

items.label: eth_getTransactionReceipt
items.code: await provider.getTransactionReceipt(txHash); // null until mined; status 1=ok 0=revert

items.label: Generate mnemonic + seed
items.note: BIP44 path: m / 44' / coinType' / account' / change / index. ETH=60, SOL=501.

items.label: Derive Solana keypair (ed25519)
items.note: Solana uses ed25519 with fully-hardened path (every level has ').

items.label: Derive ETH wallet (secp256k1)
items.note: Increment index i for multiple accounts from ONE seed phrase.

items.label: Sign & send (ETH)
items.note: sendTransaction auto-fills nonce/gas/chainId; signs locally then broadcasts.

items.label: Sign & send (SOL)
Show balances
items.code:
const ethBal = formatEther(await provider.getBalance(addr));
const solBal = (await conn.getBalance(pub)) / LAMPORTS_PER_SOL;
items.note: Poll or subscribe; RPC data may lag one block behind.

items.label: Persist seed (client-side only)
items.code:
// store encrypted mnemonic in localStorage / IndexedDB;
// NEVER send the seed or private key to any backend or RPC.
items.note: Whoever has the 12 words controls every derived account — treat as top secret.
