tips: Keccak-256 != SHA3-256: Ethereum froze the pre-standard Keccak padding. Libraries labeled 'sha3' may give the wrong hash — verify against a known vector (empty string keccak = 0xc5d2...).
Ethereum eth_* RPC results are hex strings; wrap in BigInt(hex) before math. Balances/nonces overflow Number if you don't.
Hardened derivation uses the apostrophe (') meaning index + 2^31. Solana paths are fully hardened; ETH's last two levels (change/index) are not.
Nonce = number of txs sent from an account. Reuse or gaps stall txs — read with 'pending' tag and manage sequentially when batching.
Public RPC endpoints are rate-limited and sometimes lag; use a keyed provider (Alchemy/Infura/Helius) for production and dedicated methods for history.
Solana blockhash expires (~60-90s / ~150 slots). Fetch getLatestBlockhash right before signing or the tx is rejected as expired.
One mnemonic -> unlimited accounts by bumping the address index. Same 12 words give the SAME keys everywhere — a leak is total, irreversible loss.
eth_call and getAccountInfo are free reads (no signature, no gas); only sendRawTransaction / sendTransaction cost fees and change state.

items.label: Install Solana CLI (Anza)
items.code:
sh -c "$(curl -sSfL https://release.anza.xyz/stable/install)"
solana --version
items.note: Ships solana, solana-keygen, spl-token. Windows: use WSL. Old release.solana.com URL still redirects.

items.label: Point CLI at a cluster
items.code:
solana config set --url devnet   # -ul local | -ud devnet | -ut testnet | -um mainnet
solana config get
items.note: Config lives at ~/.config/solana/cli/config.yml. Devnet endpoint: https://api.devnet.solana.com

items.label: Generate a wallet / keypair
items.code:
solana-keygen new              # writes ~/.config/solana/id.json
solana address
items.note: Keypair file is the ed25519 secret key JSON array. Back up the seed phrase.

items.label: Fund & check balance (devnet)
items.code:
solana airdrop 2
solana balance
items.note: Devnet airdrop is rate-limited (~2 SOL); retry or use faucet.solana.com if throttled.

items.label: Everything is an account
items.note: Only the owner program can mutate an account's data or debit lamports.

items.label: Web2 vs Solana data model
items.note: Web2: server code + DB rows live together, mutable in place. Solana: programs are STATELESS code; all state lives in separate accounts the program owns. Same program serves many state accounts.

items.label: Programs are executable accounts
items.note: A deployed program is an account with executable=true, owned by the BPF Loader. It reads/writes other accounts passed into each instruction.

items.label: Rent exemption
items.code: solana rent 165   # min lamports for a 165-byte account
items.note: Accounts must hold a min balance (~2 years rent) to persist, else they get purged. SDKs compute this via getMinimumBalanceForRentExemption.

items.label: Why Solana
items.note: Parallel execution (Sealevel) because txs declare which accounts they touch; ~400ms slots, sub-cent fees, PoH ordering. Contrast EVM's single-threaded state.

items.label: Create a mint
items.code:
spl-token create-token --decimals 6
# => Creating token <MINT_ADDRESS>
items.note: Mint account stores: supply, decimals, mint authority, freeze authority. It is NOT where balances live.

items.label: Create a token account (ATA) to hold it
items.code: spl-token create-account <MINT>
items.note: One token (ATA) account per (owner, mint) pair holds the balance.

items.label: Mint supply into your ATA
items.code: spl-token mint <MINT> 1000
items.note: Amount is in whole tokens; CLI scales by decimals. Requires you to hold mint authority.

items.label: Transfer
items.code: spl-token transfer <MINT> 100 <RECIPIENT_WALLET> --fund-recipient
items.note: --fund-recipient creates & pays rent for the recipient ATA if missing.

items.label: Inspect
items.code:
spl-token accounts
spl-token supply <MINT>
spl-token account-info <MINT>

items.label: High-level helpers
items.note: createMint sends createAccount + initializeMint under the hood. Amounts are base units: multiply by 10**decimals.

items.label: Low-level: build the mint manually
items.note: The mint keypair must sign createAccount. This is exactly what the CLI/createMint do.

items.label: Rust (solana-sdk + spl-token)
items.note: Cargo deps: solana-sdk, solana-client, spl-token. Same two-instruction pattern as JS.

items.label: Python (solana-py / solders)
items.code:
from solana.rpc.api import Client
from spl.token.client import Token
from spl.token.constants import TOKEN_PROGRAM_ID

token = Token.create_mint(Client(url), payer, payer.pubkey(), 6,
                          TOKEN_PROGRAM_ID)
token.mint_to(dest_ata, payer, 100_000000)
items.note: pip install solana. Newer versions back the types with solders (Rust bindings).

items.label: Go (gagliardetto/solana-go)
items.note: Illustrative: assemble createAccount + initializeMint instructions, then sign & send via rpc client.

items.label: What a PDA is
items.note: Program Derived Address: a deterministic address OFF the ed25519 curve, so it has no private key. Only the deriving program can 'sign' for it. Used for program-owned state.

items.label: Derive a PDA (JS)
items.note: Returns [address, bump]. Bump is the first byte (255..0) that pushes the hash off-curve; store/pass it to re-derive cheaply.

items.label: Derive a PDA (Rust)
items.code:
let (pda, bump) = Pubkey::find_program_address(
    &[b"vault", user.as_ref()], &program_id);

items.label: ATA is a PDA
items.note: For Token-2022 pass programId=TOKEN_2022_PROGRAM_ID (seed changes the derived address).

items.label: Program IDs
items.code:
Token:      TokenkegQfeZyiNwAJbNbGKPFXCWuBvf9Ss623VQ5DA
Token-2022: TokenzQdBNbLqP5VEhdkAS6EPFLC1PHnBqCXEpPxuEb
ATA prog:   ATokenGPvbdGVxr1b2hvZbsiqW5xWH25efTNsLJA8knL
items.note: Token-2022 is a separate, backward-compatible program adding opt-in 'extensions'. A mint belongs to exactly one program.

items.label: Create a Token-2022 mint (CLI)
items.code:
spl-token create-token --program-2022 --decimals 9
# or explicit: -p TokenzQdBNbLqP5VEhdkAS6EPFLC1PHnBqCXEpPxuEb
items.note: All later spl-token cmds infer the program from the mint's owner.

items.label: Common extensions
items.note: MetadataPointer, TokenMetadata (embedded), TransferFee, NonTransferable ('soulbound'), InterestBearing, TransferHook, ConfidentialTransfer, DefaultAccountState, PermanentDelegate.

items.label: JS: pick program + size for extensions
items.note: Extensions grow the mint account; allocate the right space or initializeMint fails.

items.label: CLI: metadata embedded in the mint
items.code:
spl-token create-token --program-2022 --enable-metadata --decimals 9
spl-token initialize-metadata <MINT> "My Token" "MTK" https://.../meta.json
items.note: No separate Metaplex metadata account needed — metadata lives inside the mint via the TokenMetadata extension.

items.label: JS: single tx (pointer + mint + metadata)
items.note: Order matters: createAccount -> initMetadataPointer -> initMint -> initMetadata. Rent must cover mint + packed metadata; the metadata init writes past mintLen so fund for the sum.

tips: Rent: every account must be rent-exempt or it gets reaped. Always fund via getMinimumBalanceForRentExemption before createAccount.
Token amounts are ALWAYS base units. 1 token at 6 decimals = 1_000_000. The CLI scales for you; the SDK does not.
Mint account holds supply/authority; balances live in separate token (ATA) accounts — one per (owner, mint). Don't confuse the two.
Set mint authority to null (freeze mint) to make supply permanently fixed; set freeze authority to null if you never want to freeze holders.

A mint is owned by ONE program. Token vs Token-2022 accounts are not interchangeable — pass the correct programId to every helper (getOrCreateAssociatedTokenAccount, getAssociatedTokenAddressSync, mintTo).
PDAs have no private key (off-curve). A program signs for its PDA with invoke_signed + the stored bump; clients can't sign for a PDA.
Token-2022 extensions must be chosen at mint creation and change the account size — use getMintLen([...extensions]) or initializeMint reverts.
Devnet airdrops are rate-limited; if `solana airdrop` fails, wait and retry or use the web faucet. Use --fund-recipient on transfers to auto-create the destination ATA.

items.label: Install deps
items.code:
npm init -y
npm i express jsonwebtoken bcryptjs cors
# mongoose for persistence, nodemon --save-dev for reload

items.label: Minimal Express app
items.note: Without express.json() req.body is undefined for JSON POSTs.

items.label: Enable CORS (frontend on another origin)
items.code:
const cors = require("cors");
app.use(cors());  // dev only; lock origins in prod
items.note: Browser blocks cross-origin fetch to :3000 from :5173 without this.

items.label: In-memory store (course pattern)
items.code: const USERS = [];  // [{ username, password }] — reset on restart
items.note: Fine for the lesson; swap for MongoDB/mongoose in the TODO app.

items.label: Mental model
items.note: Signup = open account. Signin = teller checks ID, hands you a token. Token = your ID card; show it on every request instead of re-proving identity each time.

items.label: Flow
Where the token lives (client)
items.note: Server stays stateless with JWT — no session table needed.

items.label: Hash password on signup
items.note: NEVER store plaintext passwords. Second arg = salt rounds.

items.label: Verify + issue token on signin
items.note: Return SAME error for unknown user + wrong password (no user enumeration).

items.label: Naive random token (pre-JWT lesson)
items.code:
const token = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString("hex");
tokenToUser[token] = username;  // server MUST remember mapping
items.note: Stateful: server must store token->user. JWT removes this need.

items.label: Sign (create)
items.note: Payload should carry only an identifier — never the password.

items.label: Verify (trust + extract)
items.note: verify checks the signature. Throws on tamper/expiry — always wrap in try/catch.

items.label: Decode (NO signature check)
items.code: const payload = jwt.decode(token);  // anyone can do this
items.note: Use for reading, never for trust. Verification is what makes a JWT secure.

items.label: Anatomy
items.code: header.payload.signature  // 3 base64url parts split by '.'
items.note: header + payload are just base64 — paste into jwt.io to read. Only signature needs the secret.

items.label: Reusable guard
items.note: next() passes control on; forgetting it hangs the request.

items.label: Protect a route
items.note: Middleware slots between path and handler; runs before the handler.

items.label: Bearer scheme (real-world)
items.code: const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(" ")[1]; // 'Bearer <tok>'
items.note: Course sends raw token; production convention is 'Bearer <token>'.

items.label: Signin -> store token
items.note: Must set Content-Type: application/json or express.json() ignores the body.

items.label: Authenticated request
items.note: Header key must match what the middleware reads (authorization).

items.label: Axios equivalent
items.code: axios.get("/me", { headers: { Authorization: token } });

items.label: Opaque token
items.note: Random string; server stores token->user mapping (stateful). Revoke = delete row. Needs DB lookup per request.

items.label: JWT
items.note: Self-contained + signed; server stores nothing (stateless). Scales horizontally. Verify with secret, no DB hit.

items.label: Trade-off
items.note: JWTs are hard to revoke before expiry — keep expiresIn short or maintain a blocklist. Don't put secrets in the payload (it's readable).

tips: JWT payload is base64, NOT encrypted — anyone can decode and read it. Never put passwords or secrets in it.
jwt.verify (checks signature) vs jwt.decode (does not). Only trust data that came from verify.
Always hash with bcrypt before storing; use bcrypt.compare to check — never compare plaintext or store raw passwords.
Keep JWT_SECRET in an env var (process.env.JWT_SECRET), never hardcoded/committed. Anyone with the secret can forge tokens.
Return identical errors for 'user not found' and 'wrong password' to avoid leaking which usernames exist.
Forgetting next() in middleware silently hangs the request; forgetting express.json() makes req.body undefined.
Set expiresIn on tokens — JWTs can't be revoked server-side without extra state, so short lifetimes limit damage.
Header casing: req.headers keys are lowercased by Node, so read req.headers.authorization regardless of how the client cased it.

items.label: Install
items.code:
npm install recoil
# or
yarn add recoil

items.label: Wrap app in RecoilRoot (required provider)
items.note: Every component using Recoil hooks must be inside a RecoilRoot. You can nest roots; inner root shadows outer state.

items.label: Optional: initialize atoms via initializeState
items.code: <RecoilRoot initializeState={({ set }) => set(countAtom, 10)}>
items.note: Handy for SSR / tests to seed atom values before first render.

sections.title: The Context API problem Recoil solves

items.label: Plain Context: create + provide + consume
items.note: Context is for passing data down without prop-drilling — it is NOT a state manager.

items.label: Gotcha: ANY context value change re-renders ALL consumers
items.note: Every component calling useContext re-renders when the provider value changes, even if it only reads an unrelated field. This is the core pain Recoil fixes with fine-grained subscriptions.

items.label: Object value = new reference every render
items.code:
// BAD: new object each render -> all consumers re-render
<Ctx.Provider value={{ count, setCount }}>
items.note: Wrap in useMemo to stabilize, but you still can't do per-field subscriptions like Recoil atoms.

items.label: Subscription granularity
items.note: Context: all consumers re-render on any change. Recoil: only components subscribed to the specific atom/selector that changed re-render.

items.label: Derived state
items.note: Context has no built-in derivation. Recoil selectors compute derived values with automatic caching + dependency tracking.

items.label: Async
items.note: Context: roll your own. Recoil: async selectors integrate with Suspense out of the box.

items.label: Boilerplate / mental model
items.note: Context = passing values through the tree. Recoil = a graph of atoms (state) and selectors (derived), consumed via hooks anywhere under RecoilRoot.

items.label: When to just use Context
items.note: Rarely-changing global values (theme, locale, auth user). Reach for Recoil when many components read/write shared, frequently-changing state.

items.label: Define an atom
items.note: key MUST be unique across the whole app; duplicate keys throw in dev.

items.label: useRecoilState — read + write (like useState)
items.code:
const [count, setCount] = useRecoilState(countAtom);
setCount(c => c + 1);

items.label: useRecoilValue — read only (subscribes)
items.code: const count = useRecoilValue(countAtom);

items.label: useSetRecoilState — write only (NO re-render on change)
items.code: const setCount = useSetRecoilState(countAtom);
items.note: Use in components that update state but don't display it — avoids unnecessary re-renders.

items.label: useResetRecoilState — reset to default
items.code: const reset = useResetRecoilState(countAtom);

items.label: Object/array default
items.note: Treat atom state as immutable — always return a new array/object, never mutate in place.

items.label: Read-only selector (get)
items.note: Recompiles only when a get() dependency changes; result is cached (memoized) per input.

items.label: Consume a selector
items.code: const doubled = useRecoilValue(doubledCount);
items.note: useRecoilValue works for atoms AND selectors interchangeably.

items.label: Writable selector (get + set)
items.note: set receives ({set,get,reset}, newValue). Use for two-way derived state.

items.label: Async selector (Promise get)
items.note: Suspends the component; wrap consumer in <Suspense fallback={...}>. Use useRecoilValueLoadable to handle loading/error without Suspense.

items.label: Handle async without Suspense
items.note: loadable.state is 'hasValue' | 'loading' | 'hasError'.

items.label: React.memo to stop prop-driven re-renders
items.note: Only skips re-render if props are shallow-equal. Recoil already scopes re-renders per subscribed atom; combine both to isolate expensive subtrees.

items.label: Write-only hook = the 'alternate approach' to avoid re-renders
items.note: Split display vs. mutation components so the setter component doesn't subscribe.

items.label: Selector to isolate the changing slice
items.note: Component re-renders only when title changes, not on every formAtom update.

items.label: atomFamily / selectorFamily — parameterized state
items.note: Each id gets its own atom instance -> editing one row re-renders only that row (classic list-perf fix vs one giant array atom).

tips: Every atom/selector key must be globally unique — a stray duplicate throws 'Duplicate atom key' in dev.
useRecoilValue subscribes (re-renders); useSetRecoilState does NOT. Pick the narrowest hook for the job.
All Recoil hooks must run under a <RecoilRoot>, or you get 'This component must be used inside a RecoilRoot'.
Never mutate atom state in place — spread/return new objects so Recoil detects the change.
Prefer selectors over computing derived values in render: selectors are cached and only recompute when a get() dependency changes.
Async selectors throw Promises — either wrap consumers in <Suspense> or read them with useRecoilValueLoadable.
For long lists, use atomFamily(id) instead of one array atom so a single item update re-renders only that item.
Context is for low-frequency global values (theme/auth); reach for Recoil when shared state changes often and many components read different slices.

items.label: Node.js
items.note: C++ runtime embedding Google's V8 JS engine + libuv event loop. Runs JS outside the browser (servers, CLIs, tooling). Single-threaded event loop, non-blocking async I/O.

items.label: Check version / run a file
items.code:
node -v          # runtime version
node script.js   # execute a file
node             # REPL

items.label: Bun
items.note: All-in-one JS/TS runtime (uses JavaScriptCore, not V8). Drop-in Node replacement + bundler + test runner + package manager. Runs .ts directly, much faster installs.

items.label: Run with Bun
items.code:
bun -v
bun run index.ts   # native TS, no tsc
bun index.ts       # shorthand
bun install        # fast npm-compatible installs

items.label: Runtime globals (no import needed)
items.code:
process, console, __dirname, __filename,
setTimeout, setInterval, Buffer, global
items.note: __dirname/__filename are CJS-only; in ESM use import.meta.url / import.meta.dirname.

items.label: Init package.json interactively
items.code: npm init

items.label: Init with all defaults (fast)
items.code: npm init -y

items.label: Minimal package.json
items.note: Set "type": "module" to use ESM import/export instead of require.

items.label: Run a script
items.code:
npm run start   # runs scripts.start
npm start       # start/test/stop are special: 'run' optional
items.note: Custom scripts always need 'run' (e.g. npm run build).

items.label: Gitignore node_modules
items.code: echo node_modules/ > .gitignore
items.note: Never commit node_modules — reinstall from lockfile instead.

items.label: Install all deps from package.json
items.code: npm install   # or: npm i

items.label: Add a runtime dependency
items.code: npm install express
items.note: Goes under "dependencies"; writes exact tree to package-lock.json.

items.label: Add a dev-only dependency
items.code: npm install -D typescript nodemon
items.note: -D / --save-dev → "devDependencies", excluded in `npm i --production`.

items.label: Install a specific version
items.code:
npm install lodash@4.17.21
npm install react@latest

items.label: Global install (CLIs)
items.code: npm install -g nodemon
items.note: Prefer npx for one-off use to avoid polluting global env.

items.label: Run a package binary without installing
items.code:
npx cowsay "hi"
npx create-react-app my-app

items.label: Remove / update / audit
items.code:
npm uninstall express
npm update
npm outdated
npm audit fix

items.label: Import built-ins (CommonJS)
items.note: Use the 'node:' prefix to be explicit: require('node:fs').

items.label: Import built-ins (ESM)
items.code:
import fs from 'node:fs';
import { readFile } from 'node:fs/promises';

items.label: fs — read/write files
items.note: Always pass 'utf8' or you get a raw Buffer.

items.label: path — build safe paths
process — env, args, exit
items.code:
process.argv;        // CLI args array
process.env.PORT;     // environment vars
process.cwd();        // working dir
process.exit(1);      // exit with code

items.label: Other common built-ins
items.code:
http, https, url, crypto, events,
stream, util, child_process, readline

items.label: Your own file (relative path)
items.note: Must start with ./ or ../ for local files; bare names resolve to node_modules.

items.label: Your own file (ESM)
items.note: ESM requires the file extension in the import path.

items.label: External package (installed)
Resolution order
items.note: Local ./path → core module → node_modules (up the dir tree). Directory imports use its package.json "main" or index.js.

items.label: What package-lock.json is
items.note: Auto-generated. Pins the EXACT version + integrity hash of every dep AND transitive dep so installs are reproducible. Commit it.

items.label: Semver ranges in package.json
items.code:
"express": "^4.18.2"   // caret: 4.x.x  (minor+patch)
"express": "~4.18.2"   // tilde: 4.18.x (patch only)
"express": "4.18.2"    // exact
items.note: ^ allows minor bumps, ~ only patch. Lockfile records what was actually installed within that range.

items.label: Clean reproducible install (CI)
items.code: npm ci
items.note: Installs strictly from lockfile, deletes node_modules first, fails if package.json & lock disagree. Faster than `npm i`.

items.label: dependencies vs devDependencies
items.note: Runtime code (express, dotenv) → dependencies. Build/test tooling (typescript, jest, nodemon) → devDependencies.

items.label: Read CLI arguments
items.note: argv[0]=node path, argv[1]=script path, real args start at index 2.

items.label: Basic command router
Persist data to a JSON file
Interactive prompts (readline)
Make it an executable bin
items.code:
// top of index.js
#!/usr/bin/env node
items.note: Add "bin": { "mycli": "index.js" } to package.json, then `npm link` to run `mycli` globally.

items.label: Parse flags cleanly (Node 18.3+)
items.code:
const { values } = require('node:util')
  .parseArgs({ options: { name: { type: 'string' } } });
items.note: Built-in flag parser; for anything richer use commander or yargs.

tips: process.argv real args start at index 2 — argv[0] is the node binary and argv[1] is the script path.
Commit package-lock.json; never commit node_modules. Use `npm ci` in CI for exact, reproducible installs.
`^` (caret) allows minor+patch upgrades, `~` (tilde) only patch. The lockfile records the exact resolved version.
require() is synchronous CommonJS; import is ESM. Mixing them needs "type":"module" or the .mjs/.cjs extensions — pick one per file.
Local imports must start with ./ or ../; a bare name like 'fs' or 'express' resolves to a core module or node_modules.
fs.readFileSync returns a Buffer unless you pass an encoding like 'utf8'.
Node's I/O is async/non-blocking — prefer fs.promises / await over the Sync variants in servers to avoid blocking the event loop.
Use npx to run a package binary once (npx create-react-app) instead of installing it globally.
Bun runs TypeScript and installs deps natively with no extra tooling — `bun index.ts`, `bun install` — and is largely npm/Node compatible.

items.label: Methods (verbs)
items.code:
GET     // read, no body, idempotent, cacheable
POST    // create / non-idempotent action
PUT     // full replace (idempotent)
PATCH   // partial update
DELETE  // remove (idempotent)
OPTIONS // preflight / capabilities
HEAD    // GET headers only, no body
items.note: Idempotent = same result if repeated. Only GET/HEAD are safe (no side effects).

items.label: Status code families
items.code:
1xx info    2xx success   3xx redirect
4xx client  5xx server
200 OK  201 Created  204 No Content
301 Moved  302 Found  304 Not Modified
400 Bad Request  401 Unauthorized  403 Forbidden
404 Not Found    409 Conflict     429 Too Many
500 Internal  502 Bad Gateway  503 Unavailable
items.note: 401 = not authenticated; 403 = authenticated but not allowed.

items.label: Request line + anatomy
items.code:
POST /api/users?active=1 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"name":"jae"}
items.note: Blank line separates headers from body. Query string is part of the URL, not the body.

items.label: Content negotiation
items.code:
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Accept: application/json
Accept-Encoding: gzip, br
Content-Length: 348
items.note: Server MUST set Content-Type on responses so the client parses correctly.

items.label: Auth
items.code:
Authorization: Bearer <jwt>
Authorization: Basic base64(user:pass)
WWW-Authenticate: Bearer   // sent with 401
items.note: Never log Authorization headers.

items.label: Caching
items.code:
Cache-Control: no-store | no-cache | max-age=3600
ETag: "abc123"
If-None-Match: "abc123"   // -> 304 if unchanged
items.note: no-store = never cache; no-cache = revalidate before use.

items.label: Cookies
items.code:
Set-Cookie: sid=xyz; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict; Max-Age=3600
Cookie: sid=xyz   // sent back by browser
items.note: HttpOnly blocks JS access (XSS defense); SameSite mitigates CSRF.

items.label: Read / set in Express
items.note: req.headers keys are always lowercase in Node.

items.label: GET + parse JSON
items.note: fetch only rejects on network failure, never on HTTP error status.

items.label: POST JSON
items.note: Must JSON.stringify the body AND set Content-Type manually.

items.label: Response helpers
items.code:
res.ok        // true if status 200-299
res.status    // 200
res.headers.get('content-type')
await res.text() / .json() / .blob() / .arrayBuffer()
items.note: Body can only be consumed once.

items.label: Timeout / abort
items.note: Also: AbortSignal.timeout(5000) as a one-liner signal.

items.label: Credentials & mode
items.note: credentials 'include' requires the server to send Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true (and a non-wildcard origin).

items.label: Raw Node http
items.note: res.end() must be called or the request hangs.

items.label: Express setup
items.note: Without express.json() req.body is undefined for JSON requests.

items.label: Route params & query
Send responses
items.note: Set status BEFORE json/send; sending twice throws ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT.

items.label: Signature & next()
items.note: Forget next() (and don't send a response) => request hangs forever.

items.label: Order matters
items.code:
app.use(express.json());   // 1st
app.use(logger);           // 2nd
app.get('/x', handler);    // runs after both
items.note: Middleware runs top-to-bottom; register body parsers before routes.

items.label: Short-circuit (auth gate)
items.note: return the res call so next() is never reached after responding.

items.label: Error-handling middleware
items.note: Must have exactly 4 params and be registered LAST. In Express 4, async errors need next(err); Express 5 auto-forwards rejected promises.

items.label: Per-route inline
items.note: Middleware args between path and handler run only for that route.

items.label: Chain multiple
items.code: app.post('/admin', auth, isAdmin, rateLimit, handler);
items.note: Executed left to right; each must call next() to continue the chain.

items.label: Path-mounted
items.code: app.use('/api', apiMiddleware);   // runs for any /api/*
items.note: app.use with a path prefix scopes middleware to that subtree.

items.label: Router-level
items.note: Great for grouping and mounting a feature under one prefix.

items.label: Body parsers (built-in)
items.code:
app.use(express.json());                       // JSON
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // form posts
app.use(express.static('public'));             // serve files
items.note: No more separate body-parser package; it's built into Express 4.16+.

items.label: morgan — request logging
items.code:
npm i morgan
app.use(require('morgan')('dev'));

items.label: helmet — security headers
items.code:
npm i helmet
app.use(require('helmet')());
items.note: Sets sane defaults (CSP, X-Frame-Options, etc.).

items.label: express-rate-limit
items.note: Returns 429 when exceeded.

items.label: cookie-parser
Why
items.code:
// Origin = scheme + host + port
// http://a.com:3000  vs  http://a.com:4000  => cross-origin
items.note: Enforced by the BROWSER, not the server. Server-to-server calls ignore CORS.

items.label: cors package
items.note: app.use(cors()) globally; or cors(opts) per-route.

items.label: Restrict origin + credentials
items.note: With credentials:true you CANNOT use origin '*' — must echo a specific origin.

items.label: Manual headers
Preflight (OPTIONS)
items.note: Preflight response needs 2xx + Allow-* headers, or the real request is blocked.

tips: fetch never throws on 4xx/5xx — always check res.ok before parsing.
A middleware that neither calls next() nor sends a response hangs the request; always do one or the other.
Register body parsers (express.json) BEFORE routes, and the error handler (4-arg) LAST.
Set res.status() before .json()/.send(); calling res twice throws ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT.
CORS is browser-enforced — curl/Postman/server-to-server calls bypass it, so 'works in Postman' doesn't mean CORS is configured.
credentials:'include' (fetch) requires a specific Allow-Origin (no '*') plus Access-Control-Allow-Credentials:true.
Node lowercases all req.headers keys — use req.headers['content-type'], not 'Content-Type'.
401 = who are you (unauthenticated); 403 = I know you, still not allowed (unauthorized).

items.label: Install stack (Express + Mongoose + Zod + bcrypt + JWT)
items.code: npm install express mongoose zod bcrypt jsonwebtoken

items.label: Connect to MongoDB Atlas (call before app.listen)
items.note: await/catch the connect — never hardcode creds; keep the URI in .env.

items.label: Atlas SRV connection string format
items.code: mongodb+srv://<user>:<pass>@cluster0.xxxxx.mongodb.net/todo-app
items.note: The path segment after .net/ is the DB name — omit it and you write to `test`. URL-encode special chars in the password.

items.label: Atlas free tier checklist
items.note: Create M0 cluster -> Database Access (add user/pass) -> Network Access (allow 0.0.0.0/0 for dev) -> Connect -> copy driver URI.

items.label: Define schema + model
items.note: Collection name is pluralized+lowercased automatically: `User` -> `users`.

items.label: Common field options
items.note: `unique: true` builds an index — it is NOT a validator; duplicate insert throws E11000.

items.label: Auto timestamps
items.code: new Schema({ ... }, { timestamps: true });
items.note: Adds managed `createdAt` / `updatedAt` fields.

items.label: Create
Read
items.code:
await User.find({ role: "admin" });      // array
await User.findOne({ email });            // one or null
await User.findById(id);                  // by _id
await Todo.find({}).select("title done"); // projection
items.note: Queries are thenables — always `await` (or `.exec()`). No match returns [] / null, not an error.

items.label: Update
items.code:
await Todo.updateOne({ _id: id }, { $set: { done: true } });
await Todo.findByIdAndUpdate(id, { title }, { new: true });
items.note: `{ new: true }` returns the updated doc (default returns the pre-update one).

items.label: Delete
items.code:
await Todo.deleteOne({ _id: id });
await Todo.deleteMany({ userId: uid });

items.label: Query operators
Define a schema
safeParse — no throw (preferred in routes)
items.note: Use parsed.data (coerced/typed) downstream — do not re-read req.body.

items.label: parse — throws ZodError (let error middleware catch it)
items.code: const data = SignupSchema.parse(req.body);

items.label: Handy validators
items.code:
z.number().int().positive()
z.string().min(3).max(20)
z.enum(["user","admin"])
z.array(z.string())
z.string().datetime()
z.coerce.number()  // "5" -> 5 (query params)
items.note: Validate as reusable middleware: `(schema) => (req,res,next) => {...}` to keep routes clean.

items.label: Hash on signup
items.note: Never store plaintext. Salt rounds ~10-12; higher = slower = safer.

items.label: Verify on signin
items.note: Return the same generic message for bad email AND bad password (avoid user enumeration).

items.label: Issue JWT after auth
items.code: const token = jwt.sign({ id: user._id }, process.env.JWT_SECRET);

items.label: try/catch per async route
items.note: Un-awaited/unhandled rejections in async handlers do NOT auto-reach error middleware in Express 4.

items.label: Global error middleware (4 args, register LAST)
items.note: Must have exactly 4 params or Express treats it as a normal handler.

items.label: Async wrapper (forward errors to middleware)
404 fallback
items.code: app.use((req, res) => res.status(404).json({ msg: "Not found" }));

items.label: Referenced (normalized) — store ObjectId + ref
items.note: `ref` string must match the model name passed to model("User").

items.label: populate — join the referenced doc
items.code: await Todo.find({ userId }).populate("userId", "email name");
items.note: 2nd arg = field projection on the joined doc. Adds a query round-trip.

items.label: Embedded (denormalized) — subdocuments
items.note: Embed for data read together & bounded in size; reference for shared/unbounded/large relations.

items.label: Filter by owner (auth scoping)
items.code: await Todo.find({ userId: req.userId });
items.note: Always scope queries to the authenticated user — never trust an id from the request body.

tips: MongoDB is schema-less at the DB level — Mongoose schemas + Zod are what actually enforce structure. Validate at the edge (Zod) and at the model (Mongoose).
`unique: true` is an index, not validation: it only errors on write (E11000 code 11000), and only after the index is built. Handle that error code explicitly.
Every Mongoose query returns a thenable but does nothing until awaited — a forgotten `await` gives you a Query object, not data.
Never return raw Mongo/Zod errors to clients — they leak schema and internals. Map to clean status codes (400/403/409/500) in error middleware.
Hash passwords with bcrypt BEFORE saving and strip `password` from any response (`.select('-password')`).
Store secrets (MONGO_URL, JWT_SECRET) in .env + process.env; add .env to .gitignore. A leaked Atlas URI = full DB access.
`findByIdAndUpdate` returns the OLD doc unless you pass `{ new: true }`; validators don't run on updates unless `{ runValidators: true }`.
Prefer `safeParse` in request handlers (branch on `.success`) and reserve throwing `parse` for code paths funneled through global error middleware.

items.label: Create app with Vite (recommended, fast)
items.code:
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react
cd my-app
npm install
npm run dev
items.note: Use react-ts template for TypeScript. Vite dev server has HMR out of the box.

items.label: Create React App (legacy, slower)
items.code:
npx create-react-app my-app
npm start
items.note: CRA is deprecated; prefer Vite or a framework (Next.js) for new projects.

items.label: Entry point wiring
items.note: React 18+ uses createRoot, not the old ReactDOM.render.

items.label: StrictMode double-invokes in dev
items.code: createRoot(el).render(<StrictMode><App /></StrictMode>);
items.note: Renders/effects run twice in dev only to surface impure logic. Not in production.

items.label: Function component + destructured props
items.note: Props are read-only. Component names MUST be capitalized or React treats them as HTML tags.

items.label: Default props via destructuring defaults
items.code: function Btn({ variant = 'primary', children }) { ... }

items.label: children — content between tags
items.note: children is a special prop; enables composition/slot patterns.

items.label: Fragment — group without extra DOM node
items.note: Use <React.Fragment key={id}> when you need a key in a list.

items.label: Spread + rest props
useState — local reactive state
items.code:
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
setCount(count + 1);
items.note: State updates are async & batched; the variable doesn't change until re-render.

items.label: Functional update (avoid stale state)
items.code: setCount(c => c + 1);
items.note: Use updater form when new state depends on previous, esp. in loops/handlers firing rapidly.

items.label: Never mutate — replace objects/arrays
items.code:
setUser(u => ({ ...u, name: 'Al' }));
setList(l => [...l, item]);
items.note: Mutating in place won't trigger a re-render; React compares by reference.

items.label: Lazy initial state (expensive init)
items.code: const [v] = useState(() => heavyCompute());
items.note: Pass a function so it runs only on first render, not every render.

items.label: useEffect — side effects after render
items.note: [] = run once on mount. [dep] = run when dep changes. No array = run every render.

items.label: Data fetching in effect
items.note: Guard against race conditions / setting state after unmount.

items.label: Conditional rendering
items.note: Beware {count && <X/>} — if count is 0, React renders the literal 0. Use count > 0.

items.label: Lists + keys
items.note: key must be stable & unique among siblings. NEVER use array index if list reorders/filters.

items.label: Inline styling (object, camelCase)
items.code: <div style={{ backgroundColor: 'red', fontSize: 14 }} />
items.note: Values are strings or numbers (px assumed). Prefer className/CSS for anything reusable.

items.label: className, not class
items.code: <div className="card active" />
items.note: class is reserved in JS; htmlFor replaces the label 'for' attribute.

items.label: Tracking re-renders (debug)
items.code:
console.log('Rendered:', Component.name);
// or React DevTools -> Profiler / 'Highlight updates'
items.note: A component re-renders when its state changes, its parent re-renders, or its context changes.

items.label: Prevent unnecessary re-renders
items.note: memo skips re-render if props are shallow-equal. Pair with useCallback/useMemo for reference stability.

items.label: Class component (legacy)
items.note: Prefer function components + hooks. Classes still needed for error boundaries.

items.label: Lifecycle -> hook mapping
items.code:
componentDidMount    -> useEffect(fn, [])
componentDidUpdate   -> useEffect(fn, [deps])
componentWillUnmount -> return cleanup in useEffect
items.note: One useEffect can express mount+update+unmount together.

items.label: Error boundary (class only)
items.note: Catches render-phase errors in children only. NOT event handlers, async, or SSR.

items.label: Install & set up router
items.code: npm i react-router-dom
items.note: SPA = one HTML page; router swaps components client-side without full reloads.

items.label: Route config
items.code:
<BrowserRouter>
  <Routes>
    <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
    <Route path="/users/:id" element={<User />} />
    <Route path="*" element={<NotFound />} />
  </Routes>
</BrowserRouter>

items.label: Navigation (no page reload)
items.note: Use <Link>/useNavigate, NOT <a href> — <a> triggers a full reload and loses state.

items.label: Read route params / search
items.code:
const { id } = useParams();
const [params] = useSearchParams();

items.label: Layout via nested routes + Outlet
items.code:
<Route element={<Layout />}>
  <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
  <Route path="/settings" element={<Settings />} />
</Route>
// Layout: <nav/> + <Outlet /> renders the matched child
items.note: Shared chrome (navbar/sidebar) stays mounted while <Outlet/> swaps the page.

items.label: Lazy-load routes
items.code:
const About = lazy(() => import('./About'));
<Suspense fallback={<Loading/>}><About/></Suspense>
items.note: Code-splitting keeps initial bundle small.

items.label: useRef — DOM access
useRef — mutable value that survives renders
items.code:
const renders = useRef(0);
renders.current++;
items.note: Changing ref.current does NOT trigger a re-render (unlike state).

items.label: Custom hook — extract reusable stateful logic
items.note: Must start with 'use' and may call other hooks. Shares logic, not state — each call gets its own state.

items.label: Rules of Hooks
items.code:
// ✅ top level of component/hook
// ❌ not inside if/for/nested fns
items.note: Call hooks in the same order every render so React can track them.

sections.title: State Sharing: Lift, Prop Drilling, Context, Recoil

items.label: Lift state up (rolling up state)
items.code:
// Move shared state to closest common parent,
// pass value + setter down as props.
items.note: Lifting can cause a big subtree to re-render — split components / memoize to limit blast radius.

items.label: Prop drilling (the problem)
items.code:
<A user={user}>
  <B user={user}>
    <C user={user} /> // passed through B just to reach C
items.note: Passing props through intermediate components that don't use them → Context/state lib.

items.label: Context API — avoid drilling
items.note: Every consumer re-renders when the Provider value changes. Memoize the value object.

items.label: Context re-render gotcha (testing/perf)
items.code:
const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, setUser }), [user]);
<Ctx.Provider value={value}>
items.note: New object literal each render = all consumers re-render even if data is unchanged.

items.label: Recoil — atom-level state (granular re-renders)
items.note: Only components subscribed to a specific atom re-render — fixes Context's over-rendering. selector() for derived state.

tips: State updates are async and batched — read the new value after re-render or use the functional updater setX(prev => ...).
Never mutate state/props directly; always create a new object/array so React's reference comparison detects the change.
{value && <Comp/>} renders a literal 0 when value is 0 — use value > 0 && ... or a ternary.
useEffect with a missing/wrong dependency array is the #1 bug source: [] runs once, [deps] on change, no array every render. Return a cleanup fn for subscriptions/timers.
List keys must be stable and unique; using the array index breaks when items are reordered, inserted, or filtered.
Context Provider re-renders ALL consumers when its value changes — wrap the value in useMemo; reach for Recoil/Zustand when re-render granularity matters.
Error boundaries only catch errors during rendering of children — not in event handlers, async code, or SSR (wrap those in try/catch).
Use <Link>/useNavigate for SPA navigation; a plain <a href> forces a full page reload and wipes React state.

items.label: Create React app (Vite recommended)
items.code:
npm create vite@latest my-dapp -- --template react
cd my-dapp && npm install

items.label: Install wallet-adapter + web3.js
Buffer polyfill (Vite only — web3.js needs Node globals)
items.note: Without this you get "Buffer is not defined" on Vite. CRA already polyfills it.

items.label: Provider tree: Connection > Wallet > WalletModal
items.note: Order matters: ConnectionProvider outermost, WalletModalProvider innermost.

items.label: Endpoint options
items.code:
clusterApiUrl('devnet')       // https://api.devnet.solana.com
clusterApiUrl('mainnet-beta') // rate-limited; use a paid RPC in prod
'https://api.devnet.solana.com' // or a raw string

items.label: wallets prop — usually just []
items.note: Wrap adapter array in useMemo([]) so it isn't recreated every render.

items.label: Drop-in connect / disconnect buttons
useConnection — RPC connection object
items.code:
import { useConnection } from '@solana/wallet-adapter-react';
const { connection } = useConnection();

items.label: useWallet — everything about the connected wallet
items.note: Always guard on publicKey before RPC calls; it's null until connected.

items.label: Request devnet SOL to the connected wallet
LAMPORTS_PER_SOL
items.code: LAMPORTS_PER_SOL === 1_000_000_000 // 1 SOL = 1e9 lamports
items.note: web3.js works in lamports (integers). Always multiply/divide by this.

items.label: Fetch balance (lamports → SOL)
items.code:
const lamports = await connection.getBalance(publicKey);
const sol = lamports / LAMPORTS_PER_SOL;

items.label: Fetch in an effect, re-run on key change
Live updates via account subscription
items.note: Cheaper than polling; unsubscribe in the effect cleanup.

items.label: Sign arbitrary bytes (off-chain, no fee)
Verify the signature (ed25519)
items.note: @noble/curves ships with web3.js. Signing proves ownership without spending gas — used for auth.

items.label: Build + send a transfer transaction
items.note: sendTransaction auto-fills recent blockhash + feePayer and prompts the wallet to sign.

items.label: Assignment shape (tie it together)
items.note: Test end-to-end on devnet with a throwaway Phantom account.

tips: Import '@solana/wallet-adapter-react-ui/styles.css' once — without it the WalletMultiButton renders unstyled.
publicKey is null until connected: guard every RPC/tx call with `if (!publicKey) return` to avoid runtime crashes.
Everything on-chain is in lamports (integers). Convert with LAMPORTS_PER_SOL (1e9); never send floats as lamports.
Devnet airdrops are heavily rate-limited — request 1-2 SOL max; if it 429s, use faucet.solana.com in the browser.
signMessage/signTransaction can be undefined depending on the wallet — check before calling, don't assume support.
Wrap the `wallets` array and `endpoint` in useMemo so providers don't reset the connection on every render.
Modern wallets (Phantom, Solflare, Backpack) auto-register via the Wallet Standard, so `wallets={[]}` is enough — you rarely need explicit adapters.
On Vite, polyfill Buffer (globalThis.Buffer = Buffer) or you'll hit 'Buffer is not defined' from web3.js.
Always confirmTransaction after airdrop/send before reading balance, or the UI shows stale numbers.

items.label: What HTTP is
items.note: HyperText Transfer Protocol: stateless, text-based, application-layer protocol (layer 7) riding on TCP. Each request is independent — server keeps no memory between requests (that's why we bolt on cookies/tokens for state).

items.label: Why a protocol at all
items.note: A shared contract so any client (browser, curl, mobile app) and any server (Node, Go, nginx) can talk. Defines the exact bytes: method + path + version, headers, blank line, body.

items.label: Request/Response cycle
items.code:
Client  --- REQUEST  --->  Server
        <--- RESPONSE ---
items.note: Client always initiates; server only replies. One request => exactly one response.

items.label: Raw HTTP/1.1 request wire format
items.code:
GET /users/42 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Accept: application/json
items.note: Request line, headers, blank line (CRLF CRLF), then optional body. Blank line separates headers from body.

items.label: Raw HTTP response wire format
items.code:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 27

{"id":42,"name":"Alice"}
items.note: Status line, headers, blank line, body.

items.label: URL anatomy
items.code:
https://api.example.com:443/users?id=42#top
  |      |               |    |      |     |
scheme  host           port  path  query  fragment
items.note: Fragment (#top) is client-only — never sent to the server.

items.label: Domain -> IP (DNS)
items.code:
nslookup example.com
dig +short example.com
items.note: Humans use names (example.com); machines route by IP (93.184.x.x). DNS resolves name -> IP before the TCP connection opens.

items.label: localhost / loopback
items.note: Traffic never leaves your machine.

items.label: Ports = which process on the host
items.note: IP finds the machine; port finds the app on it. 0-65535; <1024 are privileged/well-known.

items.label: Common ports
items.code:
80    HTTP
443   HTTPS
3000  Node/React dev (convention)
5432  Postgres
27017 MongoDB
6379  Redis
items.note: 80 and 443 are implied by http:// and https:// so you rarely type them.

items.label: Check / free a port (Windows)
items.code:
netstat -ano | findstr :3000
taskkill /PID <pid> /F
items.note: macOS/Linux: lsof -i :3000  then  kill -9 <pid>.

items.label: GET — read, no body
items.code: curl https://api.example.com/users
items.note: Safe + idempotent. Params go in the query string, not the body. Cacheable.

items.label: POST — create / non-idempotent action
items.code: curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"name":"Alice"}' https://api.example.com/users
items.note: Sends a body. Calling twice creates two resources.

items.label: PUT — replace/upsert (idempotent)
items.code: curl -X PUT -d '{"name":"Bob"}' https://api.example.com/users/42
items.note: Full replacement of the resource. Same call N times = same end state.

items.label: PATCH — partial update
items.code: curl -X PATCH -d '{"name":"Bob"}' https://api.example.com/users/42
items.note: Only send the fields you change.

items.label: DELETE — remove
items.code: curl -X DELETE https://api.example.com/users/42
items.note: Idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource still ends deleted.

items.label: OPTIONS — capabilities / CORS preflight
items.note: Browsers auto-fire OPTIONS before non-simple cross-origin requests to check CORS headers. You don't call it by hand.

items.label: Idempotency at a glance
items.code:
Safe:       GET, HEAD, OPTIONS
Idempotent: GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD
Neither:    POST, PATCH
items.note: The method is a convention — the server must actually enforce the semantics.

items.label: Class meanings (first digit)
items.code:
1xx  informational
2xx  success
3xx  redirect
4xx  client error (your fault)
5xx  server error (their fault)

items.label: 2xx success
items.code:
200 OK              GET/PUT/PATCH ok
201 Created         POST made a resource
204 No Content      success, empty body (e.g. DELETE)

items.label: 3xx redirect
items.code:
301 Moved Permanently
302 Found (temp)
304 Not Modified      cache hit, no re-download

items.label: 4xx client error
items.code:
400 Bad Request       malformed / validation fail
401 Unauthorized      not authenticated (no/ bad creds)
403 Forbidden         authed but not allowed
404 Not Found
405 Method Not Allowed
429 Too Many Requests  rate limited
items.note: 401 = who are you? 403 = I know you, no.

items.label: 5xx server error
items.code:
500 Internal Server Error   unhandled exception
502 Bad Gateway             upstream returned garbage
503 Service Unavailable     down/overloaded
504 Gateway Timeout

items.label: What headers are
items.note: Key:value metadata about the request/response — not the payload itself. Case-insensitive keys.

items.label: Common request headers
items.code:
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <token>
User-Agent: curl/8.4.0
Cookie: session=abc123

items.label: Common response headers
items.code:
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 27
Set-Cookie: session=abc123; HttpOnly
Cache-Control: max-age=3600
Location: /users/42   (with 201/3xx)

items.label: Body — the actual payload
items.note: Present on POST/PUT/PATCH requests and most responses. Format is declared by Content-Type. GET/DELETE typically have no request body.

items.label: Content-Type examples
items.code:
application/json
application/x-www-form-urlencoded
multipart/form-data      (file uploads)
text/html

items.label: Routes = method + path
items.code:
GET    /users        list
GET    /users/:id    read one
POST   /users        create
PUT    /users/:id    replace
DELETE /users/:id    delete
items.note: Same path, different method = different route. Path params (:id) vs query (?sort=asc) vs body serve different jobs.

items.label: curl — verbose, see full exchange
items.code: curl -v https://api.example.com/users
items.note: -v prints request (>) and response (<) lines including headers.

items.label: curl — JSON POST with auth
items.code:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/users \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer TOKEN' \
  -d '{"name":"Alice"}'

items.label: curl — headers only / status only
items.code:
curl -I https://example.com                       # HEAD, headers only
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' URL      # just the code
items.note: On Windows use  -o NUL  instead of /dev/null.

items.label: Browser as a client
items.note: URL bar / links fire GET; <form method="post"> fires POST. Inspect everything in DevTools > Network (see method, status, headers, payload, timing). fetch() from console for ad-hoc calls.

items.label: Postman
items.note: GUI client: pick method, set URL, add Headers/Params/Body tabs, save into Collections, use {{variables}} for envs. Great for auth flows and sharing; curl is faster for scripting/CI.

items.label: Bare Node.js server (no deps)
items.note: req is a readable stream — body arrives in chunks, not as a ready string.

items.label: Reading a JSON request body (raw Node)
items.note: Frameworks/body-parsers do this for you.

items.label: Express server (idiomatic)
items.note: req.params (path), req.query (?a=b), req.body (parsed). res.json() serializes + sets header. Order of app.use matters.

items.label: Client: fetch (browser + Node 18+)
items.note: fetch does NOT reject on 4xx/5xx — always check res.ok. body must be a string; JSON.stringify it yourself.

tips: HTTP is stateless: the server forgets you between requests. State (login) is faked via cookies/tokens sent on every request.
fetch() only rejects on network failure — a 404 or 500 still resolves. Guard with `if (!res.ok)` before parsing.
Query string = filtering/sorting/pagination (GET); request body = the resource payload (POST/PUT/PATCH). Don't put secrets in the query string — it lands in logs and history.
401 vs 403: 401 = not authenticated (login), 403 = authenticated but not permitted. 404 is often used to hide existence of a resource from unauthorized users.
Set Content-Type correctly or the other side mis-parses the body; a POST with JSON but no `Content-Type: application/json` often arrives as undefined/empty on the server.
The method (GET/POST/...) is only a convention — the server decides what actually happens. Keep GET side-effect-free and POST non-idempotent to match caches, proxies, and retries.
Port already in use (EADDRINUSE) = a previous process is still bound. Find and kill it: `netstat -ano | findstr :3000` then `taskkill /PID <pid> /F`.

CORS is a browser rule, not an HTTP error: cross-origin requests need the server to return Access-Control-* headers. curl/Postman ignore CORS, so 'works in Postman, fails in browser' is almost always CORS.

items.label: Instruction
items.note: Single operation aimed at one program: { programId, keys (accounts), data }. Does nothing on its own.

items.label: Transaction
items.note: Atomic bundle of 1+ instructions. All-or-nothing: if any instruction fails, the whole tx reverts.

items.label: Required tx fields
items.code:
tx.feePayer = wallet.publicKey;
tx.recentBlockhash = (await connection.getLatestBlockhash()).blockhash;
items.note: Without feePayer + recentBlockhash the tx can't be signed/sent.

items.label: Signers
items.note: Wallet signs as feePayer via sendTransaction; any NEW account (e.g. mint Keypair) must partialSign the tx before sending.

items.label: Why bundle instructions
items.note: createAccount + initMint + initMetadata in ONE tx = mint is never left half-initialized.

items.label: Scaffold Vite + React
items.code:
npm create vite@latest token-launchpad -- --template react
cd token-launchpad && npm install

items.label: Wallet adapter packages
Web3 + SPL token
items.code: npm i @solana/web3.js @solana/spl-token @solana/spl-token-metadata

items.label: Run dev server
items.code: npm run dev

items.label: Install plugin
items.code: npm i -D vite-plugin-node-polyfills
items.note: Browser lacks Buffer/process/global that @solana/web3.js expects. Without this you get 'Buffer is not defined'.

items.label: vite.config.js
Manual alternative
items.note: Some setups just alias global->globalThis and inject window.Buffer = Buffer from 'buffer'; the plugin is cleaner.

items.label: Import UI styles once
items.code: import '@solana/wallet-adapter-react-ui/styles.css';
items.note: Skipping this leaves the wallet modal/button unstyled.

items.label: Wrap app in providers
items.code:
import { ConnectionProvider, WalletProvider } from '@solana/wallet-adapter-react';
import { WalletModalProvider } from '@solana/wallet-adapter-react-ui';

<ConnectionProvider endpoint={'https://api.devnet.solana.com'}>
  <WalletProvider wallets={[]} autoConnect>
    <WalletModalProvider>
      <TokenLaunchpad />
    </WalletModalProvider>
  </WalletProvider>
</ConnectionProvider>
items.note: Order matters: Connection > Wallet > WalletModal. wallets={[]} works because standard wallets (Phantom etc.) auto-register.

items.label: Connect / disconnect buttons
Access connection + wallet
items.note: Hooks only work inside the providers. Guard on wallet.publicKey before acting.

items.label: Server-style helper (needs a Keypair)
items.note: createMint requires a payer Keypair to sign — browser wallets never expose a secret key, so this can't run client-side.

items.label: Client pattern instead
items.note: Build the raw instructions (createAccount + initMint) yourself and sign via wallet.sendTransaction. That's what the launchpad does.

items.label: Token program choice
items.code: import { TOKEN_2022_PROGRAM_ID } from '@solana/spl-token';
items.note: Use Token-2022 for built-in on-chain metadata (metadata pointer extension). Legacy TOKEN_PROGRAM_ID needs Metaplex for metadata.

items.label: Imports
Generate mint keypair + metadata
Size + rent (mint space + metadata)
items.note: createAccount space is ONLY mintLen; but fund rent for mintLen + metadataLen (metadata realloc's the account later).

items.label: Build + partial-sign tx
items.note: Metadata pointer points the mint at ITSELF, so metadata: mintKeypair.publicKey. initMint must run before initMetadata.

items.label: Derive Associated Token Account
items.note: Pass TOKEN_2022_PROGRAM_ID — omitting it derives the wrong (legacy) ATA address.

items.label: Create the ATA
items.note: ATA is the account that actually holds a user's balance for this mint.

items.label: Mint supply into the ATA
items.note: Amount is in base units: 1 token at 9 decimals = 1e9. Signer/authority = wallet (the mint authority set above).

tips: Use devnet (https://api.devnet.solana.com) and airdrop SOL first — creating a mint + ATA costs rent + fees.
'Buffer is not defined' / 'global is not defined' => the node-polyfills plugin isn't wired into vite.config.js.
Any freshly generated account (the mint Keypair) MUST partialSign the tx; the wallet only signs as feePayer.
Instruction order is load-bearing: createAccount -> initMetadataPointer -> initMint -> initMetadata. initMetadata fails if the mint isn't initialized yet.
Fund rent for mintLen + metadataLen but set createAccount space = mintLen only; Token-2022 reallocs when metadata is written.
Mint amounts are in base units — multiply human amount by 10^decimals or you'll mint 1e-9 of a token.
Match the program ID everywhere (mint, ATA derivation, mintTo). Mixing TOKEN_PROGRAM_ID with TOKEN_2022 gives 'incorrect program id' / wrong ATA.
Guard every handler on wallet.publicKey (and connected) so hooks don't throw before a wallet is connected.

items.label: On-chain account layout (every account has these 5 fields)
items.code:
pub struct Account {
    pub lamports: u64,       // SOL balance
    pub data: Vec<u8>,       // raw state bytes
    pub owner: Pubkey,       // program allowed to mutate this
    pub executable: bool,    // true = program account
    pub rent_epoch: Epoch,   // legacy rent field
}
items.note: Everything on Solana is an account — wallets, programs, program state, token mints.

items.label: AccountInfo (what your program receives at runtime)
items.code:
pub struct AccountInfo<'a> {
    pub key: &'a Pubkey,
    pub lamports: Rc<RefCell<&'a mut u64>>,
    pub data:     Rc<RefCell<&'a mut [u8]>>,
    pub owner: &'a Pubkey,
    pub is_signer: bool,
    pub is_writable: bool,
    pub executable: bool,
}
items.note: is_signer / is_writable come from the transaction, not from the account state.

items.label: The ownership rule (core invariant)
items.note: Only the OWNER program can mutate data or DEBIT lamports. ANYONE can CREDIT lamports (send SOL). Non-owners get read-only view.

items.label: Inspect accounts / rent from CLI
items.code:
solana account <PUBKEY>        # dump owner, lamports, data
solana balance <PUBKEY>
solana rent 200                # rent-exempt minimum for 200 bytes

items.label: Native entrypoint: program_id + accounts + data
items.note: Accounts arrive in a fixed order defined by the client — you validate each one manually.

items.label: Anchor Context<T> — typed, validated accounts
items.note: Access as ctx.accounts.vault, ctx.bumps.vault, ctx.remaining_accounts.

items.label: Accounts struct = declarative validation
items.code:
#[derive(Accounts)]
pub struct Deposit<'info> {
    #[account(mut)]
    pub user: Signer<'info>,
    #[account(mut)]
    pub vault: Account<'info, Vault>,
    pub system_program: Program<'info, System>,
}

items.label: ID + role
items.note: Owns all new/empty accounts & wallets. Only it can allocate space, assign owners, and create accounts.

items.label: create_account: alloc space + fund rent + set owner (one op)
items.note: new_acc must sign (it's a keypair) unless created as a PDA via invoke_signed.

items.label: transfer SOL
allocate + assign (two-step ownership handoff)
items.code:
system_instruction::allocate(acc.key, space as u64);
system_instruction::assign(acc.key, &new_owner);
items.note: Alternative to create_account when the account already holds lamports.

items.label: Loader IDs
items.code:
BPFLoaderUpgradeab1e11111111111111111111111  # upgradeable (default)
BPFLoader2111111111111111111111111111111111  # v2, immutable
items.note: A deployed program's OWNER is the loader. executable=true. Loader marks the account executable after deploy.

items.label: Program vs ProgramData account (upgradeable)
items.note: Program account is a thin pointer -> ProgramData account, which stores the actual bytecode + the upgrade_authority. Upgrading rewrites ProgramData.

items.label: Deploy / upgrade
items.code:
solana program deploy target/deploy/prog.so
solana program deploy --program-id <ID> prog.so   # upgrade in place
solana program show <PROGRAM_ID>                   # authority, size, slot

items.label: Manage upgrade authority
items.code:
solana program set-upgrade-authority <ID> \
    --new-upgrade-authority <PUBKEY>
solana program set-upgrade-authority <ID> --final  # immutable forever
items.note: --final is IRREVERSIBLE: no more upgrades, no reclaim. Only current authority can upgrade/close.

items.label: Reclaim rent / grow program
items.code:
solana program close <ID> --recipient <PUBKEY>
solana program extend <ID> <ADDITIONAL_BYTES>

items.label: Signer check (native) — is this action authorized?
Owner check (native) — is this account really mine?
items.note: ALWAYS verify owner before trusting/deserializing account data — else attacker feeds fake accounts.

items.label: Anchor: types enforce checks automatically
items.code:
pub user: Signer<'info>,               // must sign
pub cfg:  Account<'info, Config>,      // owner==program_id + discriminator
pub token_prog: Program<'info, Token>, // owner==Token program
items.note: Account<T> checks owner + 8-byte discriminator. Signer checks is_signer.

items.label: has_one — field-level authority match
items.code:
#[account(mut, has_one = authority)]
pub config: Account<'info, Config>,
pub authority: Signer<'info>,
items.note: Asserts config.authority == authority.key(). Classic admin-gate pattern.

items.label: SPL Token authorities
items.note: mint_authority (who can mint), freeze_authority (who can freeze accounts), account owner (who can transfer). Set to None to permanently disable minting.

items.label: What a PDA is
items.note: Deterministic address OFF the ed25519 curve => no private key exists. Only the deriving program can 'sign' for it. Used for program-owned accounts (vaults, state, escrows).

items.label: find_program_address — get PDA + canonical bump
items.note: Loops bump 255->0 until an off-curve address is found. Use off-chain or at init; it's compute-heavy.

items.label: create_program_address — derive with a known bump (cheap)
items.note: Store the bump on-chain and reuse it to skip the find loop and save CU.

items.label: Anchor: derive + init a PDA account
items.code:
#[account(
    init, payer = user, space = 8 + 40,
    seeds = [b"vault", user.key().as_ref()],
    bump
)]
pub vault: Account<'info, Vault>,
items.note: On later calls use bump = vault.bump to re-check the same PDA. Access as ctx.bumps.vault.

items.label: invoke_signed — program signs for its PDA (native)
items.note: Runtime re-derives the PDA from seeds; if it matches an account, that account is treated as a signer.

items.label: Anchor CPI with signer seeds
Seed constraints
items.note: Max 16 seeds, each <= 32 bytes. The bump is appended as the final seed when signing. Use the canonical (highest) bump only.

tips: Ownership asymmetry: only the owner program can debit lamports & mutate data, but ANYONE can send (credit) SOL to any account.
New accounts are owned by the System Program by default — call create_account (or allocate + assign) to transfer ownership to your program before writing state.
Always check BOTH is_signer and owner in native code. Anchor's Signer / Account<T> types do this for you; skipping owner checks is the #1 exploit vector.
PDAs have no private key (off-curve), so they can only be 'signed' by the owning program via invoke_signed with the exact derivation seeds + bump.
Use find_program_address off-chain / at init, then store the bump and use create_program_address (or Anchor's bump = state.bump) on-chain to save compute — find loops up to 256 times.
Anchor init space must include the 8-byte discriminator: space = 8 + <struct size>. Forgetting the 8 is a classic deserialization bug.
solana program set-upgrade-authority --final is irreversible: the program can never be upgraded or closed again.
Seeds: max 16, each <= 32 bytes, and the bump byte is appended last when signing — signer seeds must exactly match how the PDA was derived or the sign fails.

items.label: What it is
items.note: Full-duplex, persistent TCP connection over a single socket. Bidirectional — server can push without client polling.

items.label: Handshake = HTTP Upgrade
items.code:
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: server.example.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13

// 101 Switching Protocols -> connection becomes a ws
items.note: Starts as HTTP, then switches protocols. After 101, it's no longer HTTP.

items.label: Schemes
items.code:
ws://  -> plaintext (port 80)
wss:// -> TLS       (port 443)
items.note: Use wss:// in production. Browsers block ws:// from https:// pages (mixed content).

items.label: vs HTTP polling / SSE
items.note: Polling = repeated requests (wasteful). SSE = server->client only, text only, auto-reconnect. WS = both directions, binary + text.

items.label: Frame types
items.note: text (UTF-8), binary, ping, pong, close. Ping/pong are control frames for keepalive.

items.label: Install
items.code:
npm install ws          # server lib
npm i -D @types/ws      # TS types

items.label: Standalone server
Attach to existing HTTP/Express server
items.note: Lets HTTP routes and WS share one port. wss listens for upgrade events automatically.

items.label: noServer mode — auth on upgrade
items.note: The ONLY reliable place to authenticate — browsers can't set custom headers on WebSocket(). Reject via socket.destroy().

items.label: Broadcast to all clients
items.note: Always guard on readyState === OPEN (1); a closing socket throws on send.

items.label: Rooms / channels (no built-in support)
items.note: ws has no rooms — you track membership yourself. socket.io gives rooms out of the box.

items.label: Message framing (JSON convention)
items.note: WS gives you a byte stream, not RPC. Define your own {type,payload} envelope. Wrap JSON.parse in try/catch.

items.label: Heartbeat — detect dead connections
items.note: TCP won't tell you a client vanished (dead router, closed laptop). Ping/pong is the only reliable liveness check. terminate() is immediate; close() is graceful.

items.label: Backpressure check
items.code: if (ws.bufferedAmount < 1e6) ws.send(bigChunk);
items.note: bufferedAmount = bytes queued but not yet sent. Growing = slow client; drop or throttle.

items.label: Connect & handle events
items.note: No custom headers allowed on the constructor — pass auth via query string or subprotocol, then validate on upgrade.

items.label: readyState constants
items.code:
WebSocket.CONNECTING // 0
WebSocket.OPEN       // 1  <- only state where send() is safe
WebSocket.CLOSING    // 2
WebSocket.CLOSED     // 3
if (ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) ws.send(data);

items.label: Send / receive binary
items.note: send() accepts string | ArrayBuffer | Blob | TypedArray.

items.label: Close cleanly
items.code: ws.close(1000, 'user left');  // 1000 = normal
items.note: Codes: 1000 normal, 1001 going away, 1006 abnormal (no close frame), 1011 server error. Custom app codes: 4000-4999.

items.label: Subprotocol negotiation
Auto-reconnect with exponential backoff
items.note: Browsers do NOT auto-reconnect WebSockets (unlike EventSource/SSE). Add jitter to avoid thundering-herd reconnects after a server restart.

items.label: App-level heartbeat from client
items.code:
setInterval(() => ws.readyState === 1 &&
  ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: 'ping' })), 25000);
items.note: Proxies/LBs kill idle connections (~60s). Keep traffic flowing under the idle timeout.

items.label: Resume state after reconnect
items.note: Reconnection = fresh connection. Re-auth, re-join rooms, and request a delta since lastSeenId (server should support message replay/cursor).

items.label: Core problem: connections are stateful
items.note: Each socket is pinned to ONE process. Client on server A can't be reached by server B — unlike stateless HTTP behind a round-robin LB.

items.label: Sticky sessions at the load balancer
items.code:
# nginx: hash client to a fixed backend
upstream ws { ip_hash; server a:8080; server b:8080; }
items.note: Keeps a client pinned to its process. Needed if any per-process in-memory state exists. L4 (TCP) LBs also work well for WS.

items.label: nginx proxy — required upgrade headers
items.note: Without Upgrade/Connection headers the handshake fails (101 never happens).

items.label: Pub/Sub backplane for cross-node broadcast
items.note: The key scaling pattern: each node publishes to Redis; every node's subscriber pushes to ITS local clients. Now server A can reach clients on server B.

items.label: socket.io + Redis adapter
items.note: socket.io handles rooms, reconnection, fallbacks, and cross-node fan-out for you. Trade-off: heavier + custom protocol (not raw WS).

items.label: Capacity & tuning
items.code:
# raise open file descriptor limit (each conn = 1 fd)
ulimit -n 100000
items.note: Bottleneck is memory/fds per connection, not CPU. ~tens of thousands of idle conns per node. Offload TLS to the LB; scale horizontally + backplane, not vertically.

tips: send() throws if readyState !== OPEN. Always guard with `ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN` (=== 1) before sending.
Authenticate during the HTTP upgrade (noServer mode / server 'upgrade' event) — browsers can't set custom headers, so pass tokens via query string or subprotocol and validate BEFORE handshake.
Browsers never auto-reconnect a WebSocket. You must implement reconnect with exponential backoff + jitter yourself.
TCP can't detect a silently-dead peer. Implement ping/pong heartbeat + terminate() on missed pong, or you leak zombie connections.
Proxies/LBs drop idle connections (~60s default). Send app-level or protocol pings under that timeout, and raise proxy_read_timeout.
Horizontal scaling needs a Redis (or similar) pub/sub backplane — connections are pinned to one process, so a plain round-robin LB can't route pushes across nodes.
Use wss:// (TLS) always in prod; ws:// from an https page is blocked as mixed content. Terminate TLS at the LB to save app-server CPU.
WS gives you raw bytes, not RPC — define a {type, payload} JSON envelope and wrap JSON.parse in try/catch to survive malformed frames.

items.label: Install (both deprecated Mar 2023 — know for legacy repos)
items.code: npm i -g truffle ganache

items.label: Start local chain (10 funded accounts, chainId 1337)
items.code:
ganache            # CLI, RPC on http://127.0.0.1:8545
ganache -d         # deterministic mnemonic (same keys every run)

items.label: Scaffold a Truffle project
items.code: truffle init       # contracts/, migrations/, test/, truffle-config.js

items.label: Compile / test / migrate
items.code:
truffle compile
truffle test
truffle migrate --network development

items.label: truffle-config.js network block
items.note: network_id '*' matches any chain; Ganache defaults to 1337/5777

items.label: Interactive console against a network
items.code: truffle console --network development

items.label: Init a project
items.code:
mkdir myapp && cd myapp
npm init -y
npm i -D hardhat
npx hardhat init      # pick TypeScript + toolbox

items.label: Compile / test / clean
items.code:
npx hardhat compile
npx hardhat test
npx hardhat clean

items.label: Run the built-in in-process node (replaces Ganache)
items.code: npx hardhat node     # 20 funded accounts, RPC :8545, chainId 31337

items.label: Run a script against a network
items.code: npx hardhat run scripts/deploy.ts --network localhost

items.label: hardhat.config.ts
console.log inside Solidity (debug)
items.code:
import 'hardhat/console.sol';
console.log('bal: %s', balance);
items.note: Only works on the Hardhat network, strip before mainnet deploy

items.label: Install via foundryup (Linux/macOS/WSL)
items.code:
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash
foundryup            # installs forge, cast, anvil, chisel
items.note: On Windows use WSL; foundryup keeps the toolchain updated

items.label: New project
items.code:
forge init hello_foundry
# src/  test/  script/  lib/  foundry.toml

items.label: Build / test / format
items.code:
forge build
forge test
forge fmt

items.label: Local node (Ganache/Hardhat-node equivalent)
items.code: anvil                # RPC :8545, chainId 31337, 10 funded accts

items.label: foundry.toml essentials
items.code:
[profile.default]
src = 'src'
out = 'out'
libs = ['lib']
solc = '0.8.24'
remappings = ['@openzeppelin/=lib/openzeppelin-contracts/']

items.label: Install a dependency (git submodule under lib/)
items.code:
forge install OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts
forge install foundry-rs/forge-std

items.label: Pin a version / tag
items.code: forge install OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts@v5.1.0

items.label: Update / remove
items.code:
forge update
forge remove OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts

items.label: Auto-generate remappings
items.code: forge remappings > remappings.txt
items.note: Import then resolves e.g. `@openzeppelin/contracts/...` to lib/

items.label: Hardhat equivalent (npm, not submodules)
items.code: npm i @openzeppelin/contracts
items.note: Hardhat imports from node_modules; no remappings needed

items.label: Minimal contract (src/Counter.sol)
ERC-20 via OpenZeppelin (src/MyToken.sol)
Standard ERC-20 surface (from base)
items.code:
totalSupply() balanceOf(a) transfer(to,amt)
approve(spender,amt) allowance(o,s) transferFrom(from,to,amt)
items.note: decimals() defaults to 18; override it to change

items.label: Mint / burn helpers
items.code:
_mint(to, amount);
_burn(from, amount);
items.note: Internal — wrap in access-controlled public fns (e.g. Ownable)

items.label: Test file layout (test/MyToken.t.sol)
items.note: setUp() runs before EACH test; fns prefixed test_ are cases

items.label: Common cheatcodes (vm.*)
Test an emitted event
items.note: expectEmit flags = (topic1, topic2, topic3, data); emit the expected event, THEN make the call

items.label: Assertions & fuzzing
items.code:
assertEq(a,b); assertTrue(x); assertGt(a,b);
function testFuzz_Transfer(uint96 amt) public { ... }
items.note: Fn args = fuzzed inputs; use vm.assume() to bound them

items.label: Verbosity & gas
items.code:
forge test -vvv           # show traces on failure
forge test --match-test test_Emit
forge test --gas-report

items.label: Foundry deploy script (script/Deploy.s.sol)
Run the script (broadcast + verify)
items.code:
forge script script/Deploy.s.sol \
  --rpc-url $RPC_URL --private-key $PK \
  --broadcast --verify
items.note: Drop --broadcast for a dry-run simulation; --verify needs ETHERSCAN_API_KEY

items.label: One-liner deploy (no script)
items.code:
forge create src/MyToken.sol:MyToken \
  --rpc-url $RPC_URL --private-key $PK \
  --constructor-args 1000000000000000000000000

items.label: cast — chain interaction from CLI
items.code:
cast call $ADDR 'totalSupply()(uint256)' --rpc-url $RPC_URL
cast send $ADDR 'transfer(address,uint256)' $TO 10 --private-key $PK
cast balance $ADDR

items.label: Hardhat deploy (ethers v6)

tips: Ganache + Truffle are deprecated (ConsenSys sunset Mar 2023) — new projects use Foundry or Hardhat; only touch Truffle for legacy maintenance.
Default local chainIds differ: Ganache 1337, Anvil & Hardhat node 31337 — a wrong chainId in your wallet/config is the #1 'nonce/connection' bug.
Foundry deps are git submodules in lib/, not npm packages — after `git clone` run `forge install` (or `git submodule update --init`) or imports won't resolve.
vm.expectEmit(t1,t2,t3,data): emit the EXPECTED event first, then call the function that emits the real one — order matters and mismatched indexed topics fail silently-looking.
vm.prank sets msg.sender for only the NEXT call; use startPrank/stopPrank to span multiple calls.
Never hardcode private keys in scripts or git — load from env (`$PK`) or use `cast wallet` / keystore; Anvil's printed test keys are public and mainnet-unsafe.
forge test -vvvv gives full call traces incl. reverts; ratchet verbosity up only when a test fails to keep output readable.
Use `_mint`/`_burn` behind access control (Ownable/AccessControl) — leaving a public mint open is a classic ERC-20 exploit.

items.label: App Router file conventions
items.code:
app/
  layout.tsx     // shared shell (required at root)
  page.tsx       // route UI
  loading.tsx    // Suspense fallback
  error.tsx      // 'use client' error boundary
  not-found.tsx  // 404 UI
  route.ts       // API endpoint (GET/POST...)
items.note: Folders = URL segments. Only page.tsx / route.ts make a segment routable.

items.label: Server vs Client Components
items.note: RSC: no hooks/state/browser APIs; can be async. 'use client' is contagious to children imports.

items.label: Special file segment types (this part)
items.code:
(group)      // route group — no URL segment
[id]         // dynamic segment
[...slug]    // catch-all
[[...slug]]  // optional catch-all
_folder      // private (excluded from routing)

items.label: Organize without touching URL
items.code:
app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx   -> /about
app/(shop)/cart/page.tsx         -> /cart
