items.note: Parens are stripped from the path — purely for grouping + scoped layouts.

items.label: Scoped layout per group
items.code:
app/(marketing)/layout.tsx  // wraps only marketing pages
app/(app)/layout.tsx        // separate shell for the app

items.label: Multiple root layouts
items.code:
app/(marketing)/layout.tsx  // each has its own <html><body>
app/(app)/layout.tsx
items.note: Delete the top-level app/layout.tsx and give each group a root layout. Navigating between groups = full page reload.

items.label: Single dynamic segment
items.note: Next 15+: params & searchParams are Promises — await them (or use React.use() in a client comp).

items.label: Multiple segments
items.code:
// app/shop/[category]/[id]/page.tsx
const { category, id } = await params;

items.label: Read params in a Client Component
searchParams (query string)
items.note: Accessing searchParams opts the page into dynamic rendering.

items.label: Catch-all — one or more segments
Optional catch-all — also matches parent
items.note: The extra brackets make the whole segment optional — slug is undefined at /docs.

items.label: Typical use
items.note: Docs sites, file-tree browsers, i18n prefixes, CMS pages with arbitrary nesting.

items.label: Pre-render dynamic pages at build time
items.note: Returned params are baked to static HTML at build. Runs on the server at build time.

items.label: Control unknown params
items.code:
export const dynamicParams = true;  // default: render on-demand + cache
// dynamicParams = false -> 404 for any slug not in generateStaticParams
items.note: false = fully static allowlist; true = SSG for known + SSR/ISR for the rest.

items.label: Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)
Force static / dynamic
items.code:
export const dynamic = 'force-static';  // or 'force-dynamic'
fetch(url, { cache: 'no-store' });      // opt a page into dynamic
items.note: In RSC, fetch is cached by default; cookies()/headers()/searchParams flip a page to dynamic.

items.label: What hydration is
items.note: Server sends static HTML; React attaches event listeners on the client to make it interactive. Server output must match the first client render.

items.label: Client Component (needs state/effects/events)
Common hydration-mismatch causes
items.note: Move client-only values into useEffect, or gate with a mounted flag.

items.label: Escape hatches
items.code:
<time suppressHydrationWarning>{Date.now()}</time>
// or dynamic import with no SSR:
const Chart = dynamic(() => import('./Chart'), { ssr: false });

items.label: File location & signature
items.note: One middleware file per project. Runs BEFORE the request completes, on every matched route.

items.label: Redirect / rewrite / block
Matcher config (scope it!)
Set headers / cookies on response
items.note: Runs on the Edge runtime by default — no Node APIs (fs, native modules). Keep it fast.

items.label: Build a docs/blog site
items.note: Route group (marketing) for landing, [slug] for posts, [[...slug]] for nested docs, generateStaticParams for SSG.

items.label: Auth-gated dashboard
items.note: Combine route groups (auth) / (app) with middleware for a clean protected area.

items.label: Verify SSG output
items.code:
next build   // watch the route table: ○ (Static)  ● (SSG)  ƒ (Dynamic)
next start
items.note: Confirm dynamic pages show as SSG and that unknown slugs behave per your dynamicParams setting.

tips: Route groups (folder) never appear in the URL — use them only for organization and scoped/multiple layouts. Navigating between different root layouts triggers a full reload.
Next 15+: params and searchParams are Promises. await them in Server Components or unwrap with React.use() in Client Components — forgetting this is the #1 upgrade bug.
[...slug] does NOT match the base path; [[...slug]] does (slug is undefined there). Pick optional catch-all when the parent route needs to render.
generateStaticParams runs at build. dynamicParams=false makes it a strict allowlist (404 otherwise); true (default) renders unknown params on-demand and caches them.
In RSC, fetch is cached by default = static. cookies(), headers(), searchParams, or cache:'no-store' flip a route to dynamic — know which one you tripped.
Hydration errors come from non-deterministic first renders: Date.now(), Math.random(), typeof window checks, locale/timezone formatting, and browser extensions injecting DOM.
Middleware runs on the Edge runtime by default — no fs/Node native modules. Always set a matcher so it doesn't run on _next static assets and images.
Use NextResponse.rewrite to change what renders without changing the browser URL; redirect changes the URL. Set cookies/headers on the response you return, not the request.

items.label: Install the stack (Wagmi v2 needs Viem + TanStack Query as peers)
items.code: npm i wagmi viem @tanstack/react-query

items.label: The three layers, top to bottom
items.note: Viem = low-level TS Ethereum client (RPC, encoding, types). Wagmi = React hooks over Viem. TanStack Query = caching/dedup/refetch layer Wagmi reads/writes are built on.

items.label: "Hookifying Viem" = what Wagmi is
items.note: Every Wagmi read hook wraps a Viem publicClient action in a useQuery; every write wraps a walletClient action in a useMutation. That's why you get data/isLoading/error/refetch for free.

items.label: Wallet = an injected EIP-1193 provider
items.code: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_requestAccounts' })
items.note: MetaMask/Rabby inject window.ethereum. Wagmi connectors abstract this; only reach for window.ethereum raw when debugging.

items.label: createConfig — the single source of truth
TS: register config globally for typed hooks
A chain must be in config.chains
items.note: Reads/writes against a chain not listed here throw. Add every network you target, incl. testnets.

items.label: Wagmi is React Context — wrap the app once
Order matters: WagmiProvider OUTSIDE QueryClientProvider
items.note: Wagmi hooks call useQuery internally — they need the QueryClient in scope. Reverse the nesting and every hook throws "No QueryClient set".

items.label: Next.js App Router
items.note: Provider file needs 'use client' at top; render <Providers> in root layout.

items.label: Connect / disconnect / read account
Use connector.uid as the React key
items.note: Not c.id — uids are unique per instance; ids can collide across injected wallets.

items.label: status values
items.note: 'connecting' | 'reconnecting' | 'connected' | 'disconnected'. Prefer status/isConnected over checking address for undefined.

items.label: Switch network
Native ETH balance
Format bigint for display
items.code:
import { formatEther, formatUnits } from 'viem'
formatEther(data.value)            // '1.5'
formatUnits(raw, 6)                 // USDC (6 decimals)

items.label: ERC-20 balance via useReadContract
items.code:
import { useReadContract } from 'wagmi'
import { erc20Abi } from 'viem'

const { data: bal } = useReadContract({
  address: TOKEN,
  abi: erc20Abi,
  functionName: 'balanceOf',
  args: [address!],
  query: { enabled: !!address },
})

items.label: Gate a query on inputs
items.code: useBalance({ address, query: { enabled: !!address, refetchInterval: 5000 } })
items.note: If address is undefined the query auto-disables. Use query.enabled for explicit control; query.refetchInterval replaces v1's watch: true.

items.label: Send ETH
items.code:
import { useSendTransaction } from 'wagmi'
import { parseEther } from 'viem'

const { sendTransaction, data: hash, isPending } = useSendTransaction()
sendTransaction({ to: '0xRecipient', value: parseEther('0.01') })

items.label: Call a contract (state-changing)
Wait for confirmation
items.code:
import { useWaitForTransactionReceipt } from 'wagmi'
const { isLoading: confirming, isSuccess } = useWaitForTransactionReceipt({ hash })
items.note: hash is available immediately after submit (user signed); isSuccess flips once mined. Show a pending spinner between the two.

items.label: parseEther / parseUnits for inputs
items.code:
parseEther('0.05')      // 50000000000000000n
parseUnits('100', 6)     // USDC amount as bigint
items.note: Never build value from a float. All amounts on-chain are bigint wei.

items.label: Grab Wagmi's clients inside components
items.code:
import { usePublicClient, useWalletClient } from 'wagmi'
const publicClient = usePublicClient()          // reads
const { data: walletClient } = useWalletClient() // signing

items.label: Standalone read client (no React)
Standalone wallet client from the browser
When to use raw Viem vs hooks
items.note: Hooks for anything rendered (they cache + re-render). Raw clients for scripts, event handlers, or one-off imperative calls outside the React tree.

tips: Everything on-chain is bigint (wei). Convert at the edges only: parseEther/parseUnits going in, formatEther/formatUnits coming out. Never do float math on balances.
WagmiProvider must wrap QueryClientProvider, not the other way around — Wagmi hooks depend on the TanStack QueryClient being in context.
Wagmi v2 dropped the `watch: true` prop. For live data use `query: { refetchInterval }` or watch blocks; and pass `query: { enabled }` to defer a read until inputs exist.
A submitted tx gives you a hash the moment the user signs — that is NOT confirmation. Pipe the hash into useWaitForTransactionReceipt and only treat isSuccess as done.
walletConnect() requires a projectId from cloud.walletconnect.com; injected()/metaMask() do not. Missing projectId silently breaks WC connections.
Key connector lists by connector.uid, not id — multiple injected wallets can share an id and cause React key collisions.
Reads/writes only work for chains listed in config.chains and with a matching entry in transports; forget the testnet there and hooks throw at runtime.
Prefer useAccount().status / isConnected over `if (address)` — during reconnect the address can be briefly undefined while the wallet is still connected.

items.label: Install Solana CLI + spl-token
items.code:
sh -c "$(curl -sSfL https://release.solana.com/stable/install)"
cargo install spl-token-cli   # or comes with solana tools
items.note: Anchor for programs: `cargo install --git https://github.com/coral-xyz/anchor anchor-cli`

items.label: Point to devnet & create keypair
items.code:
solana config set --url devnet
solana-keygen new              # writes ~/.config/solana/id.json
solana address
items.note: Never fund a devnet keypair from mainnet — separate wallets per cluster.

items.label: Fund wallet (airdrop SOL)
items.code:
solana airdrop 2
solana balance
items.note: Devnet only; mainnet has no faucet. Rate-limited — use faucet.solana.com if throttled.

items.label: Airdrop in code (@solana/web3.js)
items.code:
const sig = await connection.requestAirdrop(pk, 2 * LAMPORTS_PER_SOL);
await connection.confirmTransaction(sig, "confirmed");
items.note: LAMPORTS_PER_SOL = 1e9. Confirm before assuming balance is spendable.

items.label: ETH tooling (Foundry / Hardhat)
items.code:
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash && foundryup
forge init myproj      # or: npx hardhat init
items.note: Fund testnets (Sepolia) via a faucet; mainnet costs real ETH for gas.

items.label: State model
items.code:
ETH:    contract owns its storage (mapping balances)
Solana: programs are stateless; data lives in separate Accounts
items.note: On Solana you pass every account a tx touches explicitly — no implicit storage reads.

items.label: Native token & fees
items.code:
ETH: ETH, gas priced in gwei, variable
SOL: SOL, fixed ~5000 lamports/sig, sub-cent
items.note: Solana fees are near-constant; ETH gas spikes with congestion.

items.label: Execution & speed
items.code:
ETH: EVM, Solidity/Vyper, ~12s blocks
SOL: SVM, Rust (Anchor), ~400ms slots, parallel (Sealevel)
items.note: Solana parallelizes txs that don't touch the same accounts.

items.label: Token standard
items.code:
ETH: ERC-20 = one contract per token
SOL: SPL Token = one shared program, tokens are Mint accounts
items.note: On Solana every holder needs an Associated Token Account (ATA) per mint.

items.label: Rent
items.code: Solana accounts must hold min SOL to be rent-exempt
items.note: No ETH equivalent — creating accounts (ATAs, mints) costs a refundable rent deposit.

items.label: Constant product invariant
items.code:
x * y = k          // x = reserveIn, y = reserveOut
price = y / x      // spot price of X in terms of Y
items.note: Pool can never be fully drained — output asymptotes as input grows.

items.label: Swap output with 0.3% fee (Uniswap V2)
items.code:
amountInWithFee = amountIn * 997
amountOut = (amountInWithFee * reserveOut) /
            (reserveIn * 1000 + amountInWithFee)
items.note: Fee (3/1000) stays in the pool, accruing to LPs. Larger trade = worse price (slippage).

items.label: Add liquidity (must match ratio)
items.code:
amountB = amountA * reserveB / reserveA
LP_minted = min(dx/x, dy/y) * totalLP
items.note: Deposit off-ratio and the router refunds the excess of one side.

items.label: Price impact / slippage
items.code: impact = 1 - (amountOut / amountIn) / (reserveOut / reserveIn)
items.note: Set amountOutMin = quotedOut * (1 - slippageTolerance) to protect the trade.

items.label: IL formula (vs just holding)
items.code:
r = P_new / P_old              // price ratio of the volatile asset
valueRatio = 2 * sqrt(r) / (1 + r)
IL = valueRatio - 1            // always <= 0
items.note: 'Impermanent' because it vanishes if price returns to entry; realized only on withdrawal.

items.label: Reference values
items.note: Symmetric: a 4x pump and a 4x dump cause identical IL.

items.label: Net LP return
items.code: netReturn = feesEarned - IL
items.note: LPing is profitable only when accrued swap fees outweigh IL — favor correlated/stable pairs.

items.label: Mint a new token
items.code:
spl-token create-token --decimals 6
# => Creating token <MINT_ADDRESS>
items.note: Decimals are fixed at creation. USDC uses 6; SOL/wrapped uses 9.

items.label: Create your ATA + mint supply
items.code:
spl-token create-account <MINT>
spl-token mint <MINT> 1000000
items.note: Amount is in whole tokens; CLI scales by decimals. You must be the mint authority.

items.label: Transfer (auto-create recipient ATA)
items.code: spl-token transfer <MINT> 100 <RECIPIENT> --fund-recipient
items.note: Without --fund-recipient the tx fails if the recipient has no ATA for that mint.

items.label: Lock supply / revoke authority
items.code: spl-token authorize <MINT> mint --disable
items.note: Revoking mint authority makes supply fixed forever — common trust signal for launches.

items.label: @solana/spl-token
items.note: 4th arg (freezeAuthority)=null to disable freezing. Amount is in base units — multiply by 10**decimals.

items.label: ERC-20 via OpenZeppelin
items.note: decimals() defaults to 18. Deploy with `forge create` / Hardhat; verify on Etherscan.

items.label: Uniswap V2 router (Solidity)
items.note: ERC-20 swaps need approve() first (allowance). deadline guards against stuck txs.

items.label: Get a quote off-chain (V2)
items.code:
const [ , out ] = await router.getAmountsOut(amountIn, [tokenIn, tokenOut]);
const amountOutMin = out.mul(995).div(1000);  // 0.5% slippage
items.note: Never pass amountOutMin=0 in prod — invites sandwich/MEV attacks.

items.label: Jupiter aggregator (Solana)
items.code:
GET https://quote-api.jup.ag/v6/quote?inputMint=<A>&outputMint=<B>&amount=<n>&slippageBps=50
POST /v6/swap  { quoteResponse, userPublicKey }  -> signed & sent
items.note: Aggregates Raydium/Orca/etc for best route; slippageBps=50 means 0.5%.

tips: Devnet airdrops are rate-limited and flaky — retry or use a web faucet; never rely on them in tests without a fallback.
Impermanent loss is only realized when you withdraw. If price returns to entry, IL is zero — but fees earned are kept regardless.
On Solana every wallet needs an Associated Token Account (ATA) per mint before it can receive that token; use --fund-recipient / getOrCreateAssociatedTokenAccount.
SPL decimals and ERC-20 decimals are fixed at creation and differ (SPL often 6/9, ERC-20 usually 18) — always convert to base units before mint/transfer/swap math.
ERC-20 swaps require an approve() allowance to the router before swapExactTokensForTokens; SPL uses delegate/authority instead.
Always set amountOutMin (ETH) or slippageBps (Solana) — a zero/loose slippage bound gets sandwiched by MEV bots.
x·y=k means the pool never empties and price impact grows with trade size relative to reserves — split large orders or route through an aggregator.
Revoke mint authority (spl-token authorize --disable / no _mint path) once supply is final to prove the supply can't be inflated.

items.label: World computer
items.note: Decentralized state machine; every full node re-executes every tx to converge on the same deterministic global state.

items.label: Ether units
items.code: 1 ether = 1e9 gwei = 1e18 wei
items.note: Smallest unit = wei; gas prices are quoted in gwei.

items.label: Account-based model
items.note: Ethereum tracks balances per account (mapping address -> account), unlike Bitcoin's UTXO model.

items.label: Block linkage
items.note: Ordered blocks; each header holds parentHash + stateRoot + txRoot, keccak256-chained so tampering is detectable.

items.label: EOA (Externally Owned Account)
items.code: controlled by a private key; nonce = tx count sent
items.note: Only EOAs can INITIATE a transaction; no code; codeHash = keccak256("").

items.label: Contract account
items.note: Has bytecode + storage, no private key; only acts when called; nonce = number of contracts it created.

items.label: Account state fields (both types)
items.code: { nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash }

items.label: Address derivation
items.code:
EOA:     keccak256(pubkey)[12:]
CREATE:  keccak256(rlp([sender, nonce]))[12:]
CREATE2: keccak256(0xff ++ sender ++ salt ++ keccak256(initcode))[12:]
items.note: Address = last 20 bytes; CREATE2 lets you compute the address before deploy.

items.label: Physical vs virtual
items.note: EVM is a sandboxed VM (like the JVM) abstracting host hardware/OS; the same bytecode runs identically on any node.

items.label: Stack machine
items.code: stack: max 1024 items, each 256-bit (32 bytes)
items.note: Operands are popped/pushed; there are no CPU registers.

items.label: Single-threaded execution
items.note: One tx runs at a time, sequentially, to completion — no concurrency, no parallelism. State transitions are ordered.

items.label: Deterministic & isolated
items.note: No syscalls, no real randomness, no network I/O — same input => same output on all nodes. Don't use block.timestamp for randomness.

items.label: storage
items.code: contract C { uint256 x; }  // x -> slot 0
items.note: Persistent per-contract key-value; 2^256 slots of 32 bytes; survives across txs; most expensive.

items.label: memory
items.code: bytes memory b = new bytes(64);
items.note: Volatile, byte-addressable, zero-initialized, wiped after the call; expansion cost grows quadratically.

items.label: calldata
items.code: function f(uint[] calldata a) external
items.note: Read-only immutable tx input; cheapest for external args — always prefer for external array/bytes params.

items.label: stack
items.note: Scratch space for the EVM; 1024 deep; "stack too deep" when >16 local slots are simultaneously accessible.

items.label: transient storage (EIP-1153)
items.code: TSTORE 0x5d / TLOAD 0x5c
items.note: Cancun+; storage-like but automatically cleared at end of the transaction. Great for reentrancy locks.

items.label: Fee formula (EIP-1559)
items.code: fee = gasUsed * (baseFee + priorityFee)
items.note: baseFee is burned; priorityFee (tip) goes to the validator.

items.label: Intrinsic gas (before execution)
items.code:
21000 base
+ 4 gas / zero calldata byte
+ 16 gas / non-zero calldata byte
+ 32000 for contract creation
items.note: Charged up-front regardless of what the code does.

items.label: gas limit (per tx)
items.note: Max gas you authorize; unused gas is refunded. Set too low => 'out of gas' revert.

items.label: Out of gas
items.note: All state changes revert, but ALL gas up to the limit is consumed (not refunded).

items.label: Block gas limit
items.code: ~30,000,000 gas/block  (target ~15M under EIP-1559)
items.note: Caps total computation per block; baseFee rises when blocks run above target.

items.label: Arithmetic
items.code: ADD 0x01  MUL 0x02  SUB 0x03  DIV 0x04  MOD 0x06

items.label: Compare / bitwise
items.code: LT 0x10  GT 0x11  EQ 0x14  AND 0x16  OR 0x17

items.label: Stack ops
items.code: PUSH0 0x5f  PUSH1..32 0x60-0x7f  POP 0x50  DUP1 0x80  SWAP1 0x90

items.label: Memory / storage
items.code: MLOAD 0x51  MSTORE 0x52  SLOAD 0x54  SSTORE 0x55

items.label: Context (msg / tx)
items.code: CALLER 0x33 (msg.sender)  CALLVALUE 0x34 (msg.value)  ADDRESS 0x30

items.label: Control flow
items.code: JUMP 0x56  JUMPI 0x57  JUMPDEST 0x5b  RETURN 0xf3  REVERT 0xfd  STOP 0x00

items.label: Calls / create
items.code: CALL 0xf1  DELEGATECALL 0xf4  STATICCALL 0xfa  CREATE 0xf0  CREATE2 0xf5

items.label: Cheap ops
items.code: ADD/SUB/PUSH/DUP/MLOAD/MSTORE = 3   MUL/DIV/MOD = 5   JUMPI = 10
items.note: Base context ops (ADDRESS, CALLER) = 2.

items.label: SLOAD
items.code: cold 2100 / warm 100
items.note: EIP-2929: first access to a slot in a tx is 'cold'; subsequent are 'warm'.

items.label: SSTORE
items.code:
0 -> non-zero: 20000
update non-zero: 2900 (+2100 if cold)
items.note: Refund up to 4800 gas when clearing a slot back to 0 (EIP-3529).

items.label: CALL
items.code: warm 100 / cold 2600  (+9000 if value>0, +25000 if new account)
items.note: A 2300-gas stipend is forwarded to the callee on a value transfer.

items.label: KECCAK256 (0x20)
items.code: 30 + 6 * ceil(len/32)

items.label: CREATE / LOG
items.code:
CREATE = 32000 + initcode + 200/deployed byte
LOGn = 375 + 375*n + 8/byte

items.label: Unit conversion
items.code:
cast to-wei 1 ether     # 1000000000000000000
cast from-wei 21000000000 gwei

items.label: Inspect accounts
items.code:
cast balance <addr>
cast nonce <addr>
cast code <addr>        # deployed bytecode

items.label: Bytecode -> opcodes
items.code: cast disassemble 0x6080604052...

items.label: Compile to opcodes / asm
items.code:
solc --opcodes Contract.sol
solc --asm Contract.sol

items.label: Gas
items.code:
cast estimate <addr> "transfer(address,uint256)" <to> <amt>
cast receipt <txhash> gasUsed

tips: Only EOAs can initiate a transaction — every contract execution ultimately originates from an EOA-signed tx.
storage is ~100-20000x pricier than memory: cache storage vars into a local (memory/stack) inside loops.
Reverts refund unused gas but keep the gas already consumed; a true 'out of gas' burns the entire gas limit.
Cold vs warm (EIP-2929): the first touch of an address or slot in a tx costs far more — batch accesses to reuse warmth.
EVM words are natively 256-bit; uint8 saves NO gas unless multiple small vars pack into one 32-byte storage slot.
Cost order calldata < memory < storage — mark external array/bytes params `calldata` to avoid a memory copy.
EVM is single-threaded and deterministic: no threads, no true RNG; block.timestamp is validator-influenceable.
PUSH0 (0x5f) and TSTORE/TLOAD exist only on Shanghai/Cancun+ — verify the target chain's fork before relying on them.

items.label: What a bridge actually is
items.note: Chains are isolated state machines — no chain can read another. A bridge = lock/burn on source + off-chain relayer + mint/unlock on destination. The relayer is the trust boundary.

items.label: Lock-and-mint invariant
items.note: minted(dest) == locked(source), always 1:1. Break it (double-mint, unbacked mint) and the peg collapses. This is the #1 thing to monitor.

items.label: One-way bridge scope
items.note: Value flows source→dest only (lock→mint). No burn→unlock reverse leg. Halves attack surface and state to track.

items.label: Init Hardhat project
items.code:
npm init -y
npm i -D hardhat @nomicfoundation/hardhat-toolbox dotenv
npm i @openzeppelin/contracts
npx hardhat init   # choose "JS project"

items.label: Vault that custodies tokens + emits event with nonce
items.note: safeTransferFrom, not transferFrom — handles non-standard (USDT-like) tokens that don't return bool.

items.label: Why approve + transferFrom (not raw transfer)
items.code:
token.approve(vaultAddr, amount);   // tx 1 (from user's wallet)
vault.deposit(amount, l2Addr);      // tx 2 -> runs transferFrom
items.note: A plain transfer to the contract gives no execution hook — can't emit the structured event the relayer needs, funds can be stranded. EIP-2612 permit collapses the two txs into one.

items.label: Wrapped ERC-20 with restricted mint + replay guard
items.note: mint signature mirrors the Locked event args (to, amount, nonce). Checks-effects-interactions: set processed BEFORE _mint.

items.label: Access control = the crown jewel
items.note: Whoever holds relayer/owner key can mint arbitrarily. Ronin (~$625M) = stolen validator keys. Production: multisig relayer set + rate limits + pausable circuit breaker.

items.label: Watch source event, submit mint on destination (ethers v6)
Wait for finality before minting
items.note: Reorg risk: if you mint on an event that later gets reorged out, you've minted unbacked tokens. Trade latency for safety.

items.label: Backfill missed events on restart
items.code:
const logs = await vault.queryFilter("Locked", fromBlock, "latest");
for (const l of logs) { /* re-submit; processed[nonce] dedupes */ }
items.note: Messaging is at-least-once — relayer WILL crash and re-send. The nonce mapping makes minting exactly-once.

items.label: The core defense
items.note: Each deposit gets a unique, monotonically increasing nonce. Destination stores mapping(uint256=>bool) processed, set on first mint, checked on every mint. Enforces exactly-once over an at-least-once layer.

items.label: Attack it prevents
items.note: Without it, one legit deposit → relayer replays / attacker resubmits the same mint → unbacked supply printed. Classic double-mint exploit.

items.label: Source nonce++ is the sole issuer
items.code: emit Locked(msg.sender, amount, to, nonce++); // post-increment: emits current, then bumps
items.note: Two-way bridge needs a SEPARATE nonce space for the burn/unlock leg — don't share counters across directions.

items.label: hardhat.config.js network + keys
items.note: .env only — NEVER commit a private key. Add .env to .gitignore.

items.label: Deploy script
Run deploy + verify
items.code:
npx hardhat run scripts/deploy.js --network sepolia
npx hardhat verify --network sepolia <VAULT_ADDR> <TOKEN_ADDR>
items.note: Deployed address is immutable — upgradeability needs a proxy pattern chosen up front.

items.label: Setup: mock token + both contracts in one EVM
items.note: Simulate cross-chain in ONE chain: run lock, read emitted args, then call mint with those exact args as the relayer signer.

items.label: Happy path: lock emits event, relayer mints 1:1
Negative tests (the high-value ones)
items.note: Replay reverts, non-relayer mint reverts, deposit-without-approve reverts. Bugs hide in the failure paths, not the happy path.

items.label: Run tests
items.code:
npx hardhat test
npx hardhat coverage   # if solidity-coverage installed

tips: Follow checks-effects-interactions in mint(): set processed[nonce]=true BEFORE _mint to kill reentrancy, even though _mint itself is safe.
Wait for confirmations/finality before minting — minting on a soon-to-be-reorged block creates unbacked tokens on the destination.
Nonces enforce exactly-once minting over an at-least-once relayer. Assume the relayer will crash and re-send; the processed mapping is what saves you.
Never commit private keys. Use .env + dotenv, add .env to .gitignore, and treat the relayer/owner key as the single biggest risk (Ronin, Wormhole both key/validation failures).
Use SafeERC20 — tokens like USDT don't return a bool from transfer/transferFrom and will silently break a naive vault.
approve + transferFrom, not a raw transfer to the vault: only the pull pattern lets you atomically record sender/dest/nonce and emit the event the relayer consumes.
In prod, monitor the invariant minted(dest) == locked(source) and add a pausable circuit breaker — pause minting the instant it diverges.
Extending to two-way? The unlock/withdraw path releases REAL collateral — give it its own nonce space and its own access control; most real exploits target the withdraw leg.

items.label: Install (ethers v6)
items.code: npm install ethers   # v6 (native bigint)

items.label: Browser: wrap MetaMask (injected EIP-1193 provider)
items.code:
// MetaMask injects window.ethereum
const provider = new ethers.BrowserProvider(window.ethereum);
items.note: v5: new ethers.providers.Web3Provider(window.ethereum)

items.label: Connect wallet + get signer (opens MetaMask popup)
items.note: getSigner() is async in v6, was sync in v5

items.label: Read-only RPC provider (no wallet needed)
items.note: Use for reads/server. Alchemy/Infura give you the RPC URL.

items.label: Load ethers in a plain HTML page (CDN, ESM)
items.code:
<script type="module">
  import { ethers } from "https://cdn.ethers.org/lib/ethers.min.js";
</script>

items.label: Every node speaks JSON-RPC over HTTP/WS — ethers is a wrapper
items.code:
curl <RPC_URL> -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"eth_blockNumber","params":[],"id":1}'

items.label: Core node methods
items.code:
eth_blockNumber        // latest block
eth_getBalance         // wei of an address
eth_call               // read-only exec, no state change (free)
eth_sendRawTransaction // broadcast signed tx (costs gas)
eth_getTransactionReceipt
eth_chainId  eth_gasPrice  eth_estimateGas

items.label: Fire a raw RPC method through ethers
items.code:
const hexBn = await provider.send("eth_blockNumber", []);
const bn = parseInt(hexBn, 16);

items.label: Wallet-only methods (only exist via MetaMask, not raw nodes)
items.code:
eth_requestAccounts        // connect
wallet_switchEthereumChain // change network
wallet_addEthereumChain
personal_sign              // sign a message

items.label: ETH balance of an address
items.code:
const wei = await provider.getBalance("0xAbc...");
console.log(ethers.formatEther(wei)); // "1.5"

items.label: Block number, nonce, fees
Unit conversion (wei <-> human)
items.code:
ethers.parseEther("0.1");     // 100000000000000000n (bigint)
ethers.formatEther(wei);      // "0.1"
ethers.parseUnits("100", 6);  // USDC has 6 decimals
ethers.formatUnits(raw, dec);
items.note: v6 returns native bigint (n suffix), not v5 BigNumber. Chain amounts are ALWAYS wei.

items.label: You need: contract address + ABI (a subset is fine)
Instantiate with a PROVIDER for reads
items.code: const c = new ethers.Contract(address, abi, provider);

items.label: Call view/pure functions (free, instant, no signer)
items.note: view/pure -> resolves to eth_call. No gas, no wallet popup.

items.label: Re-instantiate with a SIGNER (needs wallet + gas)
Send tx and wait for confirmation
items.note: await c.foo() only broadcasts; state isn't changed until tx.wait() resolves.

items.label: Overrides: send ETH along / cap gas
items.code:
await c.deposit({ value: ethers.parseEther("0.5") });
await c.foo(arg, { gasLimit: 100000n });

items.label: Dry-run to catch reverts before spending gas
items.code:
await c.transfer.estimateGas(to, amt); // will it fit / revert?
await c.transfer.staticCall(to, amt);  // simulate, surfaces revert

items.label: Plain ETH transfer (no contract)
No MetaMask on a server — YOU hold the key
items.note: Never hardcode/commit keys. Load from env / secret manager.

items.label: Same Contract API — the wallet IS the signer
Create / import a wallet
items.code:
const w = ethers.Wallet.createRandom(); // w.address, w.privateKey, w.mnemonic.phrase
ethers.Wallet.fromPhrase(mnemonic);     // from seed phrase

items.label: Detect / assert network
items.code:
const net = await provider.getNetwork();
net.chainId; // 1n mainnet, 11155111n sepolia, 137n polygon

items.label: Ask MetaMask to switch chains (chainId is HEX here)
items.note: ethers uses decimal chainId; wallet_* RPC params use hex.

items.label: React to wallet changes
items.code:
window.ethereum.on("accountsChanged", (a) => setAccount(a[0]));
window.ethereum.on("chainChanged", () => window.location.reload());

items.label: Contract events (live + historical)
items.code:
c.on("Transfer", (from, to, amt, ev) => { /* live */ });
const logs = await c.queryFilter("Transfer", fromBlock, toBlock);

tips: Reads vs writes: view/pure = eth_call (free, instant, provider is enough); state changes = signed tx (gas + wallet + tx.wait()).
ethers v6 uses native bigint everywhere; v5 used BigNumber. Don't mix versions — check which one the project pins.
Always await tx.wait() before assuming state changed. receipt.status === 1 = success; a revert throws.
Key custody rule: browser -> MetaMask signs (you never see the key); server -> ethers.Wallet + private key from env. Never ship a key to the client.
ABIs can be a subset — include only the functions you actually call. Human-readable ABI beats pasting giant JSON.
getSigner() is async in v6 (returns a Promise); it was synchronous in v5 — a common migration bug.
Guard for missing wallet: if (!window.ethereum) prompt to install MetaMask before calling any eth_* method.
chainId is decimal in ethers (11155111n) but hex in wallet_* RPC params ("0xaa36a7"). Use staticCall/estimateGas to catch reverts before spending gas.

items.label: Remix (fastest, zero-install)
items.note: remix.ethereum.org — in-browser IDE, compile + deploy to JS VM or injected MetaMask. Best for learning.

items.label: Foundry (Rust, fast — preferred)
items.code:
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash
foundryup
forge init myproj
forge build
forge test
items.note: forge = build/test, cast = CLI RPC calls, anvil = local node.

items.label: Hardhat (JS/TS ecosystem)
items.code:
npm i -D hardhat
npx hardhat init
npx hardhat compile
npx hardhat test
items.note: Artifacts (ABI + bytecode) land in artifacts/. Uses ethers.js in scripts.

items.label: solc directly
items.code:
solc --abi --bin Contract.sol -o out/
solc --optimize --bin Contract.sol
items.note: Rarely called by hand; frameworks wrap it. --optimize reduces gas + bytecode size.

items.label: Local chain + deploy (Foundry)
items.code:
anvil                 # spins up localhost:8545
forge create src/C.sol:C --rpc-url http://localhost:8545 --private-key 0x...

items.label: Minimal contract skeleton
items.note: SPDX + pragma are compiler-required conventions. `public` state vars auto-generate a getter.

items.label: Constructor (runs once at deploy)
items.note: No `function` keyword. Args passed at deploy time, baked into the deploy tx.

items.label: State vs local vs global
items.note: storage writes are expensive (~20k gas cold). Minimize them.

items.label: Globals cheat
items.code:
msg.sender   // caller address
msg.value    // wei sent (needs payable)
block.timestamp
block.number
address(this).balance
items.note: block.timestamp is miner-influenceable — don't use for strong randomness.

sections.title: Types (and how they differ from JS)

items.label: Statically typed, no `let`/`var`
items.note: JS has one `number`; Solidity has sized ints (uint8..uint256 in steps of 8). uint = uint256.

items.label: NO floating point
items.code:
uint256 price = 1e18;   // represent 1.0 as 1e18 wei
// use fixed-point manually; multiply before divide
items.note: Biggest JS-dev trap. Integer division truncates: 7/2 == 3.

items.label: Overflow reverts (since 0.8)
items.note: No NaN/Infinity like JS. Arithmetic errors revert the whole tx.

items.label: Reference types need data location
items.note: memory / storage / calldata must be declared for arrays, structs, strings, bytes. calldata is cheapest for external fn args.

items.label: mapping — no keys, no length, no iteration
items.code:
mapping(address => uint256) balances;
balances[msg.sender] += 100;
items.note: Unlike a JS object: can't enumerate keys or get .length. Missing key returns the zero value, not undefined.

items.label: Default values (no undefined/null)
items.code: // uint->0, bool->false, address->address(0), string->""
items.note: There is no null/undefined. Everything is zero-initialized.

items.label: Visibility (required on every fn)
items.code:
function a() public {}    // internal + external
function b() external {}  // only outside calls
function c() internal {}  // this + derived
function d() private {}   // this contract only
items.note: external calldata args are cheaper than public memory args.

items.label: State mutability
items.code:
function get() external view returns (uint256) { return count; } // reads only
function add(uint a, uint b) external pure returns (uint) { return a+b; } // no state
function pay() external payable {}  // can receive ETH
items.note: view/pure calls are free when called off-chain (eth_call), no tx needed.

items.label: Named/multiple returns
Custom errors + require (gas-cheap reverts)
items.note: Custom errors (0.8.4+) are cheaper than require strings. revert rolls back ALL state changes in the tx.

items.label: Modifiers (reusable guards)
Events (off-chain logs, indexed for filtering)
items.note: Up to 3 `indexed` params are searchable/filterable by dapps. Cheaper than storage but NOT readable from other contracts.

items.label: What compile produces
items.note: Bytecode = what runs. ABI = how off-chain code knows how to talk to it. You need BOTH to interact.

items.label: ABI = JSON interface descriptor
items.code:
[{"type":"function","name":"increment",
  "inputs":[],"outputs":[],"stateMutability":"nonpayable"},
 {"type":"function","name":"count",
  "inputs":[],"outputs":[{"type":"uint256"}],"stateMutability":"view"}]
items.note: Describes functions, args, return types, events. The EVM does NOT read this — clients do, to encode calldata.

items.label: Function selector (first 4 bytes of calldata)
items.code:
// selector = bytes4(keccak256("increment()"))
cast sig "transfer(address,uint256)"   // → 0xa9059cbb
items.note: Calldata = selector (4 bytes) + ABI-encoded args (32-byte padded). This is how the EVM dispatches to the right function.

items.label: Encode / decode calldata
items.code:
cast calldata "transfer(address,uint256)" 0xabc... 100
cast abi-decode "balanceOf(address)(uint256)" 0x...
items.note: In Solidity: abi.encodeWithSelector(sel, args), abi.decode(data, (uint256)).

items.label: Use ABI + address in ethers.js
items.note: The ABI tells ethers how to encode the call into bytecode calldata under the hood.

items.label: Interface = ABI in Solidity form
items.note: Lets one contract call another using just the signature — no source/bytecode needed.

items.label: Get on-chain bytecode
items.code: cast code 0xContractAddress --rpc-url $RPC
items.note: Empty (0x) code means it's an EOA/wallet, not a contract.

items.label: Execution cost = gas
items.note: Every op costs gas paid in ETH. There is no free loop. JS runs free on your CPU; Solidity runs on thousands of nodes.

items.label: Deterministic & sandboxed
items.note: No fetch/HTTP, no fs, no Date.now() as truth, no Math.random(). All nodes must reach identical results. Use oracles (Chainlink) for external data.

items.label: Immutable after deploy
items.note: No hot-reload. Deployed bytecode is permanent. Bugs need a redeploy or upgrade proxy. `git push` mentality does not apply.

items.label: State is global & public
items.code: uint256 private secret;  // still readable via storage slots!
items.note: `private` hides from other contracts, NOT from the public blockchain. Never store secrets.

items.label: Sync only, no async/promises
items.code: uint256 n = other.getValue();   // external call returns synchronously
items.note: No await/Promise. But external calls can re-enter your contract — guard against reentrancy.

items.label: Whole-tx atomicity
items.note: A revert anywhere undoes everything in the tx (like a DB rollback). JS throws leave partial mutations.

items.label: == vs no === , no truthy coercion
items.code:
if (x) {}   // ERROR: x must be bool
if (x != 0) {}
items.note: No loose/strict equality distinction, no truthy/falsy. Conditions must be strictly bool.

items.label: Compiled, not interpreted
items.note: solc → EVM bytecode ahead of time. Type errors caught at compile, not runtime like JS.

tips: Always pin the pragma to a tested range (^0.8.24) and include the SPDX line — the compiler warns without it.
You need BOTH the ABI and the contract address to call a deployed contract; the ABI encodes args into calldata, the address routes the tx.
Follow checks-effects-interactions: update state BEFORE making external calls to avoid reentrancy (the classic DAO hack).
Prefer custom errors (error Foo()) over require("string") — they cost less gas and deployment size.
Money is uint256 in wei; 1 ETH = 1e18 wei. There are no decimals/floats — scale by 1e18 and multiply before dividing.
`private`/`internal` only restrict contract-to-contract access; all state is publicly readable on-chain — never store secrets.
Since 0.8, arithmetic auto-reverts on overflow/underflow; only wrap in `unchecked{}` when you've proven it's safe for gas savings.
Use `calldata` for external function reference-type args and minimize `storage` writes (~20k gas each) — gas is the real constraint, not CPU.

items.label: Init project + compile
items.code:
npm i -D hardhat && npx hardhat init
npx hardhat compile   # -> artifacts/contracts/X.sol/X.json (abi + bytecode)
items.note: Compile emits BOTH the ABI (FE needs it) and bytecode (goes on-chain).

items.label: Local chain for dev
items.code: npx hardhat node        # JSON-RPC on http://127.0.0.1:8545, funded accounts
items.note: Point MetaMask at localhost:8545, chainId 31337.

items.label: Deploy to a testnet
items.code: npx hardhat run scripts/deploy.js --network sepolia
items.note: Deploy returns the contract ADDRESS — the third thing the FE needs (address + ABI + chain).

items.label: Deploy script (ethers v6)
Skeleton: state, events, modifiers
payable state-changing fn (costs gas)
items.note: Emit an event so the FE can react to the change without polling.

items.label: view/pure fn (free read)
items.note: view = reads state, pure = touches no state. Both are free via eth_call.

items.label: Access control + safe ETH send
items.note: Checks-Effects-Interactions: update state BEFORE external calls to avoid reentrancy.

sections.title: Writing the FE — provider vs signer

items.label: Connect wallet (MetaMask)
items.note: window.ethereum is injected by MetaMask; guard for undefined (no wallet).

items.label: Two Contract instances: read vs write
items.code:
import abi from "./abi/Crowdfund.json"; // artifact.abi array
const read  = new ethers.Contract(ADDR, abi, provider); // eth_call, free
const write = new ethers.Contract(ADDR, abi, signer);   // tx, costs gas
items.note: provider = read-only (works even disconnected); signer = writes (needs wallet).

items.label: Read state
items.code: const [raised, goal] = await read.getStatus();
items.note: Returns BigInt in ethers v6 — format with ethers.formatEther().

items.label: Write + await receipt
items.code:
const tx = await write.contribute({ value: ethers.parseEther("0.1") });
const receipt = await tx.wait(); // resolves only once MINED
items.note: The send resolves with a tx hash immediately; state is stale until tx.wait().

items.label: Read hook (auto-refetch, cached)
Write hook
Wait for the tx in React
items.code: const { isLoading, isSuccess } = useWaitForTransactionReceipt({ hash });
items.note: Invalidate/refetch reads on isSuccess so the UI reflects new state.

items.label: Wallet connection state
items.code: const { address, isConnected, chainId } = useAccount();
items.note: wagmi wraps viem + handles connection lifecycle, caching, re-renders.

sections.title: Add the ABI + address (the glue layer)

items.label: Address keyed by chainId
items.note: Wrong chain -> wrong/nonexistent address. Pick address from the wallet's live chainId.

items.label: Import ABI straight from artifacts
items.code:
import artifact from "../artifacts/contracts/Crowdfund.sol/Crowdfund.json";
const abi = artifact.abi;
items.note: Re-copy ABI on EVERY redeploy — a stale ABI silently breaks encode/decode.

items.label: Prompt a network switch
items.note: chainId in wallet RPC is hex; on-chain/JS values are decimal. Catch 4902 = chain not added.

items.label: Live updates via events
items.code: read.on("Contributed", (from, amount) => refresh());
items.note: Use a WebSocket provider for push; still re-read on load — events can be missed on reorg/disconnect.

items.label: Query past logs
items.code: const logs = await read.queryFilter("Contributed", fromBlock, "latest");

items.label: Handle rejection & revert
items.note: User rejection, revert, out-of-gas, and pending state only surface in the real end-to-end loop.

tips: Three things wire FE to a contract: ADDRESS + ABI + correct chainId. Get one wrong and calls silently fail or hit the wrong contract.
Reads (view/pure) go through a provider via eth_call — free, no wallet needed. Writes need a signer, cost gas, and are async transactions.
A state-changing call resolves with a tx HASH, not the new value. Always await tx.wait() (or useWaitForTransactionReceipt) before showing success.
Re-copy the ABI on every redeploy and re-check the address — a stale ABI causes silent decode failures; a stale address calls the old contract.
Never trust the FE for validation: enforce every invariant on-chain with require/revert. An attacker crafts raw txs that bypass your UI entirely.
ethers v6 returns BigInt, not number — use parseEther/formatEther and BigInt math, never Number().
Handle the wrong-network case explicitly: read wallet chainId, prompt wallet_switchEthereumChain, and only enable actions once on the right chain.
ethers/viem = low-level (providers, signers, encoding); wagmi = React hooks on top. Use wagmi in React to kill connection/caching boilerplate.

items.label: The problem: deployed bytecode is immutable
items.note: You cannot patch a bug or add features once a contract is on-chain. Naive fixes are costly.

items.label: Naive approach 1: redeploy + migrate state
items.note: New address breaks every reference/integration; copying storage on-chain is gas-expensive and error-prone.

items.label: Naive approach 2: registry / data-separation
items.note: Logic contract CALLs a separate storage contract via a lookup registry. Works but clumsy, extra gas, no shared context.

items.label: Real solution: delegatecall — run OTHER code in THIS context
items.note: call = callee's context/storage; delegatecall = caller's context/storage. This is the whole trick behind proxies.

items.label: delegatecall silent-fail gotcha
items.code:
// delegatecall to an EOA / empty address returns success=true!
require(impl.code.length > 0, "no code");
items.note: Always check the target has code before delegatecalling — otherwise it 'succeeds' doing nothing.

items.label: Split into Proxy (holds state, permanent address) + Implementation (logic, swappable)
items.note: Users only ever interact with the proxy address. Upgrade = point proxy at new impl.

items.label: fallback() forwards every unknown call to the impl
items.code:
fallback() external payable { _delegate(_implementation()); }
receive() external payable { _delegate(_implementation()); }
items.note: fallback catches any selector the proxy itself doesn't define, then delegates it.

items.label: Canonical assembly delegate (preserves calldata + return/revert data)
items.note: Must be raw assembly — Solidity can't return a dynamic value whose type isn't known at compile time.

items.label: Upgrade function (gate it!)
items.note: Without onlyOwner ANYONE can repoint the proxy to malicious logic and drain it.

sections.title: Storage layout — the #1 way to brick a proxy

items.label: Proxy & impl SHARE the same storage slots
items.note: delegatecall writes into the proxy's storage by slot index, using the IMPL's variable layout. They must agree.

items.label: Storage collision: naive `address public implementation` at slot 0
items.note: This is 'Problem with current proxy' — proxy's own vars overlap the impl's vars.

items.label: Fix: unstructured storage — stash proxy vars at a random hashed slot
items.note: Pick a slot no normal variable will ever occupy. Standardized as ERC-1967 (below).

items.label: Upgrade rule: append-only
items.note: Never reorder, remove, or change the type of existing state vars between versions.

items.label: Reserve space in base contracts with a gap
items.code: uint256[50] private __gap;
items.note: Lets you add fields to an inherited base later without shifting child storage.

items.label: Implementation slot
Beacon slot
items.code:
// keccak256("eip1967.proxy.beacon") - 1
0xa3f0ad74e5423aebfd80d3ef4346578335a9a72aeaee59ff6cb3582b35133d50

items.label: Read/write via assembly
items.note: Block explorers & tooling detect proxies by reading these exact slots. -1 keeps the slot off any keccak-derived mapping.

items.label: Transparent Proxy (TPP)
items.note: Upgrade logic in the PROXY. A separate ProxyAdmin owns it. Routing by msg.sender avoids selector clashes but adds an SLOAD to every call. Admin can't call impl functions.

items.label: UUPS (EIP-1822) — upgrade logic in the IMPLEMENTATION
items.note: Cheaper deploy + calls (proxy is minimal, no admin routing). DANGER: ship an impl without upgrade code and the proxy is permanently bricked.

items.label: Beacon Proxy — many proxies, one upgrade point
items.note: Best for factories deploying N identical instances you want to upgrade in one tx. Extra SLOAD to fetch beacon then impl.

sections.title: Initializers — no constructors in logic contracts

items.label: Why: constructor runs in the IMPL's context, so its state never lands in the proxy
items.note: State set by a constructor lives in the implementation's own storage — the proxy never sees it.

items.label: Use an initializer instead (runs via delegatecall = proxy storage)
items.note: `initializer` modifier ensures it runs exactly once.

items.label: Lock the implementation's own initializers
items.code:
/// @custom:oz-upgrades-unsafe-allow constructor
constructor() { _disableInitializers(); }
items.note: Prevents an attacker from initializing the bare impl contract directly and hijacking it.

items.label: Versioned re-init for new state added in an upgrade
items.code: function initializeV2(uint x) public reinitializer(2) { newVar = x; }
items.note: `reinitializer(n)` runs once per version; `onlyInitializing` for internal __init_ helpers of parents.

items.label: Install
items.code:
npm i @openzeppelin/contracts-upgradeable @openzeppelin/hardhat-upgrades
# foundry:
forge install OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts-upgradeable

items.label: Deploy proxy (Hardhat)
Upgrade proxy
items.note: It aborts if you reordered/retyped storage or used unsafe delegatecall/selfdestruct.

items.label: Beacon deploy
Foundry: OZ Upgrades + validation

tips: No constructors in logic contracts: use `initialize()` + `initializer`, and call `_disableInitializers()` in the impl's constructor so the bare implementation can't be seized.
Storage is append-only across versions — never reorder, remove, or retype existing vars. Add `uint256[50] private __gap;` to upgradeable base contracts to reserve room.
delegatecall to an address with no code returns success=true and does nothing. Always `require(impl.code.length > 0)` before setting/using an implementation.
Gate every upgrade path (`onlyOwner` / `_authorizeUpgrade`). An ungated upgrade lets anyone repoint the proxy to a drain contract.

UUPS bricking risk: if a new implementation omits UUPSUpgradeable / `_authorizeUpgrade`, the proxy loses its only upgrade mechanism forever. Transparent proxies keep upgrade logic in the proxy, so they can't self-brick this way.
Deploy + initialize atomically (single tx via proxy constructor data) — a two-step deploy lets an attacker front-run the initializer.
Explorers detect proxies via ERC-1967 slots; the `keccak(...) - 1` offset keeps those slots from ever colliding with a real mapping/array slot.
Transparent adds an SLOAD + admin check to every call; UUPS is cheapest at runtime; Beacon adds an extra hop but upgrades N proxies in one tx — pick per use case.

items.label: Install Rust (rustup)
items.code:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
rustup update && rustc --version && cargo --version

items.label: Install Solana / Agave CLI
items.code:
sh -c "$(curl -sSfL https://release.anza.xyz/stable/install)"
solana --version
items.note: Anza's agave replaces the old solana-labs installer.

items.label: Install Anchor via AVM
items.code:
cargo install --git https://github.com/coral-xyz/anchor avm --force
avm install latest && avm use latest && anchor --version

items.label: Configure cluster + wallet
items.code:
solana config set --url devnet
solana-keygen new            # ~/.config/solana/id.json
solana airdrop 2 && solana balance

items.label: New program + build/test/deploy
items.code:
anchor init my_prog && cd my_prog
anchor build      # -> target/deploy/*.so + target/idl/*.json
anchor test       # spins local validator
anchor deploy
items.note: anchor keys sync writes the real program id into declare_id! and Anchor.toml.

items.label: Plain cargo (non-Anchor)
items.code:
cargo new --lib hello && cargo build --release
cargo add borsh solana-program

sections.title: Core Syntax: Variables, Types, Control Flow

items.label: Immutable by default; mut to reassign
Scalar + compound types
String vs &str
items.note: String = heap-owned, growable; &str = borrowed view.

items.label: if / else are expressions
items.code: let sign = if n >= 0 { "pos" } else { "neg" };

items.label: Loops: loop / while / for
Functions
items.code:
fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { a + b }  // last expr = return, no ;
fn noop() {}                              // returns ()

items.label: Move semantics (heap values)
items.note: Copy types (i32, bool, char, fixed arrays of Copy) are copied, not moved.

items.label: Borrowing: shared (&) vs mutable (&mut)
items.code:
fn len(s: &String) -> usize { s.len() }        // read-only borrow
fn push(s: &mut String) { s.push('!'); }       // mut borrow
items.note: Rule: any number of &, OR exactly one &mut, never both at once.

items.label: Lifetimes tie reference validity
items.note: Anchor account structs are generic over <'info>: Account<'info, T>.

items.label: Struct + impl (methods & associated fn)
Enums with data
match (exhaustive)
items.note: _ => is the catch-all arm; compiler forces you to cover every variant.

items.label: if let / while let (concise match)
items.code:
if let Some(v) = maybe { use_it(v); }
while let Some(x) = stack.pop() { }

items.label: Define + implement a trait
Derive common traits via macro
items.code:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Default)]
struct Config { retries: u8 }
items.note: #[derive(...)] auto-generates trait impls at compile time.

items.label: Generics + trait bounds
Generic struct / enum
Result + ? operator
items.note: unwrap()/expect("msg") panic on Err; use ? to propagate instead.

items.label: Option enum
Iterators (lazy; chain then collect)
items.note: iter() borrows, into_iter() consumes, iter_mut() mutably borrows.

items.label: External crates (Cargo.toml)
items.code:
[dependencies]
borsh = "1"
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
anchor-lang = "0.31"
items.note: cargo add <crate>; features gate optional functionality.

items.label: Serde (JSON / off-chain, human-facing)
Borsh (on-chain, deterministic binary)
items.note: Solana/Anchor account & instruction data use Borsh — compact, no self-describing schema, order-dependent.

sections.title: Solana Program: Anchor, Context, Accounts, IDL

items.label: Program skeleton (#[program])
Context<T> — the accounts + metadata bag
items.note: Context<T> is generic over your #[derive(Accounts)] struct.

items.label: Accounts validation struct
items.code:
#[derive(Accounts)]
pub struct Increment<'info> {
    #[account(mut)]
    pub counter: Account<'info, Counter>,
    pub authority: Signer<'info>,
    pub system_program: Program<'info, System>,
}

items.label: init + PDA + space
items.code:
#[account(
    init, payer = authority, space = 8 + 8,
    seeds = [b"counter", authority.key().as_ref()], bump
)]
pub counter: Account<'info, Counter>,
items.note: space = 8 (Anchor discriminator) + sum of field sizes.

items.label: State account (#[account] adds discriminator + Borsh)
items.code:
#[account]
pub struct Counter { pub count: u64 }

items.label: Custom errors
Transaction & IDL (concepts)
items.note: Client: new Program(idl, provider).methods.increment().accounts({..}).rpc().

items.label: Native (no Anchor) entrypoint

tips: Variables are immutable by default — reach for `let mut` only when you truly reassign; prefer shadowing (`let x = ...`) to change type.
Borrow checker mantra: many `&` XOR one `&mut`, and no reference may outlive its owner. Anchor account structs carry this as the `'info` lifetime.
On-chain data is Borsh, not Serde/JSON — order and types must match exactly across program and client, and Borsh has no self-describing schema.
Always budget 8 bytes for the Anchor discriminator: `space = 8 + <fields>`; forgetting it is the classic init size bug.
Do arithmetic with `checked_add`/`checked_mul` (or enable `overflow-checks = true`) — silent wraparound on lamports/counters is an exploit vector.
Use `msg!("...")` for on-chain logging; `println!` does nothing in a BPF program.
Propagate errors with `?` and typed `Result`; reserve `unwrap()`/`expect()` for truly-impossible cases since they panic (and abort the instruction).
After `anchor build`, run `anchor keys sync` so `declare_id!` and Anchor.toml match the deployed program keypair, then redeploy.

items.label: Install OZ upgradeable libs + Hardhat plugin
items.code:
npm i @openzeppelin/contracts-upgradeable @openzeppelin/contracts
npm i -D @openzeppelin/hardhat-upgrades hardhat ethers
items.note: Use *-upgradeable for logic (initializers, __gap); plain @openzeppelin/contracts for interfaces/proxies.

items.label: Enable plugin in hardhat.config
items.code:
require("@openzeppelin/hardhat-upgrades");
// exposes ethers + upgrades: deployProxy/upgradeProxy/validateUpgrade

items.label: Foundry alternative
items.code:
forge install OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts-upgradeable
forge install OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-foundry-upgrades
# foundry.toml: ffi = true, ast = true, build_info = true

items.label: Core imports (UUPS staking)
Deployed bytecode is frozen
items.note: You cannot patch code at an address. Each contract's state (staked balances, reward index) lives at its own address — there is no in-place edit.

items.label: Naive redeploy breaks everything
items.note: New address ⇒ every UI/integration/token allowance points at the old contract, and you'd have to migrate every staker's balance (gas-heavy, needs downtime). Registry/lookup adds a hop and still doesn't move state.

items.label: delegatecall = run their code, touch MY storage
items.note: This is the primitive the whole pattern rests on. Mixing up call vs delegatecall is the #1 conceptual error.

items.label: Why a constructor cannot initialize a proxy
items.note: A constructor runs once at IMPLEMENTATION deploy, against the implementation's storage. The proxy has separate storage and never sees it ⇒ proxy state stays zero. Fix: an initialize() called via delegatecall.

sections.title: Initialize the staking contract (initializer, not constructor)

items.label: UUPS-upgradeable StakingV1 skeleton
items.note: `initializer` modifier lets the body run exactly once. Every parent must be init'd via its __X_init() (no constructors).

items.label: Stake / unstake logic
items.note: User must approve() the PROXY address first. Effects-before-interaction ordering guards reentrancy.

items.label: initializer vs onlyInitializing vs reinitializer
items.code:
modifier initializer          // top-level init, runs once (version 1)
function __Ownable_init() onlyInitializing  // parent init, callable during init
modifier reinitializer(uint64 v)  // one-time migration for version v
items.note: Parent __X_init() functions use onlyInitializing so they can be chained from your initializer without tripping the once-guard.

items.label: Minimal ERC-1967 proxy (fallback + delegatecall)
items.note: fallback catches any selector the proxy doesn't define and forwards raw calldata — that's how one proxy exposes the whole impl ABI, including v2 functions.

items.label: ERC-1967 reserved slots
items.code:
impl   = keccak256('eip1967.proxy.implementation') - 1
admin  = keccak256('eip1967.proxy.admin')          - 1
beacon = keccak256('eip1967.proxy.beacon')         - 1
items.note: Pseudo-random slots keep proxy bookkeeping from colliding with the impl's sequential slots 0,1,2... Also what lets Etherscan auto-detect proxies.

items.label: Upgrade = repoint the slot (guarded)
items.note: In UUPS this lives in the IMPLEMENTATION (via _authorizeUpgrade); in Transparent it lives in the proxy/ProxyAdmin.

items.label: Deploy behind proxy
items.note: Deploys impl + proxy and calls initialize() atomically. kind: "transparent" | "uups" | "beacon".

items.label: Upgrade + run migration atomically
items.note: Proxy address, balances and approvals are unchanged. reinitialize runs once via upgradeToAndCall.

items.label: Validate layout before shipping (dry run)
items.code:
await upgrades.validateUpgrade(proxyAddr, V2);   // throws on incompatible layout
await upgrades.forceImport(proxyAddr, F);        // adopt an existing proxy

items.label: Foundry equivalent
Beacon (fleet of identical vaults)
Golden rule: append-only
items.note: Never reorder, remove, or change the type of an existing variable — slot N must mean the same thing in every version, or you silently corrupt balances (storage collision).

items.label: Add v2 vars AFTER existing ones (consume the gap)
One-time data migration on upgrade
items.note: reinitializer(n) must strictly increase across versions; called via upgradeToAndCall so upgrade + migration land in one tx.

items.label: Namespaced storage (ERC-7201) to isolate layout
items.code:
/// @custom:storage-location erc7201:staking.main
struct MainStorage { mapping(address=>uint256) staked; uint256 rate; }
items.note: Modern alternative to __gap: puts all state under one hashed slot so inheritance order can't collide. Used by OZ v5 internals.

items.label: Disable initializers on the implementation
items.code: constructor() { _disableInitializers(); }
items.note: Blocks the uninitialized-impl takeover (Parity multisig freeze): otherwise an attacker calls initialize() on the impl, becomes owner, and can selfdestruct/brick it.

items.label: Guard the upgrade path (UUPS)
items.code:
function _authorizeUpgrade(address newImpl)
    internal override onlyOwner {}
items.note: If a new impl OMITS this / UUPSUpgradeable, upgradeability is permanently bricked. Always re-inherit UUPSUpgradeable and test the upgrade on a fork.

items.label: Upgrade key = drain key ⇒ multisig + timelock
items.code:
// transfer proxy admin / owner to a Gnosis Safe behind a TimelockController
await proxy.transferOwnership(timelockAddr);
items.note: Whoever can upgrade can swap in malicious logic and drain all staked funds. Timelock gives users a window to exit before a live upgrade.

items.label: Transparent vs UUPS vs Beacon
items.code:
Transparent: upgrade logic in proxy, routes by caller (admin vs user)
             + no bricking  - extra SLOAD/branch gas, needs ProxyAdmin
UUPS:        upgrade logic in impl (upgradeTo + _authorizeUpgrade)
             + cheapest/lightest proxy  - bricks if impl omits it
Beacon:      N proxies -> 1 beacon impl slot
             + upgrade whole fleet at once  - extra call/gas, SPOF
items.note: UUPS is the modern default. Beacon for factory-minted fleets (thousands of identical vaults).

tips: Constructors run against the IMPLEMENTATION's storage, so proxy state stays zero — always use an initialize() + `initializer` modifier and chain parents via __X_init().
Storage is append-only forever: never reorder/insert/retype existing vars. Reserve `uint256[50] __gap;` (or use ERC-7201 namespaced storage) and shrink the gap by the count of vars you add.
Call `_disableInitializers()` in the implementation's constructor — an uninitialized impl is the Parity-style takeover that permanently bricks the logic.
UUPS can be bricked: if a new implementation forgets to inherit UUPSUpgradeable / _authorizeUpgrade there's no upgrade path left. Test the upgrade itself on a mainnet fork.
Users must approve() the PROXY address, not the implementation — all funds and balances live in the proxy's storage.
The upgrade key can drain everything. Put it behind a multisig + TimelockController, never an EOA.
Run `upgrades.validateUpgrade()` (or the Foundry safety check) before every deploy to catch layout-incompatible changes.
Use reinitializer(n) with a strictly increasing version for one-time migrations, and call it atomically via upgradeToAndCall so upgrade + migration are one tx.
